observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE, direct the City Assessor to:

Provide a working schedule to the City Accounting Office showing the effects of changes made to the specific ledgers of all affected RPT taxpayers as a result of the database cleansing;

Thereafter, a coordinate with the City Accounting Office regarding subsequent changes made to the RPT database before finalizing the financial statements to ensure the accuracy and completeness of the account’s information.

Complete the database cleansing as soon as possible to ensure the accuracy of the data provided by the system;

Provide the City Accountant a database of RPT taxpayers which will serve as a subsidiary ledger to facilitate proper recording; and

Conduct further tests on the automated system to ensure the reliability of data processing and the accuracy of the information it provides.

In case there are issues discovered during the test, we also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Information and Communication Technology Office to immediately address them.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to:

Prepare necessary adjustments in the account and properly disclose relevant information associated with the adjustments upon receipt of working schedule by the City Assessor;

Create subsidiary ledgers in the eNGAS for RPT based on the database to be supplied by the City Assessor; and

Reconcile their records with the City Assessor to ensure accuracy and completeness of the account.
observations.managementAction:
The new system for the RPT is not yet fully deployed.

Migration of data is still ongoing.
observations.reason:
The new system for the RPT is not yet fully deployed.

Migration of data is still ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following:

General Services Officer to initiate proper disposal of the expired supplies totaling ₱1,750,932.00, and

The City Accountant to prepare necessary adjustments in the books after the disposal of expired office supplies with a total cost of ₱1,750,932.00 duly supported with necessary documentary requirements.
observations.managementAction:
Expired supplies are yet to be disposed.

Adjustment to be taken in the books after disposal.
observations.reason:
Expired supplies are yet to be disposed.

Awaiting actual disposal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the BAC, BAC Secretariat, and TWG to refund total amount of received BAC Honorarium (net of taxes withheld) derived from liquidated damages.
observations.managementAction: The BAC will meet and discuss the matter.
observations.reason: Collection from liable officers is yet to be made as of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

Exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables such as sending demand letters to last known addresses to private individuals and employees; confirmation letters to various organizations whether NGOs/POs, Government agencies; and provide a status to the City Auditor’s Office.



Establish/maintain and apply sound internal control procedures on the recording and collection of receivables.

Conduct further assessment as to collectability or impairment.  Thereafter, provide provision for doubtful accounts for the accounts determined to have a no and/or low collection rate.
observations.managementAction:
The CAO to exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables such as sending demand letters to last known addresses to private individuals and employees; confirmation letters to various organizations whether NGOs/POs, Government agencies

To maintain and apply sound internal control.

To provide provision for doubtful accounts after assessment.
observations.reason:
Demand letters for some accounts could not be issued due to incomplete debtor details, with the accounts already classified as dormant.

For the housing loan, the Fisherman’s Village Committee are coordinating with those grantees and collections were still being monitored.

observations.recommendation: With the foregoing, we recommended, and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to submit the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements to the Audit Team within the prescribed period under Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 to facilitate the timely audit of accounts.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2024 AAR

observations.recommendation: Based on the foregoing, we reiterated our prior year’s unresolved audit recommendations, and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (LCE): (a) in coordination with the City Accountant, ensure that a cash advance is granted only after the liquidation of the previous one by requiring a certification from the latter confirming its settlement/accounting before releasing the new one; (b) limit the cash advance granted to the maximum coverage of the fidelity bond of AOs or apply for a higher fidelity bond to safeguard government funds; and (c) ensure that applicable rules and regulations governing cash advances are strictly followed. We further recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the City Accountant, submit a written explanation regarding the accumulated CAs granted on the last working day and submit their corresponding liquidation reports to the Auditor’s Office.
observations.managementAction: The Management explained that the simultaneous granting of CAs beyond the maximum allowed to be drawn was due to the lack of manpower available to act as SDOs, and asserted to do their best to comply with the applicable rules and regulations. Additionally, they promised to submit the liquidation reports as soon as possible.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to comply with the applicable rules and regulations governing cash advances. Additionally, they promised to submit the liquidation reports as soon as possible.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books to reflect actual inventories on hand; (b) City General Services (CGS) Officer to adopt/apply the perpetual inventory method and ensure proper safekeeping of inventories to minimize the risk of possible loss or wastage of government resources; and (c) CGS Officer and City Accountant to ensure coordination between their offices for reconciliation of their reports and for the timely recording of receipts and issuances of inventories.
observations.managementAction: The Management explained that the noted discrepancy was due to the timing difference in recording the inventory issuances between the City GSO and Accounting Office. Further, the City Accountant asserted that he will closely coordinate with CGSO as regards the issuance of the inventory items and the adoption of Perpetual Inventory Method and with the reconciliation of their reports.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to settle this.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the LCE direct the: (a) Inventory Committee, CGSO and CAccO to coordinate in reconciling the balances between the Inventory Report, Registry of Public Infrastructure and the accounting records such as the general and the subsidiary ledgers to reflect the correct balance of the PPE account; (b) City Accountant to (i) recognize losses of PPE for non-existing/missing PPEs not presented upon demand and the corresponding receivable equivalent to the PPEs’ depreciated replacement cost; (ii) provide depreciation for all depreciable Property, Plant and Equipment; (iii) trace the variance noted between the balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements and the lapsing schedules; and (c) City General Service Officer to submit the RPCPPE in accordance with the Section 124 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume 1.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2024 AAR

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant (a) set up the payable account based on the receipt of goods or services rendered to ensure validity of the obligations and use appropriate terms to avoid confusion as to the nature of the transactions; (b) maintain subsidiary ledgers for each creditor pursuant to Section 111 of P.D. No. 1445; and (c) improve the monitoring of accounts in accordance with IPSAS 29 on the proper recognition and derecognition of liabilities to avoid delay and frequent corrections, and uncertainty on the accuracy of balances; and (d) ensure complete documentation of payable accounts for accuracy of recorded transactions.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to work on this.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s and Management agreed that the City Accountant: (a) require the implementing heads of barangay to (i) exhaust all efforts to coordinate with the previous Barangay Administration to facilitate submission of liquidation documents for the long-unliquidated financial assistance; (ii) religiously submit all liquidation documents to the City Accounting Office for all financial assistance received; (iii) immediately refund the unutilized balance of the financial assistance to the City Treasurer’s Office after the purpose of the granting of which have been completed; and (iv) strictly observe the proper procedures in the liquidation of financial assistance and ensure completeness of documents upon submission to the accounting personnel in-charge of the books of the barangay; (b) ensure that all required liquidation documents for previously granted financial assistance have been submitted and issue a Certification that funds previously transferred to the Implementing Agency (IA) has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before processing the grant/transfer of another financial assistance; and (c) closely monitor the compliance of the recipient barangays on the submission of liquidation documents within the prescribed period. We further recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant: (a) recognize in the books appropriate amounts of impairment losses for the non-collectability of the dormant receivables; and (b) start the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts based on the guidelines and procedures provided by COA Circular 2023-008, dated August 17, 2023.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to settle this.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated, with modification, our prior year’s audit recommendation, that the LCE: (a) consider delegating the responsibility of countersigning checks to the City Administrator, as provided in R.A. No. 7160, to minimize delays in the payment process; and (b) adhere to the timeline of activities outlined in the QMS disbursement process.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office endeavors to provide the City Mayor’s Office the list of checks under their custody in order to keep and monitor the disbursement documents that will also aid them to prevent possible misplacement or loss.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to work on this.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the City Planning and Development Office and the City Engineer’s Office to review and enhance its planning and monitoring strategies and strictly adhere to deadlines in the implementation of projects to ensure complete implementation and prompt delivery of services. We further recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the City Treasurer’s Office to release the checks for the Aid to Barangay projects earlier to give the barangays enough time to observe proper implementation of the projects and timely utilization of funds.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2024 AAR

observations.recommendation: We commended the City Government and its officials for being one of the LGUs with the Best Ecological Solid Waste Management as recognized by DENR and its responsiveness to the environment awareness cause. However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board to: (a) review and update its 10-year SWM Plan as required by RA No. 9003; (b) enforce the proper wastes segregation at source and fully implement the “No Segregation, No Collection” Policy; (c) strengthen information and education campaigns on the hazards of open burning, open dumpsites and other ill practices prohibited under Section 48 of RA 9003; (d) ensure that wastes are properly collected from households and transported to MRFs to avoid improper disposal along the roads and other public places; and (e) propose worthy PPAs in the City’s AIP that can actually be realized with the estimated budget to eliminate huge gaps between the amount of the planned projects versus the approved budget that may indicate poor planning.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to work on this.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive Officer direct the (a) CDRRMC to include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous year in the LDRRMFIP; and (b) City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the affected Trust-Liability accounts in order to conform with the provisions of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 for the proper monitoring of the Unexpended DRRMF following their five-year limitation.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the CDRRMC to (i) monitor the implementation of the LDRRMP to optimize the utilization of its LDRRMF in order to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in the community and disaster risks, including projected climate risks, (ii) submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council (LDC) to the COA auditor of the LGU as mandated in COA Circular 2012-002. We further recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant to (i) record purchase of welfare/relief goods and other expendable items to inventory account and issuances thereof to the appropriate expense accounts; and (ii) maintain corresponding Subsidiary Ledger for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment set forth under COA Circular 2014-002 and COA Circular 2012-002, respectively; and (iii) to remit all unutilized cash donations to the Bureau of the Treasury (BTr) or to the source agency in accordance with applicable rules and regulations on cash donations received.
observations.managementAction: The Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF is regularly prepared by the City Accountant’s Office (CAccO) on a monthly basis, and a copy is furnished to the City DRRM Office. It was also mentioned that the CAccO was not aware that the said report should be submitted to the Auditor’s Office. Nonetheless, the recommendation of the Audit Team regarding the submission of the report on or before the 15th day after the end of every month was duly noted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive: (a) instruct the Planning Officer to ensure that proposed projects have undergone thorough study, is adequate planning and review prior to approval and implementation thereof; (b) submit justification on the continuous non-usage of the infrastructures and the postponement of the procurement of the cremation machine; and (c) schedule a meeting with the head of Building Maintenance, Engineering and Government Service Office to assign the specific task of maintaining the infrastructures, so that government property will be taken cared of properly and further deterioration will be prevented.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to work on this.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Local School Board: (a) establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF among schools and incorporate these PPAs in the appropriate budget format to ensure project monitoring and ascertain the efficient and effective utilization of SEF in accordance with its intended purpose; and (b) continually utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020.
observations.reason: The Management is continuously exerting effort to work on this.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Treasurer to investigate the variance noted between the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and the RPT delinquency report and make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements. We also reiterated that the Local Chief Executive require the City Treasurer to ensure that necessary enhancements are in place in the RPT system in order to make it a more reliable source of data for the City’s Real Property Tax.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) and City Accountant admitted the need to improve the real property tax system and will employ further efforts to provide a more reliable source of data for the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2024 AAR

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to:
(i) send demand letters to AOs who are still in service to cause
immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances; and (ii)
exhaust all remedies to enforce settlement/ liquidation of
unliquidated cash advances of individuals who are already deceased,
separated from service, and had transferred to other government
agencies. Otherwise, request for write-off in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2023-008. Also, refrain from granting additional cash
advances to any official or employee unless the previous cash
advance has already been settled and ensure to implement the
liquidation period prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002; and (iii)
enjoin the Municipal Accountant and the OIC – Municipal
Treasurer to establish controls in the monitoring of their incoming
and outgoing documents so that the former could timely record the
grant of cash advances in the books of accounts and avoid drawing
correction entries in the RUCA.

(i) (i) (i) Not In CY 2024, the Municipal Accountant confirmed that

AOs. (ii) (ii) (ii) Not Audit of the Advances account as at December 31,
Implemented 2024 disclosed that outstanding cash advances totaling
₱4,011,356.55 remained unliquidated due to failure to send demand
letters to AOs, granting of additional cash advance to employees with
previous unliquidated balances, and failure to enforce liquidation.
From other actions: Items (i) and (ii) were reiterated in Part II of this
Report.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Treasurer has yet to send demand letters to concerned
AOs; the MAO and MTO coordination gaps and staffing constraints
prevented timely liquidation and monitoring. Outstanding balances
remain unliquidated.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management:

1) Submit the lacking documents 
2) Justify the variance noted in the land areas in the attached documents
3) Facilitate transfer the ownership in the Tax Declaration in favor of the Municipal Government;
4) Expedite the titling of the land property; and
5) Strictly follow existing laws and guidelines for subsequent purchases of land and avoid paying the owner/s of the land before the documents are complete.
observations.reason: The process of titling the land property is very tedious and it requires a longer time. The Municipal Assessors Office already started the processing for the titling of land but it is not yet release.
No collection letters yet issued to the separated officials.
“The Municipal engineering department has conducted partial inventory on Road networks.”
“The following accounts cannot be retrieved despite the efforts of the office to locate details of the said accounts.”

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to:

a. Exert more efforts in the retrieval of prior years’ records to identify the accountable officers/employees/debtors or concerned agencies liable for the unaccounted balance of ₱1.639 million; and

b. Send demand letters to the identified accountable officer/employee or recipient agency of other receivables.
observations.reason: Ongoing take up by Management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct the:

GSO to:

Prepare and secure the necessary transfer documents including a Sangguniang Panlungsod Resolution authorizing the LCE to donate the properties, Deed of Donation, and other required supporting documents to initiate the transfer of ownership to beneficiary agencies.  Thereafter, furnish the City Accountant with copies of the said documents to serve as basis for the proper derecognition of transferred assets from the books of CGSFLU; and



Henceforth, immediately prepare transfer documents of projects of the same nature, for proper and timely adjustments in the book of accounts.






B. City Accountant to:

Upon receipt of the necessary transfer documents, prepare the adjusting entry or restate financial statements to reflect proper balances of affected accounts.




Henceforth, ensure the proper derecognition of assets that have been physically transferred to beneficiary agencies upon receipt of the necessary transfer documents, for fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements;



Prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for components of local road networks to facilitate proper adjustments in the event of rehabilitation or replacement;



Coordinate with the City Engineer to determine the estimated cost of replaced road networks and thereafter prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reflect a more reliable balance of Road Network account; and



Request cost estimation of replaced assets from the City Engineer for future rehabilitation projects to ensure immediate adjustment in the books.



C. City Engineer to:

Provide the City Accountant and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of the road components for road network projects in accordance with Item VII.c of COA Circular 2015-008; and



Immediately coordinate with and assist the City Accountant in the valuation of affected assets resulting from rehabilitation projects implemented by CGSFLU.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the City Accountant to:
Prepare and record the necessary adjusting entry to correct the balance of the Office Supplies Inventory account as follows:
Dr: Office Supplies Expense		₱2,885,826.62
Cr: Office Supplies Inventory		₱2,885,826.62
Analyze the records related to the Welfare Goods for Distribution, Agricultural and Marine Supplies for Distribution, and Drugs and Medicines Inventory accounts to determine proper adjustments in the books;
Based on the results of the analysis, prepare and record the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balances of the affected inventory accounts in the financial statements;
Conduct a thorough assessment of the issues or challenges encountered in the implementation and day-to-day use of the eNGAS, particularly in relation to inventory management, to properly address these concerns and enable CGSFLU to fully maximize the benefits of the system; and
Ensure the conduct of reconciliation and update inventory account balances, if necessary, prior to the finalization of the financial statements.
Require the Department Heads of the GSO, CSWDO, CAO, and CHO to properly plan and schedule the physical count of inventories under their custody and ensure the timely submission of inventory reports to the Accounting Office to allow adequate time for reconciliation with the accounting records.

We recommended that LCE direct the:
City Accountant to:
Strategically assess and manage staff workloads, and prioritize the designation of qualified personnel who shall be responsible for the proper implementation of the capitalization threshold, and the preparation, maintenance, and safekeeping of all reportorial requirements mandated under COA Circular 2022-004;
Prepare the necessary entries to adjust affected accounts as discussed, upon receipt of the RSPI from the City GSO:
Prepare and maintain Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card to monitor Semi-Expendables maintained by CGSFLU.
City GSO to:
Prepare ICS for all serviceable semi-expendables issued in previous years as discussed;
Prepare and maintain Semi-Expendable Property Card and Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued to adequately monitor acquired and issued semi-expendables; and
Prepare and furnish copies of the Report of Semi-Expendable Property Issued and Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable Property to the City Accountant, to serve as basis for the proper recording and reporting of semi-expendable items in the FS.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Conduct a comprehensive review of previously recorded AP for CY 2024 and make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the overstatement of Accounts Payable and the understatement of Due to BIR; and

Strictly adhere to Section 7 of BIR RR No. 4-2024 by ensuring that creditable withholding taxes are computed, deducted, and recognized at the time of accrual or upon recording of procurement transactions as AP, whichever occurs first. This will align financial practices with existing tax laws and prevent misstatements in the financial records.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Trace the ₱14,006,606.68 reconciling item classified under timing difference and apply the necessary adjustments in both the SCBAA and the reconciliation statement. These adjustments must be duly supported with appropriate and sufficient documentation and submitted to the AT for verification, and

Thereafter, ensure that the reconciliation of the SCBAA with the SFPer is verifiable and consistent with the information presented in other financial statements, such as the SFPos, to promote completeness and fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Prepare the adjusting entry to correct misstated accounts brought about by the erroneous recording of prior period adjustments.


Present the adjustments in recommendation letter “a”, as reconciling items under timing difference in the reconciliation of Statement of Financial Performance and SCBAA, and

Ensure strict adherence to the accrual basis of accounting by recognizing transactions in the period when the related goods or services are actually received or rendered, rather than in the year of budget approval or obligation.

We recommended that the LCE direct all Offices and Department Heads to ensure the:

Proper safeguarding and timely submission of supporting documents at each stage of transaction processing, and


Immediate processing of payments upon completion of the acceptance and inspection of goods or services to facilitate the timely recording and reporting of transactions.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Ensure the complete and accurate disclosure of all relevant information, as discussed above, in the NFS; and


Verify the correctness of reported balances to ensure full compliance with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS 1 in the ensuing year.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:

CDRRMO to specify the activities to be charged against approved PPAs during budget preparation through the formulation of detailed project proposals, ensuring that only duly identified activities are charged against the appropriated funds; and

CBO to closely monitor budget availability by expense class by deducting the approved budgets of contracts already authorized for processing. This will ensure that only expenditures within the available budget are approved. If funds are insufficient, the implementing departments must submit a request for additional funding, utilizing savings through an appropriation ordinance.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CDRRMO to:

Submit liquidation reports for the distribution of the procured DRR supplies. These reports should be duly acknowledged by the recipients, include the dates of distribution, and be supported by photographs, especially for activities related to relief operations. Such documentation will serve as a basis for evaluating the effectiveness of the implemented programs, and

Develop and implement standard operating procedures for the distribution of supplies, incorporating clear and detailed guidelines on proper documentation, to ensure strict compliance in the implementation of all future PPAs involving the distribution of goods to constituents.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CDRRMO to:

Identify the accountable officials involved in the procurement of insurance coverage for CGSFLU personnel who are not officially recognized as volunteers and responders of the CDRRMO, and justify the necessity of such procurement by providing an appropriate legal basis;


Ensure that all qualified and officially recognized volunteers and responders of the CDRRMO are provided with personal accident insurance in compliance with Item VIII (C) of NDRRMC Memorandum No. 64, series of 2021, and

Discontinue the provision of insurance coverage, chargeable against the CDRRMF, for individuals who are not officially recognized as volunteers and responders of the CDRRMO, consistent with Item 3.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the:

Local School Board (LSB) thoroughly evaluate the nature of the transactions to be charged against the SEF, and ensure that only those which qualify as allowable expenses under RA No. 5447 and other related issuances are recommended for approval;

Payees refund the travel and training expenses totaling ₱50,330.94, and

Payees submit the Certificate of Appearance, Certificate of Travel Completed, Boarding Pass, and Official Receipt of Registration.

We recommended the LCE direct the City Accountant to monitor transactions that are not recorded as cash advances but are disbursed in a manner similar to cash advances, ensuring proper liquidation and complete documentation to support such transactions.

We recommended that LCE direct the CAgO and CEO to:

Submit periodic updates indicating the percentage of physical completion or service delivery of each project, as mandated in the Quarterly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project Implementation, avoiding the use of vague terms such as “ongoing” or “for implementation” without measurable indicators;

Develop a more proactive and timely planning process that includes the early identification, prioritization, and validation of eligible programs and projects aligned with the objectives of RA Nos. 7171 and 8240, to ensure the prompt and efficient use of available funds, and

Conduct a thorough pre-implementation assessment to anticipate and resolve potential issues such as land ownership, right-of-way, permit, and documentation requirements. Coordination with agencies such as the OCA, DENR, and relevant regulatory bodies must be initiated early in the project planning phase.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to ensure compliance with Section 23.4.2.4 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, and to enable an accurate and meaningful assessment of a prospective bidder’s experience, the BAC should clearly specify the major categories of work in Item 5.2 of the BDS to help safeguard the integrity of the procurement process and contribute to the successful and quality implementation of government projects.

We recommended that LCE direct the BAC to strictly comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and relevant GPPB issuances, particularly in validating the technical, legal, and financial qualifications of prospective suppliers, contractors, or consultants before awarding contracts. Specifically, the BAC should ensure that only bidders with the appropriate and consistent business scope, as indicated in their business permits, licenses, and other legal documents, are considered eligible.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:

CBO to record obligations in budget records only upon the execution of perfected POs or contracts, in order to properly monitor the validity of the corresponding appropriations, and

BAC to implement measures to expedite the procurement process by ensuring that all necessary actions are promptly undertaken at each stage. This is particularly crucial for transactions processed through SVP, where timelines are essential to prevent unnecessary delays and to ensure that contracts are perfected within the validity of the budget period.

We recommended that the LCE direct all Offices and Departments to:

Properly plan activities to provide specific terms and conditions in PRs and POs, and

Ensure that POs are properly and completely accomplished, particularly the delivery terms and delivery periods, to provide a clear basis for contract enforcement and to safeguard CGSFLU’s interests in cases of partial performance;

Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE require the Contract Termination Review Committee to conduct a thorough evaluation and documentation process in accordance with Annex “I” of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 before recommending contract termination.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Submit the following documents related to the payment of HEA:

List of health facilities and other health-related establishments involved in the COVID-19 response to which each recipient was assigned;
The categorization of BHWs, indicating whether they are duly accredited and/or registered in the DOH National BHW Registry, and
Daily Time Records (DTRs) of each recipient, and
Conduct a thorough review and verification of the transactions recorded as cash advances to determine their nature. For transactions confirmed to be legitimate cash advances, ensure the submission of complete and timely liquidation reports, as required by COA Circular No. 97-002. For transactions identified as misclassified, such as reimbursements or other types of disbursements, prepare a detailed report outlining these misclassifications and submit it for further verification.

We recommended that the SP revisit, reassess, and amend Section 3(A)(07) of City Tax Ordinance No. 24-001 to ensure that the deadline for the payment of fees, as well as any extensions, comply with Section 167 of the LGC.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Immediately compute the taxes due on the taxable portion of the SRI disbursed in CY 2024, withhold the corresponding taxes from the concerned CGSFLU personnel, and remit the taxes withheld to the BIR, and

Develop and implement a strategic system for the computation of taxes on compensation by formulating a centralized database that allows for real-time adjustments, particularly at year-end, to ensure the accurate and timely withholding of taxes throughout the year.

We recommended that LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Adjust the withholding tax computation for the affected transaction and immediately remit the corresponding tax deficiency of ₱44,284.37 to the BIR;

Amend and properly reflect the correct income payment and withholding tax remittances in the BIR forms 2306 and 2307 to accurately disclose the gross income payments and corresponding taxes withheld, and

Henceforth, compute withholding taxes based on gross income payments representing the cost of goods procured, and deduct any LD from the net amount payable to the supplier, after taxes have been withheld.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:

City HRMO to:

Review and revise the standard provisions of its contract template for COS personnel to ensure compliance with applicable tax laws and regulations, particularly RMC No. 51-2018. Specifically, the contract should be amended to reflect CGSFLU's obligation, as a designated withholding agent, to withhold creditable withholding taxes from service providers who meet the ₱250,000.00 gross income threshold, and

Require all COS and JO personnel to submit their Income Tax Returns (ITRs) for CY 2024 and furnish the COA Office.

City Accountant to instruct the personnel in charge of tax computations and withholding to monitor the annual gross receipts of COS and JOs and withhold the prescribed taxes accordingly, in compliance with existing tax laws and regulations.

We recommended that the LCE direct the GFPS, through the assistance of the CBO, to: use CGSFLU Annual Budget as the basis in allocating GAD Budget in compliance with item C.3 (1) of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

Moreover, we also recommended that the LCE cause the GFPS to consider the HGDG tool in developing GAD PPAs. This will ensure compliance with the minimum GAD fund requirement and enable CGSFLU to effectively and sufficiently address gender issues and concerns.

We recommended that the LCE direct the GFPS and implementing offices of the GPB to:

Implement necessary measures to enhance project proposals development by:

Ensuring that activities included are responsive to purpose and objectives;
Designing effective  execution strategies  before its approval through consultations from beneficiaries or prospective service providers; and
Accurately estimating physical and cost requirements through surveys to beneficiaries – securing adequate responses, past experiences, and other relevant data;

Ensure timely implementation of GAD PPAs to target beneficiaries and establish contingency plans, if necessary; and

Religiously monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs through submission of status reports from IOs or holding regular meetings so that challenges, if there are any, could be resolved immediately.
We also recommended that the LCE review CGSFLU’s processes for budget amendments to improve efficiency and timeliness, ensuring that IOs and target beneficiaries have enough time to execute programs and provide requirements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CENRO to:

Expedite the completion of requirements for the approval of the necessary amendments to the City Sanitation Code so that funds will be used for their intended purpose; and

Provide timely reports on collection of fees and services rendered to the City Accounting Office regularly to ensure the completeness of records.

We recommended that the LCE, as the HoPE, direct the following:

BAC to comply with the prescribed period for issuing NOAs as outlined in Section 37.1.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to prevent contributing delays in project implementation, facilitating timely delivery and completion of projects, thereby enabling constituents to access and enjoy the benefits derived from these projects at an earlier stage.


The BAC and its TWG to judiciously conduct post-qualification proceedings by thoroughly verifying, validating and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the LCB.

We also recommended that the LCE, as HoPE, enter into a contract with the winning bidder within 10 calendar days from receipt of the latter of the NOA pursuant to Section 37.2.1 of the Revised IRR, ensuring timely implementation of projects, thereby enabling constituents to promptly benefit from them.

Lastly, we recommended that LCE comply with the provisions outlined in Section 37.4.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 regarding the effectiveness of issued NTPs to streamline the overall project implementation process, thereby preventing any potential delays in the completion of projects.

We recommended that the LCE, as HOPE, oversee and instruct the designated officials responsible for preparing the source documents, including PRs, POs, RFQs, and AIRs, to ensure the accurate and comprehensive inclusion of all required information in adherence to the guidelines specified in the MNGAS for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184. This promotes transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency and economy in the procurement process.


We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the BAC, streamline the procurement process for SVP to align with its intended purpose. This should be done by developing a detailed process flowchart that includes specific timelines for each step, starting from document preparation and review to signing and issuance. Additionally, we recommended implementing regular reviews of the process to identify any bottlenecks and areas for improvement, ensuring greater efficiency in the procurement procedures.


We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to clearly specify in the RFQs sent to participating bidders the stage of the procurement process at which these requirements must be submitted to ensure a proper assessment of the bidder’s compliance. Failure to submit these documents within the designated period should result in automatic disqualification of the bidder.


We recommended that the LCE, in collaboration with the BAC, develop internal guidelines regarding the requirement for posting of PS for goods under the SVP modality. This should include setting a defined range of ABCs that would necessitate the posting of PS. This measure aims to   protect the City Government’s interests in cases of contract termination by the suppliers without valid reason/s


Furthermore, the Audit Team emphasized its recommendation on the proper accomplishment of all details embodied in the PO, particularly the delivery schedule for procured items. This practice is essential to enable the City Government to legally enforce penalty clauses and calculate liquidated damages for any delays caused by suppliers, thereby providing an additional source of income that could potentially be used to finance the City Government’s PPAs.

We recommended that the LCE promptly inform both the SA and ATL within 10 days after the award of the IP or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards are already posted, thereby facilitating the validation of the same.

We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the City Engineer, direct the Project Engineers to:

A. Closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Engineering Office to submit to the Audit Team a written justification to address the identified deficiencies, accompanied by evidence substantiating their claims, to ascertain the legality and validity of the infrastructure projects, as well as the associated procurement activities.

We recommended that the LCE require the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Engineer and BAC Chairman, to facilitate the immediate submission of the identified lacking documents for audit purposes. This is to ensure the legality, validity, and propriety of the paid transactions in adherence to the existing laws, rules and regulations, and to prevent the issuance of Notice of Suspensions

We recommended that the LCE direct:

The City Budget Officer to ensure monitoring of the full implementation of the projects appropriated under the 20% DF in order to address the needs of the constituents such as access to improved health, social, agriculture and economic services; and

The City Development Council to verify the procurement-and-implementation readiness of the proposed 20% DF PPAs.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Budget Officer to:

Revert the savings from completed projects under the Share from Tobacco Excise Tax Fund (RA No. 7171 and 8240) to the unappropriated surplus and re-appropriate the same to new PPAs allowed under the said fund

Seek the assistance of implementing offices like the City Engineering Office and Office of the City Agriculturist to come up with a menu of new PPAs that will advance the self-reliance of tobacco farmers and promote the development of the tobacco industry.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer to subject all the contractors/suppliers to an immediate examination of their books of accounts to determine the appropriate amount of taxes due from them. Additionally, if deemed correct, require the City Treasurer to cause the collection of deficient business taxes amounting to at least ₱1,205,143.95, along with associated penalties and interests, resulting from their under-declared gross sales/receipts.

We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the Sangguniang Panlungsod prioritize and streamline the enactment of the Revenue Code of 2023, in accordance with the city's mandate as provided under RA 7160, or the LGC of 1991, to maximize the city's taxing power, thereby enabling the City Government to generate additional revenues, which can then be utilized for the implementation of its PPAs.

We recommended that the LCE, direct the City Assessor to:

Provide a working schedule to the City Accounting Office showing the effects of changes made to the specific ledgers of all affected RPT taxpayers as a result of the database cleansing;

Thereafter, a coordinate with the City Accounting Office regarding subsequent changes made to the RPT database before finalizing the financial statements to ensure the accuracy and completeness of the account’s information.

Complete the database cleansing as soon as possible to ensure the accuracy of the data provided by the system;

Provide the City Accountant a database of RPT taxpayers which will serve as a subsidiary ledger to facilitate proper recording; and

Conduct further tests on the automated system to ensure the reliability of data processing and the accuracy of the information it provides.

In case there are issues discovered during the test, we also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Information and Communication Technology Office to immediately address them.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to:

Prepare necessary adjustments in the account and properly disclose relevant information associated with the adjustments upon receipt of working schedule by the City Assessor;

Create subsidiary ledgers in the eNGAS for RPT based on the database to be supplied by the City Assessor; and

Reconcile their records with the City Assessor to ensure accuracy and completeness of the account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive make the representations and collect from the concerned suppliers and contractors at least ₱189,127.01 representing the deficiency in the computed liquidated damages in accordance with Section 3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the PRAISE and LFC Committee to establish internal rules for the suitable criteria or appropriate guidelines in the selection of deserving employees who actually made exemplary suggestions, innovations or accomplishments which contributed to the efficiency, economy or improvement of the government operations as evidenced by the awards received by City Government.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following:

General Services Officer to initiate proper disposal of the expired supplies totaling ₱1,750,932.00, and

The City Accountant to prepare necessary adjustments in the books after the disposal of expired office supplies with a total cost of ₱1,750,932.00 duly supported with necessary documentary requirements.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the BAC, BAC Secretariat, and TWG to refund total amount of received BAC Honorarium (net of taxes withheld) derived from liquidated damages.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:

Submit proof of remittance of the collection from the 4,040 pieces of BIR Form No. 006 as presented in the Annex C and thereafter, update the RAAF accordingly and submit the same to the Audit Team.


Conduct cash examination and turn-over of cash accountabilities of resigned deputized collectors and furnish the Audit Team copies of turn-over documents.

We recommended the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the appropriate BIR Certificate of Registration allowing the suppliers to engage in the industries where the sale of the goods it supplied is covered, and to thereafter, ensure that all emergency procurements are awarded to a technically, legally, and financially capable supplier, contractor, distributor, or consultant in compliance with the aforementioned provisions.  In case of huge quantities required by emergencies, procure from multiple suppliers who are legally, technically, and financially capable, in the absence of a single supplier with such capabilities, instead of transacting with suppliers whose line of business is doubtful.

We recommended the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to suspend the works of the six projects and ascertain the duration of suspension is reasonable and in accordance with the provisions of RA 9184;

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

Exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables such as sending demand letters to last known addresses to private individuals and employees; confirmation letters to various organizations whether NGOs/POs, Government agencies; and provide a status to the City Auditor’s Office.


Establish/maintain and apply sound internal control procedures on the recording and collection of receivables.

Conduct further assessment as to collectability or impairment.  Thereafter, provide provision for doubtful accounts for the accounts determined to have a no and/or low collection rate.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the management to determine the amount to be collected for the medical waste treatment facility services that should be at par with the current fees being collected by private accredited waste treaters or at least breakeven with the expenses related to the hospital waste management. Thereafter, start the process so that said income-generating activity be passed into an ordinance.

1.9. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant
to coordinate with the City Treasurer and other related Officers to come up with a
plan on how deal with the bulk transactions of the City in a more efficient way to
facilitate reconciliation on time and therefore provide the Audit Team a copy of the
Bank Reconciliation Report in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No.
96-011 and thus help us facilitate the timely audit of accounts.

1.10. The City Accountant promised to work closely with the City Treasurer to ensure
timely submission of source documents needed for the reconciliation process.

2.7. In such instances, pursuant to Section 8.0 of TC No. 02-2019, should defalcations,
shortages, or any unrelieved losses occur on the account of a bonded officer, and no relief
from accountability is granted by this Commission, the payment from the fidelity fund
will be limited to the extent of the approved bond amount or the actual amount of the
loss, whichever is lower.

2.8. These deficiencies in the granting of CAs may potentially expose government
funds to risks of misappropriation or loss.

2.9. In view of the foregoing, we hereby reiterated our prior year’s unresolved
audit recommendations, with modifications and additions, that the Local Chief
Executive to (a) cease the practice of granting simultaneous CAs and only grant new
CAs upon complete liquidation or settlement of the previously issued ones; (b) limit
the CA granted to the maximum coverage of the Fidelity Bond of the SDO or apply
for a higher Fidelity Bond to appropriately safeguard Government funds, and (c)
instruct the City Treasurer to implement appropriate and strict internal controls
regarding the liquidation process of CAs so as to ensure that the previous grants
under the possession and custody of every SDO are properly liquidated or settled
before a new CA shall be granted.

2.10. The City Treasurer appreciates the audit observation and, together with the
accounting department, is committed to complying with the recommended appropriate
measures and administering the corrective actions to improve our internal controls and
process for proper accountability.

3.13. We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
require the City Accountant and City General Services (CGS) Officer to continually
ensure coordination between their offices for the subsequent reconciliation of their
reports and to conduct and re-evaluate the cost and condition of the inventoried
goods and thus reflect their fair value in the financial statements.

4.8. We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
(a) strictly set a deadline to all claimants for the submission of documents to the City
Accountant for all unsettled/unpaid lawful expenditures and obligations for
recording in the books and ensure that the set date will give ample time to the City
Accountant for the preparation of financial statements so as to comply with the submission thereof to this Commission within the prescribed period; and (b) require the City Planning and Development Officer to consider proper timing on the release of funds to barangays for the implementation of projects and set the deadline for the submission of their liquidation reports.

5.10. We recommended and management agreed that the LCE direct the: (a)
Inventory Committee shall furnish the original Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Auditor’s Office, in strict
adherence to the provisions set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January
31, 2020; and (b) Disposal and Appraisal Committee shall execute the requisite
actions for the prompt disposal of condemned properties, in accordance with
Section 79 of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

7.7. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive issue a directive to all
Department Heads, who are in-charge in the implementation of programs/projects
of National Government Agencies, to: (a) complete the implementation of the
delayed/unfinished programs and projects; (b) determine the feasibility of its timely
implementation and inform the Source Agencies of any problem/difficulties on its
implementation, otherwise return the funds to the national government, if not
viable.

7.8. The Management affirms its commitment to ensuring the proper and efficient
utilization of these funds.

7.9. Furthermore, the Department Heads will remain in close coordination with
National Government Agencies to ensure full compliance of all program requirements
and to optimize the impact of the livelihood projects for the intended beneficiaries.

8.21. We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief
Executive require the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Treasurer to
investigate the variance noted between the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and the
RPT delinquency report and make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly
the accounts in the financial statements.

8.22. We also reiterated that the Local Chief Executive require the City Treasurer
to ensure that necessary improvements are in place in the RPT system in order to
make it a more reliable source of data for the City’s Real Property Tax.

8.23. The City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) admitted the need to improve the real property
tax system and will continually work with the City Accounting Office (CAccO) to
properly settle the RPT transaction accounts as well as monitor and provide updates on
the progress of the proposed system enhancement for a more accurate reporting.

9.18. In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our prior year’s unresolved audit
recommendations and management agreed, with modifications and additions, that
the Local Chief Executive require the City Planning and Development Coordinator
and the City Engineer to carefully review and enhance its planning and monitoring
strategies and strictly adhere to the deadlines in the implementation of the PPAs
ensuring complete implementation and timely delivery of benefits derived from
these PPAs.

10.24. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Officer direct the following
actions: (a) The CDRRMC should include, under a separate caption, the list of
projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the
LDRRMFIP, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12,
2012; (b) Monitor the implementation of the LDRRMP to optimize the utilization of
the LDRRMF in order to mitigate the socio-economic and environmental impacts of
disasters in the community and address disaster risks, including projected climate
risks; (c) Instruct the City Accountant to maintain a corresponding Subsidiary
Ledger for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment, and (d) Record
the purchase of expendable items to the inventory account and/or classify the items
to the appropriate expense accounts once exhausted, as stated in NDRRMC-DBM-
DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 dated March 25, 2013.

10.25. The City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office together with the City
Accounting Office will continuously monitor proper planning and implementation of the
City’s DRRM Plan to mitigate socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in
the community and observe proper accounting procedures in compliance with the
recommendation of the Commission.

11.13. In view of the foregoing, we hereby recommended that Management: (a)
conduct sufficient intervention to address the continuous hiring of JOs and limit
essential duties in core offices to permanent and casual employees, and (b)
continually adhere to the set limitations of Section 325 of the Local Government
Code of 1991 (RA No. 7160).

11.14. The City Human Resource Management Office assured that it will continue to
uphold its commitment to service efficiency for the benefit of all employees and
constituents.

12.25. Consequently, we recommended and management agreed, for the
improvement of the utilization of the GAD Funds, that the LCE instruct the GAD
GFPS TWG and the City Planning and Development Coordinator to: (a) improve
on the planning, budgeting and implementation of the GAD PPAs so as to avoid the
incurrence of non-implemented PPAs and do not exceeded the cost of actual
implementation over the approved budgeted amounts; (b) adhere with the
provisions under Item C.7 (2) of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-
01 with regards to the submission of an adjusted GPB reflecting the change in GAD
PPAs; (c) submit to this Office, within the required timeframe, the Annual GPB
within 5 working days upon receipt of the approved plan from the regulating
agencies, and (d) institute the use of the HGDG for evaluation of the gender-
responsiveness of the GAD PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. enjoin the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of the CLT and CIB-LCCA accounts in the FS and Cashbooks to reflect accurate and reliable information as to the details of the balances of the cash accounts reported at year-end;

b. instruct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to observe timely submission of the RCD to the MAO, so that the latter could timely record the collections and deposits in the books of accounts; and

c. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) review the abnormal balance of ₱154,481.56 and draw adjustments, if warranted; (ii) make use of the electronic banking facilities in the preparation of BRS and submit the same to the Audit Team, along with the necessary supporting documents and schedules of reconciling items; and (iii) prepare the GL and SLs for each bank account showing the complete detail of transactions for the year and ensure those records are available upon request of the Audit Team.

We reiterated our recommendations with some modifications that the Municipal Mayor (i) avail of the one-time cleansing and initiate a formal request to write off all dormant accounts, amounting to ₱306,679.53 and ₱82,118.25, which pertain to receivables from deceased AOs and uncollected loans granted to various beneficiaries, respectively; and (ii) impose appropriate measures to enforce the collection of other receivables totaling ₱399,841.09. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant consistently monitor, verify and validate the receivables reflected in the FS, to determine the existence of dormant receivable accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) review the abnormal balances amounting to ₱7,251.70 and draw adjustments, if warranted; (ii) prepare and submit the individual SLs for cash advances; (iii) demand from AOs who are still in service, the immediate liquidation of their outstanding cash advances. Failure on the part of AOs to settle/liquidate their cash advances shall constitute a valid ground for the withholding of the payment from any money due them; (iv) exhaust all remedies to collect long outstanding cash advances of individuals who are already deceased, separated from service, and had transferred to other government agencies by sending confirmation, notification, or demand letters, otherwise, apply for write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2023-008; and (v) refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance has already been settled.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. require the Property Custodian to (i) prepare and maintain SCs and SPCs for all inventories to account the receipt and issuance of supplies, materials and semi-expendable properties; (ii) conduct regular physical count of inventories; and (iii) ensure the timely preparation and submission of the RPCI and RPCSP to the Audit Team within the prescribed period;

b. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) duly prepare the SLCs and SPLCs for all inventory accounts, to ensure that the recording of the acquisition, issuance and disposal of inventories are well monitored and documented.; (ii) request additional manpower to handle the recording and maintenance of GL and SL for all accounts to ensure the accuracy and reliability of account balances in the financial statements; (iii) strictly adhere to the perpetual inventory method in accounting for the purchases of supplies and materials pursuant to Section 114 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and (iv) make the necessary adjustments to appropriately classify Semi-Expendable Properties in accordance with prevailing COA guidelines; and

c. instruct the Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant to facilitate periodic reconciliation of their reports so that discrepancies, if any, are immediately detected and corrected.

We reiterated our recommendations with some modifications that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of all PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, and upon completion, prepare the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team within the prescribed period; and (ii) identify the whereabouts of unserviceable properties and facilitate the immediate disposal thereof and be guided by applicable provisions on disposal of government properties;

b. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain complete property records such as PPE Ledger Cards for each type of PPE; (ii) prepare necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the properties costing below the ₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold to appropriate semi-expendable properties account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2022-004, and misclassified PPE accounts totaling 2,985,412.00; and (iii) provide allowance for depreciation for PPE accounts totaling ₱31,944,034.64; and

c. Property Custodian to (i) prepare and maintain the PCs for each type of PPE; and (ii) coordinate with the Accounting Office for the reconciliation of their respective reports and make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a justification for the improper charging against the PFPR concerning the procured equipment used in the Municipal Accounting Office.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant to request additional manpower to handle the recording and maintenance of GL and SL for all accounts to ensure the accuracy and reliability of account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:

a) Municipal Accountant and GSO-designate to reconcile their records on PPE
and address the unreconciled difference of ₱108,630,063.16;

b) Municipal Accountant to examine the RPCPPE submitted, particularly
those PPEs reported in the physical count, and ensure that those PPEs are
supported with subsidiary ledger cards and recorded in the books and
Accounting records; and

c) GSO-designate/Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Inventory
Committee to:
1) Conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of all PPE items,
including local road networks and infrastructure assets; and
2) Regularly update its records and prepare and maintain PCs for each
class of PPE to record the acquisition, description, custody, disposal and
other information about the asset.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive or any of his authorized
representative to:
● On the evacuation center at Brgy. Tagpocol, San Fernando
a) Make representation with the DPWH RO V to secure the necessary documents,
such as the documents stating the contract cost of the EC, etc. and forward the
same to the Accounting Office for proper recognition either through recording
or disclosure in the Financial Statements;
b) Ensure that the roles and responsibilities of the LGU as specified in the MOA
were being complied with, especially on the maintenance and upkeep of the
facility, including the noted observations upon inspection;
c) Ensure that the formal and complete turn-over of the facility is being complied
with as specified in the MOA as a role of the DPWH thus need to be enforced
and request access therewith; and
d) Establish open communication line between and among the LGU personnel, if
possible, and the copy of MOA once signed, should be furnished to the
Accounting.
● On the constructed coliseum/stadium on Brgy. Del Pilar, San Fernando
a) Secure the relevant supporting documents pertaining to the project, such as
MOA, SB resolution authorizing the LCE to enter into a MOA, if there is any,
communication with SA on the said project, cost and turn over of the project to
the Municipality;
b) Furnish the Audit Team of the steps taken by the LGU regarding its initiative
to communicate with the SA and the relevant supporting documents once
available; and
c) Furnish copies to the Accounting for proper recording in the agency books.

Accordingly, it is also recommended that the LCE assign a focal person to take
charge of coordinating and monitoring nationally funded projects implemented at the
LGU in order to ensure the attainment of its program goals and objectives.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant, in close
coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to:

a) identify the completed projects under the “Construction in Progress” (CIP)
account as at December 31, 2024, then transfer these projects from the CIP
account to the appropriate Infrastructure Asset accounts;

b) timely recognize PPE in the books by diligently monitoring and/or by
devising shared progress reports of each component project of the project
packages based on the periodic Inspection Report being issued by the
Municipal Engineer; and

c) prospectively ensure that development projects completed within the
calendar year are recorded to the appropriate Infrastructure Asset accounts
and subjected to depreciation before closing the accounting books for that
calendar year.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a) assign personnel to handle full-time the preparation and maintenance of the
subsidiary ledgers of all assets, starting with CY 2024 procurement/
acquisitions;

b) review the lapsing schedule and re-compute for the correct depreciation
taking into consideration the PPEs’ proper classification and the
appropriate rate for the residual value;

c) record any changes as adjustments in the ensuing year to reflect the correct
balance of accumulated depreciation for each item of PPE; and

d) prospectively, record the depreciation expense of the LGU’s assets to
establish its correct value in the books.

We likewise recommended the Management to direct the GSO-designate, in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant to:

e) exert effort to secure the documents necessary to reconstruct the PPE
accounts so that depreciation for current and previous years may be
recomputed to arrive at the correct valuation of the property, plant and
equipment. In case the necessary data could no longer be secured after
exhausting all possible means, we recommend that appraisal of the PPEs
be considered in order to establish the correct book values; and

f) As GSO- designate, to prepare the property card for the PPEs starting with
the current acquisition/procurements and work back to the previous years
to support the PPEs of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a) Create an Inventory Committee to spearhead the conduct of inventory
taking, taking into consideration the composition and capacities of the
members, delineating the duties and functions of each member, or
appointing one employee from each department/office as a member of the
Committee who would lead the conduct of the physical count and
essential preparation of the RPCI per department/office;

b) Strictly require the department heads to submit the RPCI per type of
inventory in their custody/office to the Inventory Committee for
consolidation and validation within the period required by Section 124 of
the NGAS Manual for LGUs;

c) Require the Property Office and the Accounting Office to maintain stock
cards and supplies ledger cards, respectively for each type of supplies, and
periodically reconcile it with the stock cards and property cards maintained
by the GSO for checks and balances; and

d) Submit the relevant supporting documents to the Audit Team to support the
entries in the General Journal made in the expensing of inventory of 24.80
million in CY 2024 to validate the basis for the preparation of JEV and
recording of expenditure.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
and staff to:

a) Trace and retrieve the original source documents to establish the validity of
long-standing and dormant claims, and make appropriate actions to settle
the payable with the concerned creditor, or revert the same to the
unappropriated surplus of the general fund, or correct the entries made in
case of misstatements;

b) Review the records and determine the reason for the negative balances of
various payables, and make appropriate journal entries if warranted; and

c) Examine the transaction(s) and the related supporting documents and
ensure that only valid claims (goods were received and/or service were
rendered at year-end) are recorded as accrued liabilities at year-end; and

d) Draft the necessary correcting entries recorded under Accounts Payable
that should have been properly recorded in the appropriate accounts as
discussed above.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
immediately draw the following necessary journal entries:
(1) Construction in Progress- Buildings and Other Structures xx
Government Equity xx
To recognize the unrecorded Expense incurred in prior year CY
2024, with regard to the two Package 4 projects.
(2) Government Equity xx
Accounts Payable xx
To recognize the unrecorded Liability arising from the materials
delivered and labor services rendered in the prior year CY 2024 in relation
to the two Package 4 projects.
(3) Appropriate Asset account xx
Construction in Progress- Buildings and Other xx
Structures
To recognize the asset account for the completed Construction of
Health Center at Bocal, San Fernando under Package 4:a.

We likewise recommended that before closing the books for CY 2025 and
onwards, the Municipal Accounting Office must coordinate closely with the Municipal
Budget Officer and Municipal Engineer to fully account for obligated expenses,
goods/services delivered and completed projects within the calendar year.

We finally recommended to conduct the required reconciliation of balances
between Accounting and Budget Office in relation to SCBAA, and to prepare the
required Note Disclosure for any material difference that may arise therefrom not only
for the 20 percent DF but all other funds of the Municipality as a whole.

We recommended that Management:

a) Compute the applicable liquidated damages, and deduct the same, as well as
withhold the retention money, from the amounts paid to contractors;

b) Submit a valid written justification supported with pertinent documents
showing that the time extensions granted were warranted, and the delays
were justified; and

c) Familiarize themselves--especially those directly tasked to coordinate with
and report to the SA, to implement the project/program/activity (P/P/A),
and to liquidate the funds transferred--of the terms and conditions
stipulated in the MOA, and guidelines governing a particular grant; and
ensure strict compliance therewith.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a) Cause the return/refund of the overpayments made out of the unexpended
LDRRMF held in trust, which represented the liquidated damages from the
delayed delivery of goods, amounting to ₱522,850.30; and

b) Prospectively, direct the concerned procuring/implementing officer to (1)
monitor the actual delivery of goods to determine whether it was made
within the terms set forth in the contract; (2) observe and enforce the rules
on the grant of request for time extension, which include making sure that
the written request is made before the expiration of the contract, and
conducting an investigation to determine the validity of the reasons cited and
the number of days to be granted; and (3) in coordination with the
Municipal Accountant, impose liquidated damages in case of delay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a) Instruct the LDRRM Council to prepare, review, and update annually its
LDRRMFIP, and include therein the list of projects and activities to be
charged against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the special
trust fund account of the agency; and

b) Ensure the preparation of the revised AIP, if the unexpended LDRRMF will
be utilized for new expenditure items, and observe the proper authorization
for the release and use of LDRRMF through the preparation of Budget, and
enactment of an Appropriations Ordinance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a) The Office of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management to
observe the proper classification of allocated funds to Maintenance and
Operating Expenses (MOOE) and Capital Outlay (CO), in accordance with
the pertinent rules on planning and budgeting vis-à-vis the nature of the
identified project and activities in the annual Local Disaster Risk Reduction
and Management Investment Plan; and

b) The Municipal Accountant to reclassify the capitalizable costs identified
herein to their appropriate PPE accounts, and subject the same to proper
depreciation, in order to better reflect the expected use of the asset.

We recommended that Management direct the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) and BAC-Technical Working Group (-TWG) to conduct a thorough scrutiny of
the eligibility documents during Post-Qualification process for the LGU projects in CY
2025 and onwards, by evaluating the bidders’ eligibility documents through
verification of the availability of key personnel and manpower assigned,
inspection/testing of the equipment committed by contractor, and re-computation of
the bidder’s Net Financial Contracting Capacity (NFCC) in accordance with the
procurement guidelines.

We recommended that Management:

a) Withhold from the progress or final payments the retention money
amounting to ₱3,655,195.54 for the three ongoing projects listed above; and

b) Prospectively, ensure that the applicable retention money is retained and
deducted from gross progress payments.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor issue a policy to stop the practice of
manual payment of wages to the Job Order Workers, instead, it shall be through the
same existing Automated Payroll System of paying twice a month for wages facilitating
the direct crediting it to Job Order Worker’s’ bank accounts to prevent delay and
avoid the risk of loss or misappropriation of public funds that are inherent in a manual
system.

We recommended that Management:

a) Direct the LGU Offices concerned to use of the assigned Responsibility
Center (RC) and their corresponding RC Code starting CY 2025 in
compliance with the above Circular;

b) Also direct the Municipal Accounting Office to establish and maintain a
separate Subsidiary Ledger for GAD-related expenses; and

c) Instruct the Municipal Budget and Accounting Offices to be in close
coordination with the GAD Focal Person/-Alternate to trace and furnish the
COA Audit Team of a copy of complete GAD registry and/or schedule that
contains the GAD-related expenses incurred in CY 2024.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:

a) Issue a directive to the concerned implementing offices to submit their CY
2024 GAD Accomplishment Report (AR) for consolidation of the GFP and
prospectively follow the timeline for submission of the same in the next
succeeding year;

b) Immediately furnish the COA Audit Team of the LGU’s GAD AR for
validation in audit; and

c) Also issue a directive to strengthen the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to
address the noted areas for improvements, such as personal attendance of
concerned department heads during GAD meetings after advance notice of
meeting, proper linkage to the GAD budget by the GAD Focal Person/-
Alternate themselves, complete and accessible budget monitoring and
accounting recording of GAD-related expenses, and schedule of GAD report
submissions, among others.

We likewise recommended that the Management consider the provision of
GAD-focused space and equipment to the GFP/-Alternate, like office laptop and
supplies.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Create a new Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of
all PPE, and prepare and submit the RPCPPE; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to prepare and maintain
the PPE Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively.
Likewise, conduct the required reconciliation of the property and
accounting records.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct
the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the preparation and submission of the
required BRS, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and
effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the FS and other
documents.
