We recommended that Management:
a. Require the previous Acting MT and LO to immediately deposit the
undeposited collections to the AGDB amounting to ₱5,883,606.89 and
₱673,677.43, respectively and submit a copy of the deposit slip to the Office
of the Auditor;
b. Require the Collecting Officers to remit all collections daily and in full
amount to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO). For collections received
by the MT and LO, considering the cost and distance of travel which may
exceed one day to the AGDB, deposits may be made weekly or as soon as the
total collections reach ₱10,000.00 as prescribed in Section 28 of COA
Circular No. 92-382;
c. Institute the appropriate administrative/legal actions against those erring
Accountable Officers (AOs)/employees for continued non-compliance with
the existing laws, rules and regulations on the handling of collections and
accountabilities;
d. Review past annual reports, schedules, and supporting documentation to
identify the specific accountabilities of the unknown MTs with outstanding
accounts of ₱6,193,050.72 and recover any amount, if possible. Management
must also strengthen internal controls over the recording and monitoring of
collections to ensure accountability for all MT, LO and collectors; and
e. Require all AOs to maintain and regularly update their individual cash
books.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. Direct officers and employees with unliquidated CA to immediately submit
their liquidation papers, otherwise, may constitute a valid cause for the
withholding of salary of those officers and employees who were unable to
comply with the requirement;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of
outstanding CA and issue on a regular basis demand letter to submit
liquidation reports immediately. Likewise, fast track the recovery of the
documents relevant to the long outstanding CAs and conduct verification,
analysis and validation of them to determine their settlement/collectability
and pursue collection, refund or liquidation, if warranted; and
c. Conform with the procedures and requirements set forth in COA Circular
No. 2023-008 and immediately facilitate the request for write-off of dormant
unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to implement the perpetual inventory
system to accurately track the acquisition and disbursement of supplies and
materials and make the necessary adjustments to the affected inventory and
expense accounts to rectify any discrepancies arising from the previous
accounting practices and ensure that the FS accurately reflect the current
inventory status;
b. Require the MT and Municipal Accountant to maintain SCs and SLCs per
stock number, respectively, and regularly reconcile the balances of
inventory based on their respective records; and
c. Direct the Inventory Committee to submit the RPCI to the Municipal
Accountant for recording and reconciliation purposes, and to COA for audit
verification.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to make
the necessary adjusting entries to correct/record the noted misstatement by
debiting Cash in Bank account and crediting the Trust Liability account. Also,
ensure that the Municipality is compliant with the accounting guidelines set out
for the grants received from the national government agencies in subsequent
transactions. Moreover, direct the MTO to inform and submit the relevant
documents of any grants received by the Municipality to the Office of the
Municipal Accountant for proper recording in the books of accounts of the
LGU.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management strictly comply with the
mandatory submission of contracts, POs and their supporting documents to the
Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete
conduct of auditorial and technical review. Further, direct the personnel
responsible for accepting deliveries to notify COA of the scheduled time and
date of deliveries, furnish copies of AIRs within 24 hours upon acceptance and
submit a year-end report on all deliveries received and issued during the year.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the concerned officers to prepare the Quarterly Report on Fund
Utilization and Status of Program/Project Implementation of LGSF-SBDP
and thereafter post the same in at least three conspicuous places within the
locality;
b. Facilitate the creation of LGU’s official website to bolster transparency and
accountability to the Municipality’s constituents and stakeholders.
Likewise, post the aforementioned quarterly reports on the LGU’s website
and notify in writing the NTF-ELCAC Vice Chairperson and Regional Task
Force (RTF)-ELCAC concerned, Speaker of the House of Representatives,
President of the Senate of the Philippines, and the Chairpersons of the House
Committee on Appropriations and Senate Committee on Finance when the
reports are already posted thereon; and
c. Submit immediately the lacking DVs of the noted transactions charged
against the LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC granted to the LGU.

We recommended that Management require the concerned Municipal Officials
and Employees to devise measures and exert more effort to expedite the
preparation and submission of the accounts and financial reports, including the
supporting documents and schedules, within the prescribed period. We request
that such activities be given utmost priority for timely and better fiscal reporting.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to:
a. Formulate and issue budget prioritization policies and tentative budget
ceilings per municipality thru a Budget Call within the prescribed period;
b. Observe the timely preparation and approval of the SEF budget as
prescribed in the JC. This allows prompt submission to relevant offices,
providing them with adequate time for review, consolidation and evaluation
of the reports, and ultimately enhancing transparency and accountability;
c. Regularly submit the quarterly and annual SEF utilization reports (SEF
Budget Accountability Form No. 1) to the DepEd Central Office, thorough
appropriate channels, copy furnished the Regional Offices of the DBM and
DILG; and
d. Ensure that the SEF utilization reports are regularly posted in the LGU
website quarterly and must not be later than the 20th day after the end of
each quarter as required in the Revised Implementing Guidelines on Full
Disclosure Policy pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2024-021.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the previous Acting MT (payee in CA) to immediately submit the
aforementioned lacking documents relative to the noted deficiencies.
Additionally, DVs and liquidation documents for payroll for the months of
September to December 2024 must also be submitted to the COA;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the supporting documents
are complete, accurate and properly accomplished prior to facilitating
payments or before granting CAs for payroll; and
c. Require the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to prepare and
execute written employment contracts with all casual employees prior to
their commencement of work with the LGU.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant, MT and MDRRM Officer to transfer
immediately the unexpended balances of LDRRMF QRF and MOOE
amounting in total to ₱1,030,402.02 to the STF Trust Liability-DRRM
account; and
b. Ensure that in succeeding year, the personnel concerned must be diligent
and mindful with their basic responsibility of transferring the unexpended
LDRRMF balances to the STF Trust Liability-DRRM account before the
year end. This requires an actual cash transfer to the STF DRRM bank
account before December 31 of the applicable year to separate the
unexpended balance from the GF and not a mere issuance of check and a
journal entry made in the books.

In view thereof, we recommended that Management:
a. Require the concerned LGU officials and employees to submit the lacking
documents of the aforementioned financial transactions particularly the
Travel Order duly approved by the Municipal Mayor and in original copy;
and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that claims for the payment of
traveling expenses or CAs must be supported with complete and proper
documentation prior facilitating payment of transactions.

We recommended that Management:
a. Ensure the prompt implementation of the PPAs included in the GPB
through effective coordination among all concerned departments to
accomplish its objectives;
b. Ensure that only the PPAs that directly address the identified gender issues
within the locality are funded through the GAD budget and provide written
justifications to the Office of the Auditor for the noted PPAs that may not
be fully responsive to GAD-related issues and concerns; and
c. Direct the MPDC to initiate the establishment and maintenance of GAD
database which will serve as the basis for gender-responsive planning,
programming, and policy development.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations and Municipal Accountant and the GSO-Designate agreed to:
Prepare and maintain the required PCs and PPELCs for each category of PPE and Stock Cards and SLCs for each category of supply/inventory;
Conduct reconciliation of the PPE and inventory accounts and complete and submit the RPCPPE including real property accounts and RPCI for the supplies and inventories; and
Work together to undertake the one-time cleansing of PPE to arrive at reliable PPE balances.

We recommended and the Management agreed that through the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct periodic reconciliation of the accounting and treasury records for the Cash in Bank – Local Currency and Cash in Local Treasury accounts of the Municipality to resolve the discrepancies;
Effect the necessary corrections or adjustments, if warranted, to reflect the correct balances of both records and submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team for further verification; and
Ensure that a cash book is maintained for each depository account of the Municipality and submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to continue to validate and trace the beginning balances from its records and coordinate with the prior accounting personnel, as applicable, to establish the details and facilitate reconciliation of the subject balances.

We recommended and the:
Municipal Accountant and the General Service Officer (GSO) agreed to secure the documents necessary to establish the dates of completion of the subject PPEs;
Municipal Accountant agreed to derecognize in the municipal books the PPEs that were already transferred to the Barangays as warranted; and
Municipal Accountant agreed to refer to COA Circular No. 2003-007 in the depreciation of the subject PPEs to reflect the correct balances of the affected assets, expenses, and equity accounts, with the following proposed adjusting journal entries as applicable:

We recommended that Management:
Submit its legal basis for the grant of said contract extension for evaluation by the Audit Team;
Impose the liquidated damages to the contractor’s due as warranted.
Henceforth ensure that the grant of contract suspensions and or extensions are in accordance with the RIRR of RA No. 9184 and pertinent GPPB issuances to avoid personal liability.

We recommended and the Management agreed that through the Municipal Accountant:
Issue demand letters to LGU-Barangays for the liquidation of the outstanding fund releases in accordance with COA Circular No. 94-013; and
Submit to the Audit Team a copy of the updated status of implementation of the subject fund releases.

We reiterated our recommendation and the Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials agreed to conduct a thorough evaluation of the conditions and constraints of each project/program that restricted its timely implementation and to expedite those projects/programs with ongoing implementation. Accordingly, adopt appropriate remedial measures for the proper and speedy disposition of subject funds in accordance with the respective terms and conditions of the source agencies including the return of unutilized balances to the source agencies or Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Submit an updated status of the lots including the actions already undertaken by the Municipal Assessor and GSO for the titling of the subject lots; and
Prioritize the registration, transfer, and titling of these lots in the name of the Municipal Government of San Fernando to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in compliance with COA Circular No. 92-386.
We recommended and the Management through the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Office (LDRRMO) agreed to submit its remedial measures to ensure the optimum use of the LDRRMF.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Comply with the reportorial requirements as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2014-001; and
Submit its remedial measures to ensure that its GAD programs, projects and activities are efficiently used consistent with the provisions of R.A. No. 9710 and PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circulars Nos. 2013-a and 2014-1.

We recommended and the Municipal accountant and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
Submit JEV and proof of remittance for the unremitted balance of Due to BIR;
Conduct a reconciliation of the Due to GSIS and Due to PhilHealth accounts to reflect the correct account balances; and
Remit intact the amounts withheld monthly to BIR, GSIS, and PhilHealth to avoid paying the penalties, surcharges, and interests.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
Direct the concerned AOs to maintain and diligently update their cashbook and conduct periodic reconciliation in accordance with the provisions of NGAS and Treasury Manuals for LGUs; and
Prohibit personnel on Job Order status to perform collection functions unless an approved fidelity bond is secured in accordance with Treasury Circular 02-2009 if warranted.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
Conduct a thorough assessment on the absence and/or weakness of internal controls in the management of its cash and submit to the Audit Team its remedial measures to mitigate the risks involved;
Ensure that all prior and current collections for each designated collectors are completely and fully verified, reported, remitted and accounted for in accordance with the procedural rules set forth in the Local Treasury Manual and MNGAS and submit the corresponding result to the Audit Team; and
Impose disciplinary actions to employees who fail to render their accounts or observe the above rules and regulations, as warranted by applicable laws, rules and regulations.
We recommended and the Management agreed to submit its proposed action plans or measures for evaluation by the Audit Team.

We recommended to the management to:
Allocate annually a portion of the budget for insurance application of all insurable properties;
Instruct the Inventory Committee to reconcile records with the Property Officer and prepare the required Property Inventory Form to be used as basis of the general insurance coverage. Submit a copy of PIF to the Office of the Auditor on or before April 30 each year. Then, insure with the GIF of GSIS all insurable assets and properties with the appropriate property insurance; and
Ensure proper recording of forthcoming disbursements for similar transactions and prepare the necessary adjusting entries at year-end to recognize the unexpired portion of such prepayments.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant shall review all financial transactions with due diligence before approving all journal entries that will be recorded in the LGU's books of accounts;
Henceforth, the Municipal Accounting Office, in coordination with other offices, shall ensure complete recording of all expenditures in the year they were incurred; and
Further, before accepting the delivered items, we would like to remind the Municipal Inspectorate Committee to fully accomplish all necessary details in the Inspection and Acceptance Report (IAR) and other reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking Disbursement Voucher and the proper supporting documents for the transaction. The accounting department should be cautious of the time or deadline for submitting the financial transactions and supporting documents to COA in line with COA Circular 2009-006.

We recommended that the management strictly adhere to the cost-sharing scheme provided under the Implementation Management Agreement (IMA). Also, the Municipal Accountant should be more diligent in providing the necessary disclosures in Notes to Financial Statements, which should include material information that might affect future decision-making of stakeholders.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit a detailed breakdown of the ‘Entity Differences’ of the MOOE and CO as reflected in the Reconciliation between actual amounts presented in the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts and the Statement of Financial Performance for the year ended December 31, 2024. In addition, we strongly advised the Municipal Accountant to ensure that succeeding submissions of the SCBAA and its corresponding reconciliation should include detailed disclosures and a breakdown of the entity differences in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended to the Management to submit the disbursement voucher along with the relevant documents pertaining to the Philippine Multisectoral Nutrition Project as implemented by the LGU in CY 2024 for the continuance of our audit.

We recommended that the management justify the above-cited observation. Prospectively, we recommended that the BAC invite observers from a qualified and duly recognized private group in a sector/discipline related to infrastructure projects and an NGO that passes the criteria in sections 13.2 (a) and 13.2(b) of the IRR.
We recommended that management secure and attach the Contractor’s Affidavit on the payment of laborers and materials in every progress/final payment made to the contractor.

We recommended that the BAC be more mindful of the PRDP procurement timeline for succeeding PRDP procurement procedures to ensure the timely implementation of the project initiatives. In case of delay on the part of the other stakeholders, the management should endeavor to conduct documented follow-ups to encourage adherence of all stakeholders with regard their respective responsibilities. This recommendation holds true for other LGU procurement initiatives.

We recommended that the BAC submit a written justification about the deficiency noted, and we advised them to continuously observe the diligence of a good father of a family in all of the bidding stages, particularly during the bid evaluation phase and the post-qualification stage of the bidders. They should endeavor to meticulously review and crosscheck all documents, including financial and technical documents, to ascertain the capability of the contractor to perform the project. Further, management should request the contractor to submit proofs of ownership or lease agreement for the needed equipment in the POW or Equipment Utilization Schedule in the PRDP project, which was not in the List of Equipment for the Contract.

We recommended that management submit a justification for the noted deficiency in the Program of Works in the LGU PRDP Project and the contract agreement. Then, prospectively, the Procuring Entity should diligently review the POW and the contract agreement together with all the integral parts of the contract to ensure that all necessary information are reviewed and considered to avoid project irregularities. Lastly, we require management to ensure that all pages of the integral parts of the contract agreement are signed to protect the sanctity of the covenant and interest of the LGU.

We recommended the management to be more circumspect and diligent in issuing Certificates of Acceptance. Also, they should respect the defects liability period to safeguard the interests of the government.

We recommended that the management submit the succeeding disbursement vouchers of cash advances related to confidential expenditures, within seven (7) days after the release of the check, by an authorized representative in a sealed envelope to the COA ATL following the guidelines of the COA-DBM-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015. In addition, we require the management to submit the lacking 'Statement of Itemized POP of the LGU where the allowable CF was computed duly certified by the budget officer' in relation to Check No. 17070.

We recommended that the management submit the following:
A written justification for the delay with clearly identified problem areas or offices, including detailed management strategies to address bottlenecks to streamline liquidation processes;
All the Memoranda of Agreement related to the said projects or a written confirmation letter from the source agency that no perfected Memoranda of Agreement supported the said project; and,
We requested that management assign management personnel to monitor the timely liquidation of all fund transfers. Further, we ask that the management ensure that prospective submissions of liquidation reports (Statements of Expenditures, Statements of Receipts and Disbursements, Reports of Disbursements, Reports of Checks Issued, and the like) should include the original disbursement vouchers and their corresponding supporting documents, which should be submitted separately from the monthly bulk submissions.

We recommended that management require warranty security from every supplier of goods, either retention money or a special bank guarantee. Further, to avoid confusion, such requirements should be disclosed to the suppliers during the procurement process and be expressly included in the contract. In addition, the management should diligently choose the proper procurement method in line with IRR of 9184; and submit a justification for why this occurred.

We recommended the management to submit the lacking documents to complete the audit process. In addition, submit a justification for the non-preparation of disbursement vouchers for the ADA transactions. Moreover, we require that management should prepare and accomplish disbursement vouchers for all prospective ADA disbursements.

We recommended that the HRMO and the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of the supporting documents on the monthly claims for the salaries and other allowances of the LGU officials and Employees. In addition, we require the management to submit the lacking documents relating to all claims for Personnel Benefits for CY 2024 to avoid the possible issuance of suspension and/or disallowances.

We recommended to the management to:
Submit the lacking documents of various projects to avoid the issuance of notice of suspension and/or disallowance;
Assign a management personnel to monitor project contract compliances to ensure timely submission and require a monitoring matrix with detailed dates of receipt of indorsement and status of submission; and
Submit time bound process flow proposal complete with focal person responsible and streamlining strategies to ascertain timely response indorsements/transmittals in relation to contracts.

We recommended that the LGU review the guidelines for financial assistance covered by SB Resolution to see if there are loopholes or areas for improvement and, if necessary, amend or add supplemental guidelines to ensure fairness and efficient/effective utilization of government resources.
Moreover, we advised the Management to instruct the MSWD to diligently review the completeness and accuracy of the Crisis Intervention Unit forms when encoding details for every client in the screening phase before providing financial assistance.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CENRO to:

Expedite the completion of requirements for the approval of the necessary amendments to the City Sanitation Code so that funds will be used for their intended purpose; and

Provide timely reports on collection of fees and services rendered to the City Accounting Office regularly to ensure the completeness of records.

We have provided the Accounting Office a copy of siphoning services for the 6 months of year 2025.
observations.reason: Awaiting the result of the review of the draft ordinance by the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE, as the HoPE, direct the following:

BAC to comply with the prescribed period for issuing NOAs as outlined in Section 37.1.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to prevent contributing delays in project implementation, facilitating timely delivery and completion of projects, thereby enabling constituents to access and enjoy the benefits derived from these projects at an earlier stage.

The BAC and its TWG to judiciously conduct post-qualification proceedings by thoroughly verifying, validating and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the LCB.

We also recommended that the LCE, as HoPE, enter into a contract with the winning bidder within 10 calendar days from receipt of the latter of the NOA pursuant to Section 37.2.1 of the Revised IRR, ensuring timely implementation of projects, thereby enabling constituents to promptly benefit from them.

Lastly, we recommended that LCE comply with the provisions outlined in Section 37.4.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 regarding the effectiveness of issued NTPs to streamline the overall project implementation process, thereby preventing any potential delays in the completion of projects.

The BAC is fully aware of the findings and is committed to strictly enforcing the audit recommendations.

The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) has already developed and issued internal rules and guidelines and has participated in two training sessions on Republic Act No. 9184 and Republic Act No. 12009 to effectively address procurement-related issues and concerns in all offices.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE, as HOPE, oversee and instruct the designated officials responsible for preparing the source documents, including PRs, POs, RFQs, and AIRs, to ensure the accurate and comprehensive inclusion of all required information in adherence to the guidelines specified in the MNGAS for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184. This promotes transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency and economy in the procurement process.



We recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the BAC, streamline the procurement process for SVP to align with its intended purpose. This should be done by developing a detailed process flowchart that includes specific timelines for each step, starting from document preparation and review to signing and issuance. Additionally, we recommended implementing regular reviews of the process to identify any bottlenecks and areas for improvement, ensuring greater efficiency in the procurement procedures.



We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to clearly specify in the RFQs sent to participating bidders the stage of the procurement process at which these requirements must be submitted to ensure a proper assessment of the bidder’s compliance. Failure to submit these documents within the designated period should result in automatic disqualification of the bidder.


We recommended that the LCE, in collaboration with the BAC, develop internal guidelines regarding the requirement for posting of PS for goods under the SVP modality. This should include setting a defined range of ABCs that would necessitate the posting of PS. This measure aims to   protect the City Government’s interests in cases of contract termination by the suppliers without valid reason/s



Furthermore, the Audit Team emphasized its recommendation on the proper accomplishment of all details embodied in the PO, particularly the delivery schedule for procured items. This practice is essential to enable the City Government to legally enforce penalty clauses and calculate liquidated damages for any delays caused by suppliers, thereby providing an additional source of income that could potentially be used to finance the City Government’s PPAs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct:

The City Budget Officer to ensure monitoring of the full implementation of the projects appropriated under the 20% DF in order to address the needs of the constituents such as access to improved health, social, agriculture and economic services; and

The City Development Council to verify the procurement-and-implementation readiness of the proposed 20% DF PPAs.
observations.managementAction:
Status: The City Budget Officer has noted the observation and will comply with the recommendation.
In addition, the CBO, regularly presents the status of implemented and unimplemented projects during the Department Heads Meeting. These updates include discussions with the City Mayor regarding delayed and unimplemented projects. It also emphasized the four pillars: Health, Education, Economy, and Environment
During the budget preparation and review of identified PPAs for each department, the Civil Society Organization (CSO) actively presents and discusses both the current year’s implementation status and the readiness of the proposed PPAs. This comprehensive process encompasses not only the General Fund but also the 20% Development Fund (DF) and other mandatory accounts.
observations.reason: The City Budget Officer acknowledges the observation and commits to complying with the recommendationWe will ensure that City Government of SFLU will effectively reallocate and utilize their development funds to address the immediate needs of the constituents in accordance with the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 2020-001.

observations.recommendation: We commended Management for its continuous compliance with existing regulations on the entitlement and granting of cash advance for confidential funds. However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the City Accounting Office submit the disbursement vouchers as well as the supporting documents within the required period to the Audit Team for prompt review.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant make necessary adjustments in the books to correct the noted omission and to reflect the correct balance of the CIP account. This will lead to a more dependable information and fair presentation of the account in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Engineering Office to facilitate the completion of the remaining projects as soon as possible and ensure the proper utilization and liquidation of the funds.

We commended the City Government’s efforts in addressing the health and welfare concern of its constituents by way of entering into a partnership with a private institution thru the provision of accessible and free health care services specifically, the City’s indigent patients with chronic kidney failure disease thru the Fernandino Dialysis Center. However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive and the City Health Officer/Focal Person accomplish the remaining unaccomplished requirements of the MOA so that the continuity of the project would be assured.

We commended the Management for its proper withholding and full remittance of monthly taxes and GSIS contributions via eFPS and eBCS in compliance with Revenue Regulation 1-2013 and Republic Act No. 8291, and for its timely and complete payments of monthly dues to the PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG in accordance with PhilHealth Circular No. 0001, s. 2014 and HDMF Circular No. 274.

observations.recommendation:
Based from the foregoing, we recommended that the LCE direct the following:

General Services Officer to: 

Prepare and approve Purchase Requests for the acquisition of office and janitorial supplies which are not available in PS-DBM for quantities not exceeding the estimated consumption corresponding to a programmed three-month period;

Inform the requesting Departments or Offices of their unclaimed supplies on stock based on their monthly requirements to minimize if not eliminate losses from expiration;



Initiate proper disposal of the expired supplies totaling ₱1,750,932.00; and



Ensure that supplies in the designated stockrooms are monitored and secured to minimize if not eliminate further damage or loss.





City Accountant to prepare necessary adjustments in the books after the disposal of expired office supplies with a total cost of ₱1,750,932.00 duly supported with necessary documentary requirements; and

BAC to prepare and strategically schedule procurement and delivery of office and janitorial supplies not available in PS-DBM limiting them to quantities not exceeding the estimated consumption corresponding to a programmed three-month period in the Annual Procurement Plan to ensure that supplies are available during the quarter they are needed.

We also recommended that the LCE require all of the Department or Office Heads to religiously plan their monthly and quarterly supply requirements and ensure that the items and quantity incorporated in the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) are to be consumed during the year to reduce risk of loss from expiration.
observations.managementAction:
The Office of the City General Services Officer prepared Purchase Requests in accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan.





The Office of the City General Services Officer, through their Supply Section, informed all offices of the availability of supplies as indicated in their PPMP.





The City GSO conducted meeting to process proper disposal of the expired supplies.





The City GSO moved all office, computer, and janitorial supplies in a designated stockroom which were regularly monitored by an officer-in-charge.





The City Accountant was not able to prepare adjustments in the book of accounts.





The BAC procured office and janitorial supplies in accordance with the approved APP.








Management adequately planned for their monthly and quarterly supplies requirements and expended the same during the year.

the City Accountant to:

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) in coordination with the City Accountant, ensure that a cash advance will be granted only after the liquidation of previous one as evidenced by a Certification from the latter that the previous one is settled/accounted for; (b) limit the cash advance granted to the maximum coverage of the fidelity bond of AOs or apply for higher fidelity bond to safeguard government funds; (c) instruct the City Social Welfare Officer to properly plan the activities and programs, estimate the cost of each project and set the target date of implementation thereof to avoid the cancellation of the checks issued for the purpose; and (d) ensure that applicable rules and regulations governing cash advances are strictly followed.
observations.managementAction:
AAR CY 2022 | The City Accountant stated that internal control in the granting and liquidation of cash advances was already in place. For every cash advance request, the Internal Audit personnel reviews and monitors if the disbursing officer has outstanding advances and if the cash accountability is within the prescribed limit. Moreover, the Office also issues Statement of Cash Advance to all disbursing officer to demand the submission of supporting documents subject for liquidation. However, it was admitted that they were not able to religiously follow the said control process owing to time limitations in the implementation of programs and the inability of the new administration to adhere with the said policies and guidelines. 

On the other hand, the City Accountant added that all cash advances granted were necessary for the implementation of the City government projects/program and committed that they will take all necessary actions to ensure that all cash advances granted will be fully liquidated.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2023 AAR

observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | The noted discrepancy is due to the timing difference in recording the inventory issuances between the City GSO and Accounting Office. observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2023 AAR

(a) refrain from recording the request for cash assistance as accounts payable and follow the procedures for recording of obligations provided in the accounting for CAFOA; and

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Accountant  (a) require the implementing heads of barangay to (i) exhaust all efforts to coordinate with the previous Barangay Administration to facilitate submission of liquidation documents for the long-unliquidated financial assistance; (ii) religiously submit all liquidation documents to the City Accounting Office for all financial assistance received; 
(iii) immediately refund the unutilized balance of the financial assistance to the City Treasurer’s Office after the purposes of the grants are completed; and (iv) strictly observe the proper procedures in the liquidation of financial assistance and ensure completeness of documents upon submission to the accounting personnel in-charge of the books of the barangay; (b) ensure that all required liquidation documents for previously granted financial assistance are submitted as basis for the issuance of a Certification that funds previously transferred to the Implementing Agency (IA) has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before processing the grant/transfer of another financial assistance; and (c) closely monitor the compliance of the recipient barangays on the submission of liquidation documents within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | The City Accountant agreed to strictly comply with the accounting policies and procedures in the granting of financial assistance and to reflect the necessary adjustments in the books.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2023 AAR

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Treasurer to investigate the variance noted between the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and the RPT delinquency report and make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Treasurer to ensure that necessary improvements are in place in the RPT system in order to make it a more reliable source of data for the City’s Real Property Tax.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | The City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) admitted the need to improve the real property tax system to achieve a more reliable source of data. The interconnection of the CTO, offices of assessor and accounting could have facilitated the verification of records, accordingly, some of the reports take too long to generate and the constant data changes affect the reconciliation – a technical issue that only the experts can address. However, during one of the meetings held with City Information and Communications Technology Office (CICTO), it was discussed that an in-house system was being developed which they hope to resolve, together with some deficient records and un validated data.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2023 AAR

observations.managementAction:
AAR CY 2022 | On the non-appearance of 90 job order employees

The CHRMO will require the job order personnel who physically failed to appear to our office to present themselves for validation.  The official concerned further assured that all job order employees were well accounted for and were physically present during contract signing activities with a valid ID as proof of identify. They were also required to present requirements which will certify their identify, such as, but not limited to, birth certificates, valid IDs, and NBI clearances. 

On the functions of some job order personnel occupying supervisory position

As per CHRMO records, the duties and responsibilities stated on the Job Order contracts of the fifteen (15) employees mentioned contained no supervisory functions.  The office assured that Job Order employees were not given duties and functions which involve accountability and responsibility duly performed by permanent employees.

On the hiring of 11 consultants

The 11 consultants provide a safe distance from the agency’s challenges because they are not emotionally invested in its operation in the same way and they can more easily identify and address challenges from a bird’s eye view, whether the issue is implementing new technology or completing a merger or acquisition.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the City Government and its officials for being one of the recipients of the MANILA BAYani Award for its responsiveness to the environment awareness cause.  However, we still recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board 
(a) enforce  the proper wastes segregation  at source and implement the “No Segregation No Collection” Policy; 

(b) strengthen information and education campaigns on the hazards of open burning, open dumpsites and other ill practices prohibited under Section 48 of R.A. No. 9003; and 

(c) ensure that wastes are properly collected from households and transported to MRFs to avoid improper disposal along the roads and other public places.
observations.managementAction:
AAR CY 2022 | The mandate in Section 10 of R.A. No. 9003 is being implemented by each barangay and the City through the City Environment and Natural Resources (CENRO) who regularly monitors the implementation of the process.   The CENRO also issues City Ordinance Violation Ticket to barangays who violate the collection of mixed wastes and open burning of wastes. 

The office added that they have planned and enacted many strategies to implement the provisions of R.A. No. 9003.  Despite the many changes of the head of the office, set programs have been enhanced and continuously implemented.  They also pointed out that the discipline of individuals and political will of leaders can truly solve the problem on solid waste management.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this CY 2023 AAR

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Avoid the practice of charging administrative expenses, travelling expenses and personnel services expenditures to 20 percent DF so that development programs and projects envisioned by the government are properly implemented; and

Ensure that development projects included under the 20 percent DF are those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to promotion of the general welfare of the people.

Require all drivers and concerned officials to completely fill out the Driver’s Trip Ticket in accordance with existing regulation for transparency and accountability over the use of such government motor vehicles; and

observations.recommendation:
1.10 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the City
Accountant to:

a. prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of the
affected accounts as follows:

Debit: Other Deferred Credits ₱14,137,420.45
Credit: Other Service Income ₱ 1,007,200.00
Credit: Government Equity ₱13,130,220.45

b. stop the practice of recognizing the collection of septage fees as Other
Deferred Credits and instead recognize them as Other Income.

1.11 We also recommended that the LCE direct the CENRO to:

a. expedite the completion of requirements for the approval of the
necessary amendments to the City Sanitation Code so that funds will
be used for their intended purpose; and

b. provide timely reports on collection of fees and services rendered to the
City Accounting Office regularly to ensure the completeness of records.

3.13 We recommended that the LCE direct the following:

a. the City Health Officer and the City Accountant to closely and
regularly coordinate with PhilHealth for the reconciliation of benefit
claims and, thereafter, record the corresponding receivables and
income in their books; and

b. the City Health Officer to fast track the completion of the required
document for the accreditation of the CHO to be eligible to claim other
PhilHealth packages to augment the revenue of the health unit for the
provision of quality health services to the LGU’s constituents.

3.14 The CHO committed to submit monthly reports of approved claims to the city
accountant for recording benefit claims receivables. They also pledged to
54
provide the city accountant with the Auto Credit Payment Notice from
PhilHealth LHIO for immediate journal entry voucher issuance, preventing
unreconciled records.

3.15 The application for a License to Operate (LTO) a Birthing Home was submitted
to the DOH-Center for Health Development I on April 22, 2024. An onsite
inspection was conducted by the Regulations and Licensing Enforcement
Division (RLED) team on May 3, 2024, and compliance documents were
submitted on May 14, 2024. The LTO is in process. Once issued, the CHO will
prepare and submit requirements for PhilHealth re-accreditation for birthing
facilities for the MCP and NCP claims. However, the CHO does not have an
isolation facility; therefore, the CHO cannot claim CCIBP.

4.17 We recommended that the LCE:
1. direct the City Accountant:
a. to prepare the BRS and submit it with supporting documents
within the prescribed period;
b. base the balances of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency – Current
Account on the resulting adjusted balances of the BRS;
2. instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer:
a. to reconcile their respective records at least every quarter;
b. ensure that the CY 2022 beginning and ending balances in the
cashbook and cash in the local treasury ledger for both the GF and
SEF are reconciled; and
c. if necessary, effect adjusting entries for the collections and deposits
realized during CY 2023 which were unrecorded or erroneously
recorded by either the accountant or the treasurer.

4.18 The City Accountant agreed to prepare the bank reconciliation statements
regularly. The City Treasurer likewise agreed with the reconciliation of treasury
records with that of the accounting.

6.34 We recommended that the LCE, as the HoPE, direct the following:

b. the BAC to:
i. hold a pre-procurement conference for procurement of Goods with an
ABC exceeding ₱2,000,000.00, IPs with an ABC exceeding
₱5,000,000.00 and Consulting Services with an ABC exceeding
₱1,000,000.00, as this will determine the readiness of the agency
in the procurement projects at hand in terms of legal, technical
and financial requirements;
ii. send diligently the notices such as the (a) request for submission of
additional post-qualification documents, (b) results of post-
qualification, and (c) bidding results, and ensure that these
documents are promptly and duly received by the concerned
bidders to uphold transparency in the related results of
procurement activities, as well as to maintain fairness throughout
the process;
iii. comply with the prescribed period for issuing NOAs as outlined
in Section 37.1.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to prevent
contributing delays in project implementation, facilitating timely
delivery and completion of projects, thereby enabling constituents
to access and enjoy the benefits derived from these projects at an
earier stage;
iv. through its BAC Secretariat, prepare and send the Notification of
Bid Submission and ensure that the bidder receives the said
document to guarantee transparency and accurate representation
of bids submitted;
v. oversee that all procurements conducted through competitive
bidding strictly follow the proceedings and requirements of the
bidding process to ascertain the legality and validity of contracts
entered into by the City Government, thereby ensuring
67
transparency and accountability in procurement practices;
vi. ensure thorough completion of the clauses pertaining to key
personnel and minimum major equipment requirements in the
BDS, strengthening the post-qualification process and ultimately
ensuring the successful implementation of projects and fulfillment
of contractual obligations;
vii. diligently carry out their responsibilities at every stage of the
procurement process, ensuring that reports are meticulously
prepared to accurately reflect the actual dates of each
procurement activity.

c. the BAC and its TWG to:
i. complete the post-qualification process within the allowable time
frame. If the 12-day period proves insufficient, the BAC must
promptly request approval from the HoPE for any necessary
extensions;
ii. judiciously conduct post-qualification proceedings by thoroughly
verifying, validating and ascertaining all statements made and
documents submitted by the bidder with the LCB; and

d. The BAC Secretariat to ensure that all relevant documents issued to the
bidder, particularly the NOAs and NTPs, are duly affixed with the date
of receipt of the concerned bidder to ascertain that the perfection date
of the contract and its effectivity conforms with the required time
prescribed in Sections 37.2.1 and 37.4.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

6.35 We also recommended that the LCE, as HoPE, enter into a contract with the
winning bidder within 10 calendar days from receipt of the latter of the NOA
pursuant to Section 37.2.1 of the Revised IRR, ensuring timely implementation
of projects, thereby enabling constituents to promptly benefit from them.

6.36 Lastly, we recommended that the LCE comply with the provisions outlined in
Section 37.4.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 regarding the effectiveness of
issued NTPs to streamline the overall project implementation process, thereby
preventing any potential delays in the completion of projects.

8.11 We recommended that the LCE direct the City Engineer to enforce strict compliance among contractors with the prescribed guidelines issued under COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013. This should ensure that all posted signboards for each IP contain all relevant information, and that the project status is consistently updated in accordance with the schedule provided in the same Circular. This will achieve full disclosure in the implementation of government projects and the right of the people to access up-to-date information on matters of public concern.
8.12 Further, we recommended that the LCE promptly inform both the SA and ATL within 10 days after the award of the IP or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards are already posted, thereby facilitating the validation of the same.
8.13 The Office of the City Engineer replied that they will strictly enforce the compliance of contractors in ensuring that all posted signboards/tarpaulins for each IP contain complete information and are always updated. This is to achieve full disclosure in the implementation of government projects.

9.16 In light of these, we recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the
City Engineer, direct the Project Engineers to:

a. strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce is
wearing the required safety gear or PPE at all times within the
construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the
construction workers and the general public;
b. closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP by the
contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of
contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be
referenced during future payment processes; and

c. formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the appropriate
sanctions or penalties to be applied to contractors found in violation of
the CSHP to ensure accountability and adherence to safety protocols.

9.17 The City Engineer acknowledged the observations and committed to ensuring
strict compliance by the contractors in requiring their workforce to wear the
needed safety gears.

10.17 In light of the above, we recommended that the LCE direct the Engineering
Office to submit to the Audit Team a written justification to address the
identified deficiencies, accompanied by evidence substantiating their claims,
to ascertain the legality and validity of the infrastructure projects, as well as the
associated procurement activities.

10.18 During Exit Conference, the City Engineer conveyed that her office will look
into this matter. As of date, the Audit Team has not yet received any written
justification addressing the observations.

16.12 We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer to subject all the
aforementioned contractors/suppliers to an immediate examination of
their books of accounts to determine the appropriate amount of taxes due
from them. Additionally, if deemed correct, require the City Treasurer to
cause the collection of deficient business taxes amounting to at least
₱1,205,143.95, along with associated penalties and interests, resulting from
their under-declared gross sales/receipts.

16.13 We further recommended that the LCE instruct the City Treasurer to
conduct regular annual examination of books of accounts and pertinent
records of all businessmen, as mandated by Section 171 of RA 7160, or the
LGC of 1991, not only to ensure appropriate assessment and collection but
also to reduce the risk of non-collection of business taxes.

16.14 The Management agreed with the recommendations. In fact, some of the
identified contractors and suppliers were examined in previous years and paid
additional business taxes. Due to limited staff, examinations are not fully
realized. For CY 2024, the Office of the City Treasurer committed to examining
the books of accounts of the businesses identified in this observation.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to submit the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements to the Audit Team within the prescribed period under Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 to facilitate the timely audit of accounts.

Based on the foregoing, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE): (a) in coordination with the City Accountant, ensure that a cash advance is granted only after the liquidation of the previous one by requiring a certification from the latter confirming its settlement/accounting before releasing the new one; (b) limit the cash advance granted to the maximum coverage of the fidelity bond of AOs or apply for a higher fidelity bond to safeguard government funds; and (c) ensure that applicable rules and regulations governing cash advances are strictly followed.
We further recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the City Accountant, submit a written explanation regarding the accumulated CAs granted on the last working day and submit their corresponding liquidation reports to the Auditor’s Office.

We commended Management for its continuous compliance with existing regulations on the entitlement and granting of cash advance for confidential funds.  However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the City Accounting Office submit the disbursement vouchers as well as the supporting documents within the required period to the Audit Team for prompt review.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books to reflect actual inventories on hand; (b) City General Services (CGS) Officer to adopt/apply the perpetual inventory method and ensure proper safekeeping of inventories to minimize the risk of possible loss or wastage of government resources; and (c) CGS Officer and City Accountant to ensure coordination between their offices for reconciliation of their reports and for the timely recording of receipts and issuances of inventories.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant make necessary adjustments in the books to correct the noted omission and to reflect the correct balance of the CIP account. This will lead to a more dependable information and fair presentation of the account in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that the LCE direct the: (a) Inventory Committee, CGSO and CAccO to coordinate in reconciling the balances between the Inventory Report, Registry of Public Infrastructure and the accounting records such as the general and the subsidiary ledgers to reflect the correct balance of the PPE account; (b) City Accountant to (i) recognize losses of PPE for non-existing/missing PPEs not presented upon demand and the corresponding receivable equivalent to the PPEs’ depreciated replacement cost; (ii) provide depreciation for all depreciable Property, Plant and Equipment; (iii) trace the variance noted between the balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements and the lapsing schedules; and (c) City General Service Officer to submit the RPCPPE in accordance with the Section 124 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant (a) set up the payable account based on the receipt of goods or services rendered to ensure validity of the obligations and use appropriate terms to avoid confusion as to the nature of the transactions; (b) maintain subsidiary ledgers for each creditor pursuant to Section 111 of P.D. No. 1445; and (c) improve the monitoring of accounts in accordance with IPSAS 29 on the proper recognition and derecognition of liabilities to avoid delay and frequent corrections, and uncertainty on the accuracy of balances; and (d) ensure complete documentation of payable accounts for accuracy of recorded transactions.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed that the City Accountant: (a) require the implementing heads of barangay to (i) exhaust all efforts to coordinate with the previous Barangay Administration to facilitate submission of liquidation documents for the long-unliquidated financial assistance; (ii) religiously submit all liquidation documents to the City Accounting Office for all financial assistance received; (iii) immediately refund the unutilized balance of the financial assistance to the City Treasurer’s Office after the purpose of the granting of which have been completed; and (iv) strictly observe the proper procedures in the liquidation of financial assistance and ensure completeness of documents upon submission to the accounting personnel in-charge of the books of the barangay; (b) ensure that all required liquidation documents for previously granted financial assistance have been submitted and issue a Certification that funds previously transferred to the Implementing Agency (IA) has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before processing the grant/transfer of another financial assistance; and (c) closely monitor the compliance of the recipient barangays on the submission of liquidation documents within the prescribed period.
We further recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant: (a) recognize in the books appropriate amounts of impairment losses for the non-collectability of the dormant receivables; and (b) start the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts based on the guidelines and procedures provided by COA Circular 2023-008, dated August 17, 2023.

We reiterated, with modification, our prior year’s audit recommendation, that the LCE: (a) consider delegating the responsibility of countersigning checks to the City Administrator, as provided in R.A. No. 7160, to minimize delays in the payment process; and (b) adhere to the timeline of activities outlined in the QMS disbursement process.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Engineering Office to facilitate the completion of the remaining projects as soon as possible and ensure the proper utilization and liquidation of the funds.

We reiterated and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the City Planning and Development Office and the City Engineer’s Office to review and enhance its planning and monitoring strategies and strictly adhere to deadlines in the implementation of projects to ensure complete implementation and prompt delivery of services.
We further recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the City Treasurer’s Office to release the checks for the Aid to Barangay projects earlier to give the barangays enough time to observe proper implementation of the projects and timely utilization of funds.

We commended the City Government and its officials for being one of the LGUs with the Best Ecological Solid Waste Management as recognized by DENR and its responsiveness to the environment awareness cause.  However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board to: (a) review and update its 10-year SWM Plan as required by RA No. 9003; (b) enforce  the proper wastes segregation  at source and fully implement the “No Segregation, No Collection” Policy; (c) strengthen information and education campaigns on the hazards of open burning, open dumpsites and other ill practices prohibited under Section 48 of RA 9003;  (d) ensure that wastes are properly collected from households and transported to MRFs to avoid improper disposal along the roads and other public places; and (e) propose worthy PPAs in the City’s AIP that can actually be realized with the estimated budget to eliminate huge gaps between the amount of the planned projects versus the approved budget that may indicate poor planning.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive Officer direct the (a) CDRRMC to include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous year in the LDRRMFIP; and (b) City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the affected Trust-Liability accounts in order to conform with the provisions of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 for the proper monitoring of the Unexpended DRRMF following their five-year limitation.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the CDRRMC to (i) monitor the  implementation of  the LDRRMP to optimize the utilization of its LDRRMF in order to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in the community and disaster risks, including projected climate risks, (ii) submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council (LDC) to the COA auditor of the LGU as mandated in COA Circular 2012-002.
Management Comment:
The Management explained that the Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF is regularly prepared by the City Accountant’s Office (CAccO) on a monthly basis, and a copy is furnished to the City DRRM Office. It was also mentioned that the CAccO was not aware that the said report should be submitted to the Auditor’s Office. Nonetheless, the recommendation of the Audit Team regarding the submission of the report on or before the 15th day after the end of every month was duly noted.
We further recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant to (i) record purchase of welfare/relief goods and other expendable items to inventory account and issuances thereof to the appropriate expense accounts; and (ii) maintain corresponding Subsidiary Ledger for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment set forth under COA Circular 2014-002 and COA Circular 2012-002, respectively; and (iii) to remit all unutilized cash donations to the Bureau of the Treasury (BTr) or to the source agency in accordance with applicable rules and regulations on cash donations received.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive: (a) instruct the Planning Officer to ensure that proposed projects have undergone thorough study, adequate planning and review prior to approval and implementation thereof; (b) submit justification on the continuous non-usage of the infrastructures and the postponement of the procurement of the cremation machine; and (c) schedule a meeting with the head of Building Maintenance, Engineering and Government Service Office  to assign the specific task of  maintaining  the infrastructures, so that government property will be taken cared of properly and further deterioration will be prevented.

We commended the City Government’s efforts in addressing the health and welfare concern of its constituents by way of entering into a partnership with a private institution thru the provision of accessible and free health care services specifically, the City’s indigent patients with chronic kidney failure disease thru the Fernandino Dialysis Center. However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive and the City Health Officer/Focal Person accomplish the remaining unaccomplished requirements of the MOA so that the continuity of the project would be assured.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local School Board:  (a) establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF among schools and incorporate these PPAs in the appropriate budget format to ensure project monitoring and ascertain the efficient and effective utilization of SEF in accordance with its intended purpose; and (b) continually utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Treasurer to investigate the variance noted between the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and the RPT delinquency report and make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements.
We also reiterated that the Local Chief Executive require the City Treasurer to ensure that necessary enhancements are in place in the RPT system in order to make it a more reliable source of data for the City’s Real Property Tax.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of all the Municipality’s properties annually, prepare and submit the RPCPPE and facilitate a one-time cleansing of PPE account balances through the implementation of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
Require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain property records for all PPE and undertake the required reconciliation of the Property and Accounting records thereafter.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the preparation and submission of the required BRS, maintain SL for all bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the FS and other documents; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Source Agencies on the status of fund transfers to the Municipality and close the accounts that have become dormant/inactive and revert back to the respective Source Agencies or the Bureau of the Treasury, as the case maybe, if the balances of the said accounts represent excess of the funds after the purpose to which they were funded for by National Government Agencies (NGAs) and/or other Agencies were fulfilled.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct verification, analysis and validation of these receivable balances, to determine their collectability and pursue collection, refund or liquidation thereof, if warranted. We also recommended that the LCE file the request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts which could no longer be collected to COA, duly supported with the required documents.
Moreover, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant take appropriate action to reconcile the quarterly schedule of dormant receivable accounts and the books of accounts to be used for FS preparation.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to review or trace the cause of the negative balances posted in the ledgers of the Inter-Agency Payable and Other Payables accounts, prepare the necessary adjusting entries and update the affected ledger accounts in order to present the correct amount in the FS.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct an in-depth verification of the Due to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth accounts, perform reconciliation between the SLs and the Schedules of Remittances and remit immediately the withheld funds due to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth, if warranted; and
Evaluate the current system of withholding and remitting taxes, premiums, contributions and other deductions to prevent under or over remittance thereof.

We recommended that the LCE:
Consider hiring additional competent staffs and personnel in the Accounting Office to hasten the process of preparation and submission of monthly, quarterly and annual financial reports to the Office of the Auditor; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Budget Officer to be mindful of the deadlines and adhere with the provisions of Sections 70 and 73 of the Manual on NGAS, specifically on the submission of trial balances, FS, SAAOB and other supporting schedules.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Accounting Office all the DVs, including the corresponding reports of each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month;
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Office of the Auditor the paid DVs on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month and submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the lacking DVs, complete with supporting documents; and
Remind the concerned officials that processing of vouchers be only undertaken to those which were properly approved/signed and with complete supporting documents.

We recommended that Management:
Submit justification on the noted deficiencies and refrain from expending beyond the appropriated budget in the succeeding implementation of PPAs. Likewise, direct the Municipal Budget Officer to observe diligence in posting in RAAO to properly monitor the correct balances and to avoid overspending; and
Ensure that the expenditures to be charged against the 20 percent DF are not among the prohibited expenses stated in DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, dated November 4, 2020.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and the MDRRMO to reconcile immediately the noted differences between the CY 2023 LDRRMF Monthly Utilization Reports and CY 2023 FS;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to observe proper recording of accounts and ensure that MF-Capital Outlay must be made continuing in the GF books and any savings shall be available for use in the disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in the LDRRMFIP; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly observe prompt transfer or deposit to the STF of the unutilized portion of LDRRMF (QRF and MF-MOOE), in accordance with Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and ensure that the amount to be transferred is accurate and complete.

We recommended that Management exercise prudence in utilizing the LDRRMF and adhere to the existing rules and regulations governing the utilization thereof. Moreover, Management require the:
MDRRMO and Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds for the various expenses of MDRRM Office chargeable against MOOE in the GF;
Municipal Budget Officer to stop the practice of charging prior year’s expense against current appropriation; and
Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking supporting documents for each transaction and ensure that documentary requirements for subsequent transactions are complete prior to any disbursment of government funds.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require the BAC to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of contracts, POs and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review.
Further, direct the personnel responsible for accepting deliveries to notify COA of the time and date of the scheduled deliveries, furnish copies of AIRs within 24 hours upon acceptance and submit report on deliveries received and issued as of year-end.
We recommended the LGU to (1) communicate and solicit comments about the issue to the Source Agency, and (2) Submit Memoranda of Agreement when available.

We hereby recommended the following to the management:
Write formal request to the Provincial Government for the MOAs of said projects along with the requests for inspection.
If there are no MOAs, then evaluate the recorded liability accounts for possible adjusting/correcting entry.
Make proper adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the Due to LGU accounts in relation to the variances observed.

We hereby recommended the following to the management:
Assess the need to make proper adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the Fuel, Oil, and Lubricants Inventory Account; and
Ensure that periodic Monitoring and Reconciliation of Accounts of the Municipal Accounting Office and Office of the General Services is done.

We recommended the following to the management to:
Evaluate the transactions and make necessary adjusting/correcting entries, if warranted.
Subsequently, ensure that accounting treatment should be aligned with the intentions of the MOA or the like.
Review the roles and responsibilities of the LGU to ensure their faithful compliance.

We recommended the management to:
Create clear guidelines for all housing projects of the LGU which should detail how public raffles must be conducted, along with the criteria for eligibility, and the necessary documentary requirements. It is encouraged that the LGU should use the NHA Memorandum Circular No. 2021-021 as their guide.
Assess the correctness of the accounting entries related to the said project and create necessary correcting/adjusting entries, if warranted.
Advise the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer to regularly coordinate to ensure correct and timely asset reclassification.

We hereby recommended the following to the management:
Prepare deeds of donation to facilitate proper conveyance of assets
Prepare necessary adjusting entries in line with Paragraph 14 (a and b) of International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 17
Conduct physical inventory with the presence of the Municipal accountant or her representative to confirm accounting records.

We recommended the following to the management:
Conduct Physical Inventory of all assets in coordination with the other offices, particularly the GSO, MEO, and MASSO;
Record necessary journal entries for asset adjustments or newly discovered assets, if warranted; and
Consider applying for the One-Time Cleansing for the remaining unidentified assets following COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended to the management:
Submit the lacking documents
Facilitate transfer the ownership in the Tax Declaration in favor of the Municipal Government;
Expedite the titling of the land property; and
Strictly follow existing laws and guidelines for subsequent purchases of land and avoid paying the owner/s of the land before the documents are complete.

We recommend the Management to:
Submit the lacking documents of the afore-cited projects particularly those that are still ongoing;
Prohibit extension of work of projects with lacking documents and compute the necessary liquidated damages if requirements of the provision are not strictly followed; and
Submit the lacking contracts with complete documents for the listed projects below:
Opening with Concreting of FMR Brgy. Cayaga
Opening with Concreting of FMR Brgy. Little Baguio
Construction of Multi-Purpose Building/Barangay at Barangay Durian, Bonacao, Bulalang, Dao, San Jose & Sto. Domingo

We hereby recommended the following to the management to:
Re-assess the Statement of Work Accomplished by the contractor and demand refund if warranted.
Re-inspect all the subprojects and determine if proportionate claim against the Performance Security may be availed.
Review the veracity behind the declared accomplishment of the project prior to reclassifying the “completed” assets into its appropriate account.
Evaluate whether the filing of administrative cases to appropriate officials is substantiated.

We hereby recommended the following to the management:
Re-assess the Statement of Work Accomplished by the contractor and demand refund if warranted.
Re-inspect all the subprojects and determine if proportionate claim against the Performance Security may be availed.
Review the veracity behind the declared accomplishment of the project prior to reclassifying the “completed” assets into its appropriate account.
Evaluate whether the filing of administrative cases to appropriate officials is substantiated.

We recommended that management should set up controls and procedures on the preparation, compilation and submission of the technical documents.
We also recommended that management should assign or hire personnel that will do the above recommendation.
We recommended further that management should have a monitoring tool that will remind personnel of the deadline of the submission of contracts and its supporting documents to avoid payments that will be suspended in audit.

We recommended the management to require the warranty securities for all procurement of goods for subsequent procurement activities subject to the clear limitations under Item M of Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended the following to the management to:
Submit justification regarding the issues cited for further evaluation;
Remind the budget officer to re-acquaint herself with the 20% LDF utilization guidelines for all succeeding transactions; and
Stop charging expenditures that do not align with the 20% LDF utilization guidelines.
Revisit DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 in order for personnel to be familiar of what projects or activity that can be charged against the 20% Local Development Fund.

We recommended the management to:
Ensure that all grants of cash advances are for a legally authorized specific purpose.
Secure complete supporting documents for grants of cash advances as enjoined in COA Circular Nos. 2012-001 and 2023-004.
We recommended the management to follow strictly the correct procedures in computing the ABCs during the planning stage to avoid raising doubts relating to the procurement process of the LGU.

We recommended to the management:
Submit the lacking documents; and
Strictly Follow the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for documentary requirements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

Department Heads to be prudent and meticulous in the preparation of LBP Forms by ensuring that LBP Form No. 2 pertains to Programmed Appropriation and Obligations by Object of Expenditure while LBP Form No. 4 to specific PPAs. OIC-City Budget Officer and City Planning and Development Coordinator to provide AIP Reference Code in the AIP and LBP Form No. 4 to ensure budget linkage.
observations.managementAction:
