The City Budget Officer and City Planning and Development Coordinator ensured that AIP reference codes are assigned in the attached AIP and LBP Form 4.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to direct the BAC to submit the appropriate BIR Certificate of Registration allowing the suppliers to engage in the industries where the sale of the goods it supplied is covered, and to thereafter, ensure that all emergency procurements are awarded to a technically, legally, and financially capable supplier, contractor, distributor, or consultant in compliance with the aforementioned provisions.  In case of huge quantities required by emergencies, procure from multiple suppliers who are legally, technically and financially capable in the absence of a single supplier with such capabilities instead of transacting with suppliers whose line of business is doubtful.
observations.managementAction: The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure not only emergency procurements are awarded to a technically, legally, and financially capable supplier, contractor, distributor, or consultant in compliance with the aforementioned provisions.  Likewise, in case of huge quantities required by emergencies, procure from multiple suppliers who are legally, technically and financially capable in the absence of a single supplier with such capabilities.

AAR CY 2022 | The City Accountant will closely coordinate with the City General Services Office and committed to continue the implementation of the One-Time Cleansing of the PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular 2020-006.  Moreover, the City Accountant will validate the veracity of the records and prepare the necessary adjustments for the misclassified accounts, provision for depreciation and variance between the ledgers and schedules.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the (a) City Engineer to submit promptly the Certificate of Completion and Statement of Work Accomplished to the City Accountant to ensure that cost of partially completed infrastructure projects are accurately and timely recorded and proper reclassification could be effected in the books; and (b) City Accountant to adjust the PPE accounts to recognize completed projects and ensure that the CIP accounts reflect only the accumulated costs of partially completed infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction:
AAR CY 2022 | The City Accountant will make the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts to recognize the completed projects in accordance with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 17 and Section 50, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual. Moreover, to timely account for the partially and fully completed projects, the City Accountant will strictly require the City Engineer’s Office to submit promptly the Certificate of Completion and Statement of Work Accomplished.

On the other hand, the CEO assured the submission of a progress report as of December 31 of every year with the Statement of Work Accomplished of the City’s on-going projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant to (a) continue to be compliant with the terms and conditions of the loan agreement entered into with the Land Bank of the Philippines; (b) record the interest due for fair presentation of the Loans Payable and its related Interest Expense account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | The Management will continue to be compliant with the terms and conditions of the loan agreement as recommended by the Commission. In addition, the Accounting Office will take up appropriate adjusting entries to fairly present the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Development Council to carefully plan the City’s PPAs including the funding sources to be included in the AIP as basis for the preparation of the Annual and Supplemental Budgets pursuant to Article 410 of the IRR of RA 7160.   If there are re-alignment of appropriations, amendment of the AIP through SP resolutions should be sought. 

We reiterated that the City Planning and Development Office and the City Engineering Office review and enhance its planning and monitoring strategies and strictly adhere to the timelines in the implementation of projects to ensure complete implementation and prompt delivery of services.
observations.managementAction:
AAR CY 2022 | According to the City Planning and Development Office, the change in the leadership in the City in July 2022, translated into a shift in prioritization in terms of program implementation. The said office, however, adheres to the recommendation particularly on planning and monitoring as it endeavors to elicit a more active participation from the Local Development Council (LDC) in the formulation, implementation and monitoring of the City’s plans, programs and projects.

The City Engineer’s Office, on the other hand, committed strict adherence to the timely implementation of projects by strengthening its coordination with the concerned offices and other stakeholders to ensure the complete implementation and prompt delivery of services.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local School Board 
utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020; and 
(b) establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF across schools to ensure its efficient and effective use in accordance with its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | The Division Office thru the Division Schools Superintendent agreed with our observations and committed to abide by the Audit Team’s recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the City Officials particularly the City Health Officer-In-Charge in the successful administration of vaccines not only to the Fernandinos but also to other residents of the Province.  

However, we further recommended that the City Officials (a) improve the storage capacity of the City’s cold room and its over-all function; (b) submit a written explanation on the 364,869 unaccounted doses of vaccines; (c) install additional controls on the recording of received and utilized vaccines starting with completely filled out PTRs and other supporting documents to avoid further lost and wastage of vaccines to the disadvantage of the government; and (d) adopt additional measures to  improve the implementation of the COVID-19 vaccination program to ensure health safety of affected beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | Management assured that all written observations are well noted and shall strictly observe appropriate Cold Chain Management to ensure proper allocation and utilization of health resources.

observations.recommendation: We commended Management for the proper withholding and full remittance of the monthly taxes and GSIS contributions via eFPS and eBCS in compliance with the requirements set forth under Revenue Regulations 1-2013 and Republic Act 8291, and for the timely and complete payments made of the monthly dues to the PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG in accordance with PhilHealth Circular 0001, s. 2014 and HDMF Circular No. 274, respectively.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2022 | Management committed of its continued observance of proper withholding of taxes as well as timely remittance thereof.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that all projects to be implemented by the City are within the approved AIP. Henceforth, conduct an in-depth planning in the prioritization of projects.
Henceforth, conduct an in-depth planning in the prioritization of projects. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that all proposed PPAs be deliberated and harmonized with departmental activities and higher-level plans.
observations.managementAction:
As per checking all the proposed programs, projects and activities funded by the FY 2021 LGSF-DRRAP are listed in the 2021 AIP except for the source of funds not properly indicated as external funds.
The City conducts an extensive approach in the preparation of its AIP. Series of sessions/committee hearings is conducted involving the SP committee on appropriation, Local Finance Committee, and the concerned departments to review and deliberate proposed PPAs and harmonize departmental activities with the City’s goals/objectives and higher-level plans.
observations.reason: The Management still failed to include all concerned projects to their AIP from other funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Office direct the (a) City Engineering Office to properly plan and supervise the implementation of infrastructure projects to avoid delay, hasten project implementation and attain early the desired objectives of the projects; and (b) City Planning and Development Office to (i) evaluate the technical viability of proposed development projects before these are being requested for appropriation to avoid re-alignments; and (ii) monitor the proper and timely implementation of the projects at the barangay level.
observations.managementAction:
Infrastructure projects in CY 2021 grew in number from a total of 105 in 2020 to 146 projects in 2021. The CEO exerted their concerted efforts to provide effective and efficient engineering services despite having limited personnel, however, fortuitous events such as the unpredictability of weather conditions and other issues during the implementation period resulted to work disruptions and non-continuity of some infrastructure projects.
The City Planning and Development Coordinators Office is currently exploring the possibility of proposing a resolution indicating specific time frame in the implementation of Aid to Barangay projects. This concern will be raised in the next LDC meeting.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) reconstitute the composition of the existing GFPS Execom and TWG in consonance with Section 4.1.A of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to further strengthen the GFPS and accelerate gender mainstreaming in the City; and (b) instruct the GFPS to set up and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-aggregated data or GAD database to serve as basis in the performance-based and gender-responsive planning and budgeting.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) reconstitute the composition of the existing GFPS Execom and TWG in consonance with Section 4.1.A of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to further strengthen the GFPS and accelerate gender mainstreaming in the City; and (b) instruct the GFPS to set up and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-aggregated data or GAD database to serve as basis in the performance-based and gender-responsive planning and budgeting.
observations.managementAction:
The reconstitution of the GFPS Execom and TWG was not done since 2020 as the GFPS-TWG Head and Secretariat were augmented to COVID-19 response, but the City assured the Audit Team that they will abide with the audit recommendation to strictly follow the guidelines in the Reconstitution of the GFPS Execom and TWG as well as the election of the GFPS-TWG Chair.
With regards to the GAD database, the City is currently preparing for the updating of its socio-economic profile and other sectoral plans, which may be used to cross-reference and/or verify data from other offices or agencies. The development and full operationalization of the Centralized GAD Database System updates is included in the 2024 CICTO Development Plan.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) in coordination with the
City Accountant, ensure that a cash advance will be granted only after the
liquidation of previous one as evidenced by a Certification from the latter that the
previous one is settled/accounted for; (b) limit the cash advance granted to the
maximum coverage of the fidelity bond of AOs or apply for higher fidelity bond to
safeguard government funds; (c) instruct the City Social Welfare Officer to
properly plan the activities and programs, estimate the cost of each project and set
the target date of implementation thereof to avoid the cancellation of the checks
issued for the purpose; and (d) ensure that applicable rules and regulations
governing cash advances are strictly followed.

We reiterated our recommendations that the LCE require the (a) City
Accountant to (i) consistently record the purchased inventories under the respective
asset accounts; and (ii) ensure that the de-recognition of inventories is recorded only
when these are issued to end-users/beneficiaries; (b) City General Services (CGS)
Officer to ensure proper safekeeping of supplies and materials inventory to
minimize the risk of possible loss or wastage of government resources; and (c) CGS
Officer and City Accountant to ensure coordination between their offices for the
timely recording of receipts and issuances of inventories.

We reiterated our recommendations that the LCE direct the (a) Inventory
Team to (i) conduct complete annual physical inventory of its PPE including the
fixed assets and prepare the RPCPPE; and (ii) complete the reconciliation of the
PPE balances between the City Accountant and CGSO records based on the results
of the physical inventory and in accordance with the procedural guidelines
presented in COA Circular 2020-006 to reflect the correct balance of the PPE in the
balance sheet; (b) City Accountant to (i) provide depreciation for all depreciable
Property, Plant and Equipment and continue providing depreciation for condemned
properties until their de-recognition in the books; (ii) trace the variance noted in the
balances of PPE accounts enumerated in the table; (iii) ensure proper classification
of PPE items in the books; and (iv) make the necessary adjustments in the books to
correct all erroneous recording/classification noted for a more reliable information
and fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the (a) City Engineer to submit
promptly the Certificate of Completion and Statement of Work Accomplished to the
City Accountant to ensure that cost of partially completed infrastructure projects
are accurately and timely recorded and proper reclassification could be effected in
the books; and (b) City Accountant to adjust the PPE accounts to recognize
completed projects and ensure that the CIP accounts reflect only the accumulated
costs of partially completed infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) refrain from recording the
request for cash assistance as accounts payable and follow the procedures for
recording of obligations provided in the accounting for CAFOA; and (b) draw the
appropriate journal entries to adjust/correct all noted deficiencies for accurate and
fair presentation of the Accounts Payable; and (c) ensure complete documentation
of payable accounts for accuracy and validity of recorded transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City
Accountant to (a) continue to be compliant with the terms and conditions of the loan
agreement entered into with the Land Bank of the Philippines; (b) record the
interest due for fair presentation of the Loans Payable and its related Interest
Expense account in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Accountant (a) require
the implementing heads of barangay to (i) exhaust all efforts to coordinate with the
previous Barangay Administration to facilitate submission of liquidation documents
for the long-unliquidated financial assistance; (ii) religiously submit all liquidation
documents to the City Accounting Office for all financial assistance received; (iii)
immediately refund the unutilized balance of the financial assistance to the City
Treasurer’s Office after the purposes of the grants are completed; and (iv) strictly
observe the proper procedures in the liquidation of financial assistance and ensure
completeness of documents upon submission to the accounting personnel in-charge
of the books of the barangay; (b) ensure that all required liquidation documents for
previously granted financial assistance are submitted as basis for the issuance of a
Certification that funds previously transferred to the Implementing Agency (IA) has
been liquidated and accounted for in the books before processing the grant/transfer
of another financial assistance; and (c) closely monitor the compliance of the
recipient barangays on the submission of liquidation documents within the
prescribed period.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local
Development Council to carefully plan the City’s PPAs including the funding
sources to be included in the AIP as basis for the preparation of the Annual and
Supplemental Budgets pursuant to Article 410 of the IRR of RA 7160. If there are
re-alignment of appropriations, amendment of the AIP through SP resolutions
should be sought.
We reiterated that the City Planning and Development Office and the City
Engineering Office review and enhance its planning and monitoring strategies and
strictly adhere to the timelines in the implementation of projects to ensure complete
implementation and prompt delivery of services.

We recommended that the Local School Board (a) utilize the Special
Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the
DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2, s.
2020; and (b) establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF across schools to
ensure its efficient and effective use in accordance with its intended purpose.

However, we further recommended that the City Officials (a) improve the
storage capacity of the City’s cold room and its over-all function; (b) submit a
written explanation on the 364,869 unaccounted doses of vaccines; (c) install
additional controls on the recording of received and utilized vaccines starting with
completely filled out PTRs and other supporting documents to avoid further lost
and wastage of vaccines to the disadvantage of the government; and (d) adopt
additional measures to improve the implementation of the COVID-19 vaccination
program to ensure health safety of affected beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City
Accountant to coordinate with the City Treasurer to investigate the variance noted
between the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and the RPT delinquency report and
make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly the accounts in the financial
statements.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City
Treasurer to ensure that necessary improvements are in place in the RPT system in
order to make it a more reliable source of data for the City’s Real Property Tax.

We recommended that Management (a) require the job-order personnel who
failed to physically report to present themselves at the office of the Audit Team
personally together with their valid IDs; (b) conduct sufficient intervention to
address the continuous hiring of JOs such as lack of eligibility of those who have
been continuously hired and rehired for years; (c) limit the assignment of essential
duties in core offices to permanent and casual employees; (d) refrain from hiring
JOs who will exercise supervisory control over regular employees; (e) refrain from
hiring consultants whose functions are already being performed by the regular
employees; and (f) strictly comply with the aforementioned CSC/COA/DBM Joint
Memorandum Circular on the regularity of hiring JO employees.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) CDRRMC to
(i) include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the
unexpended LDRRMF of previous year in the LDRRMFIP; and (ii) properly
implement the LDRRMP and optimize the utilization of its LDRRMF to lessen the
socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in the community and
disaster risks, including projected climate risks, and to enhance disaster
preparedness and response capabilities of the community; and (b) City Accountant
to (i) record purchase of welfare goods for distribution to inventory account and
issuances thereof to the appropriate expense accounts; and (ii) maintain
corresponding Subsidiary Ledger for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue
Equipment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Accountant
and the Treasurer to strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 by ensuring
that payments are made in checks, except when it can be clearly shown that it is
impossible, impractical or difficult to pay the suppliers by checks; (b) Disbursing
Officer to attach original copies of attendance sheet to avoid possible duplication in
the usage thereof; (c) Accountable Officers concerned to provide adequate
information to support claims for representation expenses as these facilitate
verification and/or validation. This can be in the form of a summary list which may
contain the date, invoice/receipt number, name of the issuing establishment/party,
the nature/particulars of the claims indicating the official purpose/s of the claims,
the officials/parties/guests entertained, and the amount being reimbursed/claimed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive establish an effective
internal control on the grant of financial assistance by establishing policy guidelines,
such as the amount and limit of the grant and an adequate reporting system in the
utilization thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: (a) observe the timeline of
activities in the approval and issuance of checks to creditors as provided in the
disbursement process of the QMS; (b) facilitate the immediate evaluation of
documents and approval of checks to effect prompt release to creditors; and (c)
authorize the City Administrator to counter sign checks for payment of utilities and
payroll to lessen the volume of checks being approved by the City Mayor.

We commended the City Government and its officials for being one of the
recipients of the MANILA BAYani Award for its responsiveness to the environment
awareness cause. However, we still recommended that the Local Chief Executive
and the Solid Waste Management Board (a) enforce the proper wastes segregation
at source and implement the “No Segregation No Collection” Policy; (b) strengthen
information and education campaigns on the hazards of open burning, open
dumpsites and other ill practices prohibited under Section 48 of R.A. No. 9003; and
(c) ensure that wastes are properly collected from households and transported to
MRFs to avoid improper disposal along the roads and other public places.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (a)
reconstitute the composition of the GFPS Execom and TWG in accordance with
Item 4.1.A of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to further strengthen
the GFPS and accelerate gender mainstreaming in the City; and (b) instruct the
GFPS to set up and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would
ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-aggregated data or
GAD database considering that this will serve as basis in the performance-based
and gender-responsive planning and budgeting.

We also recommended that the GFPS follow the guide in the preparation of
the GAD AR as provided under Item 4.C.8 PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No.
2013-01 and to attach the documents required to facilitate the proper evaluation and
assessment of the implementation of the GAD PPAs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive recommend the enactment of a Revenue Measure on the imposition of RT-PCR test fees and include the additional revenue thereof in the annual budget/supplemental budget for proper appropriation.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accounting Office to effect the necessary corrections in the books of accounts to accurately reflect the true nature of the transactions to avoid the current misclassification in reporting in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City entered into a joint project with Green City Medical Center (GCMC) to ensure an affordable direct access for the mass testing of the people identified as exposed, symptomatic, and vulnerable by the City Health Unit of the City of San Fernando. The collections from the testing fee were not covered by the Revenue Code, thus, the city recognized the collection as trust liabilities under the trust fund account. Moreover, the Joint Project with the GCMC will cease operation at the end of the term of the administration, thus, the enactment of Revenue Measure on the imposition of RT-PCR test fees is deemed no longer necessary.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE require the Inventory Team to (a) (i) conduct a complete annual physical inventory of its PPE and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE; (ii) perform periodic reconciliation of the PPE balances between CAO and CGSO records based on the results of the physical inventory and in accordance with the procedural guidelines in COA Circular 2020-006, to reflect the correct balance of the PPE in the balance sheet.
We likewise reiterated our recommendations that the LCE direct the City Accountant to (a) provide depreciation for all depreciable PPE accounts; (b) review and ensure that provision for depreciation shall not exceed the residual value of PPE items; (c) accomplish accurately the supporting schedules for all PPE accounts; (d) analyze and reconcile the variance noted on the balances of PPE accounts; (e) ensure proper classification of PPE items in the books; (f) draw the necessary adjustments in the books to correct all erroneous recording/classification noted and journal entries for the disposed unserviceable properties; and (g) adopt all the principles prescribed in IPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the City’s PPE accounts for more reliable information and fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements at year-end.
observations.managementAction:
A Technical Working Group was created to study and recommend plans, procedures and set targets in compliance with COA Circular 2020-006, and to address and comply with the audit observations.
3.9. With regard to the over provision of and non-provision of depreciation, misclassified accounts and variance between ledger and schedules, the CAO will validate the veracity of records and prepare necessary adjustments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City Accountant to (i) consistently record the purchased inventories to their respective asset accounts
observations.managementAction: and (ii) ensure that the de-recognition of inventories is recorded only when these are issued to end-users/beneficiaries
observations.reason: and (c) CGS Officer and City Accountant to ensure coordination between their offices for the timely recording of receipts and issuances of inventories.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (a) Accounting Office will be (i) set up the payable account with based on the receipt of goods or services rendered in conformity with the Local Government Chart of Accounts; (ii) maintain subsidiary ledgers for each creditor pursuant to Section 111 of P.D. 1445; and (iii) improve the monitoring of accounts to avoid delay and frequent corrections, and uncertainty on the accuracy of balances; and (b) department heads concerned systematically and promptly document the transactions or services rendered to their respective Offices so as not to further delay the settlement of obligations.
observations.managementAction:
In line with the 10 Point Agenda of the City, 2021 “Gobyernong Mapagkakatiwalaan” and to help resolve some of the internal weaknesses, an in-house system named “Financial Management and Procurement System (FMaPS) was developed to further strengthen the internal control and better safeguard the government assets.
Rest assured that the recommendations referred to in the audit observation will be complied with.
observations.reason:
This was not
given due
attention by the
City Accounting
Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) strictly set a deadline to all claimants for the submission of documents to the City Accountant for all unsettled/unpaid lawful expenditures and obligations for recording in the books and ensure that the set date will give ample time to the City Accountant for the preparation of financial statements so as to comply with the submission thereof to this Commission within the prescribed period
observations.managementAction: and (b) require the City Planning and Development Officer to consider proper timing on the release of funds to barangays for the implementation of projects and set the deadline for the submission of their liquidation reports.
observations.reason:
Fully Implemented
Not Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Officer instruct the LDRRM Council to (a) provide an appropriation corresponding to each specific activity in the LDRRMFIP for ease in the monitoring of utilization; and (b) strictly follow the accounting and reporting guidelines in the receipt and utilization of cash and in-kind donations for DRRM programs.
We also recommended that the amount inappropriately charged against the 30% QRF be restored to the LDRMMF through the supplemental budget, if the corresponding funds are available. Henceforth, ensure that the LDRMMF be used solely to support disaster risk management activities.
observations.managementAction:
The City ensured to provide an appropriation corresponding to each specific activity in the LDRRMFIP. The payment of welfare cash assistance for the provision of relief and early intervention and services is deemed appropriately charged to 30% QRF since the period of distribution was still under the state of calamity per Proclamation No. 1218.
No donation was received during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LSB, together with the City Engineering Office, properly plan the implementation of infrastructure projects to avoid delay for the early attainment of the desired objectives of the projects.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office humbly recognizes the observations and appreciates the recommendations given by the Commission.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct (a) the City Accountant to submit the requisite documents; (b) henceforth, the concerned City Engineering personnel to be more diligent in the computation of the estimated project cost for future infrastructure projects; and (c) the City Engineer and the project consultant to rigidly monitor the progress of works of the project and compel the contractor to expedite the completion of works as agreed upon. For future projects undertaking, we recommend an in-depth planning process to come up with an attainable completion period and monitor the progress of works for the timely enjoyment of the project benefits by the intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction:
The City Government, thru its Bids and Awards Committee and City General Services Office, ensures its 100% adherence and compliance with the provisions of the RA 9184 throughout the procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and consulting services.
Accordingly, the documents are available for their submission and the BAC assured its compliance thereof.
The basis in the Estimated Unit Cost per Work Item as presented in the Program of Work, which was the computed amount of each work item in the Detailed Quantity and Cost Estimates rounded off to the nearest 2 decimal places. The rounded amount, as presented in the PW, which was multiplied with the total area/quantity/unit thus, resulted to a discrepancy with the total amount in the Detailed Quantity and Cost Estimates. To tally with the PW, adjustment on the amount of the Detailed Quantity and Cost Estimates was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) City Engineering Office personnel concerned to submit documents in support of the quantity and cost of filling materials spent for the project; and (b) (i) City Accountant to submit the documents as mentioned under Item 1.3, and (ii) BAC to submit an explanation on not requiring the submission thereof by the Supplier.
observations.managementAction:
The project filling materials for two phases were considered goods because the area which needs embankment was too large and irregular in shape. The amount of embankment was difficult to measure thus, estimate was done based on site inspection and average elevation of the area between 1 to 2 meters i.e. average elevation was on 1.5 meters.
Given the budget allocation for each year, the project was implemented by phase method.
For cost effective measure, the two phases were considered to have only the supply and delivery of filling materials so that no extra cost will be incurred for engineering works thus, the heavy equipment of the City were used in the spreading and compaction of the materials.
The City Engineer’s Office humbly recognizes and appreciates the observations and recommendations of the Commission. The Office will continue to be motivated in aiming for improvement and innovation and learn from its past experiences.
The City Government, thru its Bids and Awards Committee and City General Services Office, ensures its 100% adherence and compliance with the provisions of the RA No. 9184 throughout the procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and consulting services.
The Statement of Equipment Owned or Under Leased Agreement supported by proof of ownership or certification of availability of the equipment from the lessor as part of the technical documents were not required since these are inclusive of the technical documents for infrastructure projects as provided under the 6th Edition of Philippine Bidding Documents.
Nonetheless, the BAC will comply with the submission of all the documentary requirements. (a) For projects wherein the procurement process has already started, the Lowest Calculated Bidder will be required the same as additional documents during the bid evaluation process; and (b) for projects wherein the procurement process has not yet started, amend the Project Procurement Management Plan and Annual Procurement Plan particularly, the category from goods and services to infrastructure/civil works.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the CENRO to (a) review and update the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan to effectively address the current challenges in the solid waste management and include programs to mitigate the adverse impacts on global environment from the increase of waste produced in response to the COVID-19 crisis
observations.managementAction: and (b) ensure the implementation of feasible projects/programs/activities committed in the City’s 10-year solid waste management plan and regularly assess the programs to determine the effects on waste reduction.
observations.reason: Fully Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Budget Officer explain in writing the reversions of appropriations which were not considered as savings. Henceforth, strictly comply with the procedural requirements of AO 47, s. 1993 on changes in the annual budget.
observations.managementAction:
The reversions of appropriations in the Supplemental Budgets were the savings that are supported by funds actually available as certified by the local treasurer.
The savings were derived from the projects/programs/activities which remain free of any obligation or encumbrance and which are still available after the satisfactory completion or unavoidable discontinuance, or abandonment of the work, activity or purpose for which the appropriation was originally authorized or which result from unobligated compensation and related costs pertaining to vacant positions and leaves of absence without pay.
The savings were properly reverted in accordance with Article 417 under AO 47.

observations.recommendation: No specific recommendation beyond continued compliance was made.
observations.managementAction: The City commended for the proper withholding and full remittance of the monthly taxes and GSIS contributions via eFPS and eBCS in compliance with the requirements set forth under the Revenue Regulation 1-2013 and Republic Act 8291, and for the timely and complete payments made of the monthly dues towards PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG in accordance with PhilHealth Circular 0001, s. 2014 and HDMF Circular No. 274, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: enjoin the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the results of the physical count with the accounting records, and henceforth submit the RPCPPE on time; and require the Municipal Accountant to update and reconcile the SL, GL and FS.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee to reconcile the result of count with the records of the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to provide to the Audit Team the noted documentary deficiencies in the procurement process and/or the justification thereof; remind the Municipal Accountant to ensure henceforth that the payment to suppliers are supported with complete supporting documents; and to explain and/or justify the use of the fund for financial assistance for Typhoon Ursula and other COVID-19 related PPAs.
observations.managementAction: To comply with the audit recommendation. The management on July 7, 2020 submitted to the audit team some of the noted lacking documents.
observations.reason: We did not receive the lacking documents requested from the LGU nor receive a justification letter for the deficiencies noted in the procurement process. The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to return the remaining amount of ₱10,000.00 to the DSWD then furnish the Audit Team with a copy of Official Receipt. Moreover, explain or justify the absence of photos of actual receipt of financial assistance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and MSWDO already submitted their justification in writing.
observations.reason: Verification of AOM replies and other pertinent documents disclosed that no justification letter was received at our end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to reconcile their records thoroughly and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies to establish the reliability of accounts in the FS; adopt strategies in intensifying the collection of real property tax such as information campaign to encourage taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount; and require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Provincial Assessor in conducting tax mapping to update the real property units and their corresponding assessed value.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant committed to reconcile their records thoroughly and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies. The Treasurer’s Office will exert effort to enhance the collection of RPT. Also, instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Provincial Assessor.
observations.reason: RPT and SET Receivables totaling ₱13,221,968.25 at the beginning of the year increased by ₱1,598,022.62 or 12% in CY 2021 amounting to ₱14,819,990.87 as of December 31, 2021. Percentage of collection during the year is only 4%. Management has yet to request the assistance of the Provincial Assessor’s Office in conducting tax mapping to update their records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the GAD Focal Person and the Municipal Budget Officer to review thoroughly the appropriations under the GAD Budget and ensure that the GPB is properly integrated in the Annual Budget, and henceforth ensure that the GPB shall conform with the actual amount provided for GAD in the approved budget.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person and the Municipal Budget Officer said that the GPB for CY 2021 was formulated a year prior but was found to be fully compliant, reviewed and endorsed by the local DILG. They admitted that their practice of attributing a certain portion to the GAD budget depending on the scores in the HGDG Checklist caused the non-conformity of the GPB with the Annual Budget, however, they already addressed this issue in the formulation of GPB for FY 2022.
observations.reason: The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the result of the count to the balances reflected in books and submit them to the Audit Team on time.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to reconcile the result of physical count to the balances reflected in books.
observations.reason: The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: effect/record in the books the necessary adjustments by debiting Cash-in-Bank and crediting Accounts Payable, as provided for under Section 59 of the NGAs – Volume I, to guarantee accuracy of the cash balance appearing in the LGU’s financial reports;
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Treasurer has traced back all the disbursements, which have outstanding checks, and the same has been determined for proper action to be taken. However, the Office of the Municipal Treasurer failed to transmit the list of payees with outstanding checks; therefore, the Office of the Municipal Accountant was not able to draw the necessary journal entries.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: file photocopies of the vouchers with the individual check for easy reference.
observations.managementAction: The management commits to file photocopies of the vouchers with the individual check for easy reference.
observations.reason: Due to voluminous transactions, the Office of the Municipal Accountant admitted that it had overlooked the task of filing the photocopies of vouchers with unclaimed check.

observations.recommendation: record the accumulated cost of on-going projects using the Construction in Progress account as provided in Annex B of COA Circular 2015-009; and
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: preparation and maintenance of Property Ledger Cards (PLC) for each PPE account to facilitate periodic reconciliation with the accounting records;
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Treasurer maintains Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE) in lieu of PLC.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: endeavor to recover records prior to CY 2012 to establish the correct value of the properties and its corresponding depreciation;
observations.managementAction:
Until now, records of prior years were not yet retrieved and even some newly acquired assets with available data were still not provided with depreciation.

Likewise, Management admitted difficulty in recovering previous records prior to CY 2012 due to the unavailability of subsidiary ledgers.

observations.recommendation: direct the Municipal Health Officer thru its concerned officers, to immediately remit the total collections from the Municipal Lying in Clinic to the Municipal Treasurer for proper issuance of official receipts and recording thereof; and
observations.managementAction: Remind the Municipal Health Officer to immediately remit the total collections from the Municipal Lying in Clinic to the Municipal Treasurer for proper issuance of official receipts and recording thereof.
observations.reason: Unjustified refusal of the Municipal Health Officer to remit the collections from the income of the Municipal Lying In Clinic.

observations.recommendation: direct the Municipal Treasurer to promptly submit records and reports of collections of Lying Clinic to the Municipal Accountant for proper recognition of income.
observations.managementAction: Remind the Municipal Treasurer to submit records and reports of collections of Lying Clinic to the Municipal Accountant for proper recognition of income.
observations.reason: Lack of coordination between the Office of the Municipal Health Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to enforce the sad order.

observations.recommendation: Expedite the creation of solid waste management board that will be in charge of the operation of the SWMP.
observations.managementAction: Revision on the SWMP is on-going.
observations.reason: SWMP is not yet approved.

observations.recommendation: effect the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with COA Circular 92-386, in order to generate additional funds, if found valuable and to avoid the further deterioration of the unserviceable properties due to exposure to natural elements and possible loss due to theft and loss of value.
observations.reason:
No report on the disposal of the ₱3.457 million unserviceable assets were submitted to the Audit Team.

Management averred that inadequacy of documents needed to conduct auction sale, and the lack of training relative to disposal of unserviceable properties hindered the proper disposal of unserviceable assets.

observations.recommendation: direct the responsible officials to conduct the physical count of all its Inventories on a regular basis;
observations.reason: No responsible official was designate to conduct the Semestral physical count of inventories, thus only the report for the inventory of accountable forms were submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: direct the responsible official to maintain inventory records of each type of inventory, aside from the subsidiary ledgers maintained in the Accounting Section; and
observations.reason: Responsible division/department did not maintain complete records.

observations.recommendation: withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees who fail to liquidate their cash advances based on the rules set forth in the above-mentioned provisions to compel them to liquidate their outstanding balances; and
observations.managementAction: CAs granted to three employees/officers (same three who were granted additional CA) totaling ₱37,200.00, which were outstanding as of 2020, remained unliquidated/unrefunded, as appropriate.
observations.reason: Partial compliance

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned personnel to conduct complete annual physical count of PPE, reconcile the Property and Accounting records after the physical count and submit to the Auditor the RPCPPE for verification in accordance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1. 

Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant must properly maintain Stock Cards, Property Cards, SLC and PPELC and conduct periodic reconciliation of property and accounting records to come up with accurate reports.
observations.managementAction: No action taken
observations.reason: The concerned department was not able to reconcile the PPE account because from the beginning, they were not maintain stock card, property card, SLC and PPELC.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Require the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the BRSs pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 and prepare subsidiary ledgers per bank account. A revised copy of the general journal must be immediately submitted to the Office of the Auditor for us to validate the adjustments made to the Cash in Bank account;

Coordinate with the concerned SAs of the balances of fund transfers for the dormant TF accounts. If found to be excess of costs for programs, project and activities (PPAs) already implemented, the same must be reverted back to the SA or the BTr; and

Consider maintaining only one bank account for GF which contains local income collections and IRA of the LGU for a stronger internal control and convenience in the disbursement process.
observations.managementAction:
No action taken

No action taken

The management commit to follow the audit recommendation
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant was unable to prepare the BRSs despite multiple commitments to prepare them.

The Municipal Accountant explained that the unreconciled balance of TF- Cash in Bank account is existing before he assumed into office. 

The previous administration preferred to separate the collection on water customers to properly monitor the agency collection on water system.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Require the Municipal Accountant to set up RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of the year to show actual collectibles of the Agency from its taxpayers. Correcting entry must be affected to include the said accounts in the FS. In addition, subsidiary ledgers must also be maintained for proper evaluation and monitoring of accounts; and 

Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of RPT and SET taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant committed to prepare the entries for RPT and SET upon the submission of list of taxpayers to their office.

Management will require the immediate submission Assessment Roll to the Treasurer’s Office as basis for the preparation of certified list of taxpayers and thereafter forward the latter to the Accounting Office.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant had extended this matter to concerned offices but they did not submit the certified list of taxpayers.

The Municipal Assessor was not able to provide the Treasurer’s Office the Assessment roll.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the Municipal Accountant to trace all available records and initiate inquiries with the former Municipal Accountant of the information of the actual dormant accounts and prepare quarterly aging thereof; and 

Facilitate the request for write-off of the dormant accounts so that the same will be derecognized in the FS observing the procedures and requirements set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: Management is looking for alternative ways to comply with the requirements for the request of write-off as set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.reason: As per the previous Accountant, this account is dormant for several years. Further, because of that length of time it has been outstanding, the Accounting Office was not able to provide any information about the details of the amounts. Consequently, the office is considering to write-off this account this year.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant conduct a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC and the TWG: 

To include in their post-qualification a comparative evaluation showing the number of projects with the same equipment and key personnel pledged and assigned by the same lowest calculated bidder for different projects with simultaneous or overlapping implementation period.




To formulate policies on the sufficiency and availability of equipment pledged by the contractors with regard to projects simultaneously awarded to the same contractors to avoid delay in project implementation.




To extend the comparison with other government agencies before declaring the bids as responsive.




To set limits for the number of contracts to be handled by key personnel, in order to ensure reasonableness of performance without sacrificing quality and timeliness of project completion.
observations.managementAction:
Page 81 of 2019 AAR | The BAC ensures that each winning bidder are technically capable in completing project such that scrutiny is made as regards to the submitted list of equipment and personnel. Post-qualification through site visitation was likewise conducted.





No written policy established but management assured that the contractors had sufficient personnel and equipment.




Coordination with other agencies was conducted by the BAC.




The key personnel of contractors remain the same for every contract. Nonetheless, the management informed that increasing number of participants in the bidding was noted preventing at some instance, implementation of not more than three simultaneous projects.
observations.reason:
Although the post-qualification evaluation report of the BAC TWG showed enumeration of list of number of personnel and equipment, such listing was not made to evaluate whether or not there were insufficiency of manpower and equipment for implementation of simultaneous projects. The evaluation was only confined with the compliance of number of equipment and personnel. In fact, one contractor was awarded four projects with the same start date of July 14, 2021 and none was reported on how its equipment and personnel managed the simultaneous implementation of project.

Not Implemented

The formulation of internal policies of BAC TWG is necessary to provide basis for the BAC to consider contractors who have been declared as lowest bidder but were awarded with more than one projects. The intention is not to outrightly disregard contractors but to establish that they are capable augmenting their personnel and equipment despite having multiple and simultaneous projects.

Not Implemented.

The claim cannot be established as there were still on-going projects of bidders which were not declared in the Statement of on-going projects. The City must not only be confined in communicating with the DPWH but also with other LGUs or NGAs.

Not Implemented

Same key personnel were declared in the list submitted by the contractor. Although the number of simultaneous projects were reduced in some instances, this did not solve the requirement that “the project engineer must at all times be present in all stages of the construction”.
