Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds		4,931,882.69
Trust Fund:
Trust Liability-DRRMF				4,931,882.69
Due to Other Funds					4,931,882.69
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
prepare the following journal entries to adjust the journals and ledgers in the General Fund:
Transfers of Unspent Current
Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds	894,269.09
Prior Period Adjustment				894,269.09
refer to the Obligation Requests and coordinate closely with the City Budget Officer to ensure proper fund sourcing and recording of transactions using the correct accounts in the financial statements

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant:
to adjust the books with the following entry:
Welfare goods for distribution	 	3,654,800.00
Welfare goods expense			 	3,654,800.00
to record all purchases of inventory regardless of whether or not  they are consumed or distributed during the year as inventory following the Perpetual Inventory Method, and adjust the related inventory accounts using the appropriate expenditure accounts to reflect the balance of the ending inventory accordingly in the financial statements

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to submit the List of Unreleased Checks to the City Accountant specially at year-end. Further, for the City Accountant to record the Accounts Payable and its related asset and expense accounts totaling ₱988,640.87 for the unreleased checks as at year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:
submit proof of remittance of the collection from the 4,040 pieces of BIR Form No. 006 as presented in the Annex C and thereafter, update the RAAF accordingly and submit the same to the audit team.
require the deputized collectors located in upland barangays to remit their collections weekly and for other barangays on a daily basis.
conduct cash examination and turn-over of cash accountabilities of resigned deputized collectors and furnish the audit team copies of turn-over documents.
review the daily or monthly RAAF of the LGU and conduct physical count of Accountable Forms to establish existence and completeness of ending balances reported in the RAAF;

We recommended management to refrain from entering into contracts with suppliers whose line of business/industry is not within the scope of the subject transaction.
We also recommended the City Mayor to direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the appropriate BIR Certificate of Registration allowing the suppliers to engage in the industries where the sale of the goods it supplied is covered, and to thereafter, ensure that all emergency procurements are awarded to a technically, legally, and financially capable supplier, contractor, distributor, or consultant in compliance with the aforementioned provisions.  In case of huge quantities required by emergencies, procure from multiple suppliers who are legally, technically and financially capable in the absence of a single supplier with such capabilities instead of transacting with suppliers whose line of business is doubtful.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LDRRMC in coordination with the CSWDO, GSO and other offices necessary to develop a strategic plan involving focal persons per barangay and deployment of city personnel in case of the aforementioned wide-scale emergencies.

We therefore recommended the City Mayor to:
Request the Sangguniang Panlungsod to enact a resolution changing the beneficiaries of the goods from COVID-19 beneficiaries to its intended recipients; and
Direct the CSWD and the LDRRMC to develop a strategic plan on determining the quantity of goods to be procured for future emergency responses to avoid potential spoilage or wastage of procured goods.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
request authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod to execute the donation of the tablets in writing with a Deed of Donation pursuant to Article 748 of the New Civil Code of the Philippines and execute the donation and thereafter direct the Schools Division Superintendent to submit proof of acceptance of the recipient agency including the accompanying Journal Entry Vouchers showing recording of the said donations upon execution of the deed;
direct the City Accountant to ensure that donations are duly supported with complete documentary requirements such as proof of donations and acceptance pursuant to Article 748 of the NCC in the ensuing years; and
direct the City Budget Officer and City Accountant to coordinate so as to properly classify procurements as either inventory or PPE taking into consideration the threshold set by the latest COA Circular in PPE capitalization and to ensure consistent classification under both the Budget and Accounting offices.

We recommended that that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Office to limit funds to be classified as Continuing Appropriations to unobligated and unspent Capital Outlay based on section 322 of Republic Act 7160 and          stop authorizing MOOE transactions which are appropriated from the previous year.
We also recommended that the City Mayor, as the CDRRMC overall head, to optimize the use of the Special Trust Fund – DRRM by reviewing and updating their LDRRMFIP and preparing supplemental and revised financial investment program. Thereafter, request its approval from the Sangguniang Panlungsod through a resolution

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
The City DRRM Officer to provide specific titles based on the intended purpose and manner of implementation of the PPAs in the LDRRMFIP so as to ensure alignment of expenses to the respective PPAs; and
The City Budget Officer to:
Provide separate fund for the maintenance of the DRRM Office for its general and administrative expenses; and
Certify purchase requests which are in line with approved program/project/activities

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
The OIC-City Budget Officer to initiate the request from the Sangguniang Panlungsod together with concerned department heads a resolution enacted in the same manner prescribed by ordinances, for appropriations that are described in generic terms.
The City Planning Development Coordinator to ensure that PPAs specifically identified in the LEP or those generally-described appropriations be rectified through resolutions of the Sanggunian and included in the Annual or Supplemental Investment Program, as the case may be.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Officer to:
establish a Committee to monitor benefit packages which could be availed of by the City for additional funding for its COVID-19 or other health-related issues in the future to ensure prompt response in availing of the package/s by proper planning and coordination with the City Health Officer and concerned departments / personnel.
comply with the DOH certification requirements to fast track the ongoing applications for the CIUs for Philhealth accreditation for the remaining facilities to be able to claim the CCIBP benefit for its patients;
Moreover, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the heads of other departments such as the City Engineer to coordinate with the CHO and assist the latter in complying with the requirements for DOH Certification and Philhealth accreditation

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to stop the implementation of collecting in advance the business taxes from contractors, instead collect business taxes on or before January 20 of the subsequent year.  For outside contractors, demand payment through official letters in the subsequent year prior to deadline of payment.
Also, instruct the City Accountant to adjust the affected accounts in the financial statements with the following correcting entry:
Business Tax				159,743.72
Prior Year Adjustment		368,659.23
Other Deferred Credits			528,402.95

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the
City Accountant to:
immediately transfer accumulated funds of the General Fund from the Special Education and Trust Fund amounting ₱16,606,654.38 so that the said funds will be optimized to support General Fund programs, projects, and activities.
thereafter, make a regular transfer of funds for accumulated inter-fund receivables.
ensure that DVs are appropriately assigned with DV Number and properly filled-up according to the funds to be used for disbursement.
City Accountant to observe due diligence in the preparation of checks to ensure that they be drawn from the appropriate fund

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
City Planning Development Coordinator to:
ensure that PPAs are identified judiciously to avoid series of reprogramming and discontinuance of PPAs and refrain from adopting the “as the need arises” policy which indicates inadequate planning.
prepare a revised/updated PPMP/AIP, taking into consideration the duration and timing of the implementation of the newly identified PPAs, in cases where reprogramming is warranted; and
Local Finance Committee to ensure that reprogramming and discontinuance be aligned within the meaning of savings to be reviewed by the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:
Prepare and send demand letters to the fisherfolk-borrowers and emphasize the terms and conditions in the MOA particularly the provisions for penalty and forfeiture of their rights over the property for non-payment; and
Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to:
immediately conduct examination of books of businesses registered in the city to determine the appropriate amount of taxes due from them;
require payment from suppliers with unpaid taxes of at least ₱2,470,946.61 together with the associated penalties and interests; and
conduct regular annual examination of books of accounts and pertinent records of businessmen as required by Section 171 of RA No. 7160 not only to ensure appropriate assessment and collection but also to reduce the risk of non-collection of business taxes.

1.13. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Office direct the City
Accountant to submit the liquidation reports and other requisite documents of the
above-stated cash advances and refrain from processing payments without all of the
necessary documentation. Henceforth, the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Council submit a revised AIP and LDRRMP to be approved by the
Sangguniang Panlungsod and the LCE for all new projects to be funded from the
DRRM unexpended balances and include, under a separate caption, the list of
projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

2.10. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive recommend the enactment
of a Revenue Measure on the imposition of RT-PCR test fees and include the
additional revenue thereof in the annual budget/supplemental budget for proper
appropriation.
2.11. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City
Accounting Office to effect the necessary corrections in the books of accounts to
accurately reflect the true nature of the transactions to avoid misclassification in
reporting in the financial statements.

3.11. We recommended that the LCE require the Inventory Team to (a) conduct a
complete annual physical inventory of its PPE and prepare the Report on the
Physical Count of PPE; (b) perform periodic reconciliation of the PPE balances
between CAO and CGSO records based on the results of the physical inventory and
in accordance with the procedural guidelines in COA Circular 2020-006, to reflect
the correct balance of the PPE in the balance sheet.
3.12. We likewise reiterated our recommendations that the LCE direct the City
Accountant to (a) provide depreciation for all depreciable PPE accounts; (b) review
and ensure that provision for depreciation shall not exceed the residual value of
PPE items; (c) accomplish accurately the supporting schedules for all PPE
accounts; (d) analyze and reconcile the variance noted on the balances of PPE
accounts; (e) ensure proper classification of PPE items in the books; (f) draw the
necessary adjustments in the books to correct all erroneous recording/classification
noted and journal entries for the disposed unserviceable properties; and (g) adopt all the
principles prescribed in IPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the City’s PPE accounts
for more reliable information and fair presentation of the asset accounts in the
financial statements at year-end.

4.15. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City Accountant to (i)
consistently record the purchased inventories to their respective asset accounts; and
(ii) ensure that the de-recognition of inventories is recorded only when these are
issued to end-users/beneficiaries; (b) City General Service (CGS) Officer to ensure
proper safekeeping of supplies and materials inventory to minimize the risk of
possible loss or wastage of government resources; and (c) CGS Officer and City
Accountant to ensure coordination between their offices for the timely recording of
receipts and issuances of inventories.

5.10. We recommended that the (a) Accounting Office (i) set up the payable
account based on the receipt of goods or services rendered in conformity with the
Local Government Chart of Accounts; (ii) maintain subsidiary ledgers for each
creditor pursuant to Section 111 of P.D. 1445; and (iii) improve the monitoring of
accounts to avoid delay and frequent corrections, and uncertainty on the accuracy
of balances; and (b) department heads concerned systematically and promptly
document the transactions or services rendered to their respective Offices so as not
to further delay the settlement of obligations.

6.5. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) strictly set a deadline to
all claimants for the submission of documents to the City Accountant for all
unsettled/unpaid lawful expenditures and obligations for recording in the books and
ensure that the set date will give ample time to the City Accountant for the
preparation of financial statements so as to comply with the submission thereof to
this Commission within the prescribed period; and (b) require the City Planning
and Development Officer to consider proper timing on the release of funds to
barangays for the implementation of projects and set the deadline for the
submission of their liquidation reports.

7.10. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that all projects to
be implemented by the City are within the approved AIP. Henceforth, conduct an
in-depth planning in the prioritization of projects.

8.18. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Office direct the
(a) City Engineering Office to properly plan and supervise the implementation of
infrastructure projects to avoid delay, hasten project implementation and attain
early the desired objectives of the projects; and (b) City Planning and Development
Office to (i) evaluate the technical viability of proposed development projects before
these are being requested for appropriation to avoid re-alignments; and (ii) monitor
the proper and timely implementation of the projects at the barangay level.

9.18. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Officer
instruct the LDRRM Council to (a) provide an appropriation corresponding to each
specific activity in the LDRRMFIP for ease in the monitoring of budget utilization;
and (b) strictly follow the accounting and reporting guidelines in the receipt and
utilization of cash and in-kind donations for DRRM programs.
9.19. We also recommended that the amount inappropriately charged against the
30% QRF be restored to the LDRMMF through supplemental budget, if the
corresponding funds are available. Henceforth, ensure that the LDRMMF be used
solely to support disaster risk management activities.

10.9. We recommended that the LSB, together with the City Engineering Office,
properly plan the implementation of infrastructure projects to avoid delay for the
early attainment of the desired objectives of the projects.

11.17. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct (a) the City
Accountant to submit the requisite documents; (b) henceforth, the concerned City
Engineering personnel to be more diligent in the computation of the estimated
project cost for future infrastructure projects; and (c) the City Engineer and the
project consultant to rigidly monitor the progress of works of the project and
compel the contractor to expedite the completion of works as agreed upon. For
future projects undertaking, we recommend an in-depth planning process to come
up with an attainable completion period and monitor the progress of works for the
timely enjoyment of the project benefits by the intended beneficiaries.

12.5. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) City
Engineering Office personnel concerned to submit documents in support of the
quantity and cost of filling materials spent for the project; and (b) (i) City
Accountant to submit the documents as mentioned under Item 1.3, and (ii) BAC to
submit an explanation on not requiring the submission thereof by the Supplier.

13.16. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the CENRO to (a)
review and update the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan to effectively address
the current challenges in the solid waste management and include programs to
mitigate the adverse impacts on global environment from the increase of waste
produced in response to the COVID-19 crisis; and (b) ensure the implementation of
feasible projects/programs/activities committed in the City’s 10-year solid waste
management plan and regularly assess the programs to determine the effects on
waste reduction.

14.7. We recommended that the City Budget Officer explain in writing the
reversions of appropriations which were not considered as savings. Henceforth,
strictly comply with the procedural requirements of AO 47, s. 1993 on changes in
the annual budget.

15.14. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) reconstitute the
composition of the existing GFPS Execom and TWG in consonance with Section
4.1.A of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to further strengthen the
GFPS and accelerate gender mainstreaming in the City; and (b) instruct the GFPS
to set up and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would ensure the
generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-aggregated data or GAD
database to serve as basis in the performance-based and gender-responsive planning
and budgeting.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
observations.managementAction: The accounting office issued demand letters to the accountable officers and employees with outstanding cash advances aged more than 30 days to over five years and copy furnish the Audit Team; No action taken
observations.reason: The LGU has yet to inform the Audit Team of the delinquent accountable officers who refused or failed to liquidate their cash advances. The Accounting office issued demand letters to the accountable officers. The Accounting office had already drawn an entry in the 1st quarter of CY 2021 to correct the misstated account. Liquidation reports were already prepared and attached every time liquidation is made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: direct the BAC Chairperson to submit immediately all planning and bidding documents that form part of the contract of the aforementioned projects; and direct the Municipal Accountant to consider the PAJE in order to correct the account balances and present fairly the affected accounts in the FS; and ensure henceforth that progress payments are classified under the Construction in Progress account.
observations.managementAction: The planning and bidding documents for both projects were already submitted to the Audit Team on July 28, 2021. Adjusting entry was made in the General Journal for the month of January 2021 to correct the misstatement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: require the Municipal Engineer to estimate the useful life of the infrastructure assets and the Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for depreciation thereof; enjoin the Inventory Committee to reconcile the result of physical count with the records of the Accounting Office; require the Municipal Accountant to update and reconcile the SL, GL and FS balances.
observations.managementAction: Allowance for depreciation was recognized during the year. The Inventory Committee to reconcile the result of count with the records of the Accounting Office. The Accounting Office will update the SL and GL upon the conduct of physical count.
observations.reason: Out of the eight PPE accounts, only four were provided with allowance for depreciation expense as of December 31, 2021. The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: record under the Due to NGAs account the receipt, liquidation and refund of fund transfers from source agency with attached specific purpose or stipulation; consider the PAJE in correcting the noted misstatements and make sure that the receipt of grant without specific purpose are recorded as income, not a liability, all in compliance with IPSAS 1 - Presentation of FS and the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Department has already recorded the receipts, liquidations, and refund of fund transfers from source agency with attached specific purpose. The adjusting entry was made in the General Journal for the month of June 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Accountant to record completely and timely all loan proceeds in the books of accounts and consider the PAJE in order to correct the account balances and present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Loans Payable account was already adjusted to reflect its correct balance per General Journal for the month of April 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records and present fairly the Cash in Bank account in the FS; require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the BRS for CYs 2019 and 2020 and submit the same to the Audit Team together with the copies of bank statements and supporting schedules; require the Municipal Accountant to submit Subsidiary Ledgers; and follow up the claim of the municipal government before the PDIC for the un-recovered savings account.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting and Treasurer’s to reconcile their respective records. The Accounting Office has prepared and submitted the BRS for the period July 2019 to August 2021 on June 14, 2022. Management said that a follow up letter was sent to the PDIC during the time of former Municipal Treasurer, Ms. Priscilla R. Rey. The Accounting Office has prepared and submitted the BRS for the period July 2019 to August 2021 on June 14, 2022.
observations.reason: The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report. The BRS for the period September to December 2021 were not yet submitted to the Audit Team. We found no more follow-ups relative to their claim in PDIC. The observation was reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all supplies and materials every semester and prepare and submit the RPCI to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year. Moreover, enjoin the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to reconcile their records regularly and prepare supply ledger cards and stock cards, respectively.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee conducted the physical count on inventories and submitted the RPCI to Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: the Municipal Engineer to submit immediately the lacking planning documents; the BAC to (i) submit all lacking bidding documents to the Audit Team; (ii) henceforth adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in all stages of procurement process; and (iii) ensure that all bid documents are complete, evaluated, and submitted on time; and direct the Municipal Accountant to submit a copy of CAF and SB Resolution for the two projects and ensure the completeness and veracity of supporting documents attached to the vouchers in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4.6 of P.D. No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Lacking documents were already submitted to the Audit Team. Lacking documents were already submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Most of the noted lacking documents were complied with except Plans/Drawings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended/unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE to special trust fund; disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the unexpended/unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE including those pertaining to prior years; ensure that all claims charged against the LDRRMF are completely supported with proper documents; and require the Municipal Engineer that all job order employees assigned in the LDRRM Office shall be supported with accomplishment reports.
observations.managementAction: The unexpended QRF and MF-MOOE was already transferred to the special trust fund in the 2nd quarter of CY 2021. Accomplishment reports of job order employees under the MDRMO were already attached to the payroll starting January 2021. The Accounting Office to disclose the details of LDRRMF on the Notes to FS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation of the unimplemented PPAs in order to maximize the utilization of the Development Fund and disclose the implemented PPAs in the quarterly Report of Government PPAs to be submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer will fast track the implementation of uncompleted projects. The Municipal Engineer to disclose the projects in the Quarterly Report of Government PPAs. The Municipal Engineer will disclose the projects in the Quarterly Report of Government PPAs.
observations.reason: Most of the projects were already completed in CY 2021 except for the project Purchase of Farm to Market Road (FMR) Lot – San Juan which remains unimplemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to record under the Due to NGAs account the receipt, liquidation and refund of funds received from NGAs; and make sure that the SLs are detailed and complete and identify under which account the amount of P22,018.88 is appropriate to be recorded.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to use the Due to NGAs account for similar transactions in succeeding years. Management committed to identify the details of the accounts composing the P22,018.88 and promised to prepare an adjustment or remittance, as the case may be. Likewise, they committed to update the SL.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to record completely and timely all loan proceeds in the books of accounts and consider the PAJE in order to correct the account balances and present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Department has recorded the receipts, liquidations and refund of fund transfers from source agency with attached specific purpose. The adjusting entry was made in the General Journal for the month of June 2021.
observations.reason: The items which composed the account balance of ₱22,018.88 were still noted in the SL as at year-end and the management has yet to take an action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the depository banks to furnish the municipality of bank statements regularly and promptly. Make sure that it is done in writing, duly received by the bank and filed for documentation. Also, instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort in accomplishing BRS and make sure that all book reconciling items are traced and reflected in the books.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has prepared and submitted the BRS for the period July 2019 to August 2021 on June 14, 2022. The Treasurer’s office had updated already the cashbook for each account.
observations.reason: The BRS for the period September to December 2021 were not yet submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies in intensifying the collection of RPT and ensure the proper recording of the receivables and delimitation between Deferred and Recognized incomes; require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies in the FS.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant committed to reconcile their records and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies. Management said that they have conducted information campaign and sent demand letters to taxpayers.
observations.reason: Management has yet to request the assistance of the Provincial Assessor in conducting tax mapping to update their records. The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to determine the useful life of the infrastructure assets and the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation for the said accounts; and require the Municipal Accountant to update and reconcile the subsidiary ledgers with the GL and FS balances.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to estimate the useful life of the infrastructure assets and provide depreciation for the succeeding years. Management committed to update their SL and reconcile it with the balances in the GL.
observations.reason: Out of the eight PPE accounts, only four were provided with allowance for depreciation expense as of December 31, 2021. The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit as discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare detailed subsidiary ledgers (SL) for each type of tax and make sure that the withholdings and remittances are monitored and updated in the general ledgers (GL) and reflect promptly in the SL and GL. Also, make sure that the internal arrangement for the remittance of taxes from the three funds is reflected promptly in the SL and GL.
observations.managementAction: Management said that they constantly monitoring the replenishment of the general fund each time remittance of tax was made. Also, management committed to prepare detailed SL for each type of tax.
observations.reason: Management has yet to prepare a detailed SL for each type of tax.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts, determine the cause of the abnormal balances, initiate necessary corrections or adjustments, and come up with accounts in their normal balances as fairly presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office drawn adjusting entries to correct the Advances to Contractors and Due to BIR accounts. Moreover, they committed to make adjustments on the three remaining accounts.
observations.reason: We still noted abnormal balances for Cash in Bank – Local Currency Deposit (LCD), Advances for Payroll, and Other Payables accounts in CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the officers and employees to liquidate on time.
observations.managementAction: Management sent demand letters to officers/employees with unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: The same observation was noted in CY 2021 audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to submit immediately the lacking documents enumerated in Annex E; and the BAC and contractor to use the prescribed bid form and contract agreement in the 5th edition of the PBD; and direct the Municipal Accountant that henceforth, every voucher must have a complete supporting documents prior to processing of claims.
observations.managementAction: The management on July 7, 2020 submitted to the audit team some of the noted lacking documents. The Accounting Office committed that every voucher must have a complete supporting documents prior to approval of payment.
observations.reason: Still, test analysis, warranty security, and clearance from Provincial Treasurer were not submitted to the audit team. Moreover, lacking documents for 1st, 2nd and final payments of project Improvement and Expansion of Level III Water Supply System-Municipal Counterpart with contract cost of ₱1,999,950.17 were likewise not complied with.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the amount and details of LDRRMF in compliance with Item 5.1.16 and Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the amount and details of LDRRMF in compliance with Item 5.1.16 and Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.reason: The amount of unexpended/unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE disclosed in the Notes to FS was shown only as a lump-sum amount. We reiterated the audit findings and recommendations in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MPDC to justify/explain the inclusion of projects that do not squarely fit to the menu provided by Item 3 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and the non-implementation of the development projects; instruct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation of development projects and disclose them in the quarterly Report of Government PPAs to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer will justify/explain the inclusion of projects that do not squarely fit to the menu and the non-implementation of the development projects. Management said that they have reviewed the PPAs totaling P3,200,000.00 and found out that the project Improvement of Sports Facility was realigned in year 2020. Management said that the Purchase of Sea Ambulance, Improvement of Cantingas River Resort, and Development of Cresta de Gallo were already implemented in CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Treasurer to submit all monthly and quarterly financial and accounting reports and their corresponding statements within the prescribed period in order for the Audit Team to undertake timely review and post-audit of the transactions and accounts and at the same time to inform management of any defects or deficiencies.
observations.managementAction: Management said that they are trying to prepare the monthly and quarterly reports within the prescribed period and submit the same to the audit team for timely review and post-audit.
observations.reason: Still, we noted that reports were not submitted within the prescribed period, incurring one to three months of delay.

observations.recommendation: revisit the status of outstanding checks of the LGU to determine whether they are still in the custody of the cashier or in the hands of the payees;
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Treasurer has traced back all the disbursements, which have outstanding checks, and the same has been determined for proper action to be taken. However, the Office of the Municipal Treasurer failed to transmit the list of payees with outstanding checks; therefore, the Office of the Municipal Accountant was not able to draw the necessary journal entries.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: determine, for those checks already issued, whether they are subject for replacement or not;
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Treasurer has traced back all the disbursements, which have outstanding checks, and the same has been determined for proper action to be taken. However, the Office of the Municipal Treasurer failed to transmit the list of payees with outstanding checks; therefore, the Office of the Municipal Accountant was not able to draw the necessary journal entries.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: ensure that checks, when not claimed, remain in the custody of the Municipal Treasurer and be attached to the original voucher, to facilitate determination of unclaimed vouchers at year-end; and
observations.managementAction: The unclaimed checks are currently in the custody of the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.managementAction: The beginning balance of RPT Receivables for Gen. Fund and SEF was already taken up in the books as evidenced by JEV No. 02-068 dated February 28, 2021 and JEV No. 01-005 dated January 31, 2021 respectively.

observations.recommendation: reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts and immediately take up the adjustments in the books of accounts; and
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Accountant averred that it conducts regular monthly reconciliation of the said accounts by referring to its respective subsidiary ledgers; however, entries to correct the identified variances and bring the accounts in agreement were not taken up.

observations.recommendation: conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure that discrepancies are immediately identified, reconciled and adjusted.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Accountant averred that it conducts regular monthly reconciliation of the said accounts by referring to its respective subsidiary ledgers; however, entries to correct the identified variances and bring the accounts in agreement were not taken up.
observations.reason: Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
designate a staff (pending designation of a GSO) from each concerned department to:

maintain stock cards to regularly monitor the delivery and issuance of inventories;

consolidate RIS and prepare a monthly SSMI to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books; and

conduct physical count of inventory items and prepare the RPCI, every six months, to be submitted to the Accounting Office for reconciliation of records and the Audit Team.

observations.reason: Post-audit of CY 2021 transactions disclosed that some distributed relief goods were wanting of appropriate distribution lists, while those with attached distribution lists or acknowledgement receipts lacked sufficient information or details to validate the propriety of distribution, such as the quantity of items distributed per person/household, and contact number of beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: take up the necessary journal entries to recognize the public infrastructure assets in the books of the LGU totaling ₱48,441,366.92, as well as the related accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004, to present fairly the value of the assets in the financial statements.

The beginning balance of RPT Receivables for Gen. Fund and SEF was already taken up in the books as evidenced by JEV No. 02-068 dated February 28, 2021 and JEV No. 01-005 dated January 31, 2021 respectively.

observations.recommendation: stop the granting of additional cash advances to employees with previous unliquidated cash advances;

observations.recommendation: It is recommended that immediate and appropriate action be taken by management to settle these audit suspensions and disallowances in accordance with COA Rules and Regulations in the Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).
observations.managementAction:
Partial collection from persons liable had been made by the Municipal Accountant. However, letter to request for the installment payment of the disallowance was not yet submitted.

Management averred that some persons liable were uncooperative and some were cannot be located.
observations.reason: Partial compliance

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately derecognize the abovementioned inventories and effect necessary adjustments in the government equity account; and

Require its officials to strictly observe the prescribed accounting guidelines and control procedures on the receipt, issuance and storage/custodianship of supplies and materials. A Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with copies of Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) must be prepared and submitted to the Municipal Accountant as basis for the recording of appropriate expenditure accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant close the CIP accounts to its appropriate asset accounts. Detailed review must be conducted of the actual completion of corresponding assets for the computation of the depreciation expense. Upon verification, adjustments of the unrecognized depreciation expenses in the prior years must be made in the government equity accounts by recording the same under prior period adjustment account.
observations.managementAction: Management will coordinate this matter to the MPDO to review the status of completion of the projects in order to effect adjusting entries.
observations.reason: During the year, the Accounting Office was not able to conduct detailed review of this account. Moreover, the balance reflected in the financial statement is a composition of the projects still in progress.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Facilitate immediate submission of the unsubmitted accounts to avoid audit suspension thereof; 

Require the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Office of the Auditor the paid DVs on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month; and

Require the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Accounting Office all the DVs, Official Receipts including the corresponding reports of each month, on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Management is still unable to follow the reglementary period of the submission of accounts due to the workload of concerned departments.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Immediately stop the practice of granting CAs to its employees without legally authorized specific purpose, otherwise, the transactions will be disallowed in audit. Additional CAs must not be granted to officials with unliquidated balances. Moreover, elected officials must not be granted CAs except for their official travelling expenses only;


Direct its officers and employees with unliquidated CAs to immediately submit their liquidation papers, otherwise, require the immediate withholding of its salary in case of non-compliance; and


Require the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of outstanding CAs and reconcile variance between the records in the books and that of the CA report.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with all audit recommendations.
observations.reason:
Despite repeated observations, Management is yet to stop the practice of granting CAs to employees and elected officials without legally authorized specific purpose.

The Municipal Accountant is yet to demand the submission of liquidation papers to all officers with outstanding CAs.

Due to the complexity of this account, the Municipal Accountant was not able monitor variance in books and CA report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require all drivers and concerned officials to completely fill out the Driver’s Trip Ticket in accordance with existing regulation for transparency and accountability over the use of such government motor vehicles;

Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month; and

Provide a complete list of serviceable municipal vehicles to ascertain the reliability of fuel consumption and for validation purposes.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to review the transactions in order to prepare the Report on Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report. Moreover, they promised to conduct inventory of serviceable vehicles.
observations.reason:
Management was not able to prepare the Report on Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report.

Management was not able to coordinate to concerned office to conduct inventory of serviceable municipal vehicles.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the LSB to adhere to the required activities and timelines in the planning and budgeting of SEF. SEF budget must be prepared and must conform to the schedule of activities cited in Section 5.3 of DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular No. 2017-01. Moreover, we reminded Management that disbursement for SEF must not exceed shares from SET which is 50 percent of its actual collections.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Management was unable to fully adhere to the required activities and timelines in the planning and budgeting of SEF due to the recent reshuffling of the assignments of the PSDS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant abide by the rules and regulations of COA Circular No. 2012-002 for the recording of future cash donations. Moreover, it is recommended that the concerned official promptly monitor the transactions in the TF books most especially fund transfers and diligently apply relevant accounting treatment.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Error pertinent to the receipt and utilization of the fund was due to confusion of the accounting treatment to be applied thereto.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management: 

Require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP annually. It must present the 30 percent allocation for QRF in lump-sum and the allocation for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness with details as to projects and activities to be funded. The same must also include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;

Ensure that updated or supplemental LDRRMFIP be prepared for any future changes or modification in the utilization of LDRRMF; and

Require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMF utilization report in accordance with the required format cited in COA Circular No. 2012-002 and promptly submit the same to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of the month for monitoring and evaluation of the fund.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to comply with audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Prompt submission of the LDRRMF Utilization Report was not given priority due to workload of the MDRRMO personnel.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Immediately send justification letter explaining the reason/s of realigning 20 percent DF to unnecessary charges made thereto which are not beneficial during COVID-19 emergency. We reminded them that the purpose of fund realignment was to enable the LGU to undertake critical, urgent and appropriate disaster response aid and measures to curtail and eliminate the threat of COVID-19;

Ensure that the 20 percent DF is optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU; and

The Sanggunian enact/pass supplemental appropriation ordinance for future budget realignment clearly indicating the sources of funds available for appropriation, certified under oath by the local treasurer and local accountant and attested by the local chief executive, and the various items of appropriations affected and the reasons for the change.
observations.managementAction:
Management committed to comply with audit recommendations.

We commit to comply with the audit recommendation
observations.reason: Management is yet to send the justification letter on the realigning of 20 per cent DF to unnecessary charges made thereto which are not beneficial during COVID-19 emergency.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that: 

The Municipal Accountant immediately effect correcting entries to derecognize the said inventory accounts in the financial statements; and

Management direct its officials to strictly observe the prescribed accounting guidelines and control procedures on the receipt, issuance and storage/custodianship of supplies and materials.

We recommended that:

Management direct the concerned officials to stop the practice of payment execution of various operating expenses which are not allowed by law through granting of cash advance and liquidation thereafter;

Management direct its officials to stop granting additional cash advances if the previous ones remained unsettled or unliquidated. Demand letters must be sent to the erring employees for their unliquidated balances. In case of non-compliance, Management is encouraged to resort to  the rules cited in Section 5 of COA Circular 97-002, which provides that failure to liquidate the cash advance within the prescribed period constitutes a valid cause for the withholding of salary; and

The Municipal Accountant immediately come up with a reconciliation of balances that would resolve the variance between the total cash advance recorded in the books and in the report of status of unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2019.
observations.managementAction:
A liquidating officer was assigned to monitor balances for unliquidated CAs


No action taken
observations.reason:
Inability to monitor balances for Cash Advances



Lack of monitoring and supervision of the concerned official

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Direct its officials and employees concerned to strictly comply with the regulation on the effective and efficient rendition of accounts and financial reports for the timely verification of accounts and conduct of post-audit activities; and


Devote some time to address the issue on the non-submission of accounts since the same has been a repeated audit finding for several years now.
observations.managementAction: Accounts in the treasury department were immediately forwarded to the Accounting Office for the required recording and forwarding to COA.
observations.reason: Delay was still incurred in the submission of the accounts due to limitations imposed by the COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately deposit all undeposited collections as of year-end after reconciling records with the Municipal Accountant. The practice of depositing collections only after huge accumulations must be stopped. Also, we recommended to verify and effect adjustments, if necessary, to the balance of the Cash-Local Treasury account under trust fund.
observations.managementAction: Prompt deposit of collections remitted to the Liquidating Officer.
observations.reason: Not all balance in the cash-local treasury account was deposited. The collecting clerks was not able to remit on time due to alternative work arrangements. Moreover, balance of the cash-local treasury account in the trust fund was not verified and adjusted due to lack of monitoring and supervision of the assigned personnel.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately release the unremitted shares of the provincial government, barangay governments and the provincial school board out of it collections for real property and special education taxes;

The Municipal Treasurer conduct a meeting with all Barangays to discuss the policy in releasing their respective shares in the RPT; and

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer regularly coordinate with the provincial government and provincial school board with respect to the release of the said shares to avoid accumulation of the same.
observations.managementAction: Balances for the prior years were already remitted.
observations.reason: Some balances were not remitted in full

observations.managementAction:
Accumulated reports for CY 2020 were already submitted to COA

No action taken
observations.reason:
Prompt submission was not made due to restrictions imposed by covid-19 pandemic.

The assigned personnel is not aware of the prescribed format of the LDRRMFIP

observations.managementAction:
The GPB was submitted to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO).

No action taken
observations.reason:
The GPB was not submitted to DILG Regional Office V because the assigned personnel were not aware of the needed submission thereto.

Lack of coordination between the LGU and the concerned barangays

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Stop granting Cash Advance for disbursements not allowed by law. Payments must be made directly to payee/supplier through the issuance of check except those expressly allowed under COA Circular 97-002. Cash Advance must only be given or granted to authorized officials as cited also in COA Circular 97-002;

Must not allow the grant of additional cash advance if the previous ones remained unliquidated or unsettled; and

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to settle/liquidate her accountabilities for unliquidated CAs as at December 31, 2018.

We recommended that the Management:

Direct its Officers to immediately forward or submit to the Office of the Auditor the disbursement vouchers totaling ₱44.90 million, without need of further demand; and

Exert efforts in identifying causes of the delay or non-submission of Disbursement Vouchers and bring solution to this dilemma.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to identify and resolve the causes of delay of the submission of Financial Reports and FS.
observations.reason: Management was not able to strictly observe the timeline for submission and to identify the causes of delay.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Must have the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) be reviewed and approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC). Review by the NSWMC of the said plan will assess if it addresses the necessary issues concerning Solid Waste Management in the Municipality. Moreover, necessary requirements are needed for the approval of the SWMP;
observations.managementAction: The Office must gather data for the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan of the existing dumpsite of the Municipality in order to complete the information data needed in the 10-year SWMP and submit the same to the Office of the National Solid Waste Management Commission.
observations.reason: The Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality is reviewed and submitted to the Office of Environmental Management Bureau (EMB-V) and DILG Region V, Legaspi City. However, this is not yet submitted to the National Solid Waste Management Commission because the Safe Closure and rehabilitation plan (SCRP) of the existing dumpsite of San Fernando is not yet completed as one of the requirements in 10-year SWMP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality establish the Local Council for the Protection of Children and separately provide the required allocation of 1% of the IRA to be used for the strengthening and implementation of allowable programs, projects and activities of the LCPC, which are consistent with the approved annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, and Local Investment Plan and likewise, to ensure proper coordination and assistance in the adoption and implementation of the comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention.

We recommended that:

Management require the Municipal Accountant to establish Real Property Tax, Special Education Tax Receivables and Deferred Revenues at the beginning of the year. The figures must be based on the collectibles identified by the Municipal Treasurer on her assessment from the list of taxpayers in the RPT Account Register;
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant committed to comply with the audit recommendation.

Management promised to also release the share of the Barangay on RPT collection.
observations.reason: The Accounting Department’s inability to recognize some RPT accounts in its books is a result of lack of coordination with the other concerned department. Further, Management was not able to coordinate with the Provincial and Barangay government/s for the release of shares out of the RPT collections. Only the share of the province has been    released.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Require all drivers and concerned officials to completely fill out the Driver’s Trip ticket in accordance with existing regulation for transparency and accountability over the use of such government motor vehicles; and

Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant promised to secure the needed forms to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: The concerned office was not able to prepare Monthly report of official travel and the Monthly Fuel Consumption Report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct: 

The City Engineer to provide the City Accountant the Inspection Report and Certificate of Completion of 12 projects with total contract cost of ₱57,266,799.62 as reported in the Quarterly GPPA Report Direct the GSO and City Accountant to maintain the required stock cards and supply inventory cards, respectively; and

The City Engineer to regularly provide the City Accountant copies of Quarterly Report on GPPA with supporting documents such as Inspection Report and Certificate of Completion; and

The City Accountant to recognize in the books the 12 completed projects upon submission of the City Engineer of the required documents
observations.managementAction:
Page 48 of 2020 AAR | The Inspection Reports of the 12 projects were already furnished to the Accounting Office for recording in the books of the LGU.





The City Engineer religiously provides the City Accountant such reports.


The 12 completed projects were already recognized in the books of the City.
observations.reason:
Inspection Reports were furnished to the City Accounting which were used as basis of recording the appropriate asset account affecting the 12 projects.



Implemented.

The City Engineering Office was able to comply with the recommendation.



Implemented.

The financial statements of the City presented these 12 completed projects recorded under the PPE account.

observations.managementAction:
Page 50 of 2020 AAR | The AP was no longer in the 2020 ledgers.





The settlement of liabilities was adjusted through recognition of Prior Year’s Adjustments.




The City Accountant furnished the audit team schedules of the entries made.




The transactions recognized as AP was qualified for its recognition.








Closing instructions are given prior to finalizing the financial statements of the City




The departments complied with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
The City Accountant had established the balance of its AP considering existence of actual claims to the City. AP that was previously set-up but was later on disregarded was due to the fact that there was improper set-up in the previous years. The City Accountant was able to rectify these errors.

Implemented.

Prior Year’s Adjustments was used to account unrecognized AP in the previous year. Nonetheless, the Accounting Office and the Budget Office was able to provide that these expenditures were obligated in the previous year’s appropriations.

 

Implemented.

The City Accountant was able to support the adjustments made to prior period adjustments. The series of adjustments was made to correct entries of settlements.

Not Implemented.

Although some were already settled, some AP established in prior years remained unsettled. Furthermore, the City Accounting Office was not able to furnish the audit team copies of Purchase Orders, Contracts and such other documents that would support the set-up of claim. As of December 31, 2021, ₱2,804,412.50 AP were over one year casting doubt on the existence of documents to support the claim.




Implemented.

The City Accounting Office issued closing instructions to facilitate the recording of liabilities.



Not Implemented.

Lack of coordination was still noted this CY 2020 as there were still unrecognized payables as mentioned in Observation No. 7.

observations.managementAction:
Page 61 of 2020 AAR | Department heads were able to prepare LBP Form No. 4 facilitating the identification of specific PPAs under their departments.




The OIC-City Budget Officer and City Accountant ensure that proper accounts are used in budget preparations and recording of transactions.





Budget items in LBP Forms were assigned account titles.





Restatement in the SFPe was made by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason:
LBP Form No. 4 was prepared per department to particularize their PPAs.




Not Implemented.

Proper use of accounts was not duly observed due to the non-reference with the Revised Chart of Accounts. As mentioned in Observation No. 4, disbursements totaling ₱12,589,551.10 in the accounting records were inconsistent with the budget records, while ₱6,828,922.49 of the said amount were misclassified in the Financial Statements.



Not Implemented.

While account titles were assigned in the LBP Forms, the OIC-City Budget Officer failed to align it with the revised chart of accounts as mentioned in Observation No. 4.




Not Implemented.

The restatement however did not include correcting entries for OMOE and related accounts. Furthermore, the inventories were outrightly recognized as expense.

observations.managementAction:
Page 72 of 2020 AAR | DVs for cash advances were prepared per specific purpose.




Strict compliance of timely liquidation of cash advance was employed by the management.




The City Accountant ensured that no additional cash advance was granted unless prior cash advance was liquidated.




Duly complied with by the City Accounting Office.





The City Accountant had already submitted the subject the vouchers and liquidation reports with its supporting documents.




LBP PaCS was used to facilitate payroll payments.
observations.reason:
While several purposes of cash advance were still provided for in a single DV, such were related with one another and do not hamper the timely liquidation of cash advances.

Implemented. 

Cash advances were liquidated within the prescribed period. In fact, the Status of Unliquidated Cash Advance of the City as of December 31, 2021 provided a zero balance.

Implemented.

The City has designated several accountable officers or disbursing officers in order to prevent accumulation of cash advances to single disbursing officer. Verily, additional cash advance was not granted unless liquidation of prior cash advance was made.

Implemented.

Accounting for the recording of cash advances was duly complied with in accordance with the revised chart of accounts.




Implemented.

The City Accounting Office was able to submit these DVs of cash advances for the period of October to December 2020 together with their liquidation reports and supporting documents.

Implemented.

All payrolls were already facilitated through the LBP Payroll PACS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to take actual custody of cash collections at the end of the day and to acknowledge accountability of collections and deposits by affixing his signature in the cashbooks by certifying as to the correctness of entries recorded therein.
observations.managementAction: Page 77 of 2020 AAR | The City Treasurer ensures that actual custody of cash was turned over to him.
observations.reason: As observed during cash examination, actual custody of collections remained under the possession of the cashier although these were turned-over to the City Treasurer prior to deposit. Nonetheless, cashbooks were not updated to reflect actual balances           of cash in local treasury at a given time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to withhold taxes on payments of BAC honoraria and remit the same to the Bureau of Internal Revenue in conformity with National Internal Revenue Code of 1997 and avoid sanctions on non-performance thereof.
observations.managementAction: Page 84 of 2020 AAR | Taxes from BAC Honoraria are strictly withheld by the City Accountant prior to payment of claims by the BAC members.
observations.reason: BAC members’ honoraria were paid net of taxes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LFC, the City Treasurer, in particular, to properly fill-out the revenue and receipts column as provided in the BOM, specifically, the estimates column for the second semester must be derived from the past data for actual collections for the next preceding year and actual collections for the first semester of the year. Also, the City Treasurer to include all possible revenue sources and the amounts that may be derived therein for effective decision making.
observations.managementAction: Page 86 of 2020 AAR | The City Treasurer was mindful of this recommendation and during the preparation of LEP for CY 2021, the observations made by the audit team were corrected.
observations.reason: The LEP of the City for CY 2021 has already eliminated the presentation of negative estimates of collections in the projections. Furthermore, from the LEP, the estimated collections for this year can now be deduced from the historical data.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor being the Chairman of the LSB:

Convene earlier with the LSB for the timely review and approval of the SEF Budget to avoid delays in the implementation of the PPAs under the said fund; and

Instruct the BAC to properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to educational projects for                  their timely implementation.
observations.managementAction:
Page 89 of 2020 AAR | SEF budget for CY 2021 was approved within the timeline set by the City.




The SEF plan and budget was properly executed this 2021.
observations.reason:
The SEF Budget for CY 2021 was approved on December 16, 2020.




Implemented.

PPAs under SEF was included in the APP of the City. Major infrastructure projects under SEF were fully implemented in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor prioritize and maximize the use of prior years’ savings from General Fund in allocating funds for COVID-19 related PPAs and as much as possible, utilize the 20% DF to finance priority development projects.
observations.managementAction: Page 93 of 2020 AAR | Prior year’s savings was prioritized in funding PPAs especially COVID-19 expenditures.
observations.reason: In CY 2021, COVID-19 expenditures were funded by Prior Year’s Savings such as in SB No. 2 appropriating ₱92,125,745.00 for relief goods.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

Submit the documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the rights and obligations of the source and implementing agencies.




Likewise, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all concerned department heads to ensure that all fund transfers made by the LGU are properly monitored.
observations.managementAction:
Page 56 of 2019 AAR | The City Accounting Office ensures that all transactions including transfers to barangays are supported with complete documentation and submitted to the audit team.

Monitoring is conducted especially that the Accounting Office is also the record keeper of the barangays.
observations.reason:
Documentary requirements were complied with and submitted to the audit team.




Not Implemented.

No specific monitoring mechanism was established nor even simple status report was being maintained.

observations.managementAction:
Page 70 of 2018 AAR | Management was able to collect ₱1,566,936.41 of back taxes from the eight suppliers /contractors. The City Treasurer challenged the computation as he averred that only 30% of gross receipts should be the tax base for contractors whose projects were not within the City, citing the “situs” of taxation, and referred to DOF Local Finance Circular No. 3-95.

The City Treasurer’s Office was not able to complete cash examination of all business establishments.





The City has since required the submission of Income Tax Return of suppliers and businesses as basis in computing business taxes, following the audit recommendation. The same is included as a requirement in the proposal submitted to the Sangunian, for adoption in the City’s Revenue Code. 



The City Treasurer admitted that his Office was not able to conduct examination in 2020 to present but plans to conduct a three-year examination of books this CY 2022 in lieu of the non-conduct for the previous year.
observations.reason:
Considering the limitations during the conduct of audit and absence of documents to show the actual gross receipts within and outside the city, the audit team supports the computation for the contracts made with the city government which was the basis in collecting the back taxes of ₱1,566,936.41.




Not Implemented.

The City Treasurer has only presented collection of back taxes as regards contractors and suppliers audited by the team. Furthermore, as stated in Observation No. 23, there was no cash examination conducted in 2020 to present.

Not Implemented

Income Tax Return was still not required from the suppliers and businesses because there was still no revision of the Revenue Code. The proposal of City Treasurer remains to be suggestive since 2018. Furthermore, the basis of assessment for business tax still remains to be the amount declared by the business owners and not substantiated with proofs or documents.



Not Implemented

No visitorial function was exercised by the City Treasurer to assess the declared gross receipts of business owners.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant: 

(a) disclose in the Notes to FS the unreconciled difference of Cash-in-Bank account balance of prior years that remained unadjusted; and 

(b) exert further effort to communicate with concerned bank officials, if possible, to determine movements of the account in prior years, if any.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE): 

(a) require the accountable personnel to liquidate cash advances after the purpose for which they were granted have been served; and 

(b) refrain from granting cash advances to personnel who failed to completely liquidate prior cash advance; and 



(c) stop granting cash advance in excess of the SDOs authorized limit of accountability.

We also recommended that the LCE: 

(a) require all accountable officers to maintain the prescribed cashbook for all their cash advances; 

(b) cause the stop of the practice of procuring supplies and materials through special cash advances and ensure that procurement of supplies, materials are done through the prescribed rules and regulations on the disbursement of public funds; 

(c) ensure timely submission of DVs and liquidation reports to facilitate prompt and complete audit thereof; and 



(d) ensure that sufficient controls will be consistently implemented in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended the LCE direct the  CGSO to prepare IIRUP as basis in recording the de-recognition from the books of the unserviceable properties carried in the PPE accounts and cause the proper disposal of the said properties pursuant to Section  79 of P.D. No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Disposal of various unserviceable properties was conducted on May 28, 2021.  Request for inspection was submitted to the COA Office for the disposal of additional unserviceable properties in January 2022.
The remaining balance was used to procure relief goods for COVID-19 patients.

Inventory report for the donated goods at year-end was prepared, however, these were not taken up in the books.





In-kind donations were included in the year-end Inventory Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City LSB:

(a) use the correct format in the preparation of the SEF budget as mandated in the DepEd-DBM-DILG JCNo. 1, s. 2017; 


(b) utilize the SEF for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020, in addition to JC No. 1, s. 2017, of these three agencies; and 

(c) establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF among schools to ensure its efficient and effective use in accordance with its intended purpose.
We commended the Management for its timely withholding and remittance of contributions to the GSIS via eBCS in compliance with the requirements set forth under R.A. No. 8291.

We commended the City officials for its full compliance with the rules and regulations on the distribution of SEP-SAP as provided by the DSWD MC. 

We also recommended and the LCE agreed to continue their effort in crafting its own database of complete list of beneficiaries so that it will serve as future reference for identification of the most affected families and vulnerable members of society who may be eligible for any social grant that the government intends to implement, most specifically amidst the pandemic.
observations.managementAction: The City Information and Communication Technology is currently developing the Fernandino Profile which is composed of multiple application system such as but not limited to Person with Disability, Solo Parent, 4Ps and Senior Citizen.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) officials to: 

(a) submit a written explanation on the failure to procure the aforementioned items from the PS-DBM; 

(b) ensure that succeeding procurement will be coursed through the proper process; and 



(c) submit the required documents for the procured medicines such as the (i) Certificates of Product Registration Good Manufacturing Practice and Batch Release Certificate from the FDA; and 

(ii) Certification from the Manufacturer that the supplier is an authorized distributor of the product/items.

We recommended that the LCE: 

(a) direct the (i) City GFPS to submit GAD AR duly reviewed by the DILG with all the required attachments enumerated in C.8 of Item 4 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to permit proper evaluation thereof; and 

(ii) the City Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the enhancement and further development of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information and to coordinate with the City GFPS and the Local DILG Field Officer on the generation and processing of the data in accordance with the JMC; 



(b) request the Local Sanggunian with the assistance of the GFPS to update the GAD Code as necessary; and 



(c) GFPS to establish, enhance, strengthen and/or maintain a gender-responsive M and E system.
observations.managementAction: The amended GAD Code was approved on February 27, 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the Human Resource Office personnel to require the attendees to strictly comply with the documentary requirements to support the amount spent in meals, particularly the complete filling up of the attendance sheets for verification purposes.

observations.managementAction:
Stockpiling of rice was not recommended due to the absence of warehouse or adequate space to accommodate the goods.









COVID-19 patients undergoing home quarantine were given relief goods.

During the year, no large-scale rice purchases were done.

observations.recommendation: We commended the City for its consistent compliance with the revenue regulations in the withholding and remittances of the taxes and for the maintenance of separate records for the special accounts of the City that facilitated a more convenient review of the taxes withheld and remitted to BIR.
We commended the Management for its timely withholding and remittance of contributions to the GSIS via eBCS in compliance with the requirements set forth under R.A. No. 8291.

We commended the Management for being compliant with the regulations of the National Health Insurance Act of 1995 as amended in 2013 via R.A. No. 10606 on the deduction and timely remittance of premium contributions due to PhilHealth.

We commended the Management for being compliant with the provisions of Pag-IBIG in the deduction and remittance of monthly contributions of the members and loan payments and the maintenance of the separate ledgers for the Due to Pag-IBIG account in an effort to accurately monitor the actual amounts due to the HDMF.

observations.managementAction:
Management commented that through the issuance of Executive Order No. CMO2019-109, the complete and accurate inventory of Road Networks will be realized.  There was already coordination with CICTO for the incorporation in the General Information System (GIS) of road networks included in the initial inventory conducted by the CEO.  The CGSO will communicate with the Office of the Sangguniang Panglungsod for the naming of all roads within the City for the easier rendering of report of national, provincial, city and barangay roads per area including their length.

Moreover, offices concerned and the City Inventory Committee will undertake the necessary actions including the adoption of the audit recommendations.
The CAccO on the other hand agreed to comply with the recommendation to maintain supplies ledger cards for each type of supplies.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Management:
