(a) execute thorough and careful planning and exercise prudence and due diligence in the procurement of transport and heavy equipment to ensure maximum utilization of public resources; and 

(b) if resources are available, refrain from procuring second-hand/reconditioned vehicles to avoid short period of warranty and costly repairs and maintenance on reconditioned equipment in the long run.

We also recommended that the Management develop adequate procedures for the purchase of vehicles which will include: 

(a) assessment of need and analysis of how the vehicle would support the organization’s mission, goals and objectives; 



(b) assessment of alternatives; and 




(c) analysis of expected acquisition costs as well as estimated recurring future costs such as repairs, maintenance and insurance.

We commended the Local Chief Executive and the GAD focal person and the technical working group for their continuous compliance with JMC No. 2013-01 and implementation of programs which are gender-responsive to address concerns of both women and men.

We recommended that Management: 

(a) establish a Preventive Maintenance Program to ensure that all necessary maintenance  is done on all monitor vehicles on a timely basis; 


(b) set up scheduled maintenance and require the official concern or the personnel in charge of the vehicle to have it regularly checked and inspected to avoid the incurrence of higher costs in case of vehicle’s breakdown; and 

(c) require the Motorpool Division to follow the scheduled check-up/services offered by the car manufacturer for the newly acquired vehicles so that any defect that may possibly arise during the warranty period will be addressed accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO has already implemented Guidelines No. 2019-01, Guidelines on the Purchase, Delivery and Utilization of Motor Vehicles and Heavy Equipment of the City Government of San Fernando, Pampanga which provides not only the preparation of a Preventive Maintenance Program but also general guidelines on the utilization of motor vehicles and equipment that should be followed by the Motorpool Division and rest assured that the recommendations indicated in the Audit Observation Memorandum will be adopted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:


(a) continue its effort to campaign on its slogan of “clean and green environment” which will serve as a reminder to its constituents to maintain cleanliness in the community; 

(b) continue to promote re-cycling which is the best way to reduce waste; and 



(c) give source of “rewards” for barangays, business establishments and other sectors of society  that promote reuse, recycling and reduction of garbage to encourage each and every resident to be more accountable on  their waste.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-CENRO took note of our recommendations and gave the assurance of continuous commitment towards environmental sustainability.  She further stated that several programs were launched to encourage solid waste management awareness such as the search for Best Barangay on Ecological Solid Waste Management which posed a big challenge to barangays and homeowners associations to improve their solid waste practices and eventually reduce the volume of residual waste collected.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LCE instruct:

The City Accountant and OIC-City Budget Officer to reconcile the differences noted and to submit the same for further verification. Thereafter, require them to reconcile their records regularly.




The City Accountant to restate the affected 2018 financial statements pertaining to the above-mentioned observations for fair presentation.




We have also recommended that the City improve their processes particularly procurement and disbursement.  Likewise, establish cut-off date as part of their annual closing activity to ensure that all transactions are properly recognized in the correct period.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant and OIC-City Budget Officer tried to trace all discrepancies noted by the audit team, however, no reconciliation statement was submitted. Nonetheless, they made sure that 2020 SCBAA and SAAOB were reconciled.




The City Accountant was not able to restate 2018 financial statements but nevertheless, applied and adopted the recommendation in the 2019 financial statements since 2020 transactions showed number of Prior Period Adjustments. The 2019 financial statements were restated. 




Cut-off date was disseminated to all departments and in fact, required submission of POs and Certifications from concerned departments for the recording of accounts not yet paid or processed by the LGU.
observations.reason:
The City Accountant and OIC-City Budget Officer were unable to present or submit the results of reconciliation of the 2019 SCBAA and SAAOB.

The 2019 balances in the SCBAA will affect the implementation in 2020 particularly the continuing appropriations which provided funding for unimplemented capital outlays. As mentioned in Observation No. 1(b), Part II of this report, the City Accountant did not adopt the balance from 2019 SCBAA. The overstatement of ₱16,501,590.14 implies that projects were implemented even without proper authority if the 2019 SCBAA was not restated or reconciled.

observations.recommendation:
Exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables such as sending demand letters to last known addresses to private individuals and employees; confirmation letters to various organizations whether NGOs/POs, Government agencies; and provide a status to the City Auditor’s Office.




Exert efforts to locate the documents to prove the claims.




Establish/maintain and apply sound internal control procedures on the recording and collection of receivables.




Conduct further assessment as to collectability or impairment.  Thereafter, provide provision for doubtful accounts for the accounts determined to have a no and/or low collection rate.
observations.managementAction:
This was not prioritized due to difficulties brought by the pandemic and the change in administration. 

Instead, a dialogue was conducted in September 2020, specifically with the beneficiaries of Housing Loan at the Fishermen’s Village, to which at least 36 out of 89 have resumed paying their dues collecting at least ₱280,627.00 as of December 31, 2020.

The City Accountant was able to trace earliest records of trial balances and schedules of receivables from CY 1999 to which the Due from NGAs amounting to ₱3,135,665.00 was included but did not have sufficient information on the particulars. The same with some other accounts. Management will consider sending a request for write-off of the account. 

The City Accountant mentioned that she has reiterated to her staff, during meetings, that recording of transactions must have complete and proper supporting documents especially pertaining to recognition and collections of receivables.  

Management is unsure how to proceed with the provision of doubtful accounts, especially that they were not able to send out demand/ confirmation letters to debtors and supporting documents are lacking.
observations.reason:
This was not prioritized due to difficulties brought by the pandemic and the change in administration. 

The audit team acknowledged the efforts made. However, the ₱3M only pertained to one out of at least 54 accounts amounting to ₱7.8M   

The City Accountant was able to trace earliest records of trial balances and schedules of receivables from CY 1999 to which the Due from NGAs amounting to ₱3,135,665.00 was included but did not have sufficient information on the particulars. The same with some other accounts. Management will consider sending a request for write-off of the account.

observations.recommendation:
The General Services Officer (GSO) and City Accountant to maintain the required stock cards and supplies ledger cards, respectively.




Strengthen the function and responsibilities of the Physical Inventory Committee by requiring them to conduct semestral physical count of all inventories and submit the RPCI to the Accounting Office for reconciliation with the reported inventory balances per books and to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction:
The GSO has started maintaining stock cards for supplies at the warehouse located at the lower ground of General Services Office. 

On the other hand, the Accounting Office is yet to implement the creation/ maintenance of individual ledger cards for supplies.  

Management was able to conduct physical count of common-use supplies, IT supplies and equipment, and janitorial supplies as of December 28, 2020. The same was forwarded to the Accounting Office.  

With the new leadership at the GSO, physical inventory committee have been reinforced and will closely monitor the implementation in 2021, especially the Medical, Dental Laboratory supplies which remained non-moving for the past years.
observations.reason:
Based on observation and inquiry, stock cards have been created and updated only for some items stored at the GSO warehouse. The office started doing this in December 2020.  

Whereas, the City Accounting Office does not have a dedicated staff to religiously maintain and update supplies ledger cards. Thus, what they have is only a listing and they rely mostly on the RPCI forwarded to them at year-end.

observations.recommendation:
Strictly adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines on property management particularly Section 114 of the NGAS Manual. Prepare and maintain appropriate property cards and property ledger cards




Properly fill-out the needed information in the physical inventory reports and property/ledger cards with substantial details for easy tagging and reconciliation




Reconcile the records of PPE accounts between the Accounting Office and General Services Office and the RPCPPE, and adjust accordingly.




Strengthen the function and responsibilities of the Physical Inventory Committee




Facilitate the transfer of ownership of the School Buildings, Health and Wellness Center and Multi-Purpose Hall to the appropriate agencies upon proper authority and documentation. 




Expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties to free up storage spaces, prevent further deterioration of PPE and generate additional income from sale
observations.managementAction:
The Management said that due to COVID-19 related tasks, not much attention was dedicated to this matter.




Physical inventory count of PPE was not prioritized in 2020 due to the pandemic, as most staff were tapped to help in the relief operations. 

The City GSO was able to provide a listing of the property, plant and equipment to the Accounting Office, however, reconciliation was not made due to voluminous workload brought about by pandemic. 

With the new leadership at the GSO, physical inventory committee will be reinforced and will closely monitor the implementation in 2021.  

This was not prioritized due to the pandemic. 

Management has committed to complete this in 2021.




The disposal committee has convened in October 2020 and an initial list of unserviceable properties were identified and furnished to the audit team.
observations.reason:
There were no property cards maintained by the City GSO for all PPE under his supervision. Records kept were mere listings in soft copy by type of property. 

No physical count was conducted for properties. As such no RPCPPE was rendered and property cards and ledger cards were not yet created.  

Despite having a list of PPE at the GSO and Accounting offices, these remained unreconciled at year-end. 

Due to time constraints, no physical count was conducted for PPE. As such no RPCPPE was rendered and property cards and ledger cards were not yet created.  

Per inquiry, processing of the documents needed for the transfer of health centers and multi-purpose halls to respective barangays during the latter part of 2020.

Whereas, school buildings remain to be recognized at the books of the City, as the lots where these structures are situated belongs to the City Government. 

The audit team has received an inventory of properties for disposal, however, we required submission of supporting documentation such as, appraisal report conducted by the Appraisal Team, stencil report as most in the list comprised of motor vehicles, and photo documentation of said properties. Awaiting further action from Management.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Strictly enforce the rental rates prescribed by the City Market Code, particularly on the Imposition of Fees. 




11.2. Execute and submit Contracts of Lease between stallholders and City Government in order to properly enforce rights and obligations between parties. 




11.3. Prioritize the rehabilitation and repair of the major portions of the shopping mall in order to provide a safer and convenient location for stallholders, vendors and customers alike.
observations.managementAction:
Management has temporarily suspended the implementation of annual 10% increase in the market stall fees due to the pandemic. 




Market Supervisor has updated the database/list of actual occupants at the Auxiliary Wet Market and San Fernando Shopping Mall, which will serve as reference in executing Contracts of Lease.

Renovation of the major portion of the old market roofing and facilities have been implemented during 2nd quarter of 2020.
observations.reason:
Apart from the suspension of stall fee increase, there has not been any action from the Management and the Sanggunian to review and re-evaluate the current rates and propose revisions, as applicable. 

Not Implemented

The issuance of pertinent Contracts of Lease for existing and new stall occupants has not been prioritized due to the change of administration. 



Implemented

Said PPAs were completed in 2020: a) Renovation of the Shopping Mall Building, funded from the 20% DF; and b) Greening of the City Public Market – Phase I, funded by DBM.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LCE direct the BAC and the TWG: 

To include in their post-qualification a comparative evaluation showing the number of projects with the same equipment and key personnel pledged and assigned by the same lowest calculated bidder for different projects with simultaneous or overlapping implementation period.




To formulate policies on the sufficiency and availability of equipment pledged by the contractors with regard to projects simultaneously awarded to the same contractors to avoid delay in project implementation.




To require the City Engineering office to provide status of on-going implementation of contracts with the City Government by the bidders and utilize the same to validate the submitted list of on-going projects. 




To extend the comparison with other government agencies before declaring the bids as responsive.




To set limits for the number of contracts to be handled by key personnel, in order to ensure reasonableness of performance without sacrificing quality and timeliness of project completion.
observations.managementAction:
Post-qualification was conducted and results were included in the report of the BAC TWG.




No written policy established but management assured that the contractors had sufficient personnel and equipment.




Monthly inspection of projects was being conducted by Office of the City Engineer and status report was furnished to the BAC. 




The BAC usually tapped the reports of DPWH in some of the projects being implemented by their contractors.




The key personnel of contractors remain the same for every contract. Nonetheless, although number of simultaneous projects were reduced, in some instances, this does not solve the requirement that “the project engineer must at all times be present in all stages of the construction”.
observations.reason:
Although the post-qualification evaluation report of the BAC TWG showed enumeration of list of number of personnel and equipment, such listing was not made to evaluate on whether or not there were insufficiency of manpower and equipment for implementation of simultaneous projects. The evaluation was only confined with the compliance of number of equipment and personnel.

Not Implemented

The formulation of internal policies of BAC TWG is necessary to provide basis for the BAC to consider contractors who have been declared as lowest bidder but nevertheless awarded with more than one projects. The intention is not to outrightly disregard contractors if they are capable of showing their ability to augment their personnel and equipment. 

Not Implemented

Although status report was being provided to the BAC particularly the TWG, it failed to consider delays noted in the implementation of projects such as those mentioned in Observation No. 15. The status report was not fully appreciated in accordance with its purpose.

Not Implemented

Perusal of the GPPA Report submitted by the LGUs of La Union, there were actually on-going projects of the contractors from other LGUs which were not declared in the Statement of On-Going Projects. The statement submitted by the contractors was usually limited to the on-going projects within the City.  

Not Implemented

Same key personnel were declared in the list submitted by the contractor. However, although number of simultaneous projects were reduced, in some instances, this does not solve the requirement that “the project engineer must at all times be present in all stages of the construction”.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Government submit latest updates on the status of completion of the mentioned infrastructure projects.  Also, if the same were not completed within the target completion date, provide justification and supporting documents for validation and verification by the audit team.

Thereafter, we have recommended for strict monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure completion on time.
observations.managementAction:
The City Engineer informed that liquidated damages were collected against the contractors. 




For the ensuing year, pertinent circumstances warranting issuance of suspension, resumption, and to justify delay in the completion of projects were complied with.
observations.reason:
Only two (PB-039-19 and PB-041-19) out of the six mentioned projects were computed liquidated damages which was likewise mentioned in Observation No. 15, Part II of this Report.

Implemented 

For FY 2020, at least ₱129,481.68 of liquidated damages was collected from seven projects noted with delay.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct:

The concerned officials to expedite the completion of said project in order to comply with RA No. 6969 and generate additional income for the city.




The CENR Officer prepare for the accreditation by the DENR to avoid wastage of fund, particularly, on the initial investment of ₱4M.




Lastly, management to determine the amount to be collected for the medical waste treatment facility services that should be at par with the current fees being collected by private accredited waste treaters or at least breakeven with the expenses related to the hospital waste management. Thereafter, start the process so that said income-generating activity be passed into an ordinance.
observations.managementAction:
Project was completed on 01/10/2020. 




This has not been prioritized due to the pandemic as most staff of the CENRO were also tapped as members of the City COVID-19 task force and assisted in the distribution of relief goods. Management will comply with this in 2021.

A Waste Analysis and Characterization Study will further be conducted to determine volume of hazardous wastes at present and to determine the level of treatment, strategies and technology to be employed, after which, costing will be properly determined.
observations.reason:
Although physical project has been completed, accreditation (recommendation 15.2.) has not been complied with. Thus, operation of the facility has not commenced.

Not Implemented

Accreditation is yet to be secured despite completion of the waste facility in early January 2020. Per inquiry with the CENR staff-in-charge, pertinent requirements were initially submitted to DENR-EMB via online. The Office will follow-up on the status and submit additional requirements as necessary. 

Not Implemented

This has not been prioritized due to the pandemic. We reiterate that Management will prioritize this in 2021.

observations.managementAction:
The Accountant has made an adjustment to the Land account, amounting to ₱37,257,560.00 to take up the value of 16 lots with Tax Declarations but were not booked up in previous years.




No action taken by Management. Previously, they have made reference to the Assessed values as determined by the City Assessor as basis of recording in the books. 




Tax Declarations have been updated to the account of the City Government, actual use of the parcels of land have been updated, accordingly. 

Only the transfer of patrol cars and dump trucks which were donated to Barangays in 2017 to 2018 were completed.
observations.reason:
While the items of land were already booked up, updated assessed values were used per Tax Declaration, instead of the value at acquisition cost, or fair value if acquired through non-exchange transaction.

Not Implemented  

No adjustments were taken up due to non-availability of relevant documents to support the proper valuation at acquisition date, hence, assessed values were used to record in the books of accounts.

Implemented 

At least 29 Tax Declarations have been updated to the account of City Government of San Fernando as owner as well as the actual use of land or property. 

Not Implemented

The preparation of documentary requirements for the transfer of health centers and multi-purpose halls were not prioritized in 2020 due to change in leadership.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the City GAD database to ensure an updated and operational database, which will better serve the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) in attaining an evidence-based GAD planning and policymaking.




Comply with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 and 2016-01 on the establishment of a gender responsive monitoring and evaluation system through the creation of GAD M and E Team who shall evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies, programs, and projects.
observations.managementAction:
Included in the ensuing year’s GAD Action Plan is the creation and maintenance of a database which shall be a joint responsibility of the GFPS and TWG.




Monitoring and Evaluation (M and E) Team had been reinforced under Executive Order No. 70-2019, dated October 16, 2019.
observations.reason:
No GAD database has been institutionalized by the City Government as they are awaiting the completion of CBMS. The last update on GAD database was in 2018, during which the CSWDO was still in-charge of updating it, although it didn’t have significant data to be considered relevant. 
Reiterated in Part II of this report.

Not Implemented

GAD M and E Team was created, however, there has not been an evaluation and assessment conducted and no report was submitted on the projects that were implemented during the LCE’s term of office from 2016 to 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management strictly implement City Ordinance No. 2003-007 in conformity with RA No. 9003, particularly:

Waste segregation at source be strongly monitored (The City to partner with barangay personnel to monitor every household);

Schedule of garbage collection with regard to biodegradable, non-biodegradable and recyclables likewise be strictly implemented (The City to strategize techniques and methods like provision of personnel to MRFs or dumping areas to oversee that residents dump wastes that are properly segregated); and

Penalty be strictly enforced to violators.
observations.managementAction:
Unfortunately, despite intensive information and education campaigns, most garbage stations, even at the City proper do not follow segregation at source. Nonetheless, the CENR Officer assured that wastes are sorted at the sanitary landfill. 




CENRO stated that they are more inclined on implementing positive reinforcement than penalizing the offenders. Likewise, they had verbal agreements with barangay officials that penalties be imposed by the latter within their respective jurisdiction.
observations.reason:
Despite existence of ordinance imposing the segregation of wastes at source, these were not strictly enforced. 

Not Implemented

Based on inquiry and observation, garbage trucks tend to collect all wastes at once even when they are not segregated at source. 

Not Implemented

Compliance to City Ordinances pertaining to the imposition of penalties were not strictly adhered to.

observations.managementAction:
Management was able to collect ₱1,566,936.41 of back taxes from the eight suppliers /contractors. The City Treasurer challenged the computation as he averred that only 30% of gross receipts should be the tax base for contractors whose projects were not within the City, citing the “situs” of taxation, and referred to DOF Local Finance Circular No. 3-95.

The City Treasurer was able to collect ₱67,696.30 of back taxes from various suppliers of goods and services. Compliance for the remaining business establishments’ applications/ renewals were applied prospectively.  

The City has since required the submission of Income Tax Return of suppliers and businesses as basis in computing business taxes, following the audit recommendation. The same is included as a requirement in the proposal submitted to the Sangunian, for adoption in the City’s Revenue Code. 

The City Treasury regularly conduct examination of books of accounts of business establishments with the City, annually, despite the absence of fiscal examiners under the department.
observations.reason:
The Audit Team, in a rejoinder, answered that the DOF circular only applies to suppliers/contractors with an actual branch office established outside the City for the contractor not to pay 100% of the business tax at a rate of ½ of 1% of its gross sales/receipts. 



Not Implemented

The collections on back taxes only pertained to 4 out of the 12 suppliers as sampled in the audit observation.  



Not Implemented

Income Tax Return was still not required from the suppliers and businesses because there was still no revision of the Revenue Code. The proposal of City Treasurer remains to be suggestive since 2018. Furthermore, the basis of assessment for business tax still remains to be the amount declared by the business owners not substantiated with proofs or documents.

Not Implemented

The City Treasurer was able to provide results of examination of books of some businesses in the City. However, it has shown that such exercise of visitorial function was still inadequate. 

It is noted that the audit observation was done only to a small sample of contractors since the audit team had some limitations in the accuracy of assessment such as unavailability of Income Tax Returns to substantiate Gross Receipts/Sales. Basing only on those sampled it has shown that there was significant understatement of payment of business taxes, to which the Treasurer could have applied to other suppliers/ contractors within the City.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management require the City Assessor to prepare the SMV pursuant to Section 212 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and cause the passage of an Ordinance incorporating therein the proposed SMV as basis for the conduct of general revision of real property assessments in adherence to Section 219 of the same Act and DILG-DOF JMC No. 2010-001.

Thereafter, require the City Assessor to conduct general revision of real property assessments every after three years as provided under Section 219 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The City Assessor, since her assumption to office, has prioritized the cleansing of real property database due to findings that there are duplicate entries and property units that are not classified to their actual use. She noted that this will facilitate the updating of SMV and implementing a general revision of real property assessments.
observations.reason:
We acknowledge the efforts made as mentioned. However, we reiterate the urgency and importance of these steps so the Management can fast track its implementation. Reiterated in Part II of this report.

Not Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended strict compliance by management with the requirements of the above mentioned DBM Circular to ensure propriety and necessity in procuring second-hand vehicles. Such procurement should always be in line with the State’s policy of optimizing the utilization of public resources and the enhancement of economy in government operations. In addition, management should: a. Explain and provide justification on their non-compliance with important provisions of National Budget Circular No. 466-A and why they procured the second-hand vehicle despite the risk not acquiring right of ownership; and b. Submit the required certification or documentation for the transaction to be allowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2013 No action plan provided by the Management.
observations.reason: No updates provided by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that close monitoring on the submission of liquidation reports be undertaken by the Municipality. Concerned LGU Accountant should be reminded/required to immediately submit the Reports of Disbursements and to return unused balance of fund transfer so that corresponding expenditures can be recorded in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2012 No breakdown as to details of funds transferred to other LGUs.
observations.reason: Financial Statements as of December 31, 2020 showed a Due from LGUs account amounted to P3,715,377.39.

observations.recommendation: We recommended strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 relative to settlement of audit disallowance. Agency officials should strictly enforce settlement of long outstanding disallowances.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2012 No breakdown as to details of disallowance.
observations.reason: Proofs of disallowance were not submitted yet.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary journal entries to recognize in the books the public infrastructure assets totaling ₱48,441,366.92, as well as the related accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004, to present fairly the value of the assets in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason:
The transfer of public infrastructure from the registry to Property, Plant and Equipment account as required by COA Circular No. 2016-004 was not performed by the Municipal Accountant.

Restated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
delegate an employee who will be assigned to prepare, monitor and submit the required Monthly Fuel Consumption Report; and
ensure that the forms, records, and reports prescribed under COA Circular No. 77-61, on the reporting and control over fuel consumptions, are prepared, submitted and properly accomplished so that wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures are minimized and disbursements are effectively controlled and properly accounted for.
observations.reason:
No employee was delegated to prepare, monitor and submit fuel consumption report which resulted to the overstatement of Fuel and Lubricants Inventory account as of December 31, 2020.
Since no employee was designated, the required forms and control over the fuel consumption were not achieved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and mitigation fund-MOOE for CY 2019, and every year-end thereafter, to Trust Liabilities-DRRM in the Trust Fund books and revert the unutilized amounts of CYs 2010-2014 to unappropriated surplus of the General Fund in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The amount was transferred in January 2021.
observations.reason: Unexpended LDRRMF were not transferred to Trust liabilities at the end of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Accounting Department to prepare the required Subsidiary Ledgers in accordance with the prescribed instructions provided under Volume II, NGAS for LGUS in order to reflect the needed details of the balances so that information/specifics pertaining to accounts in the General Ledger will be readily available.
observations.reason: Subsidiary ledgers maintained by the Accounting Section still did not contain the necessary details or breakdown of balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management to direct the GAD Focal Person to conduct gender analysis of their programs and projects through the HGDG tool using the appropriate checklists to ensure that the proposed programs are gender-responsive and can be attributed to GAD.
observations.reason: No GAD report was submitted for the Audit Team to validate.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the Municipal Health Officer thru its concerned officers, to immediately remit the total collections from the Municipal Lying in Clinic to the Municipal Treasurer for proper issuance of official receipts and recording thereof; and
direct the Municipal Treasurer to promptly submit records and reports of collections of Lying Clinic to the Municipal Accountant for proper recognition of income.
observations.reason:
Until now the Municipal Health Officer have not submitted records nor remitted the collections from the Municipal Lying in Clinic to the Municipal Treasurer.
No records and reports were submitted to the Municipal Treasurer by the MHO pertaining to the collections from the Lying in Clinic thus, income thereof was not yet recognized in the books of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management thru the concerned members of the Disposal Committee, effect the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with COA Circular 92-386, in order to generate additional funds, if found valuable and to avoid the further deterioration of the unserviceable properties due to exposure to natural elements and possible loss due to theft and loss of value.
observations.reason: No report on the disposal of the ₱3.457 million unserviceable assets were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the responsible officials to conduct the physical count of all its Inventories on a regular basis;
direct the responsible official to maintain inventory records of each type of inventory, aside from the subsidiary ledgers maintained in the Accounting Section; and
direct the personnel responsible for the various inventory accounts to require end-users to acknowledge the receipt of the inventories requested and require the adequate monitoring of issued and balances of inventories.
observations.reason:
No responsible official was designate to conduct the Semestral physical count of inventories, thus only the report for the inventory of accountable forms were submitted to the audit team.
Responsible division/department did not maintain complete records.
Required SSMI from concerned department were not submitted to the Municipal Accountant overstating all inventories at the end of the year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that: 26-27
1) The Municipal Accountant immediately effect correcting entries to derecognize the said inventory accounts in the financial statements; and
2) Management direct its officials to strictly observe the prescribed accounting guidelines and control procedures on the receipt, issuance and storage/custodianship of supplies and materials.
observations.managementAction:
Continuous absence of the concerned personnel in the actual duty and weak internal control of the concerned department
No action taken

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that: 27-30
3) Management exercise its duties and responsibilities to comply with one of its many functions initiating the creation of Inventory Committee and conduct of physical inventory taking of the Agency’s assets. The corresponding report must be submitted to the Local Chief Executive and to the Audit Team; and
4) Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain property records and the Municipal Accountant to prepare ledger cards for each class of PPE to come up with a reconciliation of the balances in the book of accounts of these PPE items.
observations.managementAction:
Physical Inventory was not conducted due to COVID-19 situation. Priorities were given more on the implementation of COVID-19 PPAs. the inventory committee was not created.
Property records were not maintained by the Municipal Treasurer because she is currently holding two concurrent core positions in two Municipalities, hence, too much workloads precluded her from performing the assigned tasks.
observations.reason: No action taken

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that: 30-34
5) Management direct the concerned officials to stop the practice of payment execution of various operating expenses which are not allowed by law through granting of cash advance and liquidation thereafter;
6) Management direct its officials to stop granting additional cash advances if the previous ones remained unsettled or unliquidated. Demand letters must be sent to the erring employees for their unliquidated balances. In case of non-compliance, Management is encouraged to resort to the rules cited in Section 5 of COA Circular 97-002, which provides that failure to liquidate the cash advance within the prescribed period constitutes a valid cause for the withholding of salary; and
7) The Municipal Accountant immediately come up with a reconciliation of balances that would resolve the variance between the total cash advance recorded in the books and in the report of unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2019.
observations.managementAction:
Controls were improved pertinent to liquidation reports of huge Cash Advances
A liquidating officer was assigned to monitor balances for unliquidated CAs
No action taken
observations.reason:
The urgent need to spend during the COVID-19 pandemic
Inability to monitor balances for Cash Advances
Lack of monitoring and supervision of the concerned official

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect correcting entries to include the actual RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Revenues in the Statement of Financial Position for CY 2019. The Municipal Accountant must coordinate with the Treasury Department to come up with the lists of unsettled tax liabilities. Subsidiary ledgers must also be maintained for these accounts for proper monitoring. Lastly, ensure that receivable accounts are valued at the actual amount of tax due and collectible to attain fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
Lack of supervision and monitoring of the Municipal Accountant
No action taken

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and reconcile the Due from the Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and effect adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances. Likewise, ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same so as not to affect the presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
Lack of supervision and monitoring of the concerned official.
No action taken

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant gather additional information pertinent to the CIP-Land Improvement account by retrieving available documents and inquiry with the former Municipal Accountant to ascertain if appropriate asset account must still be recognized. If it is found out that said account recognition is not necessary anymore, it is suggested that the Municipal Accountant apply correcting entries to derecognize the CIP – Land Improvement account.
observations.managementAction: Absence of supporting records to the account. The Municipal Accountant initiated to retrieve all necessary documents and records in the prior years for the recognition of the said asset is not necessary anymore.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and trace the transactions pertinent to the Other Prepayments account. Adjustments must be effected immediately. We also recommended to improve the controls with regard to the monitoring of accounting transactions specifically on the said account.
observations.managementAction: Lack of records to support the account. The Municipal Accountant initiated to retrieve all necessary documents and records in the prior years for the adjusting entries at the statement date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, guided by COA Circular 2016-005, initiate the verification, review and analysis of the abovementioned non-moving receivable accounts to effect necessary adjusting entries/correcting journal entries for the eventual disposition thereof. Further, the said accounts can be requested for write-off as these affect the fair presentation of the financial statements. Further, we recommended that controls must be set in place in the monitoring of receivable accounts by maintaining lists and/or subsidiary ledgers for these accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Absence of available records to support for the non-moving accounts.
No action taken

Prompt submission was not made due to restrictions imposed by covid-19 pandemic.
Accumulated reports for CY 2020 were already submitted to COA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
28) Exert effort in implementing the recommendations of the Audit Team by creating an Inventory Committee and conducting a complete count of the Municipality’s PPE and prepare the Report as provided by Section 57, Volume II of NGAs Manual and submit the same to the Local Chief Executive and Auditor. The Audit Team would like to emphasize that this has been a subject of repeated recommendations for many years now, yet remains not implemented; 
29) Require the General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer to maintain records on all properties of the agency; and
30) Require the Municipal Accountant to properly maintain ledger cards of all properties. This must be reconciled with stock/property cards maintained by the GSO or Municipal Treasurer.
observations.managementAction:
An initial count was made by the staff of the Municipal Treasurer but only in some selected department. Records on all properties were not maintained because of too much workload on the part of the Municipal Treasurer. An initial inventory committee was promised to be created.
No action taken.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
31) Stop granting Cash Advance for disbursements not allowed by law. Payments must be made directly to payee/supplier through the issuance of check except those expressly allowed under COA Circular 97-002. Cash Advance must only be given or granted to authorized officials as cited also in COA Circular 97-002; 
32) Must not allow the grant of additional cash advance if the previous ones remained unliquidated or unsettled; and
33) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to settle/liquidate her accountabilities for unliquidated CAs as at December 31, 2018.
observations.managementAction: Because of the geographical location of the agency, some supplier/payee are paid in the form of cash advances. The Municipal Treasurer made liquidations during the year but was unable to fully liquidate her accountabilities. The agency still practice granting additional unliquidated or unsettled balances. The Municipal Treasurer was reminded to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to establish Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year. The figures must be based on the collectibles identified by the Municipal Treasurer on her assessment from the list of taxpayers in the RPT Account Register; and effect the necessary adjusting entries of prior years’ unaccounted RPT and SET receivables.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant was not able to set up RPT and SET Receivables because of lack of coordination between the concerned tax and offices. The Municipal Accountant committed that he will coordinate with the treasury office to set up real property tax and special education tax receivable as per RPT account register and effect the necessary adjusting entries of prior years’ unaccounted RPT and SET receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to submit copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders and their complete supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) days after their perfection for purposes of review of said contracts and technical evaluation by COA TAS.
observations.managementAction: No designated GSO. The Management committed to designate a GSO that will handle the submission of POs and other supporting documents of all perfected contracts.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated compliance to the audit recommendations offered in the previous audit reports which primarily dealt on the assurance of conducting physical count of LGU properties and submitting updated RPCPPE to the auditor concerned.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented. Please see No. 3-4

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant (a) disclose in the Notes to FS the unreconciled difference of Cash-in-Bank account balance of prior years that remained unadjusted; and (b) exert further effort to communicate with concerned bank officials, if possible, to determine movements of the account in prior years, if any.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) (a) require the accountable personnel to liquidate cash advances after the purpose for which they were granted have been served; and (b) refrain from granting cash advances to personnel who failed to completely liquidate prior cash advance; and (c) stop granting cash advance in excess of the SDOs authorized limit of accountability.
We also recommended that the LCE (a) require all accountable officers to maintain the prescribed cashbook for all their cash advances; (b) cause the stop of the practice of procuring supplies and materials through special cash advances and ensure that procurement of supplies, materials are done through the prescribed rules and regulations on the disbursement of public funds; (c) ensure timely submission of DVs and liquidation reports to facilitate prompt and complete audit thereof; and (d) ensure that sufficient controls will be consistently implemented in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City Accountant to (i) consistently record the purchased inventories to their respective asset accounts; and (ii) ensure that the de-recognition of inventories is recorded only when these are issued to end-users; (b) City Health Officer to prepare and submit monthly the corresponding control documents such as Stock Cards (SCs) to ensure proper accounting and promote better control in the monitoring and accountability for supplies and materials; and (c) City General Service Officer to ensure proper safekeeping of supplies and materials inventory to minimize the risk of possible loss or wastage of government resources.

We recommended and the LCE agreed torequire the Inventory Team to (a) conduct annual physical inventory of its PPE and prepare the RPCPPE; and (b) perform periodic reconciliation of the PPE balances between CAO and CGSO records to reduce to minimum, if not to zero out, the variance noted.
We likewise recommended and the City Accountant agreed to (a) provide depreciation for all depreciable PPE accounts; (b) review and ensure that provision for depreciation shall not exceed the residual value of PPE items; (c) ensure proper classification of PPE items in the books; (d) accomplish accurately the supporting schedules for all PPE accounts; (e) analyze and reconcile the variance noted on the balances of PPE accounts enumerated above; (f) make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct all  erroneous recording/classification noted;  and (g) adopt all the principles prescribed in IPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the City’s PPE accounts for a more reliable information and fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements at year-end.

We recommended the LCE direct the  CGSO to prepare IIRUP as basis in recording the de-recognition from the books of the unserviceable properties carried in the PPE accounts and cause the proper disposal of the said properties pursuant to Section  79 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the City Treasurer (a) remit to the National Treasury the unutilized balance of ₱10,400.50, copy furnish the Audit Team with the documents to support the same, and prepare the necessary journal entry to fully liquidate the fund;  (b) City Accountant prepare a separate account under the GF for similar transactions in the future for improved monitoring and tracking of utilization and ensure that immediate actions will be taken to utilize received funds for the timely enjoyment by the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Treasurer, City General Service Officer, City Disaster Risk and Management  Officer and City Social Welfare DepartmentOfficer to (a) take heed of the basic controls in the receipt, recording, inventory, storage and distribution of the all donations received even in the midst of a crisis; (b) submit the required reports together with the supporting documents; (c) require the Committee in charge of the Physical Inventory to include In-kind Donations received by the City in the physical count and Inventory Report; (d) maintain a separate bank account with an authorized government depository bank (AGDB) where cash donations are to be deposited.

We also recommended that the LCE require the (a) CDRRMC Head to (i) prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP that would include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years; and the revised AIP to include proposed PPAs to be charged against the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in accordance with the approved LDRRMP; (ii) refrain from using general terms in presenting the PPAs in the AIP and LDRRMFIP, indicate the corresponding appropriation for each specific PPA for ease of monitoring of project implementation; (iii) ensure periodic assessment and performance monitoring on the implementation of the DRRM Plans; (iv) ensure that the DRRMF is strictly utilized for the implementation of DRRM programs; (b)  City Budget Officer to maintain separate RAAO for the DRRMF QRF, MOOE and CO; and (c) City Accountant to (i) ensure the accurate recording in the books of equipment purchased/acquired through donation; and (ii) maintain corresponding SLs for each type of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment.

We recommended that the LCE require the Planning Officer to (a) include in their PPAs that will have an impact on health and environment issues to control the spread of the virus; (b) provide additional allocation for PPAs for COVID-19 preventive measures in accordance with Item 3.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; and (c) continue to be compliant in the preparation of COVID-19 related  PPAs and assure inclusion  thereof in the AIP in accordance with Item 2.1  DBM DILG JMC No. 02.

We recommended that the City LSB (a) use the correct format in the preparation of the SEF budget as mandated in the DepEd-DBM-DILG JCNo. 1, s. 2017; (b) utilize the SEF for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020, in addition to JC No. 1, s. 2017, of these three agencies; and (c) establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF among schools to ensure its efficient and effective use in accordance with its intended purpose.

We commended the City officials for its full compliance with the rules and regulations on the distribution of SEP-SAP as provided by the DSWD MC. We also recommended and the LCE agreed to continue their effort in crafting its own database of complete list of beneficiaries so that it will serve as future reference for identification of the most affected families and vulnerable members of society who may be eligible for any social grant that the government intends to implement, most specifically amidst the pandemic.

We recommended  and the LCE agreed to (a) instruct theChairperson of the POC to use the intended funds  to actively respond to pandemic crisis  by identifying related PPSAs; and (b) request the  Chief of Police to maximize the use of the POF set as an assistance during the crisis, in accordance with DILG MC No. 2020-079 and DBM LBC No. 124, also known as “Policy Guidelines on the Provision of Funds by Local Government Units for Programs, Projects, and Activities to Address the COVID-19 Situation”.

We recommended that the(a) OIC- City Budget Officer submit a detailed and a per department report on the charging of  COVID-19 Hazard Pay grants to JO personnel to determine appropriation/budget used for payments amounting to ₱405,900.00; (b) City Human Resource Officer submit the (i) copy of special order from the agency/department head covering the assignment to hazardous/difficult areas; (ii) approved DTRs/service reports of employees who physically reported to work during the ECQ as required under COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012; and (iii) satisfactory explanation for the adjustments made on the total number of days of employees to be paid and any supporting documents that would prove their attendance and work accomplished.

We recommended that the LCE require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) officials to (a) submit a written explanation on the failure to procure the aforementioned items from the PS-DBM; (b) ensure that succeeding procurement will be coursed through the proper process; and (c) submit the required documents for the procured medicines such as the (i) Certificates of Product Registration Good Manufacturing Practice and Batch Release Certificate from the FDA; and (ii) Certification from the Manufacturer that the supplier is an authorized distributor of the product/items.

We recommended that the LCE (a) direct the (i) City GFPS to submit GAD AR duly reviewed by the DILG with all the required attachments enumerated in C.8 of Item 4 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to permit proper evaluation thereof; and (ii) the City Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the enhancement and further development of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information and to coordinate with the City GFPS and the Local DILG Field Officer on the generation and processing of the data in accordance with the JMC; (b) request the Local Sanggunian with the assistance of the GFPS to update the GAD Code as necessary; and (c) GFPS to establish, enhance, strengthen and/or maintain a gender-responsive M and E system.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Human Resource Office personnel to require the attendees to strictly comply with the documentary requirements to support the amount spent in meals, particularly the complete filling up of the attendance sheets for verification purposes.

We recommended that the City Health Officer and other City officials concerned (a) require the GCMCI to submit documents to account the ₱2.7 million subsidy; (b) submit justification and proof of the reasonableness of the amount of financial assistance granted to GCMCI for the construction of the testing facility; (c) propose for the revision of the MOA to include provisions on income-sharing from the income of GCMCI, equipment maintenance and repair, and other terms and conditions that would be beneficial to the City and its constituents; and (d) request the Sanggunian Panlungsod (SP) to pass an ordinance to authorize the collections of  swab testing fee to facilitate proper recording in the City’s books of accounts.

We recommended that the LCE instruct BAC officials to (a) submit a written explanation on the absence of a written agreement  for the staggered delivery by the supplier; (b) require the City Social Welfare Development Officer to (i) prepare a  distribution plan for the undistributed goods to ensure that sufficient controls will be in place in the receipt, recording, distribution, and storage not only for rice but for all relief goods as well; (ii) ensure that adequate controls are in place to account fully the movements of goods/rice  at any given time; (iii) submit complete accounting of rice distributed  as of year-end based on CSWD records and Accounting records for reconciliation purposes; and henceforth, (iv) ensure timely distribution of relief goods especially during state of calamities to achieve the prompt delivery of services to its constituents; and (v) assure that rice as well as other goods procured will be of good quality to prevent possible infestation and avoid prolong overstocking.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC officials to (a) exert best effort to obtain lower prices in the procurement of basic goods and medical supplies not only during calamities but also in regular times; (b) obtain data on prices of goods procured by other LGU as basis of price comparison in the City’s procurement; and (c) adhere with the rules of the Price Act Law, to set an example with other buyers and, if possible, coordinate with the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) to hold the hoarders of goods liable for the violation of the said law.

(a) direct the BAC Chairperson to submit immediately all planning and bidding documents that form part of the contract of the aforementioned projects; and (b) direct the Municipal Accountant to consider the PAJE in order to correct the account balances and present fairly the affected accounts in the FS and ensure henceforth that progress payments are classified under the Construction in Progress account.

(a) require the Municipal Engineer to estimate the useful life of the infrastructure assets and the Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for depreciation thereof; (b) enjoin the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the results of the physical count with the accounting records and henceforth submit the RPCPPE on time; and (c) require the Municipal Accountant to update and reconcile the SL, GL and FS.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) record under the Due to NGAs account the receipt, liquidation and refund of fund transfers from source agency with attached specific purpose or stipulation; (b) consider the PAJE in correcting the noted misstatements and make sure that the receipt of grant without specific purpose are recorded as income, not a liability; and (c) identify under which account the amount of P19,361.02 is appropriate to be classified, all in compliance with IPSAS 1 - Presentation of FS and the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Accountant to record completely and timely all loan proceeds in the books of accounts and consider the PAJE in order to correct the account balances and present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements.

With the foregoing, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor          (a) direct the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to reconcile thoroughly their respective records and present fairly the Cash in Bank account in the FS; (b) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the 2019 and 2020 BRS and submit the same to the Audit Team, together with the copies of bank statements and supporting schedules; and (c) follow up the claim of the municipal government before the PDIC for the unrecovered savings account with the then Rural Bank of Cajidiocan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all supplies and materials every semester and prepare and submit the RPCI to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year. Moreover, enjoin the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to reconcile their records regularly and prepare supply ledger cards and stock cards, respectively.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor      (a) require the BAC to provide to the Audit Team the noted documentary deficiencies in the procurement process and/or the justification for the lacking documents thereof; (b) remind the Municipal Accountant to ensure henceforth that the payment to suppliers are supported with complete supporting documents; and (c) explain and/or justify the use of the fund for financial assistance for Typhoon Ursula for COVID-19 related PPAs.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to return the remaining amount of P10,000.00 to the DSWD then furnish the Audit Team with a copy of Official Receipt. Moreover, explain or justify the absence of photos of actual receipt of financial assistance.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended/unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE to special trust fund and disclose the same in the Notes to Financial Statements including those pertaining to prior years and ensure henceforth that all claims charged against the LDRRMF are completely supported with proper documents in order to establish its regularity and propriety before processing for payment.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to reconcile their records thoroughly and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies to establish the reliability of accounts in the FS. Also, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies in intensifying the collection of real property tax such as information campaign to encourage taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation of the unimplemented PPAs in order to maximize the utilization of Development Fund and disclose the 14 implemented PPAs in the quarterly Report of Government PPAs to be submitted to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the GAD Focal Person and the Municipal Budget Officer to review thoroughly the appropriations under the GAD Budget and henceforth ensure that the GPB is properly integrated in the annual budget.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
adjust the original budget amounts of GF in the amount of ₱847,816,974.15 as per AO No. 8 and the final budget amounts to ₱1,126,154,893.54 in consideration of the net changes of budget of ₱278,337,919.39 based on the 13 supplemental budgets of the LGU.
trace the details of the Continuing Appropriation in the amount of ₱247,506,615.72 under the original budget column and thereafter, adjust the amounts presented in 2020;
recommend to the LFC and LCE for reversion to beginning balance available for appropriation, balances of completed CO; and
maintain RAAOs in support of the actual amounts in the SCBAA and the reconciliation with the amounts presented in the SFPe.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
The City Engineer to provide the City Accountant the Inspection Report and Certificate of Completion of 12 projects with total contract cost of ₱57,266,799.62 as reported in the Quarterly GPPA Report.
The City Engineer to regularly provide the City Accountant copies of Quarterly Report on GPPA with supporting documents such as Inspection Report and Certificate of Completion; and
The City Accountant to recognize in the books the 12 completed projects upon submission of the City Engineer of the required documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
The City Treasurer to immediately transfer the cash balances totaling ₱18,925,241.41 to the Trust Fund depository bank accounts pertaining to Due to NGAs and Trust Liabilities – DRRMF;
The City Accountant to:
Prepare correcting entry to recognize the income from reverted unutilized LDRRMF amounting to ₱14,463,007.84 and Septage fee of ₱17,750,367.45;
Maintain registries for unutilized LDRRMF reverted to General Fund and septage fee to ensure that the same will specifically be used for social services, septage and other sewerage related expenditures, respectively; and
Recognize assets amounting to ₱3,317,124.57 and expenses and income both amounting to ₱6,064,850.84.
We also recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Development Council to prepare PPAs for social services and septage and other sewerage related expenditures with total appropriations of ₱14,463,007.84 and ₱17,750,367.45, respectively.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person and CPDC to strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the CBMS and GAD databases of the LGU through preparation of master plan indicating the timelines, process, budget and other necessary actions. Thereafter, start the implementation in CY 2021 in order to ensure updated, operational, and reliable information provided by said databases which will serve the LGU in attaining an evidence-based planning, policy-making, and relief distribution in the future.
Additionally, we recommended that the City Mayor augment the number of employees responsible in the maintenance and development of the databases, more specifically to update the data contained therein as data gathering and collating mechanism is certainly a tedious work.
Lastly, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the CPDC to coordinate with data-gathering government agencies and enter into a partnership or an agreement, if necessary, to facilitate the sharing of data and information.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to take actual custody of cash collections at the end of the day and to acknowledge accountability of collections and deposits by affixing his signature in the cashbooks by certifying as to the correctness of entries recorded therein.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to adopt clear-cut, written guidelines or policy on the acceptance of checks from taxpayers for the payment of taxes, dues and other indebtedness to the government ensuring that controls are in place to prevent returned/dishonored checks. Thereafter, propose the said guidelines/policy to the SP for a resolution approving the same.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
CPDC, to ensure in the ensuing years that specific programs and projects are described in the LEP of the LGU in providing assistance to barangays and associations, and to initiate establishment of written policies and procedures in the Grants and Donations; and
City Budget Officer to see to it that the implementation of Grants and Donations are within the nature or purpose of its appropriation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to withhold taxes on payments of BAC honoraria and remit the same to the Bureau of Internal Revenue in conformity with National Internal Revenue Code of 1997 and avoid sanctions on non-performance thereof.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LFC, the City Treasurer, in particular, to properly fill-out the revenue and receipts column as provided in the BOM, specifically, the estimates column for the second semester must be derived from the past data for actual collections for the next preceding year and actual collections for the first semester of the year. Also, the City Treasurer to include all possible revenue sources and the amounts that may be derived therein for effective decision making.

We recommended that the City Mayor being the Chairman of the LSB:
Convene earlier with the LSB for the timely review and approval of the SEF Budget to avoid delays in the implementation of the PPAs under the said fund; and
Instruct the BAC to properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to educational projects for their timely implementation

We recommended the City Mayor to direct:
The City Engineer to closely monitor the documentation of suspension of works and extension of contract time, so that issues with the technicalities of the provisions of the RA No. 9184 can be prevented;
The CPDC to coordinate with other NGAs, such as the DPWH, within the City so that projects by either party will not overlap and be redundant with projects of the other. In cases of projects associated with private-owned properties, the CPDC is advised to consider the propriety, legality, and remedies associated with such properties; and
The City Engineer to oversee the execution and imposition of the liquidated damages imposed against the contractors.

We recommended that the City Mayor prioritize and maximize the use of prior years’ savings from General Fund in allocating funds for COVID-19 related PPAs and as much as possible, utilize the 20% DF to finance priority development projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Assessor to:
Prepare and implement a plan to conduct general revision of real property assessments, which shall detail the activities for the appraisal of land, buildings, and preparation of the SMV, including the determination of operational requirements (budget and manpower resources); and
Submit the results of appraisal through the General Revision Forms to the Sanggunian concerned for public hearings.

We recommended that Management direct the responsible officials to submit
justification on the inclusion of ineligible beneficiaries as well as the
lacking documentary requirements. If warranted, demand for a refund for funds
illegally received and/or impose corresponding sanctions as provided for in
Memorandum Circular No. 09 Series of 2020 dated April 9, 2020.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit
justification for undertaking the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan
Act without complying with the requirements of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 and
to be always updated with the issuances of the GPPB as the governing board for
all the procurement activities of the government.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit
explanation/justification on the non-compliance with the mandatory posting of
the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Emergency
Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB Online Portal as mandated
in GPPB Circular 01-2020.

Henceforth, we also recommended that the BAC always be updated with
the issuances and advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to
avoid non-compliance in the future.

We recommended that the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) submit
justification on awarding the contracts on procurements of various goods for the
relief operations of COVID-19 to Muntuerto Management & Development
Corporation which is not in line of the latter’s business. We also recommended
the Management to submit the aforementioned lacking documents for further
verification of the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the purchases of
the relief goods as inventory and shall be recognized only as expense when these
relief goods are distributed to the recipients and have these properly monitored
by the GSO who will closely and regularly coordinate the proper management
and reporting of the relief goods inventories.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
observe the procedural rules and the proper classifications in the accounting and
recording of its supplies and properties in accordance with the Government
Accounting Manual for LGU’s IPSAS on inventories and the internal control
principles and other similar issuances to avoid financial misstatements.

We recommended that Management submit the authority of entitlement
of the allowance, certificate of attendance during the ECQ period and IATF
Resolution of the Risk Classification in the workplace to substantiate the validity
of the transaction.

We recommended that Management submit a justification on the
aforementioned observation together with its references or results of market
scanning conducted.

We recommended that Management submit the FDA Certificate of
Product Registration and RITM validation to substantiate the regularity of the
transaction.

We recommended that Management submit justification/explanation on
the non-posting and delay in the preparation of the BGCM monthly report on
fund utilization and status of implementation of PPAs. We also recommended
that Management submit to the team copies of the written notice from the LCE
when the said reports were posted on its website, and to the government offices
where the submission of the reports is required.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned
officials to ensure the timely submission of copies of government contracts and each
of all the documents forming part thereof within five (5) working days from
its execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009,
to ensure timely review and evaluation of the same, so that the Management
could be immediately notified of any defects/deficiencies noted thereon.

We recommended that Management secure sales invoices from
Muntuerto Management & Development Corporation instead of, M. Citi
Suites – South Park Minimart. We also recommended that henceforth, the
Municipal Treasurer ensure that all official receipts/sales invoices issued on all
payments made by the Municipality shall be from the supplier who is also the
payee pursuant to internal control principles and COA Circular No. 2004-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant issue Confirmation,
and Demand Letters to those with unliquidated cash advances either by
returning the money advanced if unspent, or by presentation of required
supporting documents. We also recommended that a separate filing system be
made for the granting of cash advance and liquidation thereof. Such supporting
documents for the granting and liquidation of cash advances, including the proof
of exhaustion of all remedies to collect should be submitted to the Auditor.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare a reconciliation report of the reciprocal accounts and take up the
necessary adjustments in the books of accounts on a timely basis and perform
constant review of the transactions to avoid errors and omissions.

We recommended that Management conduct an actual physical count of
inventory of the breeding stocks and maintain an inventory records in
accordance with Section 17, Chapter 8 Volume 1 of the Government Accounting
Manual for LGU’s. Likewise, make a thorough investigation on those recipients
of the breeding stocks if these were already distributed and make an adjustment
duly supported to come up with correct balance of these accounts as of year-end.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant
for the immediate submission of the November to December 2020 disbursement
vouchers and supporting documents for proper accountability. Otherwise, the
Municipal Mayor suspend the payment of salaries of concerned officials
pursuant to Section 122(2) of PD No. 1445.

Strict compliance with Section 100 of PD No. 1445, Section 7.2.1(a) of
COA Circular No. 2009-006 and Government Accounting Manual is hereby
recommended.

We recommended that management conduct a complete physical
inventory count of properties, prepare and submit to the Audit Team Report on
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than
January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 266 of the Government Accounting
Manual (GAM) for LGU’s. Continued failure on the part of the officials
concerned to submit the reports mentioned shall automatically cause the
suspension of payment of their salaries until they shall have complied with the
requirements of the Commission by virtue of Par. 2, Section 122 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that Management strictly require the:
Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant, based on the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayers’ index card, a duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and
Municipal Accountant that upon receipt of the certified list from the Municipal Treasurer, record the expected revenue to be realized from the Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax at the beginning of each year.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts and immediately take up the adjustments in the books of accounts; and
conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure that discrepancies are immediately identified, reconciled and adjusted.

We recommended the Management:
designate a staff (pending designation of a GSO) from each concerned department to:
maintain stock cards to regularly monitor the delivery and issuance of inventories;
consolidate RIS and prepare a monthly SSMI to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books; and
conduct physical count of inventory items and prepare the RPCI, every six months, to be submitted to the Accounting Office for reconciliation of records and the Audit Team.
require the LDRRMO to submit the distribution lists for relief goods distributed to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for proper recording in the books.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary journal entries to recognize the public infrastructure assets in the books of the LGU totaling ₱48,441,366.92, as well as the related accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004, to present fairly the value of the assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
make the corresponding entry to reclassify the accounts to Construction-in Progress.
maintain a subsidiary ledger for each on-going infrastructure project to regularly monitor its progress and record the proper account upon 100% completion.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
record the accumulated cost of on-going projects using the Construction in Progress account as provided in Annex B of COA Circular 2015-009; and
recognize the asset account of the completed infrastructure projects as soon as the project is completed for fair presentation of the financial statements.
