We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant, Treasurer and Engineer to adhere to their duties and responsibilities, and the reporting guidelines and procedures on the Local Roads Asset Management of the Local Government Unit as provided by COA Circular 2015-008.

We recommended that Management release these warranties to the concerned suppliers or contractors when the following conditions exist:
for procurement of goods, after the lapse of the warranty period, or in the case of expendable supplies, after consumption thereof; Provided, however, That the supplies delivered are free from patent and latent defects and all the conditions imposed under the contract have been fully met; or
for procurement of infrastructure projects, after the lapse of one (1) year from the date of issuance of the Certificate of Final Acceptance by the Procuring Entity.

We recommended that the LGU, through the Accounting Department, recognize in the books the distribution of the sacks of rice by debiting the Government Equity account (since the books for CY 2020 were already closed) and crediting the Welfare Goods for Distribution inventory account for the amount of ₱7,410,000.00, to properly reflect the correct balance of the inventories in the financial statements. The journal entry voucher should invariably be supported with documents related to the distribution.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
assign personnel to handle full-time the preparation and maintenance of the subsidiary ledgers of all assets, starting with current acquisitions/procurements which data are still available;
prepare a lapsing schedule to facilitate the proper recording of depreciation of each PPE; and
consistently record the depreciation expense of the LGU’s assets to establish its correct value in the books.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Engineer to:
ensure the existence of the project signboard as soon as the project has been awarded, in the form and specifications required by the above cited circular; and
include as part of the regular documentary requirements pictures of the project showing the existence of the signboards at the project location.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the
concerned personnel to conduct complete annual physical count of PPE, reconcile the
Property and Accounting records after the physical count and submit to the Auditor
the RPCPPE for verification in accordance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS
for LGUs, Volume 1.

Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant must properly maintain
Stock Cards, Property Cards, SLC and PPELC and conduct periodic reconciliation
of property and accounting records to come up with accurate reports.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a) Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately derecognize the
abovementioned inventories and effect necessary adjustments in the
government equity account; and

b) Require its officials to strictly observe the prescribed accounting
guidelines and control procedures on the receipt, issuance and
storage/custodianship of supplies and materials. A Summary of Supplies
and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with copies of Requisition Issue Slip
(RIS) must be prepared and submitted to the Municipal Accountant as
basis for the recording of appropriate expenditure accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a) Require the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the
preparation and submission of the BRSs pursuant to COA Circular No.
96-011 and prepare subsidiary ledgers per bank account. A revised copy of
the general journal must be immediately submitted to the Office of the
Auditor for us to validate the adjustments made to the Cash in Bank
account;

b) Coordinate with the concerned SAs of the balances of fund transfers for
the dormant TF accounts. If found to be excess of costs for programs,
project and activities (PPAs) already implemented, the same must be
reverted back to the SA or the BTr; and

c) Consider maintaining only one bank account for GF which contains local
income collections and IRA of the LGU for a stronger internal control and
convenience in the disbursement process.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a) Require the Municipal Accountant to set up RPT and SET Receivables at the
beginning of the year to show actual collectibles of the Agency from its
taxpayers. Correcting entry must be effected to include the said accounts in
the FS. In addition, subsidiary ledgers must also be maintained for proper
evaluation and monitoring of accounts; and

b) Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a
duly certified list of RPT and SET taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year.

We recommended that Management:

a) Require the Municipal Accountant to trace all available records and
initiate inquiries with the former Municipal Accountant of the information
of the actual dormant accounts and prepare quarterly aging thereof; and

b) Facilitate the request for write-off of the dormant accounts so that the
same will be derecognized in the FS observing the procedures and
requirements set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant close the CIP accounts to its
appropriate asset accounts. Detailed review must be conducted of the actual
completion of corresponding assets for the computation of the depreciation expense.
Upon verification, adjustments of the unrecognized depreciation expenses in the prior
years must be made in the government equity accounts by recording the same under
prior period adjustment account.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant conduct a
detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled accounts.
Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to
present the correct balances of the affected accounts. Henceforth, exercise care in
recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the
balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same.

We recommended that Management:

a) Require the Municipal Accountant to comply with the following journal
entries relevant to transfer of unutilized LDRRMF:

- If there is an actual transfer during the year (unutilized balance of
current year)

General Fund
Dr. Transfers of Unspent DRRMF to the Trust Fund xxx
Cr. Cash in Bank xxx
Trust Fund
Dr. Cash in Bank xxx
Cr. Trust Liabilities-DRRMF xxx

- If transfer was made in the subsequent year (unutilized balance of
prior year)
General Fund
Dr. Prior Period Adjustment xxx
Cr. Cash In Bank xxx

Trust Fund
Dr. Cash in Bank xxx
Cr. Trust Liabilities-DRRMF xxx

Moreover, subsidiary ledgers for the accounts must be maintained so that
annual transfer from the GF to the TF could be monitored and recorded.
Likewise, the Accounting Office must exercise diligence in the recording of
financial transactions to achieve fair presentation of the account in the FS;
and

b) Require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly observe prompt transfer or
deposit to the TF of unutilized portion of LDRRMF (QRF and DRRMF-
MOOE).

We recommended that Management:

a) Facilitate immediate submission of the unsubmitted accounts to avoid
audit suspension thereof;

b) Require the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Office of the Auditor
the paid DVs on or before the 10th day of the ensuing month; and

c) Require the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Accounting Office all
the DVs, Official Receipts including the corresponding reports of each
month, on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month.

We recommended that Management:

a) Immediately stop the practice of granting CAs to its employees without
legally authorized specific purpose, otherwise, the transactions will be
disallowed in audit. Additional CAs must not be granted to officials with
unliquidated balances. Moreover, elected officials must not be granted CAs
except for their official travelling expenses only;

b) Direct its officers and employees with unliquidated CAs to immediately
submit their liquidation papers, otherwise, require the immediate
withholding of its salary in case of non-compliance; and

c) Require the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of
outstanding CAs and reconcile variance between the records in the books
and that of the CA report.

We recommended that Management:

a) Require all drivers and concerned officials to completely fill out the
Driver’s Trip Ticket in accordance with existing regulation for
transparency and accountability over the use of such government motor
vehicles;

b) Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel
Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the
Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and
evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month; and

c) Provide a complete list of serviceable municipal vehicles to ascertain the
reliability of fuel consumption and for validation purposes.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to adhere to the
required activities and timelines in the planning and budgeting of SEF. SEF budget
must be prepared and must conform to the schedule of activities cited in Section 5.3
of DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular No. 2017-01. Moreover, we reminded
Management that disbursement for SEF must not exceed shares from SET which is
50 percent of its actual collections.

We recommended that Management:

a) Require the concerned official to comply with the mandatory minimum
allotment for GAD which is five percent of the Agency’s total budget which
includes both IRA and other local income sources;

b) Require the GAD Focal Person to observe compliance with the submission
of the copy of GAD Plan and Budget to the DILG Regional Office on or
before March of the preceding year for appropriate review and approval.
DILG-approved GPB must include a certificate of approval from the
DILG Regional Office and must be incorporated in the year’s annual
budget to be enacted by the Sanggunian. Also, GAD Accomplishment
Report for CY 2020 must be submitted immediately to the Provincial
Government for the required review and consolidation;

c) Ensure that GAD PPAs are implemented and must be in accordance with
the approved GAD Plan and Budget; and

d) Ensure that disbursement for a particular class of transactions must only
be up to the alloted budget in accordance with Section 4(1) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant abide by the rules and
regulations of COA Circular No. 2012-002 for the recording of future cash donations.
Moreover, it is recommended that the concerned official promptly monitor the
transactions in the TF books most especially fund transfers and diligently apply
relevant accounting treatment.

We recommended that Management:

a) Require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP annually. It must
present the 30% allocation for QRF in lump-sum and the allocation for
disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness with details as to
projects and activities to be funded. The same must also include under a
separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the
unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;

b) Ensure that updated or supplemental LDRRMFIP be prepared for any
future changes or modification in the utilization of LDRRMF; and

c) Require the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMF utilization report in
accordance with the required format cited in COA Circular No. 2012-002
and promptly submit the same to the Office of the Auditor on or before the
15th day after the end of the month for monitoring and evaluation of the
fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance
Committee to immediately post in the LGU website and to at least three conspicuous
places within the locality the accumulated Monthly Reports on Fund Utilization and
Status of PPA Implementation of the Bayanihan Grant amounting to ₱7.83 million
and afterwards, notify in writing the DBM, House of Representatives, Senate of the
Philippines, House Committee on Appropriations, Senate Committee on Finance, and
other offices when these monthly reports were posted on the LGU’s website.

We recommended that Management:

a) Immediately send justification letter explaining the reason/s of realigning
20 percent DF to unnecessary charges made thereto which are not
beneficial during COVID-19 emergency. We reminded them that the
purpose of fund realignment was to enable the LGU to undertake critical,
urgent and appropriate disaster response aid and measures to curtail and
eliminate the threat of COVID-19;

b) Ensure that the 20 percent DF is optimally utilized to help achieve the
desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the
LGU; and

c) The Sanggunian enact/pass supplemental appropriation ordinance for
future budget realignment clearly indicating the sources of funds available
for appropriation, certified under oath by the local treasurer and local
accountant and attested by the local chief executive, and the various items
of appropriations affected and the reasons for the change.

We recommended that Management:

a) Direct the BAC to require the aforementioned suppliers to prepare or
submit the Mayor’s/Business Permit, OSS and the Income/Business Tax
Returns. After these documents were verified and examined, the same
must be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for the required validation;
and

b) Require the concerned Offices to submit the revised/corrected copies of
the already attached documentary requirements namely, POs, PRs, Sales
Invoices, ORs and RFQs, to avoid audit suspension of the involved
transactions.

We recommended that Management:

a) Strictly observe the timeline for submission of Financial Reports and FS in
accordance with the existing rules and regulations;

b) Submit the FS and other accounting reports that are already due for
submission; and

c) Identify and resolve the causes of delay and non-submission of the
mandatory FS and project reports with complete supporting
documents/schedules within the prescribed period, otherwise, institute
appropriate sanctions against the responsible officers for the continued
non-compliance with existing regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Accountant to strictly observe the reglementary period within which to submit the
DVs and their supporting documents in compliance with existing rules and
regulations to preclude the issuance of Notice of Suspension, as applicable.

We recommended that Management enforce strict compliance with the
provisions of IMA especially the schedules and timeline of implementation of the
activities/project.

We recommended that Management:

a) Furnish copies of the perfected contracts and POs together with the
complete supporting documents to the Audit Team within the prescribed
period in the ensuing year; and

b) Ensure that all the required information in the POs are completely filled-
up and impose sanctions against concerned personnel on late submission
of the contracts/POs to the Auditor.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and recommended to the Management to undertake the steps provided in the attached COA Circular 2020-006 in order to have a cleansing of the Property, Plant, and Equipment account of the LGU. Once the one-time cleansing is done require the –
Municipal General Services Office to:
Maintain an updated property cards per category of PPE;
Keep complete records of the PPE of the LGU;
Issue the necessary Property Acknowledgement Receipt once PPE is issued or assigned to an end-user; and
Undertake the annual physical inventory and reconcile with the records of Municipal Accountant’s Office.
Municipal Accountant’s Office to:
Maintain an updated Property, Plant and Equipment Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for Land; and
Record and disclose the PPE based on the recognition criteria and disclosure requirement set under IPSAS 17 and Government Auditing Manual for LGUs.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the execution of the required Deeds of Donation between the Municipal Government of San Fernando and the Provincial Government of Bukidnon and make adjusting entries to correct the recording of two projects amounting to ₱29.699 million pending its approval in order to present fairly the financial statements of the LGU.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to make adjusting entries to correct the recording of transfers of PPE accounts amounting to ₱6.958 million pending approval of the Sanggunian Bayan and the receiving Barangays and to ensure that all recorded transfers of assets are duly approved in accordance with Section 381 of Republic Act 7160 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the LGU.

We recommended and management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately record the purchased DRR-related Equipment in accordance with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2019-71 and IPSAS 23 to present fairly the balance of the PPE account.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to verify each item charged under the “Trust Liability – DRRMF” account and to prepare a Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) together with its supporting documents including the working papers for the correcting entries and submit to the Audit Team for further verification.
We also recommended to the management to ensure that proper accounting treatment must be adapted to financial transactions relating to “Trust Liability – DRRMF” Account to establish strong inventory control mechanism of the LGU.

We recommended to the management to instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to:
Exert more efforts in the retrieval of prior years’ records to identify the accountable officers/employees/debtors or concerned agencies liable for the unaccounted balance of ₱1.639 million and file request for authority to write-off for claims which could no longer be accounted; and
Send demand letters to the identified accountable officer/employee or recipient agency of other receivables.

We recommended and management agreed to require the Municipal Accounting Office to verify each item under the “Motor Vehicles” account and if warranted, reclassify erroneously recorded items to the proper PPE accounts including the accumulated depreciation.

We recommended to the management to require:
The GSO to prepare and submit, weekly, the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (together with the RIS) as basis of the Accounting Office in recording its utilization in the proper expense or asset account;
The accounting Office to record each payment for supplies and materials not directly as Construction in Progress or expense but as inventory account and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards per stock number; and
The GSO to conduct inventory in order to have reconciliation of its records with that of the Accounting Office.

We reiterated our recommendation to the management to:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to exert efforts to locate the disbursement vouchers and submit to the Municipal Accounting Office for recording.
Require the Municipal Accountant to account the unrecorded disbursement voucher upon receipt, and submit the same to the audit team for audit and custody.

We recommended to the management to:
Submit justification for the payment of PBB to employees and officials with unliquidated cash advances.;
Require the HRMO, in cooperation with the Municipal Accountant, to strictly observe the guidelines in determining eligible individuals in the granting of Performance-Based Bonus.; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to demand immediate liquidation of long-outstanding cash advances and implement strict observance of the prescribed period in liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended to the management to:
Formulate internal policy in the payment of mobile charges on top of the monthly subscriptions and to require the concerned employees to personally shoulder the items that are not necessary in the performance of their duties.
Require the Municipal Accountant to demand submission of all pages of monthly bills and the previous billing statements in case there are arrears and to exclude in the payment of monthly bills all items that, upon review, are not necessary in the performance of duty.

We recommended to the management to submit the Certified List of beneficiaries as defined under DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 14 which will be the basis for the determination whether only those determined by the LGU as eligible for ESP were actually paid. Also, we maintain our recommendation to submit authorization letters for beneficiaries whose cash subsidies were received by other individuals and justification for the inclusion of the names of deceased individuals in the payrolls.

We recommended to the management to ensure that subsequent purchases of goods are supported with Activity / Project Designs and require the GSO to support all issuances for distribution to end-users/beneficiaries with Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) and maintain complete records of the same as required under COA Circular 2014-002 to ensure that all the goods procured and received by the LGU for social amelioration were actually and fairly received by intended families.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management: 

(a) require accountable personnel to judiciously liquidate cash advances after the purposes for which they were granted are consummated; 


(b) refrain from granting cash advances to personnel who failed to completely liquidate prior cash advance; and 



(c) ensure that sufficient controls are consistently implemented in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.




We also recommended that Management: 

(a) stop granting cash advances to employees not designated as Special Disbursing Officer; and 

(b) stop granting cash advance in excess of the Special Disbursing Officer’s authorized limit.
observations.managementAction: The Management concurred with the audit recommendations and assured compliance in the rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Inventory Team to: 

(a) conduct complete annual physical inventory-taking of PPE by type; 




(b) submit a complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); and 



(c) conduct a periodic reconciliation of the PPE balances between Accounting records and CGSO records to reduce to minimum, if not zero out, the variance noted.

We recommended that the City Accountant: 

(a) provide depreciation for all depreciable Property, Plant and Equipment and continue providing depreciation for condemned properties until their de-recognition in the books for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements; 

(b) coordinate with the City Engineer’s Office with regard to the status of the infrastructure projects to ensure accurate and timely recording of these transactions; and 

(c) effect the de-recognition of the disposed Property, Plant and Equipment in the books for a more reliable balances of the PPE accounts at year-end.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to fully comply with all the existing rules and regulations set forth by CAccO and COA and gave assurance that the audit recommendations will be adopted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant: 

(a) evaluate the propriety and validity of the recorded Accounts Payables and those found valid should be immediately settled or paid; 

(b) revert to the unappropriated surplus those payables determined to be without valid/legal claims; and 


(c) henceforth, ensure that only payables with valid and adequate documents are recognized in the books.

We recommended that Management: 

(a) establish an effective waste management strategy by ensuring the active participation of all stakeholders by allowing them to be involved in programs and projects so that they will be more  aware of the need to maintain the cleanliness of their surrounding and protect the environment, but most importantly, to incorporate the proper attitude and practice in their daily lives;  

(b) consider the possibility of passing an Ordinance on the collection of wastage fees  from  the business establishments to increase the City’s resources to cover the payment of tipping fees; and 

(c) advise and recommend to the 35 Punong Barangays the imposition of wastage fees from residents as share on the hauling expenses being solely borne by the City, given that bulk of the residual wastes being transported to the CTS is barangay wastes.
observations.managementAction: The Officer-In-Charge  of City Environment and Natural Resources Office commented that a proposed Ordinance imposing fees from the disposal of residual waste to the  sanitary landfill based on “ Polluter Pays Principle” is undergoing further study.  She mentioned that the proposed ordinance is already included in the 2019-2022 Executive-Legislative Agenda.  In addition, she stated that the proposed “Unified Barangay Tax Code” which includes provisions on the charging of garbage collection fee by the barangay on households is already with the Sanguniang Panlungsod for referral to the proper authorities.

The City DRRMO Officer assured the Audit Team of continuous dedication and performance in the field of disasters and calamities not only to the Fernandinos but to the other LGUs as well who need their immediate response.  In this regard, management agreed with our recommendation on the upgrading of the Organization and Composition staffing pattern of the said office to assure that it will be adequately manned by competent personnel as mandated by regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the City Local School Board: 

(a) use the correct format in the preparation of the SEF budget as mandated in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017; and 

(b) utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the Joint Circular.

We also recommended that the LSB establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF across schools to ensure its efficient and effective use and in accordance with its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

(a) require the CEO to complete the remaining projects in the AIP for CY 2019; 

(b) direct the City Engineering Office and City Planning and Development Office to conduct thorough site inspection and investigation to prevent suspension of work due to right-of-way issues; and 

(c) ensure the immediate completion of all on-going projects which already incurred delays, as well as require the City Engineering Office to conduct regular monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure that infrastructure projects are accomplished in accordance with the terms of the contract.
observations.managementAction:
Management commented that the right-of-way issue is an isolated case since the project is intended to improve an existing barangay road utilized by the public. Said problem only arose after the installation of signage informing the public of the temporary road closure to pave way for the construction of the canal and its improvement. Series of meetings and negotiations were made to facilitate the matter. 

Management ensured that the project will be completed the soonest time possible.

Management also submitted copies of suspension orders for projects with delay in completion. Other observations were considered and favorably taken by management in the implementation of the 20% DF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: 

(a) direct the GPBP focal person of the respective implementing offices to improve monitoring of projects to ensure timely completion and delivery of services thereof; and 

(b) consider returning the unutilized balance to the source agency, if additional expenses for the specific program are no longer needed.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with our recommendations and will observe timelines in future NGA-funded projects/ programs.

The City Treasurer commented that tax mapping activities are regularly being conducted by the city officials concerned to improve collection efficiency and assured the Audit Team that targets set during the year will be positively realized. Further, the Geographical Information System (GIS) is fully employed by the City in all collecting departments, hence efficiency in collections was attained. Online payments were also accepted through the use of GIS.

The City Accountant commented that the telephone service company provides rebates on bills payment.  She informed the Audit Team that she will file a request for rebates by way of deductions on the payment for succeeding bills.  She also stated that adequate internal control on the billing and payment thereof will be adopted to ensure accuracy and reliability of transactions.

The City Accountant stated that a MOA between the City and the BFP has already been executed awaiting the signature of concerned officials to legalize the practice of collecting fire code fee by the city and that a written request to the Bureau of Fire on the status of BFP shares is already underway.

The legislative department stated that they currently follow management policy on cellphone allowance which is fixed at ₱1,500.00 for department heads and ₱3,500.00 for SP members.  The excess amount will be shouldered by the concerned SP member.

The CGSO office provided more data showing date of PO creation to date documents were scanned and submitted to the Accounting Office for pre-audit. With this information, of the 12-25 months needed from PO date to date of payment, only 2-17 months is spent at the CGSO office and the remaining period could be taken from the pre-audit and payment process. Management agreed to adhere with all the recommendations for the faster and even shorter procurement process without sacrificing the provisions of R.A. No. 9184. In addition, owing to the COVID-19 pandemic, management was able to employ negotiated procurement which shortened the time for the entire procurement process.

observations.recommendation:
We commended Management for the feat of being one of the top ideal LCPCs in the Province of Pampanga. We still recommended that the LCE require that: 

(a) the LCPC official maximize the utilization of child care and protection activities that are relevant, doable and operational; 


(b) allocate the required appropriation of one percent of IRA as mandated by law; and 

(c) coordinate and facilitate the integration of the LCPC activities and mainstream the same to other departments’ PPAs so that the one per cent allocation thereof will be fully utilized.
observations.managementAction: Management continued to implement programs in line with child-care which are doable and operational as required by RA 9344.

The concerned heads acknowledge the observations noted and committed to instruct the JOs assigned in their respective offices to report to our office for verification purposes and assured compliance with the recommendations noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the: 


(a) City Accountant to: 

ensure that required documents are attached and complete before payment is made; and 

(ii) submit to COA the aforementioned unsubmitted documents for review; 

(b) City Engineer to submit, for evaluation a written explanation/ justification for the: 

absence of inspection report; and 

(ii) discrepancies noted in the delivered project as compared with plans; and 

(c) all concerned divisions implement necessary controls to protect government funds from loss and wastage in the delivery of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: Additional documents were submitted to support payment for the construction of the Civic Center Park. Justifications were also given on the discrepancies noted in the delivered project. Management ensured that the same will not be repeated in the future.

observations.recommendation: We commended the City for its regular compliance with the revenue regulations cited above. The maintenance of separate records for the special accounts of the City facilitated the review of taxes withheld and remitted to BIR.

We commended Management for its continuous and timely withholding and remittance of contributions and payments to the GSIS in compliance with the requirements set forth under Republic Act 8291, also known as the GSIS Act of 1997.
We commended the City Accountant for being compliant with the deduction and prompt remittance of premium contributions to PhilHealth in accordance with the National Health Insurance Act of 2013.

We commended the City Accountant for being compliant with the deduction and remittance of premium contributions, loan amortizations to Pag-IBIG and improving the ledgers for the Due to Pag-IBIG account for ease in monitoring, reconciliation and determination of the actual amounts due to the HDMF.

We have recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

Recognize fund transfers as receivable instead of an outright expense as basis in monitoring the utilization




Submit the documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the rights and obligations of the source and implementing agencies




Likewise, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct all concerned department heads to ensure that all fund transfers made by the LGU are properly monitored.
observations.managementAction:
Financial assistances to barangays were already recorded as “Due from LGUs”, instead of outright expenses, following the audit recommendation.   

Liquidation reports were now being required for financial assistances to barangays. 




The City Accountant, being in charge of keeping the books of the barangays, monitored its implementation.
observations.reason:
At the end of the year, the City Accountant was able to recognize total assistance to barangays under the account “Due from LGUs” totaling ₱6,095,866.66.

Not Implemented

The financial assistances made to barangays were usually supported only with official receipts and barangays’ request through resolutions. Nonetheless, documents supporting the actual utilization of fund in accordance with the intended purpose were not provided nor attached to the liquidation reports. 

Not Implemented

No specific monitoring mechanism was established nor even simple status report were being maintained.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LCE require the concerned department heads to formulate guidelines and policies considering the nature, eligibility of beneficiaries and the amount of financial assistance and such other measures that would facilitate the effective and efficient implementation of the AICS program of the LGU

Likewise, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that all financial assistance under AICS will undergo the process of assessment by the CSWDO.
observations.managementAction:
The CSWD Officer has drafted internal guidelines based on the existing procedures that the Office is following.




All AICS grants were processed at the CSWDO, following the audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
Guidelines on the Implementation of AICS and Educational Assistance/Subsidy was adopted as approved by the LCE on April 15, 2021.  

Implemented

Requests for financial assistances through the AICS are now required to undergo the assessment by the CSWDO, for recommendation to the LCE.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive: 

Direct the City Accountant and Treasurer to refund and remit to the Bureau of Treasury the collection for documentary stamps and savings from implemented projects




Instruct the Accounting Office to maintain the required subsidiary ledgers in order to establish the correct balances for each Philhealth Packages as basis for utilization and payment of professional fees.




Prioritize the implementation of the projects funded from National Agencies so that benefits derived therefrom could be enjoyed by the constituents and to avoid reversion of said funds to the National Government
observations.managementAction:
The balance at year-end pertaining to the collection of documentary stamp tax for remittance to BIR and Share from Building Permits for remittance to the BTr are current and shall be remitted in January 2021.




Payment of professional fees due to CHO personnel have been processed. The balance at year-end pertain to supplies needed for the delivery of services. 

The City Accounting Office will communicate to the CHO of available balances of ₱567,355.10 under the TB DOTS, and ₱496,273.44 under the Animal Bite capitation fund. 

Management has substantially implemented projects pertaining to fund transfers from NGAs, such as, the Greening Program funded by the DBM, Supplemental Feeding of DSWD and the Performance Challenge Fund, Bayanihan Grant to Cities, and the Social Amelioration Program by DSWD.
observations.reason:
Collections were subsequently remitted in January 2021. 

Whereas, savings from implemented projects funded by the DILG for the amount of ₱478,229.61 and DepEd amounting to ₱27,417.31 have been communicated to respective offices, awaiting response.   

Not Implemented

While some payments were already made, the City Accountant still failed to establish separate subsidiary ledgers for Maternal Care Package, Animal Bite and Outpatient Anti-Tuberculosis Directly Observed Treatment Short-Course which prevented immediate implementation of these funds especially that medical supplies and equipment may be urgently needed at the CHO. 



Implemented

The remaining amount of Due to NGAs account pertain to those that were recently received by the LGU this year.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LCE: 

Enjoin the concerned City officials, particularly the CPDC and the BAC to ensure that procurement activities are promptly carried out through effective identification and management of foreseen circumstances that affect timely delivery of the identified PPAs.




Direct the City Engineer to strictly monitor the execution of projects and submit required supporting documentation should there be delays in construction of infrastructure projects.




Prioritize the inclusion of needed medical and office equipment for the Dialysis Center and CHO Special Services in the City’s Annual Appropriation and Procurement Plan.
observations.managementAction:
The management was able to implement PPAs under 20% DF despite suspension of works during COVID-19.




Management was able to make sure that delays in the construction of infrastructure projects were valid and properly justified. In fact, liquidated damages were collected accordingly.   
 
The Dialysis Center has not been fully operational, but Management has started to occupy the second level of Marcos Building for CHO special services, and as temporary office of the City Contact Tracing team and nurses under the DOH NDP Project. 

There is also an ongoing project: Construction of pathway/rampage for the City Dialysis Center which is funded by DPWH.
observations.reason:
For CY 2020, management was able to effectively implement majority of its PPAs under 20% DF with utilization rate of 64.36 compared to last year’s rate of 25.93.  The utilization rate however includes those re-aligned PPAs for COVID-19 totaling ₱100,859,269.69.

Implemented

Five projects were suspended due to the imposition of ECQ and localized restrictions brought by the pandemic. These were subsequently resumed and completed within the year. 

Not Implemented

Per inquiry and on-site inspection, the intended Dialysis room and X-ray room were rather bare. Review of approved APP and Budgets provide no appropriations identified specifically for the needed equipment and facilities for this purpose. 

Management said that needed medical equipment for the Dialysis Center will be provided by a private entity through a partnership. Audit team has requested documents, such as MOA, but none was provided.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LSB provide justification on charging the above-mentioned expenditures totalling ₱3,824,288.18 in the SEF which are not allowed.  Thereafter, ensure that all claims against the SEF were not appropriated and charged to DepEd.




We have also recommended that the LSB identify in the budget the specific projects for capital outlays in order to efficiently and effectively implement the same during the budget year.
observations.managementAction:
Management ensured that disbursement of expenditures related to travel, meals and snacks of coaches, trainers, student-athletes for the Region 1 Athletic Association (R1AA) were not appropriated and paid out of DepEd funds. 




AIP and Budget have been prepared in more detail as to particulars, such as location/ name of school and a brief description of the PPA.
observations.reason:
Management ensured that disbursement of expenditures related to travel, meals and snacks of coaches, trainers, student-athletes for the Region 1 Athletic Association (R1AA) were not appropriated and paid out of DepEd funds. 

AIP and Budget have been prepared in more detail as to particulars, such as location/ name of school and a brief description of the PPA.

Implemented

Expenses relating to the conduct of R1AA were not appropriated and charged to DepEd funds as certified by the Schools Division Accountant. Hence, these were funded under the Sports Development allocation of the SEF.  

As to the expenses related to Alternative Learning System, management had discontinued the charging of these expenditures to the SEF.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Head of Procuring Entity:

Require all the Department Heads and end-users to be specific on their Project Procurement Monitoring Plans (PPMPs), specifically, for timelines in order to have a basis in the consolidation of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP);

Instruct the members of the BAC to prepare judiciously and meticulously the APP of the agency; and




Direct the BAC to submit the APP to the GPPB as required.

observations.reason:

Not Implemented

Implemented

Management was able to comply with the submission of APP to GPPB on time.

observations.managementAction:
The City has continuously committed to hire the best talents to join its workforce. From 478 permanent positions in 2018, the City now has 534 filled-up permanent positions as of December 31, 2020. Most of these were staff-level positions, as well as assistant department heads and City department heads. 

Management contended that during budget preparation, as long as the plantilla position is approved, PS allocation will be appropriated although vacant, especially that these positions may be filled at any time during the year. 




The Human Resource Department ensured that they have complied with the requirements of notices and publication of vacant positions.

Majority of 28 personnel which were hired as permanent 1st level positions were from job orders and COS, such as Administrative Aides I to III, Assessment Clerks, Administrative Assistants, Laboratory Aides, Utility Workers, Security Guards, among others. 

Several positions whether permanent and COS were hired in 2020 to further supplement needed staff for COVID-19 response. 




Designation orders were renewed accordingly, following the audit recommendation. 

Department heads / assistant department heads positions were filled by qualified personnel either internally or by hiring external applicants.
observations.reason:
The LGU was able to significantly increase its workforce hiring 56 original-permanent positions including eight department heads and assistant department heads positions from 2019 to 2020. 



Not Implemented

While Management contended that they cannot disregard non-inclusion of appropriations for positions with approved plantilla, in previous years, however, it has shown that the management   had no intention to fill all the approved positions of the LGU. The unfilled positions were too significant to cause non-utilization of the fund that could have been used to fund additional PPAs. 

Implemented

The team acknowledged the effort of LGU to fill these plantilla positions by promoting some of its Job Orders who had rendered significant number of years in the City.



Implemented

Medical Officer III (2), Nurse I (5), Pharmacist I, Psychologist I, Radiologic Technologist, Sanitation Inspectors (2) were among the positions filled in 2020. 

Implemented

Previously designated OICs in the CHO, LEBDO, GSO, Treasury, among others were appointed as full-fledged employees as department heads or assistant department heads.  

Likewise, department heads for GSO, HRMO and LYDO were filled-up from external hires.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipal mayor direct the municipal accountant to submit the complete CY 2019 year-end financial statements enumerated in PPSAS 1 with supporting schedules on the deadline set by the COA. We also recommended for strict compliance with COA Circular Nos. 2015-002 dated March 9, 2015 and 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010, otherwise withhold the salaries of the concerned personnel pursuant to Section 41 (3) of PD 1445.

We recommended the refund of the excess payment of honoraria by the claimants. The Municipal Accountant should immediately stop paying full amount of honoraria to Sangguniang Bayan Members who is unable to attend the required four (4) regular monthly sessions. A complete attendance in the required regular sessions will entitle them to full honoraria. Moreover, in Macatangay vs. The Chairman of the Commission on Audit (GR No. L-38728, September 30, 1962), the Supreme Court took judicial notice of the fact that elective officials rendition of service does not follow a certain or definite work schedule as they are deemed in service of their constituents regardless of time and place. This was also reiterated in the DILG Legal Opinion No. 15, S. 2014 dated April 22, 2014.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 Explanation letter with supporting documents dated May 16, 2019 which was received by COA on May 22, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management ask the contractor to issue a certification to the Municipality of San Fernando indicating the payments made with the name of the projects based on the issued official receipts. We also recommended that henceforth, the municipal treasurer ensure that all official receipts issued by the contractors/suppliers are in the name of the Municipality of San Fernando who is the payor and ensure that official receipts data are all accurate pursuant to Sections 123 and 124 of PD 1445.

Accountable officers with outstanding balances be required to immediately submit their liquidation report/documents pursuant to Section 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. The Accounting Section be required to record the liquidation documents submitted by the concerned Accountable Officers so that the corresponding expense or related accounts will be booked up. Management should impose sanctions on Accountable Officers who fail to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period thru the withholding of their salaries as provided under Section 5.1.3 of the above Circular, especially those with unliquidated balances totaling more than ₱50,000.00 and those with cash advances which have remained outstanding for more than one (1) year. Management should strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that an inventory committee should be created to conduct the annual physical inventory of all assets under PPE and a report therein promptly prepared. In addition, when the physical inventory is already available, it shall be reconciled with the (a) PPE ledger card, (b) control accounts and (c) property cards. If discrepancies exist, it should be immediately verified and necessary adjustments in the books then prepared to ensure fairness of the accounts presentation in the financial statements. Henceforth, the Municipality should strictly observe the provisions of Sec. 119, 120 and 124, Vol. 1 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System. We reiterated our recommendation that management should exert efforts to provide for the depreciation of PPE accounts in order to present accurate and reliable balances of fixed assets and expense accounts in the financial statements. We reiterated our recommendation that municipal officials concerned should initiate the titling of the parcels of land owned by the LGU in order to validate the ownership thereof. Further, the Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers for the land account and exert effort in coordination with the Municipal Assessor to locate the source documents pertaining to the acquisition thereof for titling and valuation purposes.
