observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the LCE
instruct:
a. The City Accountant and OIC-City Budget Officer to reconcile the
differences as presented in Table 1 and to submit the same for further
verification. Thereafter, require them to reconcile their records
regularly; and
b. The City Accountant to restate the affected 2018 financial statements
pertaining to the above-mentioned observations for fair presentation.
We have also recommended that the City improve their processes
particularly procurement and disbursement. Likewise, establish cut-off date
as part of their annual closing activity to ensure that all transactions are
properly recognized in the correct period.
Management comment: The City Accountant explained that as of this writing,
she and the City Budget Officer are currently working hand in hand to determine
the differences in detail. They assured the audit team that a detailed report of
those differences will be submitted to the auditor’s office supported with the
necessary documents.
Auditor’s rejoinder: The audit team maintained that the detailed RAAOs and
supporting documents be submitted immediately so that timely evaluation and
cross-referencing of these transactions can be established as to when these were
actually charged and recorded in the proper budget year.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Local Chief
Executive direct the City Treasurer to:
1. Update his cashbooks daily and footings shall not only be limited to
total collections and deposits but more so to the balances reflected at
the end of each day and month. Further, adjustments shall be written
in ink and be included in the total balances;
2. Provide details of each line item in the cashbooks in order to facilitate
identification and nature of transactions; and
3. Ensure collections be deposited intact within the prescribed period;
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the
accounting and treasury office to reconcile their records regularly to avoid
huge discrepancies.
Management Comment: The City Treasurer said that it is inevitable that daily
deposits do not coincide with collections especially for the peak months of
December and January, wherein there were instances that taxpayers make use of
bank-to-bank arrangement or direct deposits to the account of the City. This in
turn, would overstate deposits compared with collections on a given day, as they
would only be notified of said deposits several days later, only at such time will
official receipts be issued and collections be recorded, thus delay in recording.
Likewise, there were instances that check collections were subsequently
returned by bank as “NSF”, making deposits seemingly not intact. The City
Treasurer’s Office is looking into the possibility of not accepting checks as
payments.
For the discrepancies between the Treasurer’s Cashbook and the
Accounting Ledgers, both offices will reconcile their records and make necessary
adjustments.
Additionally, they have tried to maximize the use of the WeAccess facility
of the Land Bank of the Philippines as their depositor, however, it is frequently
inaccessible.
Auditor’s rejoinder: While the audit team recognizes the occurrence of the
instances as mentioned by Management, we noted that the main cause of the delay
in recording and the discrepancies between cashbooks and accounting records was
the untimely receipt of bank statements and supporting documents.
The audit team further recommended to raise this concern to their
servicing bank the difficulty of accessing Land Bank WeAccess portal, in order to
facilitate reviewing of direct deposits made by taxpayers and identification of
book/bank errors.
Likewise, we emphasized that the City improve the functionality of the
existing computerized system on tax collection to facilitate retrieval of collectors’
balances on hand.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management:
1. Exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables such as sending
demand letters to last known addresses to private individuals and
employees; confirmation letters to various organizations whether
NGOs/POs, Government agencies; and provide a status to the City
Auditor’s Office.
2. Exert efforts to locate the documents to prove the claims.
3. Establish/maintain and apply sound internal control procedures on
the recording and collection of receivables.
4. Conduct further assessment as to collectibility or impairment.
Thereafer, provide provision for doubtful accouns for the accounts
determined to have a no and/or low collection rate.
Management Comment: The City Accountant explained that some of the
accounts had originated from various dates prior to the migration from OGAS to
NGAS and that most of the balances do not have supporting documents at hand,
but they are trying their best to investigate by digging into old files, journals and
reports as basis for further action. They are also looking into the possibility of
requesting for write-off, as applicable, especially that of Due from NGAs
amounting to P3.1M, Receivables from Trust Fund amounting to P2.67M,
Receivables from various NGAs and Organizations, among others.
Further, the City Administrator committed to look into the various loans
(e.g. housing, rice, materials, livelihood) granted to individual beneficiaries,
barangays, NGOs and private organizations and will send collection notices and
shall schedule dialogues as needed, to warrant collectability of the accounts.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Local Chief
Executive:
1. Direct the GSO and City Accountant to maintain the required stock
cards and supply inventory cards, respectively; and
2. Strengthen the function and responsibilities of the Physical Inventory
Committee by requiring them to conduct semestral physical count of
all inventories and submit the RPCI to the Accounting Office for
reconciliation with the reported inventory balances per books and to
the Office of the Auditor.
Management Comment: The GSO expressed her commitment to see to it that
physical count of properties shall be more comprehensive to include inventories,
but at the same time voiced out her concern that, if possible, Physical Inventory
Committee should not only be limited to the staff from GSO and Accounting, but
rather to include staff from other offices such as City Engineering, Assessor’s
Office and Legal, this is due to the voluminous list of properties owned by the
City.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management:
1. Strictly adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines on property
management particularly Section 114 of the MNGAS. Prepare and
maintain appropriate property cards and property ledger cards.
2. Properly fill-out the needed information in the physical inventory
reports and property/ledger cards with substantial details for easy
tagging and reconciliation.
3. Reconcile the records of PPE accounts between the Accounting Office
and General Services Office and the RPCPPE, and adjust
accordingly.
4. Strengthen the function and responsibilities of the Physical Inventory
Committee.
5. Facilitate the transfer of ownership of the School Buildings, Health
and Wellness Center and Multi-Purpose Hall to the appropriate
agencies upon proper authority and documentation and make these
pro-forma entries to adjust the City’s Property, Plant and Equipment
account:
Donations xxx
Accumulated Depreciation-
School Buildings xxx
Hospital and Health Centers xxx
Other Structures xxx
School Buildings xxx
Hospital and Health Centers xxx
Other Structures xxx
6. Expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties to free up storage
spaces, prevent further deterioration of PPE and generate additional
income from sale.
Management Comment: The Accountant mentioned that reconciliation of the
PPE records per ledger with that of GSO remains a continuing task for both
offices from the time when the conduct of annual physical count and preparation
of RPCPPE was included in the audit recommendations in 2016.
The Management likewise mentioned that the listed Buildings and Other
Structures held for use by Barangays were not yet transferred to the actual users
because the barangay officials did not accept responsibility for its eventual repairs
and maintenance. The Administrator, however, signified that they will look into
the issue and will act on the recommendation of transferring the ownership of
assets by way of donation.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City
Accountant to:
a. Recognize fund transfers as receivable instead of an outright
expense as basis in monitoring the utilization; and
b. Submit the documentary requirements as prescribed in COA
Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the rights and obligations of the
source and implementing agencies.
Likewise, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all
the concerned departments to ensure that all fund transfers made by the
LGU are properly monitored.
Management comment: The City Accountant mentioned that it has been their
practice to recognize this as expense but she signified compliance as
recommended by the audit team. She also explained that although no direct
monitoring was being made as to the implementation by the barangays of the
City’s funded projects, she assured that the barangays were able to fully
implement these projects in compliance with the purpose to which the fund was
transferred, as she was able to see the transactions of the barangays having the
duty of preparing the barangay financial reports. She provided however exception
in the full implementation of projects in the case of Barangay Santiago Norte. The
fund remained not fully utilized.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Local Chief
Executive require the concerned department heads to formulate guidelines
and policies considering the nature, eligibility of beneficiaries and the
amount of financial assistance and such other measures that would facilitate
the effective and efficient implementation of the AICS program of the LGU.
Likewise, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive
ensure that all financial assistance under AICS will undergo the process of
assessment by the City Social Welfare and Development Office.
Management comment: The management admitted that indeed, no specific
policies and procedures were established by the City Government for AICS.
Although the CSWD Officer had mentioned that it had initially submitted
proposal, the audit observation paved way for the prioritization of establishment
of policy especially that the bulk of transactions of the LGU concerns the grant of
financial assistance/AICS. The management assured that the policies and
procedures will be in line with the laws, rules and regulations as mentioned by the
audit team.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the City Accountant and Treasurer to refund and remit to
the Bureau of Treasury the collection for documentary stamps and
savings from implemented projects;
b. Instruct the Accounting Office to maintain the required subsidiary
ledgers in order to establish the correct balances for each
Philhealth Packages as basis for utilization and payment of
professional fee; and
c. Prioritize the implementation of the projects funded from National
Agencies so that benefits derived therefrom could be enjoyed by
the constituents and to avoid reversion of said funds to the
National Government.
Management comment: The management concurred with the observation of the
audit team especially that the fund may be reverted to the National Government if
projects are not implemented immediately. The management said that they will go
over with the specifics of the funds transferred from NGAs and cause the
immediate implementation of these projects especially the ₱5.1M fund from the
DILG.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Local Chief
Executive:
1. Enjoin the concerned City officials, particularly the City Planning and
Development Council and the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure
that procurement activities are promptly carried out through effective
identification and management of foreseen circumstances that affect
timely delivery of the identified PPAs.
2. Direct the City Engineer to strictly monitor the execution of projects
and submit required supporting documentation should there be
delays in construction of infrastructure projects.
3. Prioritize the inclusion of needed medical and office equipment for the
Dialysis Center and City Health Office Special Services in the City’s
Annual Appropriation and Procurement Plan.
Management comment: Management acknowledged the audit observation and
recommendation. The CPDC added that past events on changes in leadership and
the national mid-term election schedules really intervened with the planning,
proper scheduling and eventual implementation of PPAs. However, for the
ensuing year, they have taken necessary steps to improve the crafting of their
annual plans and budgetary priorities.
As to the Dialysis Center, the City Heath Officer mentioned that there
were still necessary steps to be done in order for the said center be ready for use,
and that these were delayed due to the pandemic. Nonetheless, management
assured that they will prioritize the inclusion of needed additional improvement
and required equipment in their plans and budget.

We have recommended that the LSB provide justification on charging
the above-mentioned expenditures totalling ₱3,824,288.18 in the SEF which
are not allowed. Thereafter, ensure that all claims against the SEF were not
appropriated and charged to DepEd.
We have also recommended that the LSB identify in the budget the
specific projects for capital outlays in order to efficiently and effectively
implement the same during the budget year.
Lastly, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin
offices and committees to coordinate and provide relevant information
particularly for financial which is essential in planning and decision making.
Management comment: The LSB, particulary, the City Division Superintendent
has yet to submit justification especially that those expenses were not incurred
during her time as she just recently assumed office. Nevertheless, she assured
compliance with the audit recommendations. On the other hand, the management,
during the exit conference was pleased to be informed of the availability of funds
especially that the schools have been requesting funding of projects in line with
the pandemic.
Auditor’s rejoinder: The audit team, however, maintained that the expenditures
to be spent should not contravene with the guidelines of the uses of fund.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management:
1. Strictly enforce the rental rates prescribed by the City Market Code,
particularly on the Imposition of Fees.
2. Execute and submit Contracts of Lease between stallholders and City
Government in order to properly enforce rights and obligations
between parties.
3. Prioritize the rehabilitation and repair of the major portions of the
shopping mall in order to provide a safer and convenient location for
stallholders, vendors and customers alike.
Management comment: The City Vice Mayor said that they will re-evaluate the
existing Market Code, specifically, on the imposition of rental fees for market
stalls, taking into consideration the prevailing rates and the guidelines set forth by
the DPWH.
Further, the Management assured to look into the issuance of Contracts of
Lease between the LGU and the occupants and furnish the same to the audit team.

The Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE) should:
1. Require all the Department Heads and end-users to be specific on
their PPMPs, specifically, for timelines in order to have a basis in the
consolidation of the APP;
2. Instruct the members of the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare
judiciously and meticulously the Annual Procurement Plan of the
agency; and
3. Direct the BAC to submit the APP to the GPPB as required.
We have further recommended that the HOPE ensure that awards of
contract must be made within the allowable time in order not to hamper the
operation of the LGU. Likewise, we have recommended that the City
determine the specific activity to be undertaken for each of the PPAs to
efficiently achieve their goals for the benefit of their constituents.
Management comment: Management agreed with the observations and agreed
with the recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) and Technical Working Group (TWG):
a. To include in their post-qualification a comparative evaluation
showing the number of projects with the same equipment and key
personnel pledged and assigned by the same lowest calculated bidder
for different projects with simultaneous or overlapping
implementation period.
b. To formulate policies on the sufficiency and availability of equipment
pledged by the contractors with regard to projects simultaneously
awarded to the same contractors to avoid delay in project
implementation.
c. To require the City Engineering office to provide status of on-going
implementation of contracts with the City Government by the bidders
and utilize the same to validate the submitted list of on-going projects.
d. To extend the comparison with other government agencies before
declaring the bids as responsive.
e. To set limits for the number of contracts to be handled by key
personnel, in order to ensure reasonableness of performance without
sacrificing quality and timeliness of project completion.

We recommended that the City Government submit latest updates on the
status of completion of the above-mentioned infrastructure projects. Also, if
the same were not completed within the target completion date, provide
justification and supporting documents for validation and verification by the
audit team.
Thereafter, we have recommended for strict monitoring and
supervision of projects to ensure completion on time.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned
officials to expedite the completion of said project in order to comply with
RA 6969 and generate additional income for the city.
We also recommended that the CENR Officer prepare for the
accreditation by the DENR to avoid wastage of fund, particularly, on the
initial investment of ₱4 Million.
Lastly, management to determine the amount to be collected for the
medical waste treatment facility services that should be at par with the
current fees being collected by private accredited waste treaters or at least
breakeven with the expenses related to the hospital waste management.
Thereafter, start the process so that said income-generating activity be
passed into an ordinance.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management (a) require accountable personnel to judiciously liquidate cash advances after the purposes for which they were granted are consummated; (b) refrain from granting cash advances to personnel who failed to completely liquidate prior cash advance; and (c) ensure that sufficient controls are consistently implemented in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
We also recommended that Management (a) stop granting cash advances to employees not designated as Special Disbursing Officer; and (b) stop granting cash advance in excess of the Special Disbursing Officer’s authorized limit.

We recommended that Management require that (a) the Inventory Committee conduct an inventory of the local roads under the jurisdiction of the City and render a Report on the Physical count of the Road Networks System; (b) the City General Service Officer (i) render a Report on Local Road Network and Road Map; and (ii) keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components; (c) the City Engineer provide the City Accountant and the City General Service Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; (d) the Municipal Accountant (i) prepare the JEV to record the beginning balance of the local road network and its components; (ii) prepare and maintain a Local Road Network Ledger Card; (iii) keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and (iv) prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component at the end of the year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Inventory Team to (a) conduct complete annual physical inventory-taking of PPE by type; (b) submit a complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); and (c) conduct a periodic reconciliation of the PPE balances between Accounting records and CGSO records to reduce to minimum, if not zero out, the variance noted.
We recommended that the City Accountant (a) provide depreciation for all depreciable Property, Plant and Equipment and continue providing depreciation for condemned properties until their de-recognition in the books for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements; (b) coordinate with the City Engineer’s Office with regard to the status of the infrastructure projects to ensure accurate and timely recording of these transactions; and (c) effect the de-recognition of the disposed Property, Plant and Equipment in the books for a more reliable balances of the PPE accounts at year-end.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical inventory count of supplies and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semester, respectively; (b) City Accountant to maintain supplies ledger cards for each type of supplies; (c) City General Services Officer to maintain stock cards for each type of supplies; and (d) City Accountant and City General Services Officer to reconcile their inventory records to verify the accuracy of the reported balances of the Inventory account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) evaluate the propriety and validity of the recorded Accounts Payables and those found valid should be immediately settled or paid; (b) revert to the unappropriated surplus those payables determined to be without valid/legal claims; and (c) henceforth, ensure that only payables with valid and adequate documents are recognized in the books.

We recommended that Management (a) establish an effective waste management strategy by ensuring the active participation of all stakeholders by allowing them to be involved in programs and projects so that they will be more  aware of the need to maintain the cleanliness of their surrounding and protect the environment, but most importantly, to incorporate the proper attitude and practice in their daily lives;  (b) consider the possibility of passing an Ordinance on the collection of wastage fees  from  the business establishments to increase the City’s resources to cover the payment of tipping fees; and (c) advise and recommend to the 35 Punong Barangays the imposition of wastage fees from residents as share on the hauling expenses being solely borne by the City, given that bulk of the residual wastes being transported to the CTS is barangay wastes.

We recommended that the Management  (a) continue to perform  with  utmost dedication  and excellence in the field of calamities and disasters to maintain its standing  in the GAWAD KALASAG award-giving body; (b) if budget permits, consider the creation of CDRRMO into a Department inasmuch as the function of the office is so rigorous since  it covers all disaster-related activities which are so important in this current situations; (c) upgrade the Organization and Composition of the staffing pattern of the CDRRMO Office;  and (d) ensure that the CDRRMO is  adequately manned by permanent personnel as mandated under Section 12 of R.A. No. 10121 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 of the NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Local School Board (a) use the correct format in the preparation of the SEF budget as mandated in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017; and (b) utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the Joint Circular.
We also recommended that the LSB establish the needs-based criteria in allocating SEF across schools to ensure its efficient and effective use and in accordance with its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) require the CEO to complete the remaining projects in the AIP for CY 2019; (b) direct the City Engineering Office and City Planning and Development Office to conduct thorough site inspection and investigation to prevent suspension of work due to right-of-way issues; and (c) ensure the immediate completion of all on-going projects which already incurred delays, as well as require the City Engineering Office to conduct regular monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure that infrastructure projects are accomplished in accordance with the terms of the contract.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the GPBP focal person of the respective implementing offices to improve monitoring of projects to ensure timely completion and delivery of services thereof; and (b) consider returning the unutilized balance to the source agency, if additional expenses for the specific program are no longer needed.

We recommended that the LFC (a) conduct a more frequent analysis and study in forecasting revenues/income to further improve targets set for collections; (b) through the City Assessor’s Office conduct a more frequent tax mapping activities for better assessed values; (c) through the CTO, employ new strategies and coordinate at the barangay level for a wider reach of taxpayers; (d) consider increasing payment methods such as, but not limited to, bank payments, mall-based kiosk, and payment centers at barangays.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management (a) execute thorough and careful planning and exercise prudence and due diligence in the procurement of transport and heavy equipment to ensure maximum utilization of public resources; and (b) if resources are available, refrain from procuring second-hand/reconditioned vehicles to avoid short period of warranty and costly repairs and maintenance on reconditioned equipment in the long run.
We also recommended that the Management develop adequate procedures for the purchase of vehicles which will include (a) assessment of need and analysis of how the vehicle would support the organization’s mission, goals and objectives; (b) assessment of alternatives; and (c) analysis of expected acquisition costs as well as estimated recurring future costs such as repairs, maintenance and insurance.

We recommended that Management (a) establish monitoring control on the payment of landline telephones to ensure that only those serviceable units would be charged and settled;  (b) direct the CICTO to coordinate with the CAO on the monthly status of telephones being used by the City before  settlement of accounts is effected; (c) require the telecommunication company to refund the payment for unserviceable units which were charged and settled or if possible, provide rebates thereon; and (d) explain or justify on the status of a telephone unit assigned at the Register of Deeds but being  paid by the City.

We recommended that Management (a) comply with the BFP Director’s memorandum and let BFP assess and collect fire code fees; (b) discontinue the practice of collecting fire code fees unless a written MOA between the City and BFP is approved and signed by both parties; (c) make a written communication with BFP on the actual and updated status of BFP shares,  copy furnished the Audit Team for the response received; and (d) utilize BFP shares on the intended purpose  thereof, to fund programs and projects related to BFP operations.

We recommended that the legislative department (a) require the personnel in-charge of monitoring supplies to prepare a control sheet showing all procured and distributed supplies on a monthly basis to fully establish balances as of the end of the month; (b) assign a personnel that would monitor delivery and distribution of printed paraphernalia such as copies of printed codes and ordinances, posters, and pamphlets for a better control; (c) indicate titles of ordinances to be printed or reproduced in DVs and SDs; (d) consider more austerity measures in the procurement of meals, providing meals only to those who are directly involved in the activity; (e) consider creating a management policy setting a maximum threshold for  the amount to be reimbursed for communication allowance; and (f) diligently and judiciously plan all official activities and undertake available austerity measures to minimize and/or reduce expenses to generate savings for more beneficial programs/ projects of the City.
We also recommended that Management submit all procurement DVs and SDs for procured supplies and printed paraphernalia for audit and evaluation.

We recommended that Management (a) establish a Preventive Maintenance Program to ensure that all necessary maintenance  is done on all monitor vehicles on a timely basis; (b) set up scheduled maintenance and require the official concern or the personnel in charge of the vehicle to have it regularly checked and inspected to avoid the incurrence of higher costs in case of vehicle’s breakdown; and (c) require the Motorpool Division to follow the scheduled check-up/services offered by the car manufacturer for the newly acquired vehicles  so that any defect that may possibly arise during the warranty period will be addressed accordingly.

We recommended that Management, in close coordination with all department heads and procurement officers concerned, re-assess procurement and payment policies, such as (a) submit complete documentations of all transactions to prevent delays in payment; (b) take note of  the number of days required in the procurement cycle and assure that correct PRs are posted in the system prior to the target date of program implementation; (c) coordinate and promptly inform the CGSO of onsite deliveries; (d) ensure efficient and timely signing of documents required in the procurement and payment of  claims; (e) plan early and strictly comply with timelines from budget preparation to payment of transaction; and (f) include only programs which are implementation ready.

We still recommended that the LCE require that (a) the LCPC official maximize the utilization of child care and protection activities that are relevant, doable and operational; (b) allocate the required appropriation of one percent of IRA as mandated by law; and (c) coordinate and facilitate the integration of the LCPC activities and mainstream the same to other departments’ PPAs so that the one per cent allocation thereof will be fully utilized.

We recommended that Management (a) require  job order personnel who were not physically interviewed in our office to present themselves personally together with their valid ID; (b) submit a written explanation of  overlapping functions in various departments supported with a copy of each department’s mandate; (c) require the JOs assigned at the legislative department to register and comply with  the mandatory use of the biometric system and  limit the number of field works of each employee so that the use of locator slip would not be abused; (d) consider the filing in advance and approval of   locator slip to ensure adequate control thereof; (e) conduct sufficient intervention to address the continuous hiring of  JOs such as lack of eligibility and those who have been continuously hired and rehired by the City for years; (f) limit positions in core offices who perform  regular functions; and (g) require all department heads to carefully evaluate or assess the need for  a job order personnel with due consideration on their qualification to the job requirement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) City Accountant to (i) ensure that required documents are attached and complete before payment is made; and (ii) submit to COA the aforementioned unsubmitted documents for review; (b) City Engineer to submit, for evaluation a written explanation/ justification for the (i) absence of inspection report; and (ii) discrepancies noted in the delivered project as compared with plans; and (c) all concerned divisions implement necessary controls to protect government funds from loss and wastage in the delivery of infrastructure projects.

We recommended that Management (a) continue its effort to campaign on its slogan of “clean and green environment” which will serve as a reminder to its constituents to maintain cleanliness in the community; (b) continue to promote re-cycling which is the best way to reduce waste; and (c) give source of “rewards” for barangays, business establishments and other sectors of society  that promote reuse, recycling and reduction of garbage to encourage each and every resident to be more accountable on  their waste.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) record under Due to NGAs account the receipt, liquidation and refund of funds received from NGAs; (b) consider the PAJE totaling P44,295,754.79 in adjusting the misstated asset, liability and equity accounts; and (c) make sure that the SLs are detailed and complete and identify under which account the amount of P22,018.88 is appropriate to be recorded.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the Other Payables account by considering the PAJE above and to make sure that the SLs are detailed, complete and reconciled with the GL accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the depository banks to furnish the municipality of bank statements regularly and promptly. Make sure that it is done in writing, duly received by the bank and filed for documentation. Also, instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort in accomplishing BRS and make sure that all book reconciling items are traced and reflected in the books. Lastly, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to see to it that her cashbook is updated and complete.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies in intensifying the collection of real property tax such as information campaign to encourage taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount; (b) require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to reconcile their records and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies; (c) insruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the year-end balances of RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred RPT and SET accounts; and (d) direct the Acting Municipal Assessor to coordinate with the Provincial Assessor in the conduct of tax mapping to update the real property units and their corresponding assessed value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) create an Inventory Committee for the conduct of semestral physical count of all inventories and instruct the said Committee to ensure that all pertinent columns of the RPCI are filled up; and (b) instruct the said Committee and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the result of the count to books and submit them to the Audit Team on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Engineer to determine the useful life of the infrastructure assets and the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation for the said accounts; (b) create an Inventory Committee for the conduct of annual physical count and the submission of properly accomplished RPCPPE; and (c) require the Municipal Accountant to update and reconcile the subsidiary ledgers with the GL and FS balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare detailed subsidiary ledgers (SL) for each type of tax and make sure that the withholdings and remittances are monitored and updated in the general ledgers (GL). Also, make sure that the internal arrangement for the remittance of taxes from the three funds is reflected promptly in the SL and GL.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the AAO to update their GSIS records thru submission of ARA and then make sure that the actual amounts of contributions, shares and loan amortizations as reflected in the officers and employees’ payrolls are remitted to the GSIS intact and on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts, determine the cause of the abnormal balances, initiate necessary corrections or adjustments and come up with accounts in their normal balances as fairly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to process only the request for cash advance of the officers and employees who have no outstanding balance; and (b) require the officers and employees to liquidate on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the municipal officials and employees to refund the traveling expenses paid to them in excess of the allowed amount and instruct the Municipal Accountant that henceforth ensure that the claims against government funds are allowed, proper and documented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Engineer to submit immediately the lacking documents enumerated in Annex E; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant that henceforth, every voucher must have a complete supporting documents prior to processing of claims.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the LDRRMO to ensure the implementation of the above PPAs so that benefits that can be derived therefrom will be extended to the constituents; and                (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the amount and details of LDRRMF in compliance with                   Item 5.1.16 and Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the MPDC to justify/explain the inclusion of projects that do not squarely fit to the menu provided by Item 3 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and the                                non-implementation of the development projects; and (b) instruct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation of development projects and to disclose them in the quarterly Report of Government PPAs for submission to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Treasurer to submit all monthly and quarterly financial and accounting reports and their corresponding statements within the prescribed period in order for the Audit Team to undertake timely review and post-audit of the transactions and accounts and at the same time to inform management of any defects or deficiencies.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant immediately effect correcting entries to derecognize the said inventory accounts in the financial statements; and
Management direct its officials to strictly observe the prescribed accounting guidelines and control procedures on the receipt, issuance and storage/custodianship of supplies and materials.

We recommended that:
Management exercise its duties and responsibilities to comply with one of its many functions initiating the creation of Inventory Committee and conduct of physical inventory taking of the Agency’s assets. The corresponding report must be submitted to the Local Chief Executive and to the Audit Team; and
Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain property records and the Municipal Accountant to prepare ledger cards for each class of PPE to come up with a reconciliation of the balances in the book of accounts of these PPE items.

We recommended that:
Management direct the concerned officials to stop the practice of payment execution of various operating expenses which are not allowed by law through granting of cash advance and liquidation thereafter;
Management direct its officials to stop granting additional cash advances if the previous ones remained unsettled or unliquidated. Demand letters must be sent to the erring employees for their unliquidated balances. In case of non-compliance, Management is encouraged to resort to  the rules cited in Section 5 of COA Circular 97-002, which provides that failure to liquidate the cash advance within the prescribed period constitutes a valid cause for the withholding of salary; and
The Municipal Accountant immediately come up with a reconciliation of balances that would resolve the variance between the total cash advance recorded in the books and in the report of status of unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2019.

We recommended that Management:
Direct its officials and employees concerned to strictly comply with the regulation on the effective and efficient rendition of accounts and financial reports for the timely verification of accounts and conduct of post-audit activities; and
Devote some time to address the issue on the non-submission of accounts since the same has been a repeated audit finding for several years now.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately deposit all undeposited collections as of year-end after reconciling records with the Municipal Accountant. The practice of depositing collections only after huge accumulations must be stopped. Also, we recommended to verify and effect adjustments, if necessary, to the balance of the Cash-Local Treasury account under trust fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect correcting entries to include the actual RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Revenues in the Statement of Financial Position for CY 2019. The Municipal Accountant must coordinate with the Treasury Department to come up with the lists of unsettled tax liabilities. Subsidiary ledgers must also be maintained for these accounts for proper monitoring. Lastly, ensure that receivable accounts are valued at the actual amount of tax due and collectible to attain fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that:
Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately release the unremitted shares of the provincial government, barangay governments and the provincial school board out of it collections for real property and special education taxes;
The Municipal Treasurer conduct a meeting with all Barangays to discuss the policy in releasing their respective shares in the RPT; and
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer regularly coordinate with the provincial government and provincial school board with respect to the release of the said shares to avoid accumulation of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and reconcile the Due from the Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and effect adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances. Likewise, ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times the same so as not to affect the presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant gather additional information pertinent to the CIP-Land Improvement account by retrieving available documents and inquiry with the former Municipal Accountant to ascertain if appropriate asset account must still be recognized. If it is found out that recognition of the said asset is not necessary anymore, it is suggested that the Municipal Accountant apply correcting entries to derecognize the CIP – Land Improvement account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and trace the transactions pertinent to the Other Prepayments account. Adjustments must be effected immediately. We also recommended to improve the controls with regard to the monitoring of accounting transactions specifically on the adjusting entries at the statement date.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, guided by COA Circular 2016-005, initiate the verification, review and analysis of the abovementioned non-moving receivable accounts to effect necessary adjusting entries/correcting journal entries for the eventual disposition thereof. Further, the said accounts can be requested for write-off as these affect the fair presentation of the financial statements.
Further, we recommended that controls must be set in place in the monitoring of receivable accounts by maintaining lists and/or subsidiary ledgers for these accounts. In this way, the age of the receivable account as well as the name of erring debtors or Accountable Officers could be easily identified.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace all available documents to gather data on how the said accounts were derived and effect necessary adjustments. The said accounts must be derecognized in the financial statements if the same were proven as an accounting error in the prior years.

We recommended to the management to direct the Municipal Accountant to trace and determine the “TBD” or “To be Distributed” Accounts to ascertain the correct balances of the sixteen (16) general ledgers under the PPE account as reflected in the Financial Statements as of December 31, 2019.
We also recommended to the management to direct the inventory committee to conduct thorough physical count of the various aforementioned properties which shall be used to reconcile with those recorded in the agency books and if warranted, adjust accordingly.

We recommended to the management to:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to use the Municipal Treasurer’s Office’s certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due for the year in setting up the Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax receivable and deferred income at the beginning of the year and to record the total amount (100% of the total delinquent Basic Tax Collectible and SEF Tax Collectible) of the receivable accounts as required under Section 20, Volume 1 of NGAS Manual; and
Also, we require the submission of the complete list of delinquent taxpayers report as of December 31, 2019 by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to determine the total amount of understatement of the RPT and SEF Receivable accounts.

We recommended to the management to instruct the accounting personnel  in charge to exert more efforts in the retrieval of prior years’ records to identify the accountable officers/employees/debtors or concerned agencies liable for the unaccounted balance of ₱1.639 million and, if appropriate, file request for authority to write-off for claims which could no longer be accounted.
We also recommended to the management to require the Accounting Office to send demand letters to the identified accountable officer/employee or recipient agency of other receivables.

We recommended to the management for the refund of the overpayment to the supplier in the amount of P132,477.70 and submission of the Official Receipt of the said refund to the Office of the Auditor.
We further recommended that the recording of financial transactions especially those that involve computation be closely monitored and checked by the Head of the Municipal Accounting Office. Furthermore, we recommended to the management to review the existing accounting flowchart and initiate improvement in the internal control system in the handling of financial records.

We recommended to the management, particularly Municipal Accounting Office, to facilitate the submission of the documents stated in Appendices M and N of this Report in order that the same will not be subjected to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance.
We further recommended to the management that thereafter, to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 as to the submission of reports and supporting documents.

We recommended to the management to direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the completed projects to proper PPE accounts and compute the applicable depreciation, and coordinate with the Municipal Engineer on the status of construction projects in order to enable timely reclassification.

We recommended to the management, particularly Municipal Accounting Office to verify each item under the Investment Property account and if warranted, reclassify the buildings/projects recorded to the proper PPE accounts including the accumulated depreciation and submit the journal entry voucher (JEV) to the Audit Team.

observations.managementAction: The LGU was only able to adjust an amount of ₱37,257,560.00 as observed, both the Accounting and Assessor’s offices are in coordination to correct those needing adjustment.

We were able to reconcile the balances as of the date of examination conducted in 2019. While it is noticeable that some figures and adjustments were written in pencil. Likewise, the totals were not footed and running daily balances were not reflected. Deposits were made daily but not intact as there were still accumulated balances.

observations.recommendation:
3. Management to:
a. Discontinue the practice of transferring the custody of cash to other personnel other than to whom the cash advance was granted;
b. Require the Accountable Officers concerned to maintain separate cashbooks for cash advances granted to them and record the cash advances granted and liquidation; and
c. Strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 on the Rules and Regulations on the Granting, Utilization and Liquidation of Cash Advances.
observations.managementAction: The City government observation regarding grants of AICS was lightly discussed in Observation No. 7.

observations.recommendation:
4. The City Mayor through the Program and Monitoring Executive Committee of the Fishermen’s Village:
a. Reinforce collections by sending out demand letters for delinquent beneficiaries/vendees as provided for in the Conditional Deed of Sale;
b. Schedule a dialogue with the beneficiaries/vendees to engage their attention on the matter, particularly those who succeeded occupancy following the death of the original beneficiary/vendee; and
c. Consider the inclusion of the City Treasurer as member of the Program Committee and delegate as member-in-charge of collection to better monitor the collectability of the receivables.
observations.managementAction: We were not able to prioritize the implementation of the recommendation, more so that they were not able to conduct a dialogue with the beneficiaries.

The positions mentioned in the observation is currently filling up remained vacant in CY 2019 due to the six-month suspension of the City Mayor. However, the new administration is on the process of filling-up those positions.

observations.recommendation: 6. The City revert/refund to the General Fund the CY 2011 unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in compliance with Section 5.1.13 of COA Circular 2012-002 in order for it to be incorporated in the planning of 2019 programs/projects/activities for social services.
observations.managementAction: The City Government reverted the 59 unexpended balance to the General Fund account on April 24, 2019 and the Sangguniang Panlungsod has enacted Appropriation Ordinance No. 3 dated April 24, 2019 and was approved by the City Mayor on April 26, 2019 for the supplemental budget.

observations.recommendation: 7. Management revisit and abide with the terms and conditions embodied in the Memorandum of Agreement and agreed upon by both parties. For future fund transfers, assign a focal person who will oversee the implementation of the program to avoid delays.
observations.managementAction: The City transferred the ₱3M worth of projects to the ITRMC as agreed upon in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: 8. The LSB to see to it that no lump sum appropriations be included in the Annual Budget of SEF. Further, to provide justification on the excess of expenditures over appropriation and so with charges of MOOE and CO not in line with the nature of these expenditures. The Bids and Awards Committee to require the LSB to formulate their own PPMP and the same be included in the APP of the LGU. Likewise, appropriation and utilization of the Special Education Fund be in line with the provisions of DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and so with RA9184 and other applicable laws, rules and procedures.
observations.managementAction: Program, Projects and Activities of the Local School Board were not reflected in the APP of the LGU except those Infrastructure Projects which underwent public bidding. Although breakdown of appropriation and expenses was provided, lump sum appropriations were noted to some line items like the Construction and Repair of School Buildings and Sports Facilities.

observations.recommendation:
9. Management: a. Strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the City GAD database to ensure an updated and operational database, which will better serve the GFPS in attaining an evidence-based GAD planning and policymaking.
b. Comply with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 and 2016-01 on the establishment of a gender responsive monitoring and evaluation system through the creation of GAD M&E Team who shall evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies, programs, and projects.
observations.managementAction: We have included in our Action Plan to create a GAD Database as the Gender Development Monitoring System Focal Point (CBMS) System TWG Chair was included in the Executive Order No. 70-2019 dated October 16, 2019. The Community-Development TWG although targeted by the concerned personnel.

observations.recommendation:
10. Management strictly implement City Ordinance No. 2003-007 in conformity with RA 9003, particularly:
a. Waste segregation at source be strongly monitored (The City to partner with barangay personnel to monitor every household);
b. Schedule of garbage collection with regard to biodegradable, non-biodegradable and waste recyclables likewise be strictly implemented (The City to strategize techniques and methods like provision of personnel to MRFs or dumping areas to oversee that residents dump wastes that are properly segregated); and
c. Penalty be strictly enforced to violators.
observations.managementAction: The problem in solid waste management involves a collective effort of the community, and the management commits to continuously create ways to implement a better system of waste collection and management. The solid waste ordinance was not implemented. The City is making efforts to institutionalize the ordinance as they are awaiting the completion of the Development of the Solid Waste Management Plan.

observations.recommendation: 11. The Local Chief Executive direct the City Treasurer to: a. Require the eight (8) suppliers/contractors to pay the correct amount of business taxes totaling to ₱6,731,377.79 and the corresponding fines and penalties due to the City Government; b. Compel the 8,026 business establishments to submit a copy of their 2017 Annual Income Tax Return, compute the correct business tax and collect tax deficiency, as well as fines and penalties, if the amount declared in the Permit Application form is lower than the amount appearing in the 2017 Annual Income Tax Return; c. Strictly enforce Section 2A.02A and D of the City of San Fernando Revenue Code requiring the taxpayers to submit a copy of the Annual Income Tax Return and impose surcharge and interest provided in the said Code any false entry resulting to the basis of underpayment of the taxes or require to alternative documents such as monthly copies of VAT returns of the preceding year as basis for the assessment of gross sales/receipts while awaiting for the copy of Income Tax Return; and d. Exercise the visitorial function provided under Section 2A.04 of the Revenue Code on these businesses to check on the accuracy of the assessment and collection of taxes, in view of the significant differences in the declared gross receipts against the Income Tax Return. Also, management to provide the Audit Team the database of taxpayers and view access of the system in order to facilitate the conduct of Revenue Audit and help the City Government in improving their collection efficiency. Lastly, align the Citizens’ Charter with the provisions of the Revenue Code.
observations.managementAction: We were able to collect only 30% of the amount specified in the observation as they were faced with the difficulty of collecting contractors. The City collecting the contractors challenged the computation as he averred that gross receipts should be the tax base for those engaged in the sale of goods. As for the contractors, we are faced with the difficulty of collecting the amount when the contractors' projects were not within the situs of City, citing taxation.

observations.recommendation: 12. The management require the City Assessor to prepare the SMV pursuant to Section 212 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and cause the passage of an Ordinance incorporating therein the proposed SMV as basis for the conduct of general revision of real property assessments in adherence to Section 219 of the same Act and DILG-DOF JMC No. 2010-001. Thereafter, require the City Assessor to conduct general revision of real property assessments every after three years as provided under Section 219 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The City Assessor still follows the 25-year old schedule which is unfavorable to the City Government as the schedule is unrealistic.

observations.recommendation:
13. Management:
a. Ensure and monitor the full implementation of the programs/projects/activities appropriated in the Annual and Supplemental budget in order to address the needs of the constituents;
b. Ensure strict observance of the provisions of Section 54.1 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 or Republic Act No. 9184 in the procurement of goods; and
c. Consider the general policy of the government in the acquisition of brand new vehicles by outright purchase in order to avoid costly repairs and maintenance on reconditioned vehicles in the long run; and d. If it could not be avoided, in the future and the City Government still needs to procure second-hand or reconditioned vehicle, ensure that minimum requirements as provided under pertinent provisions of NBC Nos. 446 and 446-A. Further, the Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working Group conduct Post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid. Lastly, ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements.
observations.managementAction: In the procurement of the City in CY 2019, the provisions of Section 54.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 was fully complied with. Observation regarding acquisition of vehicles was not noted in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: 14. The Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working Group judiciously conduct post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements to avoid future legal or administrative sanctions as provided under RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The Statement of On-going projects of these contracts were not fully validated as there were under-declaration of projects. The BAC is in the process of developing strategies as they take on a new leadership.

observations.recommendation: 15. The BAC Secretariat to see to it the Annual Procurement Plan depicts the entirety of procurement activities of the LGU and that the PPMPs of end-users were consolidated accordingly and for the Budget Office to see to it that these coincide with the approved budget of the LGU. The APP of the LGU must be prepared/amended/updated as soon as the Annual Budget is enacted and if there is Supplemental Budget, the PPMP and APP must be prepared/amended/updated. Several purchases be consolidated and undertaken through the competitive bidding. Improve the procurement process and see to it that procurement under alternative modes of procurement will expedite process as compared to public bidding. Compute for the appropriate penalty of the supplier and to require them to refund the amount. Further, the delay incurred by these suppliers may be considered in ascertaining the responsiveness of the supplier before awarding contracts.
observations.managementAction: Review of the CY 2019 APP of the agency depicted several deficiencies which includes the APP does not depict the entirety of the procurement activities of the LGU, hence it affected the implementation of projects. The APP was discussed in Observation No. 12.

observations.recommendation: 16. The Bids and Awards Committee refrain from transacting with the City Government’s employees’ cooperatives, the AKAMPC in line with Republic Acts 6713 and 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 08-2017. Further, the Bids and Awards Committee to require all the departments to prepare PPMP and consolidate the same in order to come-up with Annual Procurement Plan. As soon as the Annual Budget is enacted and if there is Supplemental Budget, the PPMP and APP must be prepared/amended/updated.
observations.managementAction: The City has stopped transacting with AKAMPC although there were still transactions between the City Government and its employees’ cooperative, AKAMPC. Payments were rendered after the AOM was issued. Henceforth, strict adherence to the provisions of the above-mentioned laws and regulations which enjoin all public officials or employees to avoid conflict of interest at all times.

observations.recommendation: 17. Suspension and resumption orders be provided with the records, the suspension orders and its supporting documents of the projects be likewise submitted. Further, that in the absence of such documents, the City Engineer be directed to compute Liquidated Damages and require the contractor to pay the same to the City Government.
observations.managementAction: We shall comply with the recommendati on prospectively. Per audit office records, the suspension and resumption orders, and documents to support reasons thereof were submitted to the audit team within five days upon its perfection. Notices of suspension and resumption orders, together with supporting documents were submitted.

observations.recommendation: 18. The City Mayor direct the contractors to refund excess payment in the amount of ₱488,012.18. Likewise, Variation Orders be likewise submitted within the five days upon its perfection.
observations.managementAction: Notices of Disallowance were issued and the persons liable were able to fully settle the amount.

observations.recommendation: 19. (General/summary recommendation) Ensure continued compliance with COA recommendations and follow-up actions.
observations.managementAction: The table indicates various actions taken across the recommendations above and the status summary that six (6) were fully implemented, five (5) were partially implemented and eight (8) were not implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

(a) reclassify the current portion of the long-term loans payable of ₱26,580,089.28 to Current Liabilities; 

(b) present these payables under the Current Liabilities category in the financial statements; and 

(c) ensure full compliance with the provisions cited under PPSAS No.1.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with our recommendations and assured full compliance thereon.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

(a) adjust the books and capitalize the borrowing costs incurred in the improvement and rehabilitation of the New Public Market and construction of various school buildings; and 

(b) disclose the necessary details in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Management informed the Audit Team that the treatment of borrowing costs as capitalizable expense has already been adopted and agreed to properly disclose the said details in the Notes to FS henceforth.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive require the continuous investigation of the properties not found and take appropriate actions against personnel/officials accountable thereon.

We also reiterated our recommendations that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:

(a) ensure that all government property officially received by the City are accounted for and recognized in the books at acquisition cost or at appraised value, as applicable; and 



(b) adopt all the principles prescribed for proper accounting of the City’s PPE for a more reliable information on PPE and fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management had already instructed the officials concerned to facilitate the necessary actions in processing the transfer of titles of properties.  Further, accounts misclassified will be adjusted for proper reclassification.  Management also reported that continuous updating of account and cleansing of records carried in the books since the 80s and 90s are being done to reflect a more accurate PPE accounts.
observations.reason:
Failure to account for other properties such as roads which are currently being tagged by various offices for the GIS.

Difficulty encountered by the City in identifying and booking up of all properties such as roads.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require the inventory team to:

(a) conduct physical count of all inventory items regularly at the end of every semester and render a report thereon within the prescribed deadline; and 



(b) continue their effort in reconciling the records of the CGSO and the Accounting records, investigate the cause of the discrepancy and take appropriate actions to resolve the problem.

We also recommended that the City Accountant ensure the accuracy of the cost of the Inventories recognized at year-end and presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management admitted that the Inventory System of Recording in the Books of the City Accounting Office and the Physical Count maintained by the City General Services Office need to be reviewed in which they committed to correct and reconcile the records thereof.
observations.reason:
Failure to conduct count at year-end owing to absence of tallying records between departments

Absence of sufficient records to support the same.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

(a) conduct periodic review of all outstanding payables to ensure that any invalid claim which had been outstanding for more than two years and without actual claim, administrative or judicial, or which were not covered by perfected contracts or records are reverted to the General Fund; and 

(b) ensure the timely and complete recording of valid accounts payable in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
Management concurred with our recommendations. The City Accountant’s Office in coordination with the City General Service Office committed to improve the process of procurement up to delivery and acceptance of goods and services.  The following are recommended for compliance and implementation:

Monitor timeliness of submission of documentation of payables to warrant payment; and
Conduct periodic reviews of claims.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented

Complete supporting documents are not timely submitted by the CGSO to CACCO

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GPBP focal person of the respective implementing offices to:

(a) fast tract and complete the remaining projects and strictly monitor the implementation of projects as targeted and planned; 

(b) take note of any gap for future inputs especially on the delay of implementation and completion of GPBP-funded projects and programs; and 

(c) consider returning the funds to source agencies if project completion is futile.
observations.managementAction: Management assured compliance with our recommendations and agreed to return the remaining and unutilized funds to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:

(a) CEO to complete the remaining projects in the AIP for CY 2018; 

(b) City Engineering Office and City Planning and Development Officer to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period; and 



(c) Planning and Development Coordinator to coordinate with the CBO and CEO regarding the utilization of the 20% DF and prepare accurate year-end report thereon showing actual completed projects.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering office as well as all implementing Offices that have allocations under the 20% DF are tasked to submit a physical accomplishment on the Utilization of the Fund.  The City Accounting Office is reconciling inputs noted in the abovementioned AOM.  Also, a working paper on the Utilization of the Fund is being prepared to report the actual completion of works.
observations.reason:
Occurrence of fortuitous events leading to suspensions of work during rainy season

Not Implemented 

Poor monitoring in the completion of projects.

observations.managementAction: Management concurred with our recommendations and agreed full compliance with COA Circular 2012-002 starting with the revision of the 2019 DRRMP to adhere with the substance and form required in the LRRMFIP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 

(a) Local Health Board and/or the City Health Office prepare a PhilHealth Per Family Payment Rate Fund Utilization and Program Plan; 

(b) allocate funds to RHUs based on the needs and necessity of each through careful evaluation and planning; and 

(c) City Health Office abide by the provisions of PhilHealth Circular No. 10, s. 2012 on the proper disposition and allocation of the Fund.
observations.managementAction:
The City Health Office through the Local Health Board agreed to:

Prepare a fund utilization and  program plan based on the paid PFPR per Rural Health Unit; and 
Improve the implementation plan per RHU to ensure proper liquidation reporting to PHIC and make a thorough review of the PPMP of CYs 2016-2019.

The City Engineer’s Office assured us that the recommendations will be positively considered and that they commit to review and double check all program of works especially the preparation of ABC so that the computation will be in accordance with the Department Orders of the DPWH.

observations.recommendation: We commended the effort of management and recommended that efforts be sustained for continuous and timely distribution of RPT shares to ensure efficient delivery of services of concerned component barangays.

observations.managementAction: Management through the City Treasurer’s Office gave the assurance that they will abide by the Commission’s recommendations.

The Local Chief Executive has issued Executive Order CMO2019-03 creating a Technical Working Group (TWG) that will oversee the implementation and monitoring of the City programs that will support and strengthen the education sector in the City.  Currently, the TWG has already developed a framework that will harmonize the City’s education program, the scholarship fund program as well as the operation of the City College.  Also, the City Social Welfare and Development Office is now developing an enhanced data base to readily evaluate with accuracy the social status of a family and individual sectoral groups.

With the enactment of the 2017 Revised Revenue Code and Market Code of the City and the implementation thereof in CY 2018, there was a need to enhance the system to adopt to the new Revenue Code.  Since INFOMAN was the developer of the application system, it has the only technology or tools to enhance the current system because they have the sole control on the source code of the whole application system.

Currently, both the Real Property Tax Assessment System and Real Property Collection System are running for stress testing and checking of possible system glitch and checking of all reports for accuracy of data until the time that it will be fully operational.

As an audit rejoinder, we maintain our recommendation that management should hold the processing of claims until the pending issues on the cause of delay of the project in accordance with the terms of the original contract are fully resolved. Also liquidated damages should be impose to the system developer for the long-delayed project.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented

Unpaid continuous billings of INFOMAN.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

(a) conduct sufficient intervention to address the possible increase in JOs in the coming years for lack of eligibility of employees who have been continuously hired and rehired by the City for years; 

(b) limit positions in core offices performing regular functions to those with employer-employee relationship and social protection and equality in benefits with permanent personnel; and 



(c) require all department heads to carefully evaluate or assess the need for a job order personnel with due consideration on their fitness to the job requirement.
observations.managementAction: Management assured the Audit Team that with the implementation of the PRSSP, this would not result in the displacement of City employees particularly with the retention of existing casual and job order employees. Further, the CHRMO requires the Heads of Offices to evaluate the need of hiring an employee so that only those needed would be hired.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented

Absence of eligibility requirements of some workers who have been working with the City for years.

Not Implemented

Absence of sufficient intervention over JOs in the legislative department.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD focal person and technical working group to continue to plan, implement, and evaluate programs and activities which are gender responsive and are compliant with JMC No. 2013-01 to continuously provide socio-economic services to the fund’s intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City:

(a)  allocate in the Annual Budget at least 1% of IRA for LCPC-related programs and activities pursuant to Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344; and 

(b) maximize the utilization of the 1% of IRA allocation to LCPC-related programs, projects and activities that are relevant, operational and doable.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented

Failure to conduct mainstreaming in all departments to fully utilized and implement target activities under the fund.

observations.recommendation: We commended Management for its compliance and recommended that it sustain its efforts in the continuous and timely withholding and remittance of contributions and payments to the GSIS and compliance with the requirements set forth under Republic Act No. 8291, also known as the GSIS Act of 1997.

We recommended that the City Accountant  continue being compliant in the deduction and remittance of premium contributions, loan amortizations to PAG-IBIG and continue to improve its ledger for the Due to PAG-IBIG account for ease in monitoring, reconciliation and determination of the actual amounts due to PAG-IBIG.

We recommended that the City Accountant continue being compliant in the deduction and prompt remittance of premium contributions to PhilHealth in accordance with the National Health Insurance Act of 2013.

We commended the City for its regular compliance with the revenue regulations cited above. The maintenance of separate records for the special accounts of the City facilitated the review of taxes withheld and remitted to BIR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the General Fund shall consist of monies and resources of the local government which are available for the payment of expenditures and obligations, to eliminate the incurrence of service charge and the risk of being prone to the liability of issuing bouncing checks.

We recommended that Management:
provide separate depository accounts for the 20% Development Fund and 5% Calamity Fund, to properly monitor and determine cash availability on the said funds.
request the Sangguniang Bayan to pass a resolution properly authorizing the Municipal Mayor and Treasurer to open a separate bank account for the 20% Development Fund and 5% Calamity Fund; and
direct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain separate cashbooks and/or SL intended for the 20% Development Fund and 5% Calamity Fund.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts by taking up immediate adjustments in the books of accounts; and
conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure that discrepancies are immediately identified, reconciled and adjusted.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the deficiencies noted in the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) and carefully check entries for subsequent transactions to minimize errors in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units under COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer:
to conduct physical count of all properties and submit the required RPCPPE; and maintain Property Ledger Cards for each PPE account to facilitate periodic reconciliation with the accounting records; and
issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for all properties, including transfer of properties from one accountable officer to another, and said ARE be renewed every three years, to pinpoint accountability.
observations.managementAction: Some properties were issued with Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE)
observations.reason:
No Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment was prepared and submitted and property ledger cards were not maintained.
ARE were issued only for the current purchases.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require:
the Municipal Agriculturist to submit detailed report on the actual condition and disposal of the breeding stocks to the Municipal Accountant.
the Municipal Accountant to make the adjusting entry to drop the amount from the books, upon receipt of the report from the Municipal Agricultural Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accountant the 32 DVs totaling ₱918,639.12, who shall eventually submit them to COA for audit, to forestall the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended that Management:
require the submission of all supporting documents of transactions identified above to the COA Audit Team to facilitate the final inspection of these projects; and
ensure that complete supporting documents are required on future disbursements in compliance to the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2012-001 to avoid suspensions or disallowances in audit.
