observations.reason: The unexpended balance for CY 2018 were already transferred to Trust Liabilities. However, again for this year unexpended balance for CY 2019 was not transferred at the end of the year.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
record the adjusting entries to correct the balances of the accounts;
review the composition of balances of the Advances to Contractors account and reconcile the same for each contractor to warrant that the advance payment and the corresponding recoupment are properly recorded; and
maintain separate subsidiary ledger for each contractor with advance payment for the mobilization cost for easier monitoring of balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to revert back the stale checks to Accounts Payable account by debiting the Government Equity Account and crediting the former.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. similar to the previous years, the Mayor to direct the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the annual budget appropriation 5% for the Gender and Development (GAD) programs/activities as required under the above-stated circular.  If the budget is incorporated or streamlined in other offices of the LGU, the appropriation should be marked as GAD related activities for proper identification and monitoring; b. the Mayor to direct the Focal Point Person, to comply with the mandates of the above-mentioned provisions by preparing a GAD Plan to embrace all GAD activities for the year, and the accomplishment report at the end of the year that will prove compliance to said mandate; and c. the Mayor to direct the GAD Focal Point Person to prepare the GAD Accomplishment Report in accordance with the above-stated joint circular.

We recommended that a budget for enrollment of indigent senior citizens of the Municipality for Philhealth membership be appropriated by Management in accordance with the mandatory requirement set forth in Article 20, Section 2 of the Expanded Senior Citizens Act of 2010.

It is recommended that immediate and appropriate action be taken by management to settle these audit suspensions and disallowances in accordance with COA Rules and Regulations in the Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).
observations.managementAction: Partial payments were made by persons liable still connected with the LGU.
observations.reason: Total settlement as of this date is ₱204,000.00. Balances suspensions and disallowances as of the December 31, 2019 are ₱2,310,983.05 and ₱9,836,750.00, respectively,

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert more time in reviewing records and PPE ledgers pertinent to the mentioned PPE items and compute the depreciation in accordance with the PPSAS and existing COA issuances and make proper adjustments in the accounts, where necessary.

We recommended that the Management:

Stop granting Cash Advance for disbursements not allowed by law. Payments must be made directly to payee/supplier through the issuance of check except those expressly allowed under COA Circular 97-002. Cash Advance must only be given or granted to authorized officials as cited also in COA Circular 97-002;



Must not allow the grant of additional cash advance if the previous ones remained unliquidated or unsettled; and



Direct the Municipal Treasurer to settle/liquidate her accountabilities for unliquidated CAs as at December 31, 2018.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer made liquidations during the year but was unable to fully liquidate her accountabilities hence resulted to unliquidated balances at year-end.
observations.reason: Because of the geographical location of the agency, some supplier/payee are paid in the form of cash advances. Moreover, the agency still practice granting additional cash advance despite of unliquidated or unsettled balances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Direct its Officers to immediately forward or submit to the Office of the Auditor the disbursement vouchers totaling ₱44.90 million, without need of further demand; and

9. Exert efforts in identifying causes of the delay or non-submission of Disbursement Vouchers and bring solution to this problem.
observations.managementAction: Some of the disbursement accounts referred to were already forwarded to the Office of the auditor

observations.recommendation: 10. We recommended that the Management direct its officers to fully implement the GAD activities and the implementation must be monitored regularly by the GFPS. Moreover, we suggest that the GAD Focal Person be more vigilant in performing her responsibility in the monitoring of the implementation of GAD programs/activities. She must also assure that Accomplishment Reports are submitted to the oversight agencies. Further, creation of a Local GAD Code and establishment of VAWs Desk must be also be prioritized.

We recommended that Management:

Must have the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) be reviewed and approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC). Review by the NSWMC of the said plan will assess if it addresses the necessary issues concerning Solid Waste Management in the Municipality. Moreover, necessary requirements are needed for the approval of the SWMP;











Strictly comply with RA No. 9003 on the establishment of solid waste and material recovery facility (MRF); and

Exert continuous effort in educating the people of   San Fernando the importance of Solid Waste Management.
observations.managementAction: The Office must gather data for the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan of the existing dumpsite of the Municipality in order to complete the information data needed in the 10-year SWMP and submit the same to the Office of the National Solid Waste Management Commission.
observations.reason: The Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality is reviewed and submitted to the Office of Environmental Management Bureau (EMB-V) and DILG Region V, Legaspi City. However, this is not yet submitted to the National Solid Waste Management Commission because the Safe Closure and rehabilitation plan (SCRP) of the existing dumpsite of San Fernando is not yet completed as one of the requirement in 10 year SWMP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Management that disbursement of Special Education Fund (SEF) must be done in accordance with the guidelines and accounting procedures set forth in the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I to effectively monitor and determine whether or not the funds were utilized for its intended purpose and that expenses incurred were appropriately charged to fund. Granting of cash advance and cash payments to suppliers by local school boards or any other school officials must be stopped. Moreover, we require that the lacking Inspection and Acceptance Reports of the various purchases be attached to the liquidation reports.

We recommended that the Management:

Comply with the documentary requirements of the four (4) enumerated transactions above together with the Sanggunian Resolution and proof of declaration of State of Calamity; 

Be cautious in disbursing government fund by adhering to complete documentations with reference to COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, the Revised Guidelines and Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions; and

Exercise prudence in recording accounting entries particularly on the proper selection of accounts.

We recommended that the Management:

24. The Management stop granting a monthly gasoline allowance to any LGU Official who are also receiving Representation and Travelling Allowance (RATA); and

25. Exercise caution in the disbursement of fund by adhering to rules and regulations to avoid those Irregular, Unnecessary, Excessive, Extravagant, or Unconscionable (IUEEU) expenditures.

27. We recommended that the Management direct its officers to immediately release/remit all the balance of 2018 GSIS contribution so as to avoid further penalties. Moreover, the agency must stop the practice of accumulating the balance before it is remitted. Prompt remittance must be adopted to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) and submit the same, together with the copies of Bank Statements and paid checks, to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 20th day of the following month as prescribed under COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.  Moreover, the Audit Team also suggested additional manpower in the Accounting Department to help perform accounting duties so that the Municipal Accountant can devote time to prepare the bank reconciliation.

We recommended that Management:

Must have the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) be reviewed and approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC). Review by the NSWMC of the said plan will assess if it addresses the necessary issues concerning Solid Waste Management in the Municipality. Moreover, necessary requirements are needed for the approval of the SWMP;





Strictly comply with RA No. 9003 on the establishment of solid waste and material recovery facility (MRF); and

Exert continuous effort in educating the people of San Fernando on the importance of Solid Waste Management
observations.managementAction: Appropriate separate budget that will support the development and implementation of solid waste management programs.
observations.reason: The 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan is still not approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission. Moreover, lack of budget for solid waste management programs, limited manpower and financial capacity of the LGU to implement solid waste management program.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Require the GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Plan and Budget to the Provincial Planning and Development Office and DILG Regional Office V for appropriate review and approval. The submission of the said plan evaluates the programs/activities if it addresses the necessary issues of the Municipality. The corresponding accomplishment report must also be prepared and submitted;



Follow the proper way of budgeting in the allocation of five per cent (5%) for Gender and Development transactions; and

Create a GAD Focal Point System. Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 provides the guidelines in the proper way of structuring the GFPS.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to exert continuous effort in monitoring the function of the GFPS. The GAD programs and activities set forth will be implemented. GPB will be submitted on time and Accomplishment Report will be submitted to oversight agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately release the trust collections due to other government agencies. We also recommended to require the Local Development Committee to improve planning and budgeting capabilities so that PPAs would not be described in generic terms and prioritize the development projects that are workable and attainable to avoid delays in the implementation and completion, thus attaining the socio-economic development and environmental outcomes in an efficient and effective manner.

We recommended that Management strictly require the:
Municipal Assessor to furnish the Municipal Treasurer with the updated Report on Real Property Assessment; and
Municipal Treasurer to provide/submit (having not submitted a certified amount for the last five years) a duly certified list of taxpayers with the amount of the RPT due and collectible for the year, as the basis for the setting -up of the receivable in the books by the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to record the asset accounts of the six completed infrastructure projects in the books of the LGU for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
maintain a schedule of completed infrastructure projects and monitor the recording of assets in the books upon completion; and
record the completed projects identified above under the appropriate PPE accounts to facilitate fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary journal entries to recognize in the books the public infrastructure assets totaling ₱48,441,366.92, as well as the related accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004, to present fairly the value of the assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer:
to conduct the annual physical count of all properties and submit the required RPCPPE;
maintain Property Ledger Cards for each PPE account as required by the above-mentioned Manual to facilitate periodic reconciliation with the accounting records; and
maintain and issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for all properties, including transfer of properties from one accountable officer to another and the same shall be renewed every three years, to determine accountability.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
make the necessary entry to recognize the Motor Vehicles in the books of the Municipality and provide for its depreciation expense for CYs 2017-2019; and
adhere to the provision of COA Circular 2012-002, that assets should be recognized upon purchase in the Trust Fund and transferred to the General Fund upon its issuance to end user, particularly for future acquisition of properties purchased from Trust Liability-LDRRMF.

We recommended that Management refrain from incurring printing expenses since it has its own tarpaulin printing machine, and adhere to the declared policy of the State that all resources of the government shall be managed and utilized, with a view of ensuring economy, in its operations.

We recommended that Management:
delegate an employee who will be assigned to prepare, monitor and submit the required Monthly Fuel Consumption Report; and
ensure that the forms, records, and reports prescribed under COA Circular No. 77-61, on the reporting and control over fuel consumptions, are prepared, submitted and properly accomplished so that wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures are minimized and disbursements are effectively controlled and properly accounted for.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to stop making advance payments for all government transactions, prior to the delivery of services and goods, as provided under Section 88 of PD 1445.

We recommended that Management require the accountable officers to:
require the payee to issue official receipt for the amount received and require proof of deposit to ensure that the same were accounted and became part of the barangay funds;
in future transactions, require that checks to be issued in the name of the barangay for all payments accruing to them; and
strictly comply with relevant rules and regulations to ensure protection of government funds.

We recommended that Management:
revisit its policy/practice of hiring consultants to ensure that the field of expertise needed is beyond its optimum in-house capability, in accordance with the General Principles on Consulting Services of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184;
refrain from hiring consultants, especially when the information needed for a specific Plan/Program/Project could be generated from its offices and from other government agencies, and the services required are mandated to be performed by a specific official of the LGU; and
ensure that Municipal funds are safeguarded against improper disposition with a view of ensuring economy and avoiding incurrence of unnecessary expenses which may result to loss or wastage of scarce resources.

We recommended that Management:
call the attention of its officials and employees that the time for lunch, or the period from 12:01 PM to 12:59 PM, cannot be included in computing for the required eight hours of work; and
require its Human Resource Management Officer to be strict in imposing the eight-hour work, and should the employee fail to render the required hours, a deduction from the leave credits or deduction from their salary shall be resorted to.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and mitigation fund-MOOE for CY 2019, and every year-end thereafter, to Trust Liabilities-DRRM in the Trust Fund books and revert the unutilized amounts of CYs 2010-2014 to unappropriated surplus of the General Fund in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that Management direct the Accounting Department to prepare the required Subsidiary Ledgers in accordance with the prescribed instructions provided under Volume II, NGAS for LGUS in order to reflect the needed details of the balances so that information/specifics pertaining to accounts in the General Ledger will be readily available.

We recommended that Management require the BAC and the Municipal Engineer to correctly calculate the period expressly stipulated and agreed upon in the contract, to properly account for damages, should the contractor incur delay in the performance of its obligation to complete the project.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GAD Focal Person to conduct gender analysis of their programs and projects through the HGDG tool using the appropriate checklists to ensure that the proposed programs are gender-responsive and can be attributed to GAD.
We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all EDF projects proposed in the Annual Investment Plan of the LGU be fully realized within the budget year.
We recommended that Management impose fees based on the Revised Revenue Code of the Municipality to ensure that correct income will be generated from market stalls.

a. Rigorously train more members of the association on bamboo processing especially on those bamboo crafts for replication.
 b. Closely supervise the production, marketing and management of the Bamboo Processing Center to sustain the technology improvement facility for the bamboo industry.
 c. Conduct a demonstration on charcoal-making in order to utilize the equipment and machineries procured for the purpose to capacitate the farmers and increase their income.
 d. Review the proposal of the livelihood projects to ascertain that only the necessary equipment and machineries directly attributable to the long-term implementation of the project will be procured.
 e. Create a committee to review the proposals of different associations for the livelihood projects funded by National Government Agencies so that holistic approach will be applied and increase the success in the implementation of the projects.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 Much as they want to produce more, they do not have the stable market. They were compelled to look for alternative jobs. The City Government is still responsible in monitoring the operation of the center. They should help the center in formulating marketing strategies. No consultant was availed by the Management this CY 2018 for the conduct of the “Lakas ng Pagbabago” Program of the LGU.
observations.reason: Still, the Bamboo Processing Center was not able to operate in full scale this CY 2018. Lack of appropriate marketing strategy hindered the success in the operation of the Bamboo Processing Center.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local School Board of City of San Fernando: 

furnish the Audit Team a copy of the Teacher Deployment Analysis Report and Registry of Qualified Applicants (RQA) for CY 2017; and

utilize the Special Education Fund only for purposes explicitly allowed in DeEp-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.
observations.managementAction: The Local School Board agreed to compensate eco-aids for services rendered until the said allocation under SEF will be budgeted under the General Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GPBP focal person of the respective implementing offices to: 

complete the remaining projects and strictly monitor the implementation of projects as targeted and planned;

take note of any gap for future inputs especially on the delay of implementation and completion of GPBP-funded projects and programs; and

submit and coordinate with the City Accounting Office regarding the timely liquidation of project disbursements to their source agencies.
observations.managementAction: Management stated that as of February 20, 2018 six additional projects were already completed awaiting appropriate documentations on the disbursements thereof. Further, management committed to implement all remaining BUB funded projects and assured prompt implementation of proposed projects to avoid delay in the execution thereof.

Last February 2, 2018, a second migration of data was conducted. Migrated data and generated reports by the New Real Property Tax Collection System of the GIS were compared with the Palay System. Currently, February 2018 data are encoded at the MITD data center. Parallel testing is currently done at the City Assessor’s Office. RPTAS system at the Treasurer’s Office cannot be done until successful cleansing and parallel testing under the Assessor’s Office is completed.

The Sangguniang Panlungsod enacted Ordinance No. 2017-035 dated December 18, 2017 amending S.P. Ordinance No. 2008-018 otherwise known as the “Charter of the City College of San Fernando, Pampanga”. Under the new ordinance, subsidy for the College was set to a minimum of 2% of the proceeds of the collections from the General Fund and IRA of the next preceding year, instead of the maximum 3% stipulated in the previous charter. Management also concurred with other recommendations and committed compliance thereto.

PADC coordinated with their clients and agreed gradual increase of fees to be collected. Currently, new rates were already imposed and collected by PADC. The City also enacted S.P. Ordinance No. 2017-033 dated December 12, 2017 enacting the “2017 Revised Revenue and Market Code of the City of San Fernando, Pampanga” which includes the revised rates in slaughterhouse operations. Management also concurred with our other audit recommendations and further assured compliance thereto.

that the
