observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Create an Inventory Committee and require the same to conduct a complete count of the Municipality’s PPE and prepare the Report as provided by Section 57, Volume II of NGAs Manual and submit the same to the Local Chief Executive and Auditor;

Require the General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer to maintain records on all properties of the agency; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to properly maintain ledger cards of all properties. This must be reconciled with stock/property cards maintained by the GSO or Municipal Treasurer
observations.managementAction: Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Inventory Committee to conduct annual inventory taking and prepare the necessary forms needed to simplify the updating of property, plant and equipment. The Municipal will also maintain a ledger of this account.
observations.reason: Lack of coordination between the treasury and accounting department on how this account be maintained and the necessary forms were not prepared to facilitate immediate tracing of unreconciled account.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Must have the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) be reviewed and approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC). Review by the NSWMC of the said plan will assess if it addresses the necessary issues concerning Solid Waste Management in the Municipality. Moreover, necessary requirements are needed for the approval of the SWMP;

Strictly comply with RA No. 9003 on the establishment of solid waste and material recovery facility (MRF); and

Exert continuous effort in educating the people of San Fernando on the importance of Solid Waste Management
observations.managementAction: Appropriate separate budget that will support the development and implementation of solid waste management programs.
observations.reason: The 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan is still not approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission. Moreover, lack of budget for solid waste management programs, limited manpower and financial capacity of the LGU to implement solid waste management program.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
Management require the Municipal Accountant to establish Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year. The figures must be based on the collectibles identified by the Municipal Treasurer on her assessment from the list of taxpayers in the RPT Account Register; and

The Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to identify all prior year receivables from real property taxpayers and effect necessary adjustments to present the same in the Financial Statements in CY 2018.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will coordinate with the treasury office to set up real property tax and special education tax receivable as per RPT account register and effect the necessary adjusting entries of prior years’ unaccounted RPT and SET receivables.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant was not able to set up RPT and SET Receivables because of lack of coordination between the concerned offices.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the noted deficiencies, we recommended that Management:
Require the GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Plan and Budget to the Provincial Planning and Development Office and DILG Regional Office V for appropriate review and approval. The submission of the said plan evaluates the programs/activities if it addresses the necessary issues of the Municipality. The corresponding accomplishment report must also be prepared and submitted;

Follow the proper way of budgeting in the allocation of five per cent (5%) for Gender and Development transactions; and

Create a GAD Focal Point System. Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 provides the guidelines in the proper way of structuring the GFPS. Roles and responsibilities could have been acted upon, only if the GAD Focal Point System is fully-functional. Also, GFPS must submit the agency’s   GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) within the month of January to February of each year to see to it that the GPB of the municipality is aligned with the priorities of the provincial government. Likewise, from the PPDO the GPB must be forwarded to the DILG Regional Office within the month of March of each year for appropriate review and approval. Actual GAD projects and activities be also aligned with the GAD plan per COA Circular 14-001 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01. 

Lastly, the 5% allotment for GAD budget be followed to maximize GAD projects and activities.
observations.managementAction: Exert continuous effort in monitoring the function of the GAD Focal Point System. The GAD programs and activities set forth will be implemented. GPB will be submitted on time and Accomplishment Report will be submitted to oversight agencies.
observations.reason: Lack of monitoring in the implementation of GAD programs and activities

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality establish the Local Council for the Protection of Children and separately provide the required allocation of 1% of the IRA to be used for the strengthening and implementation of allowable programs, projects and activities of the LCPC, which are consistent with the approved annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, and Local Investment Plan and likewise, to ensure proper coordination and assistance in the adoption and implementation of the comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention.
observations.managementAction: Coordinate with the concerned offices to strengthen the use and implementation of the 1% LCPC budget.
observations.reason: The municipality was not able to prioritize the establishment of local council for protection of children and its LCPC plan, however, it has already existing programs and services for children.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to submit copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders and their complete supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) days after their perfection for purposes of review of said contracts and technical evaluation by COA TAS.
observations.managementAction: Designate a GSO that will handle the submission of POs and other supporting documents of all perfected contracts. Also, coordinate with the head of the offices that these documents be submitted immediately to the GSO so that the same will have enough time to forward to COA.
observations.reason: No designated GSO

observations.recommendation: We reiterated compliance to the audit recommendations offered in the previous audit reports which primarily dealt on the assurance of conducting physical count of LGU properties and submitting updated RPCPPE to the auditor concerned.
observations.reason: Please see No. 1

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to prepare the updated bank reconciliation statements for all funds and submit the same to COA for verification. The same will be used as basis to ascertain and validate the cash in bank balances recorded in the Financial Statements for CY 2016.
observations.reason: Please see No. 2

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the General Services Officer to submit copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders and their complete supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) days after their perfection for purposes of review of said contracts and technical evaluation by COA TAS.
observations.reason: Please see no. 12

observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 | The Municipal Accounting Office will send demand letters to officers and employees with outstanding cash advances after the submission of Financial Reports. The Municipal Accounting Office is deducting the 13th and 14th month pay for those officers and employees with outstanding cash advances.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accounting Office was not able to send demand letters to officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances. The net take home pay of the officers and employees will be below the required NTHP if the full outstanding cash advances will be deducted, thus only minimal amount was deducted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to instruct the Accounting to exert more efforts in the retrieval of prior years’ records to identify the accountable officers/employees/debtors or concerned agencies liable for the unaccounted balance of P1,106,438.33 and, if appropriate, file request for authority to write-off for claims which could no longer be accounted. We also recommended to require the Accounting to send demand letters to the identified accountable officer/employee or recipient agency of other receivables.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 | The Municipal Accounting Office will continue to retrieve prior year’s file.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accounting Office is still in process of digging the prior years’ file and since the file is very old, the possibility of identifying the responsible person is remote. Reiterated in Findings No. 1.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to observe the required schedule of submission of the monthly Financial Statements, together with the SAAOB, journals and the disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents as required in Section 347 of RA No. 7160 and Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006, for timely verification of transactions and accounts.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 | The Municipal Accounting Office is exerting efforts and creating systems to comply with the deadlines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to management to direct the Municipal Accountant to provide the lacking disclosures and to strictly adhere to the disclosure requirements of PPSAS in the Notes to Financial Statements and ensure that the financial reports can be easily understood by the intended users.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 | The Municipal Accounting Office will prepare the necessary  Notes to Financial statements in 2018 FS
observations.reason: Adequate disclosures of material balances and significant changes of account balances were not yet prepared.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:
1. Reconcile the records between Assessor and Accounting Office on
the 29 lots owned by the City government;
2. Make the necessary adjustment by recording the land account at
acquisition cost, or fair value, if acquired through non-exchange
transaction, based on available supporting documents;
3. Update the Tax Declarations of land, particularly the declared
owner to the account of City Government of San Fernando and
reclassify the properties according to its actual use, as necessary;
and
4. Transfer the ownership of the barangay health and wellness and
multi-purpose hall to the various barangays which are not held for
use by the City government and make necessary entries to properly
present the City’s Property Plant and Equipment.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the City
Treasurer:
a. Reconcile his cashbooks with that of the subsidiary ledgers of the
accounting office documented by Reconciliation Statement;
b. Make necessary adjustments in his cashbooks particularly the Cash in
Local Treasury account based on the reconciliation statement to be
made and must be written in ballpen and included in the total balances;
c. Deposit all remaining balances of collections if there still any after
reconciliation;
d. Certify in the Report of Collection and Deposit based on the actual
amount of cash received so that under/over-remittances could be easily
traced; and
e. Update his cashbooks daily and footings shall not only be limited to
total collections and deposits but moreso to the balances reflected at the
end of each day and month.

We have recommended Management to:
a. Discontinue the practice of transferring the custody of cash to other
personnel other than to whom the cash advance was granted;
b. Require the Accountable Officers concerned to maintain separate
cashbooks for cash advances granted to them and record the cash
advances granted and liquidation; and
c. Strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 on the Rules and
Regulations on the Granting, Utilization and Liquidation of Cash
Advances.

We have recommended that the City Mayor through the Program and
Monitoring Executive Committee of the Fishermen’s Village:
1. Reinforce collections by sending out demand letters for delinquent
beneficiaries/vendees as provided for in the Conditional Deed of
Sale;
2. Schedule a dialogue with the beneficiaries/vendees to engage their
attention on the matter, particularly those who succeeded
occupancy following the death of the original beneficiary/vendee;
and
3. Consider the inclusion of the City Treasurer as member of the
Program Committee and delegate as member-in-charge of
collection to better monitor the collectability of the receivables.

We have recommended that Management:
a) Decide on what vacant positions to hire for the year;
b) Include only the identified positions to be employed in the
preparation of the budget so that funds will be utilized for more
valuable programs/projects/activities that could cater a substantial
number of beneficiaries;
c) Identify factors contributory to the non-application for vacancies
posted;
d) Consider the current COS in the selection of personnel for
employment; and
e) Prioritize hiring vacancies in the Office of the City Health Officer
in order to promptly address the needs of the constituents and also
in preparation for the City government’s vision by 2020.

c. Designation Orders of OICs not renewed after a year and exceeded the two-year
period

We have recommended that Management monitor the issuances of
designation orders to fully comply with the provisions of the 2017 ORAOHRA.
In addition, renew designation order, designate a new OIC, or appoint a
department head to ensure optimal performance of every office or department.

We have recommended that the City revert/refund to the 20%
Development Fund the amount of ₱78,211,893.43 from the General Fund,
representing total appropriations not allowed to be charged under the DF and
will be subject for re-appropriation to finance development projects otherwise
the payment shall be disallowed in audit.

Thereafter, refrain from using the Development Fund for purposes other
than those provided under item 3.0 of the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-01
dated February 22, 2017. Prioritize investing in high-impact development
projects in the utilization of the development fund that would benefit majority
of the constituents.

Lastly, avoid including names of elected officials in the identified
programs/projects/activities of the City as provided under DILG Memorandum
Circular No. 2010-101.

We have recommended that Management revert to the General Fund the
CY 2011 unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in compliance with Section 5.1.13
of the COA Circular 2012-002 in order for it to be incorporated in the planning
of programs/projects/activities for social services.

We recommended that Management revisit and abide with the terms and
conditions embodied in the Memorandum of Agreement and agreed upon by
both parties. For future fund transfers, assign a focal person who will oversee
the implementation of the program to avoid delays.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the following:
1. The LSB to see to it that no lump sum appropriations be included in the
Annual Budget of SEF. Further, to provide justification on the excess of
expenditures over appropriation and so with charges of MOOE and CO
not in line with the nature of these expenditures.
2. The Bids and Awards Committee to require the LSB to formulate their
own PPMP and the same be included in the APP of the LGU.

We likewise recommended that appropriation and utilization of the
Special Education Fund be in line with the provisions of DepEd, DBM and DILG
Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and so with RA9184 and other applicable laws, rules
and procedures.

We have recommended that Management:
1. Strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the City GAD
database to ensure an updated and operational database, which will better
serve the GFPS in attaining an evidence-based GAD planning and
policymaking.
2. Comply with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular
2013-01 and 2016-01 on the establishment of a gender responsive
monitoring and evaluation system through the creation of GAD M&E
Team who shall evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies,
programs, and projects.

We recommended that Management strictly implement City Ordinance
No. 2003-007 in conformity with RA 9003, particularly:
1. Waste segregation at source be strongly monitored (The City to partner
with barangay personnel to monitor every household);
2. Schedule of garbage collection with regard to biodegradable, non-
biodegradable and recyclables likewise be strictly implemented (The City
to strategize techniques and methods like provision of personnel to MRFs
or dumping areas to oversee that residents dump wastes that are properly
segregated); and
3. Penalty be strictly enforced to violators.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City
Treasurer to:
1. Require the eight (8) suppliers/contractors to pay the correct amount of
business taxes totaling to ₱6,731,377.79 and the corresponding fines and
penalties due to the City Government;
2. Compel the 8,026 business establishments to submit a copy of their 2017
Annual Income Tax Return, compute the correct business tax and collect
tax deficiency, as well as fines and penalties, if the amount declared in the
Permit Application form is lower than the amount appearing in the 2017
Annual Income Tax Return;
3. Henceforth, strictly enforce Section 2A.02A and D of the City of San
Fernando Revenue Code requiring the taxpayers to submit a copy of the
Annual Income Tax Return and impose surcharge and interest provided
in the said Code any false entry resulting to the underpayment of the taxes
or require alternative documents such as monthly copies of VAT returns
of the preceding year as basis for the assessment of gross sales/receipts
while awaiting for the copy of Income Tax Return; and
4. Exercise the visitorial function provided under Section 2A.04 of the
Revenue Code on these businesses to check on the accuracy of the
assessment and collection of taxes, in view of the significant differences in
the declared gross receipts against the Income Tax Return filed with the
Bureau of Internal Revenue.

Also, we recommended that Management provide the Audit Team the
database of taxpayers and view access of the system in order to facilitate the
conduct of Revenue Audit and help the City Government in improving their
collection efficiency.

Lastly, align the Citizens’ Charter with the provisions of the Revenue
Code.

We have recommended that management require the City Assessor to
prepare the SMV pursuant to Section 212 of the Local Government Code of 1991
and cause the passage of an Ordinance incorporating therein the proposed SMV
as basis for the conduct of general revision of real property assessments in
adherence to Section 219 of the same Act and DILG-DOF JMC No. 2010-001.
Thereafter, require the City Assessor to conduct general revision of real
property assessments every after three years as provided under Section 219 of
RA No. 7160.

Based from the foregoing, we have recommended that Management:
a. Ensure and monitor the full implementation of the
programs/projects/activities appropriated in the Annual and
Supplemental budget in order to address the needs of the constituents;
b. Ensure strict observance of the provisions of Section 54.1 of the Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations or Republic Act No. 9184 in the
procurement of goods and COA Circular No. 76-41 dated July 30, 1976,
with a view of attaining efficiency, economy and effectiveness of City
Government’s operations;
c. Consider the general policy of the government in the acquisition of brand
new vehicles by outright purchase in order to avoid costly repairs and
maintenance on reconditioned vehicles in the long run; and
d. If it could not be avoided, in the future and the City Government still
needs to procure second-hand or reconditioned vehicle, ensure that
minimum requirements as provided under pertinent provisions of NBC
Nos. 446 and 446-A.

Further, the Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working
Group conduct Post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all
statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest
Calculated Bid. Lastly, ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-
depth results on the verification of technical requirements.

We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee and the
Technical Working Group judiciously conduct post-qualification by verifying,
validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by
the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification
Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements to
avoid future legal or administrative sanctions as provided under RA No. 9184.

In view hereof, we have recommended that:
1. The BAC Secretariat to see to it the Annual Procurement Plan depicts
the entirety of procurement activities of the LGU and that the PPMPs
of end-users were consolidated accordingly and for the Budget Office
to see to it that these coincide with the approved budget of the LGU.
2. Several purchases be consolidated and undertaken through the
competitive bidding. We require then the BAC to submit justifications
on the piecemeal procurement undertaken in the medical, dental and
laboratory supplies.
3. Improve the procurement process and see to it that procurement under
alternative modes of procurement will expedite process as compared to
public bidding.
4. Compute for the appropriate penalty of the supplier and to require
them to refund the amount. Further, the delay incurred by these
suppliers may be considered in ascertaining the responsiveness of the
supplier before awarding contracts.

We have recommended that Bids and Awards Committee refrain from
transacting with the City Government’s employees’ cooperatives, the AKAMPC
in line with Republic Acts 6713 and 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 08-2017.
Further, the Bids and Awards Committee to require all the departments
to prepare PPMP and consolidate the same in order to come-up with Annual
Procurement Plan. As soon as the Annual Budget is enacted and if there is
Supplemental Budget, the PPMP and APP must be prepared/amended/updated.
Henceforth, strict adherence to the provisions of the above-mentioned
laws and regulations which enjoin all public official or employee to avoid conflict
of interest at all times.

In view thereof, we have recommended that suspension and resumption
orders be provided with the audit team within five days upon its perfection.
Further, we recommend that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to submit
PAG-ASA Weather Reports during suspensions of work of the said projects and
documents to support other reasons stated in the suspension orders. Further, the suspension and
resumption orders together with its supporting documents of the projects
indicated in the attached schedule be likewise submitted.

We have further recommended that in the absence of such documents,
the City Engineer be directed to compute Liquidated Damages and require the
contractor to pay the same to the City Government.

We have recommended that the City Mayor direct the contractors to
refund excess payment in the amount of ₱488,012.18. We have likewise
recommended that Variation Orders be likewise submitted within the five days
upon its perfection.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) reclassify the current portion of the long-term loans payable of ₱26,580,089.28 to Current Liabilities; (b) present these payables under the Current Liabilities category in the financial statements; and (c) ensure full compliance with the provisions cited under PPSAS No.1.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) adjust the books and capitalize the borrowing costs incurred in the improvement and rehabilitation of the New Public Market and construction of various school buildings; and (b) disclose the necessary details in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive require the continuous investigation of the properties not found and take appropriate actions against personnel/officials accountable thereon.
We also reiterated our recommendations that the LCE direct the City Accountant to (a) ensure that all government property officially received by the City are accounted for and recognized in the books at acquisition cost or at appraised value, as applicable; and (b) adopt all the principles prescribed for proper accounting of the City’s PPE for a more reliable information on PPE and fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements.
Management had already instructed the officials concerned to facilitate the necessary actions in processing the transfer of titles of properties.  Further, accounts misclassified will be adjusted for proper reclassification.  Management also reported that continuous updating of account and cleansing of records carried in the books since the 80s and 90s are being done to reflect a more accurate PPE accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require the inventory team to (a) conduct physical count of all inventory items regularly at the end of every semester and render a report thereon within the prescribed deadline; and (b) continue their effort in reconciling the records of the CGSO and the Accounting records, investigate the cause of the discrepancy and take appropriate actions to resolve the problem.
We also recommended that the City Accountant ensure the accuracy of the cost of the Inventories recognized at year-end and presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) conduct periodic review of all outstanding payables to ensure that any invalid claim which had been outstanding for more than two years and without actual claim, administrative or judicial, or which were not covered by perfected contracts or records are reverted to the General Fund; and (b) ensure the timely and complete recording of valid accounts payable in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Accounting Office to (a) exercise prudence and due diligence in the processing of transactions pertaining to the issuance of cash advances; (b) require the disbursing officer to liquidate the cash advances on time; (c) ensure that no cash advances are granted unless the previous ones are liquidated; (d) refrain from granting cash advances to the DepEd official who is not an employee of the City; (e) see to it that the maximum cash accountability set for an accountable officer is not exceeded to safeguard the interest of the City; and (e) abide with all applicable laws, rules and regulations affecting cash advances and disbursement of funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GPBP focal person of the respective implementing offices to (a) fast tract and complete the remaining projects and strictly monitor the implementation of projects as targeted and planned; (b) take note of any gap for future inputs especially on the delay of implementation and completion of GPBP-funded projects and programs; and (c) consider returning the funds to source agencies if project completion is futile.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) CEO to complete the remaining projects in the AIP for CY 2018; (b) City Engineering Office and City Planning and Development Officer to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period; and (c) Planning and Development Coordinator to coordinate with the CBO and CEO regarding the utilization of  the 20% DF and prepare accurate year-end report thereon showing actual completed projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) CDRRMC to prepare a comprehensive and detailed LDRRMFIP that will address risk reduction and management system effectively. It should be in accordance with the provisions of R.A. No. 10121 and COA Circular 2012-002 to meet the needs for prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery of the Local Government. PPAs shall be listed specifically and allotment for each should be properly classified as either MOOE or CO. It shall also be prepared in the format prescribed by the same Circular and shall include the details of the projects charged to the previous years’ unexpended balance of the DRRMF; (b) CDRRMO to strictly comply with the submission of monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the Audit Team for timely verification and to prepare the report in accordance with the format prescribed under COA Circular 2012-002 with emphasis on the inclusion of balances of previous years’ appropriation transferred to the Special Trust Fund and the utilization thereof; (c) City Accountant to follow the provisions of COA Circular 2012-002 as regards the reporting requirements for the DRRMF, specifically the proper transfer of previous year’s unexpended balances of the DRRMF to the Trust Fund. Only the expended balances of DRRMF for QRF and MOOE shall be transferred to the Trust Liability account and the DRRMF for Capital Outlay made continuing in the General Fund books until the projects funded therefrom are completed; and (d) City Budget Officer to maintain separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for the DRRMF on the following: (i) RAAO - for Quick Response Fund; (ii) RAAO- for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses; and (iii) RAAO- for Capital Outlay.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board (a) ensure that the piles  of garbage stocked at the CTS be disposed of immediately to the designated landfill; (b) intensify the campaign to enjoin majority of the households’ participation on waste segregation, collection and disposal at the barangay level; (c) coordinate with the barangay officials to conduct information campaign  on the ill practices of throwing garbage along the barangay roads; and (d) strictly enforce sanctions among the violators of the Ecological Law.

We recommended that the (a) Local Health Board and/or the City Health Office prepare a PhilHealth Per Family Payment Rate Fund Utilization and Program Plan; (b) allocate funds to RHUs based on the needs and necessity of each through careful evaluation and planning; and (c) City Health Office abide by the provisions of PhilHealth Circular No. 10, s. 2012 on the proper disposition and allocation of the Fund.

We recommended that the City Local School Board (a) follow the schedule of activities and use the correct format in the preparation of the SEF budget as mandated in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017; (b) utilize the Special Education Fund only for purposes explicitly allowed in the Joint Circular; and (c) review the terms and conditions in the loan agreement and, if possible, settle in full the outstanding obligations to avoid the incurrence of additional interest expenses.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) City Engineer to  abide by the latest/applicable rules and regulations of the DPWH in the computation of the ABC for infrastructure projects; (b) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), BAC TWG and BAC Secretariat to (i) use the latest GPPB-prescribed Edition of the Philippine Bidding Documents for the succeeding public biddings for the projects of the City of San Fernando; (ii) submit proof that required extension of post-qualification on the Construction of DepEd Training Center at San Isidro; and (iii) ensure that timelines on procurement activities are observed to avoid delay in contract implementation and delivery of services.

We commended the effort of management and recommended that efforts be sustained for continuous and timely distribution of RPT shares to ensure efficient delivery of services of concerned component barangays.

We recommended that the Local Finance Committee (a) make a systematic and realistic forecast or estimate of revenues/income; (b) setup the performance targets on income accounts without previous projections; (c) continue the cleansing of data regarding delinquent taxpayers of real property taxes for more reliable projections; and (d) enforce strategies in collecting RPT for machineries such as the conduct of frequent tax mapping activities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that sufficient internal controls are in place over check disbursements to prevent loss of government funds arising from misuse thereof and require the (a) City Treasurer (CT) to completely include all checks issued whether released or unreleased  in the RCI; (b) City Accountant to ensure completeness of DVs and SDs before check preparation; and (c) CT to release checks only to named payees or their authorized representative with corresponding signature in the check register at all times.

We recommended that management require  (a)  the CSWD office to continue to conduct thorough evaluation of the applicants for the scholarship grant  to limit the grantees to those who are truly indigent and needy; (b) committees concerned review the IRR for the ESAP project considering the changes brought by R.A. No. 10931 by limiting  the number of scholars  enrolled in private universities and colleges; (c) the concerned committee to study the possibility of augmenting  the fund of the City College by re-channeling additional funds from the ESAP and other means  to improve the College sustainability and growth.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, thru the City Administrator (a) require the overall Project Supervisor to (i)  submit written explanation for the lack of intervention for the delay in the delivery of systems to the City; (ii) submit a written explanation on the rehiring of the consultant for enhancement of an incomplete system; (b) direct the City Accountant to hold the processing of claims of the system developer until pending issues are resolved in the City’s favor; (c) impose liquidated damages for the implementation delays of the system developer, as warranted; and (d) ensure that the project works accurately and efficiently before an enhancement is made and additional billings given by the systems developer are accepted.

We recommended that management (a) conduct sufficient intervention to address the possible increase in JOs in the coming years for lack of eligibility of employees who have been continuously hired and rehired by the City for years; (b) limit positions in core offices performing regular functions to those with employer-employee relationship and social protection and equality in benefits with permanent personnel; and (c) require all department heads to carefully evaluate or assess the need for  a job order personnel with due consideration on their fitness to the job requirement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD focal person and technical working group to continue to plan, implement, and evaluate programs and activities which are gender responsive and are compliant with JMC No. 2013-01 to continuously provide socio-economic services to the fund’s intended beneficiaries.

We recommend that the City (a)  allocate in the Annual Budget at least 1% of IRA for LCPC-related programs and activities pursuant to Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344; and (b) maximize the utilization of the 1% of IRA allocation to LCPC-related programs, projects and activities that are relevant, operational and doable.

We commended Management for its compliance and recommended that it sustain its efforts in the continuous and timely withholding and remittance of contributions and payments to the GSIS and compliance with the requirements set forth under Republic Act No. 8291, also known as the GSIS Act of 1997.

We recommended that the City Accountant  continue being compliant in the deduction and remittance of premium contributions, loan amortizations to PAG-IBIG and continue to improve its ledger for the Due to PAG-IBIG account for ease in monitoring, reconciliation and determination of the actual amounts due to PAG-IBIG.

We recommended that the City Accountant continue being compliant in the deduction and prompt remittance of premium contributions to PhilHealth in accordance with the National Health Insurance Act of 2013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that: (a) the Municipal Accountant monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advance per accountable officer in compliance with Item 5.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and send demand letter for those who failed to liquidate their cash advances after the lapse of the prescribed period, copy furnish the Audit Team in compliance with Item V.2 of COA Memorandum No. 2017-010; (b) the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer comply with Item 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 by processing only the request for cash advance of the officers and employees without an outstanding balance; and (c) the Municipal Accountant reclassify the amount P636,220.57 from the                      Advances to Officers and Employees account to Due from Officers and Employees in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 for proper presentation in the Financial Statements.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to reconcile the results of the physical count as reported in the RPCI and RPCPPE with the records of the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that: (a) the Municipal Accountant record only valid claims against government funds for the goods delivered and services rendered, then, without complete documentary requirements, the total amount of P811,425.52 should be adjusted by debiting the Accounts Payable and crediting Prior Period Adjustment accounts; (b) the Municipal Accountant introduce a correcting entry for the improper recording of unliquidated cash advances for travel amounting to P252,012.91 by debiting Accounts Payable and crediting Prior Period Adjustment accounts; and (c) henceforth, refrain from drawing JEVs for the unliquidated cash advances but make sure that the same are being monitored under the Advances to Officers and Employees account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that: (a) the Municipal Accountant reclassify the Due to NGAs of P107,625.75 to its appropriate account based on its nature in compliance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs; (b) the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to return any unexpended funds after project completion to its respective source agencies in compliance with Item Nos. 4.9 of COA Circular                          No. 94-013; and (c) the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the amount of P12,644,888.12 to Due to NGAs account and henceforth record to that account all fund transfers with specific purpose from the NGAs. Moreover, prepare subsidiary ledgers for all fund transfers from NGAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Accountant to submit the aforementioned lacking documents in compliance with Item 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Moreover, prioritize the registration and transfer of title of the parcels of land to the municipal government to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the government. Henceforth, all claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation as required under Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to comply with DOF Department Order No. 27-05 and Department Circular No. 001-2015 relative to a sound and efficient management of government funds by transferring all accounts from private bank to AGDB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) continue the practice of implementing GAD-related PPAs and increase the utilization rate of GAD Fund pursuant to Item 4.1.C.1.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and (b) ensure that the GAD TWG attribute the accurate amount to the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with Annex E of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to fast track the implementation of the unexpended DRRM programs, projects and activities; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the amount and details of previous years' unexpended balance of LDRRMF in compliance with Item 5.1.16 and                Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) instruct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) to fast track the implementation of identified developmental PPAs and maximize the utilization of 20% Development Fund; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness and veracity of supporting documents prior to processing of claims for payment of the above infrastructure projects in compliance with COA Circular                 No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the BAC to submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team and henceforth adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 in all stages of procurement process and ensure that all bid documents are complete, evaluated and submitted on time; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness and veracity of supporting documents prior to processing of claims for payment of the above infrastructure projects in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to: (a) follow up the approval of the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan by the NSWMC;                 (b) encourage active participation of the 12 barangays to support the SWM program of the municipality particularly the proper segregation wastes; and (c) ensure the establishment of sanitary landfill and require the establishment of MRFs in the barangays in compliance with Republic Act No. 9003 and its IRR.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the General Fund shall consist of monies and resources of the local government which are available for the payment of expenditures and obligations, to eliminate the incurrence of service charge and the risk of being prone to the liability of issuing bouncing checks.

We recommended that Management:
provide separate depository accounts for the 20% Development Fund and 5% Calamity Fund, to properly monitor and determine cash availability on the said funds.
request the Sangguniang Bayan to pass a resolution properly authorizing the Municipal Mayor and Treasurer to open a separate bank account for the 20% Development Fund and 5% Calamity Fund.
direct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain separate cashbooks and/or SL intended for the 20% Development Fund and 5% Calamity Fund.

We recommended that Management require the:
Municipal Assessor to review and update the assessment record and seek the assistance of the Municipal Treasurer in reconciling their records to establish a complete and accurate list of taxpayers and the expected receivables due from them;
Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant, based on Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayers’ index card, a duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and
Municipal Accountant that upon receipt of the certified list from the Municipal Treasurer, record the expected revenue to be realized from the Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax at the beginning of each year.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts by taking up immediate adjustments in the books of accounts; and
conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure that discrepancies are immediately identified, reconciled and adjusted.
We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to record the completed projects as assets in the books of the LGU to facilitate fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the deficiencies noted in the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) and carefully check entries for subsequent transactions to minimize errors in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units under COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer:
to conduct physical count of all properties and submit the required RPCPPE;
maintain Property Ledger Cards for each PPE account to facilitate periodic reconciliation with the accounting records; and
issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for all properties, including transfer of properties from one accountable officer to another, and said ARE be renewed every three years, to pinpoint accountability.

We recommended that Management require:
the Municipal Agriculturist to submit detailed report on the actual condition and disposal of the breeding stocks to the Municipal Accountant.
the Municipal Accountant to make the adjusting entry to drop the amount from the books, upon receipt of the report from the Municipal Agricultural Office.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accountant the 32 DVs totaling ₱918,639.12, who shall eventually submit them to COA for audit, to forestall the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended that Management:
require the submission of all supporting documents of transactions identified above to the COA Audit Team to facilitate the final inspection of these projects; and
ensure that complete supporting documents are required on future disbursements in compliance to the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2012-001 to avoid suspensions or disallowances in audit.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and mitigation fund-MOOE for CY 2018, and every year-end thereafter, to Trust Liabilities-DRRM in the Trust Fund books and revert the unutilized amounts of CYs 2010-2013 to unappropriated surplus of the General Fund in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
ensure that proposed projects under the Economic Development Fund be made in detail showing the specific name of project/s, location and corresponding amount; and
that all proposed projects under the fund be fully realized within the budget year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert more time in reviewing records and PPE ledgers pertinent to the mentioned PPE items and compute the depreciation in accordance with the PPSAS and existing COA issuances and make proper adjustments in the accounts, where necessary.

We recommended that the Management:
Exert effort in implementing the recommendations of the Audit Team by creating an Inventory Committee and conducting a complete count of the Municipality’s PPE and prepare the Report as provided by Section 57, Volume II of NGAs Manual and submit the same to the Local Chief Executive and Auditor. The Audit Team would like to emphasize that this has been a subject of repeated recommendations for many years now, yet remains unimplemented;
Require the General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer to maintain records on all properties of the agency; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to properly maintain ledger cards of all properties. This must be reconciled with stock/property cards maintained by the GSO or Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Stop granting Cash Advance for disbursements not allowed by law. Payments must be made directly to payee/supplier through the issuance of check except those expressly allowed under COA Circular 97-002. Cash Advance must only be given or granted to authorized officials as cited also in COA Circular 97-002;
b. Must not allow the grant of additional cash advance if the previous ones remained unliquidated or unsettled; and
c. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to settle/liquidate her accountabilities for unliquidated CAs as at December 31, 2018.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Direct its Officers to immediately forward or submit to the Office of the Auditor the disbursement vouchers totaling ₱44.90 million, without need of further demand; and
b. Exert efforts in identifying causes of the delay or non-submission of Disbursement Vouchers and bring solution to this dilemma.

We recommended that the Management direct its officers to fully implement the GAD activities and the implementation must be monitored regularly by the GFPS. Moreover, we suggest that the GAD Focal Person be more vigilant in performing her responsibility in the monitoring of the implementation of GAD programs/activities. She must also assure that Accomplishment Reports are submitted to the oversight agencies. Further, creation of a Local GAD Code and establishment of VAWs Desk must be also be prioritized.

We recommended that Management:
Must have the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) be reviewed and approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC). Review by the NSWMC of the said plan will assess if it addresses the necessary issues concerning Solid Waste Management in the Municipality. Moreover, necessary requirements are needed for the approval of the SWMP;
Strictly comply with RA No. 9003 on the establishment of solid waste and material recovery facility (MRF); and
Exert continuous effort in educating the people of San Fernando the importance of Solid Waste Management.

We recommended to the Management that disbursement of Special Education Fund (SEF) must be done in accordance with the guidelines and accounting procedures set forth in the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I to effectively monitor and determine whether or not the funds were utilized for its intended purpose and that expenses incurred were appropriately charged to fund. Granting of cash advance and cash payments to suppliers by local school boards or any other school officials must be stopped. Moreover, we require that the lacking Inspection and Acceptance Reports of the various purchases be attached to the liquidation reports.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Comply with the documentary requirements of the four (4) enumerated transactions above together with the Sanggunian Resolution and proof of declaration of State of Calamity.
b. Be cautious in disbursing government fund by adhering to complete documentations with reference to COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, the Revised Guidelines and Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions; and
c. Exercise prudence in recording accounting entries particularly on the proper selection of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipality establish the Local Council for the Protection of Children and separately provide the required allocation of 1% of the IRA to be used for the strengthening and implementation of allowable programs, projects and activities of the LCPC, which are consistent with the approved annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, and Local Investment Plan and likewise, to ensure proper coordination and assistance in the adoption and implementation of the comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention.

We recommended that:
Management require the Municipal Accountant to establish Real Property Tax, Special Education Tax Receivables and Deferred Revenues at the beginning of the year. The figures must be based on the collectibles identified by the Municipal Treasurer on her assessment from the list of taxpayers in the RPT Account Register;
The Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to identify all prior year receivables from real property taxpayers and effect necessary adjustments to present the same in the Financial Statements in CY 2019; and
The Management release the shares of the Province and Barangays from its RPT collections without need of any further action.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Require all drivers and concerned officials to completely fill up the Driver’s Trip ticket in accordance with existing regulation for transparency and accountability over the use of such government motor vehicles; and
b. 	Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that the Management:
a. The Management stop granting a monthly gasoline allowance to any LGU Official who are also receiving Representation and Travelling Allowance (RATA); and
b. Exercise caution in the disbursement of fund by adhering to rules and regulations to avoid those Irregular, Unnecessary, Excessive, Extravagant, or Unconscionable (IUEEU) expenditures.

We recommended that the Management submit the lacking documents to the Office of the Auditor pertinent to the mentioned transaction. Also, require the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly observe the applicable provisions in the procurement of drugs and medicines provided under RA 3720 as amended and Executive Order No. 49 and other pertinent laws. Also, instruct the accounting office to require adequate documentation on each transaction involving procurement of goods before payment is effected.

We recommended that the Management direct its officers to immediately release/remit all the balance of 2018 GSIS contribution so as to avoid further penalties. Moreover, the agency must stop the practice of accumulating the balance before it is remitted. Prompt remittance must be adopted to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that Management abide by the rules and regulations on the submission of the copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders and their complete supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) days after their perfection for purposes of review of said contracts and technical evaluation by COA TAS. The Audit Team emphasizes that this finding has been reiterated for several years now.

We recommended strict compliance with Sections 32 of the Manual  on  the
NGAS for Local Government Units.  The  Municipal  Treasurer  should  deposit
intact his collections as well as all collections turned over to him by  the
collectors/tellers with the authorized depository bank daily  or  not  later
than the next banking day to prevent possible loss or misuse  of  government
funds.

It is recommended that a physical count of all PPE be conducted by the
Inventory  Committee  annually  and  a  copy  of  the  inventory  report  be
submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.


      It is also recommended that  the  General  Services  Officer  and  the
Accountant maintain property ledger cards to facilitate  the  reconciliation
between inventory and accounting records.

We recommended  refund  of  the  excess  payment  of  honoraria  by  the
claimants. The Municipal Accountant  should  immediately  stop  paying  full
amount of honoraria to Sangguniang Bayan Members who is unable to attend
the required four (4) regular monthly sessions.  A  complete  attendance  in
the required regular sessions will entitle them to full honoraria.

We recommended that the payment of social pension to  indigent  senior
citizens be made strictly in accordance  with  pertinent  provisions  of  RA
9994, DSWDO AO #15, s. of 2010, MC #25, s. of 2014 and MC #17, s of 2015.


      We also recommended  that  the  Special  Disbursing  Officer/Municipal
Treasurer should not be allowed to draw cash advances for  payroll  fund  in
excess of the net amount of the duly certified and approved  payrolls.   Any
unused cash  should  be  returned  to  the  Treasurer  who  will  issue  the
corresponding Official Receipt.

We recommended  that  the  Management  ensure  that  the  program  and
activities of the 20% Development Fund and 5%  DRRM  budget  be  implemented
and optimized for the  benefit  of  their  constituents.   Furthermore,  the
Municipal Officials should exercise prudence in the use of its limited  cash
resources to ensure that adequate funds are maintained to  finance  all  the
intended projects of the municipal government.

We recommended that management require  the  Municipal  Accountant  to
maintain complete subsidiary ledgers and prepare  the  respective  financial
statements of each                 special account in the  General  Fund  to
provide them adequate  information  in  determining  the  profitability  and
viability of operating such economic enterprises.
      We also recommended strict compliance with Sections 04.c, 106, 107 and
110, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual and Section 313 of RA 7160.

We recommended refund of  the  P5,000.00  CY  2018  PEI  paid  to  the
Municipal Vice-Mayor. The  Municipality  should  strictly  comply  with  the
guidelines in the grant of PEI.

We recommended that management instruct the Accounting in Charge to exert more efforts in the retrieval of prior years’ records to identify the accountable officers/employees/debtors or concerned agencies liable for the unaccounted balance of ₱1,639,580.81 and, if appropriate, file request for authority to write-off for claims which could no longer be accounted. We also recommended to require the Accounting to send demand letters to the identified accountable officer/employee or recipient agency of other receivables.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to recognize liability only to claims with complete supporting documents in order to sufficiently establish the existence and valuation of the obligations as of reporting period in accordance PPSAS I and the IRR of RA 7160.

We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking disbursement vouchers, journal entry vouchers, payroll and collection reports in consistent with the existing rules and regulations and in order to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended and management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to observe the proper recording of retention money and mobilization payments to private contractors in order to present fairly the value of PPE, liability and advances to contractors account in the financial statements.

We recommended and management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the lacking disbursement vouchers totaling P351,926.42 to the Municipal Accounting Office for recording.
Require the Municipal Accountant to account the unrecorded disbursement voucher upon receipt, and submit the same to the audit team for audit and custody.

We recommended and management agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to conduct a reconciliation of the Cashbooks – Cash in Bank and the Subsidiary Ledgers and take up the adjustments to reconcile both books; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile his cashbook balances every end of the month or as the need arises with the subsidiary accounting records.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that management direct the Municipal Accountant to provide the lacking disclosures and to strictly adhere to the disclosure requirements of PPSAS in the Notes to Financial Statements and ensure that the financial reports can be easily understood by the intended users.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to observe the required schedule of submission of the monthly Financial Statements, together with the SAAOB, journals and the disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents as required in Section 347 of RA No. 7160 and Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006, for timely verification of transactions and accounts.

a. Exhaust all able to collect the receivables such as sending the last known addresses.
b. Exert efforts to locate the documents to support the claims and ascertain accuracy and fair presentation of the receivable accounts.
Further, the City Government establish a sound internal control on the collection and recording of receivables.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2017 Monitoring of cash advances of the LGU showed the management was able to liquidate cash advances of the employees within the prescribed period, however, long outstanding receivables remained not have documents to support it. The management was not able to address the audit recommendation.
Reports on the Receivables were submitted every semester as per requirement of COA.
observations.reason: Long outstanding receivables remained not have documents to support it. The management was not able to address the audit recommendation.

observations.managementAction: AAR 2017

AAR 2017 The CDRRM Officer now holds the position of LDRRM Officer III as a requirement to component cities. Further, the Management complied the following: a. Reports of Utilization of LDRRMF were already submitted monthly; b. Emergency Evacuation routes and maps were already visible in the City; c. Standby food packs were being stored/maintained d. Evacuation center was set to be at the 4th Floor Marcos Building; e. Although Business Continuity Plan was not prepared, we have already conducted orientation and the preparation of which comes after f. No training on Management of the Dead and Missing was conducted, however, the personnel concerned have attended the Orientation during the Summit Resiliency facilitated by DILG which were joined by different government agencies.

observations.recommendation: For the Officiating and Delegation Officials to submit Certification from their Agency Accountant that no traveling expenses (which includes meals and incidental expenses) were claimed by them and for the concerned officials to refund the amount totaling to ₱63,000.00.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 The amount of ₱63,000.00 was already refunded.

observations.recommendation:
a. The Department Heads ensure that all entries in the DTRs are filled-out (leaves, holidays, travels/seminars must all be indicated) and for the Accounting Department to see to it that the Application for Leave, Orders and necessary supporting documents are attached to DTRs.
b. The HRMO to submit to this Office, certified photocopy of employees leave card as of December 31, 2017, prioritizing the employees mentioned in the sampling schedule. Further, we recommend that the HRMO certify balances of leave credits in the Application for Leave as of the month preceding the month when the employee applied for a leave.
c. The Local Chief Executive to grant monetization of leave credits judiciously especially that inconsistencies of balances of leave credits were observed. Further, we recommend for the City Administrator or his representative to review records and look into the reasons of inconsistent balances and that sanctions be imposed to those officials and employees responsible for the inconsistent balances.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 Post-audit of transactions still revealed that DTRs were not properly filled-out. Further, grants of monetization casted doubt on its validity (there were grants that exceeded their leave credits). Interview however revealed that there were inconsistencies in the application for leave however, the leave card of employees shows otherwise. Moreover, some certified copies of employees’ leave cards as mentioned in the observations were not yet submitted to the audit team for verification.
observations.reason: Post-audit of transactions still revealed that DTRs were not properly filled-out. Some certified copies of employees’ leave cards as mentioned in the observations were not yet submitted to the audit team for verification.

observations.recommendation: For the consultant to refund the amount of ₱10,495.00 for the hotel accommodation and meals. Provisions of the Revised IRR be strictly adhered to. Advance Payment be strictly not to exceed 15% of the contract amount and for the LGU to compel the posting of security.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 Much as they want to produce more, they do not have the stable market. They were compelled to look for alternative jobs. No consultant was availed by the Management this CY 2018 for the conduct of the “Lakas ng Pagbabago” Program of the LGU. The Management however refunded the amount of ₱10,495.00 for the hotel accommodation and meals.

observations.recommendation:
a. Properly accomplish the Vehicle Trip Tickets for every gasoline requisition/purchase because speedometers of some of the vehicles of government vehicle were not functioning, thus, details cannot be provided in the DTT. The Trip Ticket should be prenumbered with the inclusion of certification from passenger that he used the vehicle for authorized purpose.
b. Be guided by the average minimum and maximum gasoline and oil consumption of the different types of motor vehicles in authorizing gasoline issuance.
c. Prepare and submit within the prescribed period the Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report to closely monitor and control the fuel consumption.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 Due to the charge mentioned the person-in-charge mentioned that DTTs were not properly accomplished. The Trip Ticket because speedometers of some of the vehicles were not functioning, thus, details cannot be provided in the DTT. The Trip Ticket should be prenumbered with the inclusion of certification from passenger that he used the vehicle for authorized purpose. The Trip Ticket should be prenumbered with the inclusion of certification from passenger that he used the vehicle for authorized purpose. The Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption were not still submitted because the person-in-charge was not aware of the requirement. The new person-in-charge however explained that he was able to prepare Monthly Fuel Consumption for the months of September to December, 2018 but was unable to furnish copy to the office of the auditor.
observations.reason: Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption were not still submitted because the person-in-charge was not aware of the requirement. The new person-in-charge prepared reports for September to December 2018 but was unable to furnish copy to the auditor.

observations.recommendation:
Specifically:
 a. Financial assistance is to be given for public purposes only and not for funding of specific activities of private groups and individuals as provided in Sections 4 (2) of PD 1445 and 335 of RA 7160. Further, the guidelines of COA Circular No. 2007-001 be followed in the release of funds to NGOs/POs.
 b. Assistance in the form of goods, supplies or items shall not be granted through cash assistance to ensure compliance of rules and regulations of procurement law.
 c. The City Government ensure actual utilization of assistance given and require submission of Fund Utilization Report especially to assistance made to government agencies. Likewise, assistance be not made to representatives of government offices and instead, procedures be followed in the liquidation or disbursement of government transactions.
 d. Endorsements of the members SP need not be the basis in the grant of financial assistance to ensure equitable distribution of financial assistance among qualified beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2017 Due to the Notices of Disallowance issued by the COA on the grant of financial assistance, the management limited the grant of financial assistance which are to be given for public purposes and those covered in Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS). The management was able to refund the said disallowance. However, another set of NDs were issued by the audit team, however, these transactions pertain to CY 2017 that were only paid this CY 2018.
observations.reason: Management refunded the disallowance; however another set of NDs pertain to CY 2017 paid in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive: 

direct the City Accountant to: (i) record the land and other properties that remained nor recognized in the books; (ii) classify the PPE accounts to its proper category/group; (iii) use the EUL in the computation of depreciation expense as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2003-007; (iv) adopt the principles prescribed in PPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the City’s PPE; 

require the official concerned to facilitate the transfer of title of all the City properties to recognize the ownership thereof; and

instruct the CGSO and CAO to effect the reconciliation of all PPE accounts.

We also reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive require the:

investigation of the properties not found during the physical count and take appropriate actions against accountable personnel/officials as warranted; and

Property Team to identify additional personnel to reinforce the inventory team to pave the way for the completion of the physical count of all properties for a more reliable information on PPE and fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements at year-end.
observations.managementAction: Management informed the Audit Team that a special and detailed inventory taking will be conducted to validate the veracity of property records. In addition. The City Accountant will facilitate the transfer of properties in the name of the City. Adjusting entries were also taken up under JEV No. 100-18-01 for the reclassification of identified PPEs to their appropriate accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require:

the inventory team to: (i) conduct physical count of all inventory items regularly at the end of every semester and render a report thereon within the prescribed deadline; and (ii) reconcile the records of the CGSO with the Accounting records on a per category basis; and

all offices to request only for the required quantity of supplies to avoid obsolescence of goods, or loss due to misuse or possible theft.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with our recommendations and committed that the quantity of supplies to be procured will be strictly controlled and monitored to avoid over stocking of items. Also, reconciliation of goods between the CGSO and Accounting Office will be done to come up with a more reliable and accurate inventory account balance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant continue to: 

review timely all outstanding payables to ensure that cancelled, invalid or waived obligations are removed from the account reverted to the General Fund and which had been outstanding for more than two years and without actual claims, administrative or judicial, or which were not covered by perfected contracts or records; and 


maintain an aging of accounts payable to facilitate proper monitoring of the movement of the Accounts Payable.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the City Accountant follow the check disbursement process stipulated in Section 44 of the NGAS Manual and ensure the recognition of accounts payable is based on valid claims supported with complete documentation and approved DVs.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with our recommendations. The City Accountant’s Office (CAO) signified to include the “Disbursement Process” aligned under ISO 9001:2015 to standardize the processes that are requisites for payment of obligation. An initial meeting is scheduled in March 2018 regarding the said innovation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant ensure the timely submission of cash advance vouchers for Confidential Fund along with the pertinent documents to enable auditor for a timely review in the grant thereof as well as the immediate submission of liquidation documents to the ICFAU at the COA Central Office pursuant to Sections 6.1.8.4 and 6.2.2 of COA-DBM-DILG-GOCC-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant assured the Audit Team of full compliance with the timely submission of cash advance vouchers together with the pertinent documents to the Audit Team for an appropriate review in the granting thereof and to facilitate the prompt submission of liquidation documents to the ICFAU at the COA Central Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE:

require the Disposal Committee to proceed with the prompt disposal of unserviceable assets in accordance with prescribed procedures; and

direct the City General Services Officer to ensure that all government properties no longer serviceable are disposed of at the earliest opportunity to prevent further deterioration and reduction in their economic value.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO has already filed an application for disposal of all unserviceable properties which was submitted to the Audit Team on February 21, 2018 awaiting proper disposition thereon.

The City Engineering Office (CEO) committed compliance with the latest Department Orders of the DPWH. All POWs prepared will be in accordance with the new Dos issued by the aforesaid department. The said office will also coordinate with the DPWH to be abreast with the new guidelines for the procurement and implementation of infrastructure projects. In addition, the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), along with its Technical Working Group (TWG) and Secretariat concurred with all our recommendations and assured the Audit Team on full compliance with the Revised RA 9184 and its RIRR to attain transparency in the procurement of government projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Local Chief Executive: 

require the CEO to complete the remaining projects in the AIP for CY 2017;

direct the City Engineering Office and City Planning and Development Officer to undertake the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period; and

require the Planning and Development Coordinator to coordinate with the CBO and CEO regarding the utilization of 20% DF and prepare accurate year-end report thereon showing actual completed projects.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the preparation of Program of Works and the tedious process of procurement caused the delay in the implementation of the projects at hand for CY 2017. The CEO will revise its Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) for CY 2018 to come up with a more realistic implementation date so that proposed projects during the year will be implemented and subsequently completed during the 1st and 3rd quarter of the year. The CEO will also provide a monthly status report of projects to the City Accounting Office to facilitate monitoring and reporting of actual completed project.

To fully account for all the City’s programs and services for the senior citizens and PWD sector, the CSWD office will develop tracking mechanism to be utilized by all program implementers and will present annual accomplishment as to the attainment of goals for both sectors during National Advocacy Celebrations. Moreover, social marketing plans and advocacy materials will be developed to continuously promote CSWDO services. The CSWDO will also conduct mapping and validation of client/sectoral database to identify and update status of indigency and eligibility of clients in the database.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management on its continuous efforts in addressing disaster risk reduction and management and climate change issues by substantially adhering to the provisions of RA 10121, DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-079 and NDRRMC Memorandum Circular No.4, s. 2012. On the other hand, we recommended that the Management:

prioritize the establishment of permanent evacuation centers and warehouse dedicated only for DRRM use to be fully disaster-ready at all times; and

comply with the essential facilities listed in the provisions of Unnumbered Joint Memorandum Circular of DILG, DSWD, DOH and DepEd series of 2013 entitled “Guidelines on Evacuation Center Coordination and Management” in the establishment of evacuation centers.
observations.managementAction: The establishment of a permanent evacuation center is the major priority of the City to fully address disaster preparedness. This ensuing year, management committed that the said project will be implemented to temporary house and give shelter to the affected Fernandinos during calamities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management settle the audit suspensions within the prescribed period and continue to view claims properly for regularity to prevent audit suspensions and disallowances.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to comply with recommendations

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

perform a thorough planning and due diligence in the procurement of transport and heavy equipment to ensure maximum utilization of public resources;

as far as financial resources allow, refrain from procuring second-hand/reconditioned vehicles to avoid costly repairs and maintenance on reconditioned equipment in the long run; and

strictly adhere to DBM NBC 446-A in the procurement of reconditioned motor vehicles in cases when acquisition of brand new units by outright purchase is not feasible.
observations.managementAction: Management stated that the procurement of 4x4 vehicles was intended for use by the Public Order Safety Coordinating Office (POSCO), a newly created division by the City which was under the CDRRMO. Since the said division was not yet fully established in CY 2017, the-wheel drive vehicles were temporarily stored at the City’s Motorpool. To date, there vehicles were already transferred to POSCO and are fully operational. Management also committed that no additional 4x4 vehicles will be procured without exigent purpose.

maintain individual subsidiary ledgers for each PPE account for proper monitoring and to facilitate periodic reconciliation with the accounting records.

No updated report on physical count was submitted for the year.
