The City Accountant make necessary adjustment by recording the land account at acquisition cost and to reconcile PPE accounts with the records of the General Services Officer. Likewise, for the City Assessor to reclassify the Land account to conform to its actual use.
observations.managementAction: The management was able to start reclassification of land accounts and actual inventory of PPE was conducted by the GSO. Report was then submitted to the Auditor’s Office.
observations.reason:
Reclassification of land account was acted by the agency but failed to target 100%. Likewise, despite reconciliation made between the GSO and Accounting Office, differences were still noted though lesser than last year’s.

Reiterated in Observation No. 1.

observations.recommendation:
Intensify the collection of long outstanding Receivables.

Exert effort to locate the following:
Documents to establish the balance of Due from National Government Agencies account.

Documents to support the negative balances in Other Receivables account in order to ascertain the accuracy of the receivable accounts.
Details of the “unreconciled portion” in the Other Receivables account and list of LSB teachers with their corresponding overpayment of salaries in order to facilitate collection.

Establish a sound internal control on the collection receivables.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant was able to enforce collection to the employees. The agency wishes to request for the write-off of long-outstanding accounts but was not able to submit requirements this CY 2017.
observations.reason:
Submission of Report on Receivables were submitted every semester as per requirement of COA. The agency was only able to collect receivables from the employees who are still connected with the agency amounting to ₱38,703.82. Long outstanding receivables were still in the books of the agency and some do not have documents to support it. The account remains to be doubtful.
Reiterated in Observation No. 2.

observations.recommendation: The City Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure the accuracy of the accounts to ascertain their reliability.
observations.managementAction: Unreconciled accounts were already corrected/adjusted.

observations.recommendation:
All payments be made by check, for effective internal control and to be able to pinpoint responsibility unless it would be impractical to do so.

The practice of transferring cash advances from one Accountable Officer to another be stopped in conformity to the provisions of Section 4.1.6 of COA Circular 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The management said that cash advances were particularly granted for payrolls and travels.
observations.reason:
While payments were made thru checks, the City was not able to fully stop the grant of cash advances of MOOE.

The transfer of cash advances was discontinued except for disbursements under the Special Education Fund.

observations.recommendation:
For the CSWDO to require the preparation and submission of the Fund Utilization Reports of the four (4) associations pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and 2012-001 for them to conduct periodic inspection and monitoring of the livelihood projects to ascertain economy, efficiency and effectiveness of operations.

Require the associations to record their transactions in a cashbook and for the disbursements to be authorized and supported with official receipts.

Conduct assessment/ feasibility study of livelihood project proposals in the future to ascertain their sustainability.

Require the President of Masaganang Samahan ng Ilocanos Sur to immediately deposit the amount of ₱143,447.00 representing withdrawals made which were not accounted for.

Require the President and Treasurer of Saoay Hog Raisers SLPA to submit all documents pertaining to the withdrawals, disbursements and record of sales of the hog raising project for audit.
observations.managementAction: The management has ordered concerned officers/employees to strictly monitor implementation of projects and actual inspection be conducted as well.
observations.reason: This audit observation pertains to four associations that were audited last year wherein funds were released for livelihood projects. Follow-up on the implementation of the audit recommendation revealed that the Masaganang Samahan ng Ilocanos Sur still failed to deposit the unaccounted withdrawals. Two associations in the Swine/Hog Raising incurred losses. Only the Great Bacsil SLPA has continued operating effectively.

observations.recommendation: The City Officials review the Approved 2016 New Staffing Pattern of the City Government to ensure compliance with Section 13 (c) of CSC Memorandum Circular No. 19 and ascertain that all positions created are necessary and needed.  In the interest of efficiency and economy, the functions of any office may be consolidated with those of another.
observations.managementAction: The Staffing Pattern of the City Government is under review.
observations.reason:
Positions which are not provided in RA7160 in a LGU namely Economist II, Investment Specialist and those mentioned in last year’s audit report are still existing.

There is an on-going review of the plantilla of the City Government.

observations.recommendation: The LGU create an Inspectorate Team or Project Monitoring Committee to facilitate extensive monitoring and evaluation of implemented projects having the City Engineer as mandatory member inorder to establish a sound internal control in the implementation of projects. Inspection Reports resulting hereto be used as basis for the issuance of Certificate of Completion. Likewise, close monitoring of projects be undertaken during its construction to ensure compliance with plans and specifications.
observations.managementAction: Inspectorate team was created with members handling inspection depending on the type of transaction.

observations.recommendation:
Only the actual fare for travel be charged against the SEF.

Expenses charged against SEF conform to Sec. 272 of RA 7160 and Paragraph 4.0 of conform DECS (now DepEd), DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 s. 1998.

Liquidation reports be submitted by recipients of cash assistance to be attached as supporting documents to disbursement voucher.

Procurement of goods and services comply with the provisions of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The management will strictly comply with the rules and regulations as recommended by the audit team.
observations.reason: Succeeding travel expenses of LSB were charged to DepEd. However, liquidation papers in support for the cash advance for their activities were not fully complied with disregarding likewise the rules and regulations of RA 9184 in procurement of goods.

observations.recommendation: Policies and procedures in procurement under alternative mode be strictly adhered to. We likewise recommended that Purchase Orders be properly and duly accomplished and require suppliers to strictly adhere to terms and conditions of procured items.
observations.managementAction: Purchase Orders was submitted regularly and policies and procedures in procurement were strictly adhered to.

observations.recommendation: The Head of the Procuring entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Project Monitoring Report approved by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Project Monitoring Report was prepared and submitted as per provisions of RA 9184.

observations.recommendation: The management propose/implement projects in an efficient and effective manner pursuant to Section 2 of PD 1445 to avoid loss or wastage of government funds and property.
observations.managementAction:
The present administration see to it that projects will be efficiently and effectively implement as to avoid wastage of government funds.

 As to the Construction of Food Terminal at Barangay Biday, it was proposed to be used for organic products. Preparation for the action plan/project proposal is on-going.

Likewise, committee has been assigned for the re-study/review for the North Transport Terminal.

observations.recommendation:
Citizen Participatory Audit

For both BHCs:

Availability of drugs and medicines at all times
Period of procurement be observed so that target implementation dates are followed as scheduled
Family planning supplies and other medicines be not DOH dependent and be considered and included in the City’s APP

Provision of above-mentioned supplies and equipment.

Form requirements be adhered to

DOH-mandated courses be attended by personnel

Information dissemination especially to least known programs be conducted

Support to services under cancer, STD/HIV and tuberculosis control program be established, at least on preventive manner.

II. For Poro BHC 
Provision of additional ventilation equipment


III. For Bangbangolan BHC

Consider construction of stairs and/or installation of handrails to Bangbangolan BHC

Properly accomplish Monthly Drugs Inventory Report
observations.managementAction: The City Health Office has complied with the recommendations of the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LDRRMC and CPDO (a) continue their concerted efforts in providing valuable programs and projects through various development plans aligned with the regional plans; and (b) ensure that the yearly updates of the respective plans are committed as well, whenever necessary.
observations.managementAction: The City, through the LDRRM council and the City Planning and Development Office will uphold its initiatives and actions in adapting to environmental changes. Further, the City ensured that plans will be updated and carried out through rational PPAs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (a) CTO and the support offices continue their commitment in complying with existing rules and regulations related to disaster preparedness particularly on the receipt and utilization of in-kind donations; and (b) the General Service Officer and the City Treasurer make the proper communication with the donor on the appropriate action for the spoiled items.
observations.managementAction: The CSWDO will closely coordinate with the CGSO for the inventory of goods, including expired, and near expiry date at Alasas warehouse.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and the concerned offices continue to comply with the appropriate documentation of disbursements relative to the PCL seminars and those pertinent to the Leagues of Cities, Municipalities, Barangays and others.

We recommended that the Accounting Office: (a) continue to abide with the provisions of the GSIS Act of 1997 as regards the remittances of applicable contributions and premiums as well as government share within the prescribed period; and (b) review the prior years’  transactions to eliminate the discrepancy of ₱114,634.60 in the books. The Accounting Office may check with the records whether all Personal Share (PS) deducted from the payroll of employees and the Government Share (GS) and Employee’s Compensation Contribution (ECC) for all employees in previous years were all remitted and cause the immediate remittance to GSIS for the unremitted premiums and contributions of employees.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with our recommendation to continuously abide with the GSIS Act of 1997 and to exert efforts to reconcile the discrepancy on the Due to GSIS account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant and her staff (a) continue being compliant with the revenue regulations on the withholding of taxes from compensation of employees and creditable taxes for payments to suppliers and contractors and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period; and (b) exert immediate efforts to resolve the cause of the discrepancies in the Due to BIR – General Fund account.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to continue being compliant with the revenue regulations on the withholding of taxes from compensation of employees and creditable taxes for payments to suppliers and contractors and remittance thereof within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the (a) investigation of the properties not accounted for during the physical count and take appropriate action against accountable personnel/officials; and (b) completion of the physical count of all assets under PPE and related accounts for improved accountability over the assets and accuracy of the year-end reported balances of the PPE accounts. 

We also recommended that the LCE (a) provide the needed additional personnel to the property team to ensure that property records are complete and updated and conduct of the physical count is accomplished within the prescribed period; and (b) direct the City Accountant to (i) ensure that all government property officially received by the City are accounted for and recognized in the books at acquisition cost or at appraised value, as applicable; (ii) maintain adequate property records for PPE so that relevant details are available to users thereof; and (iii) adopt all the principles prescribed in PPSAS 17 for proper accounting of the City’s PPE.
observations.managementAction: The Property Team committed to continue its effort to regularly conduct physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment including real properties and ensure accurate and complete recording thereof.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant draw the necessary adjusting entry to transfer the balance from the Cash-Local Treasury account to the Due from Officers and Employees account to fairly present balances of affected accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to make the necessary adjustment in the books for a fair presentation of affected accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Treasurer (a) exercise prudence and due diligence in the processing of transactions; (b) require the disbursing officer to liquidate the cash advances on time; (c) ensure that no cash advances are granted unless the previous ones are liquidated; (d) refer to the revised chart of accounts for guidance to avoid misclassification of accounts; and (e) see to it that all applicable laws, rules and regulations affecting cash advances and disbursement of funds are strictly adhered to.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with the liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed period. The City Accounting Office and City Treasurer’s Office modified their system and processes in the granting and liquidation of cash advances. Implementers where given 30 days to liquidate received cash advance.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the (a) Inventory Team (i) conduct physical count of all inventory items regularly at the end of every semester and render a report thereon within the prescribed deadline; and (ii) reconcile the records of the CGSO with the Accounting  records on a per category basis; (b) the CGSO and Accounting Office  (i) ensure the conduct of regular reconciliation of subsidiary records on a quarterly basis and (ii) address the issues surrounding the failure to reconcile the records between  the Accounting Office and the CGSO; and (c) Department Heads of all offices request only the necessary supplies to avoid over-stocking and to avoid accumulation of various supplies at the CGSO’s CSA and prevent spoilage of goods, or loss due to misuse or possible theft.

We further recommended that the (a) CGSO consolidate weekly the RIS using the SSMI and submit to the Accounting office for recording of issued supplies; and (b) the Accounting office follow the prescribed point in the recognition of supplies purchases or inventory in the books.
observations.managementAction: To resolve the issue on overstocking, the CGSO will assess the request of office supplies from each department by looking into the supplies still unclaimed, if any, stockpiled at the Central Storage Area.  Further, the request for procurement of supplies will be done semestrally instead of quarterly which was being done in the previous years to avoid stockpiling thereof.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant submit to the Audit Team without further delay the cash advance vouchers together with the pertinent documents required under COA-DBM-DILG-GOCC-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 for post audit.
observations.managementAction: Management henceforth assured the submission of cash advance vouchers for Confidential Funds together with the pertinent documents to support the granting thereof in accordance with the latest circular.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: (a) City Accountant and City Treasurer: (i) strictly comply with the timely rendition of accounts and financial reports so as not to delay the verification of transactions and post-audit activities; and (ii) observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team; (b) City Accountant require the accounting staff concerned to scan only documents which did not undergo scanning or reproduction from preceding or originating departments; and (c) Local Chief Executive: (i) require all concerned accountable officers to submit on time all financial documents and reports for their respective transactions for each month for the preparation of the financial reports; and (ii) impose sanctions against erring accountable officers who continuously fail to abide with the timely submission of documents and reports.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office stated that the total scanning of disbursement vouchers and supporting documents which serve as back-up copies caused the delay in the submission thereof.  However, management agreed to speed up the process on scanning wherein only those material transactions will be scanned.  As of to date, disbursement vouchers for November 2016 were already submitted, while those for December 2016 remained unsubmitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the department head/s or personnel concerned to comply with dispatch the completion and submission to the Audit Team of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) for CY 2015 AAR and on every AAR thereafter.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to communicate to the concerned department heads with issued audit observation memoranda to consolidate comments thereof and formulate corrective actions for implementation in the ensuing year.  The City Mayor also instructed concerned officials to cause the timely submission of the AAPSI in compliance with the aforementioned law.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: (a) LSB (i) carefully study, plan and determine the annual budgetary needs for the operation and maintenance of public schools within the City which shall be supported by programs, projects and activities under Section 100 of R.A. No. 7160 as updated in DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular 2017-001; and(ii) members of the LSB maximize the utilization of SEF within the ambit of its intended objectives; and(b) City Budget Officer and City Accountant monitor the proper utilization and charging of expenditures to SEF.
observations.managementAction: The recent issuance of  Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s 2017 of the  Department of Education (DEPED),  Department of Budget and Management  (DBM) and Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG)  gave more specific guidelines on the utilization  and optimum  use of  SEF.   In line with this,   the Local School Board, through its letter-response dated March 14, 2017, committed strict adherence to the provisions thereon to maximize the use of the fund and to fully support the maintenance and operation of the City’s several public schools.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CDRRMO and the LDRRMC take the necessary action for the timely and proper recommendation to the SP for the lifting of the state of calamity under appropriate conditions thereafter, in order to prevent any untoward abuse of the circumstances.
observations.managementAction: Management gave the assurance that no untoward abuse will be made in lieu of the non-lifting of the City’s state of calamity.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CSWDO as the lead office in the disaster preparedness aspect of determining the adequate and appropriate relief goods both for stockpiling and distribution to disaster victims (a)  summarize in database form immediately as possible all information in the access cards; and (b) conduct actual validation of the affected families and individuals sheltered in the designated evacuation camps as well as those in the community on a per barangay basis,  to include  data such as age group, assessed needs, and health condition for those with maintenance medication, in order to come up with the updated disaster victims’ profile to ensure adequacy of the volume of relief goods for stockpiling and distribution and to serve as basis for the ensuing year’s procurement planning.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the CSWD has an existing list/profile of disaster victims of 2012 Habagat.   However, there was no updated profile available in CY 2016 due to lack of data extracted from the existing CSWD database.  As per letter-response dated March 9, 2017, it was  informed that this year, a sharp estimate of in displaced persons (IDPS) by age group and gender  can already be provided based on the consolidated profiles generated from the 2016 Habagat Access Cards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) CSWDO  to (i) closely monitor camp situations in order to provide reasonable comfort to disaster victims through appropriate services, goods, and facilities giving due importance to gender concerns; and (ii) ensure that the planning of procurement needs is made in the first quarter of the year, ahead of the anticipated disaster to address the basic needs for food, clothing, medicines, and shelter and procurement inputs are communicated to concerned officials in a timely manner (iii) submit the validated list/profiles of affected families and individuals per age group to the head of the GSO to come up with a realistic number of disaster victims in order to procure the necessary and adequate basic needs of victims during disaster, taking into consideration the latest data on the recent calamity; (b) CDRRMO to (i) ensure the availability and serviceable condition of all rescue equipment and vehicles for efficient and effective disaster response; (d) City Health Officer to submit to the CDRRMO the procurement request, ideally in the 1st quarter of the year for the necessary medicines and medical supplies for water and air-borne diseases for consolidation of procurement for disaster preparedness; and (e) City Engineer to (i) prepare a program of work for the tail dike project for submission to the National and Provincial Governments for possible funding allocation; (ii) include in its budget proposal the allocation for evacuation center equipped with adequate facilities for senior citizens, special children, newborn, and with consideration to gender concerns.
observations.managementAction: Management stated that the tail dikes are part of national projects, hence proper maintenance thereof rests with the national government. However, management shall continue to improve its systems on disaster preparedness to achieve a much better disaster response and committed to utilize the Local Disaster Risk and Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) in accordance with existing regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GAD Focal Person and Technical Working Group: (a) prepare the GAD database in coordination with the LPDO which will serve as basis for planning, implementing, and formulating GAD-responsive activities; (b) use monitoring tools such as the HGDG and PIMME in monitoring and evaluating GAD activities; (c) submit to the Audit Team the annual accomplishment report for evaluation; and (d)  improve further the schedule in GAD planning and budgeting as prescribed under Joint Circular 2013-01 of DILG, PCW, DBM, and NEDA.
observations.managementAction: Management concurred with our recommendations particularly in the establishment of a GAD database which is expected to be completed in the current year. In addition, the utilization/ accomplishment report for CY 2016 was already submitted to the audit team for review.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: (a) Local Health Board and/or the City Health Office prepare a PhilHealth Per Family Payment Rate Fund Utilization and Program Plan that is integrated in the City’s Annual Investment Plan; (b) Accounting Office comply with the submission of the required PFP Disposition and Allocation Form every quarter and other reports needed for monitoring and evaluation of the program relative to the releases of the PFPR Fund as required by PhilHealth Circular No. 015, s. 2014; and (c) City Health Office and Accounting Office abide by the provisions of PhilHealth Circular No. 10, s. 2012 on the proper disposition and allocation of the Fund.
observations.managementAction:
The management, through its letter - reply dated March 2, 2017, explained that the PhilHealth TWG and Local Health Board regularly convene wherein appropriate management mechanisms on the proper utilization of the fund were discussed. While the parties exerted efforts to integrate the PFPR Funds in the Annual Investment Plan of 2015 and 2016, yet it did not materialize since some particular items of expenditures were adjusted based on the existing needs of the Rural Health Units.

The Board also admitted the delay in the submission of quarterly PFPR Fund Disposition/Allocation liquidation report and committed to cause the timely submission thereof on a quarterly basis. They also cited that constant delays in the regular releases of the PFPR Fund by PhilHealth contributed to the accumulation of the PFPR Fund.  

As for the utilization of the fund, Management stated that some administrative expenses of the RHU were actually charged thereto. However, infrastructure projects were not charged to the Fund since the City is a beneficiary of several other fund sources such as BUB, Health Facility Enhance Program and the Department of Health.  Also, various drugs and medicines included in the lists which were allowed to be procured and supposedly charged to the PFPR fund were already included in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) of the Central Pharmacy, but were inadvertently charged to the City’s General Fund instead of the PFPR Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the: (a) CAVO to (i) closely monitor the deliveries of livestock for an efficient dispersal program in the future; and (ii) perform a regular visit to dispersed animals to determine their actual condition and prevent further undue losses;  and (b) GPBP focal person of the respective implementing offices to strictly monitor the implementation of projects as targeted and planned and take note of any gap for future inputs especially on the delay of implementation and completion of GPBP-funded projects and programs.
observations.managementAction: The GPBP focal persons agreed with our recommendations and assured the audit team of better implementation, monitoring, and evaluation of GPPB funded projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: (a) City Traffic Management Division continue to implement the DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2007-001, adopted thru City Ordinance No. 2008-015, and consider strengthening its information dissemination initiatives by posting more specific notices along the City boundaries on the  banning of tricycle operation in national highways; (b) Management (i) expedite the transfer of the CCTV control unit to facilitate a better monitoring operation not only by the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office but also by the City Traffic Management Division and Bureau of Fire Protection; and (ii) prioritize the need to upgrade the entire communication platform from wireless communication to a more reliable upgraded technology that will serve the recording and monitoring purpose of CCTV units.
observations.managementAction: As per letter- response dated March 6, 2017, management informed the Audit Team of the ongoing transfer of the CCTV Command Center.  It was also stated that the providers currently conduct fine-tuning of the cables and inspection of existing cameras for the initial set up of the monitors. They committed immediate commencement of the second phase of the project which includes upgrading and purchase of high-resolution CCTV cameras. Further, the CDRRMO will coordinate with the suppliers and concerned offices to expedite its completion.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board: (a) intensify the campaign to enjoin the majority of households’ participation on waste segregation, collection and disposal especially at the barangay level; (b) in coordination with the barangay officials and other offices/organizations, conduct information and education campaigns on the hazards of open burning, open dumpsites and other ill practices and enforce strictly the sanctions against violators; (c) ensure that the piles of garbage  of all barangays are properly collected  to prevent the residents from improperly disposing of their waste along the roads and other public places and from resorting to open burning of waste; (d) include in the Annual Investment Program PPAs that would ensure long-term management solid waste and implement the same; and (e) strengthen the information campaign by putting up signage in public places the acts prohibited under Section 48 of R.A. No. 9003 and the corresponding sanctions thereof.
observations.managementAction:
As per letter-reply dated March 2, 2017, it was confirmed that there are still some barangays that are lagging behind despite the implementation of the provisions of RA 9003.  However,   quite a number of barangays have also sustained the said program.  The City has identified four (4) barangays which are to be issued “Notice of Non-Compliance”. These barangays were already reported to the City Solid Waste Management Board pending issuance of the said notice.

Also, the City Government, through CENRO, is looking for more innovative ways to assist concerned barangays to be fully compliant in the environmental law. An ordinance strengthening the enforcement of RA 9003 for both individual and barangay violators were included in the Executive-Legislative Agenda (ELA).  CENRO will also pursue the issuance of Notice of Non-Compliance to four (4) barangays that have continuously failed the quarterly evaluation..

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, thru the City Administrator (a) require the overall Project Supervisor to (i)  submit written explanation for the lack of intervention to the delay in the delivery of systems to the City; (ii) submit an assessment report on the project’s actual status including issues surrounding the Project; (b) demand from the system developer the submission of a revised workplan  providing the timelines for the immediate completion of the required deliverables; (c) direct the City Accountant to hold the processing of claims of the system developer until pending issues are resolved in the City’s favor; (d) impose liquidated damages for the implementation delays of the system developer, as warranted; (d) ensure that projects are properly monitored and assessed to prevent further delays in implementations; (e) submit written explanation on the full payment to the system developer despite non-completion of the deliverables in the contract; (f) ensure that pilot testing is done, if parallel run is too costly,  during implementation of the system to mitigate the risk of data loss in case the system proved to be unsuccessful; (g) conduct change readiness briefing to departments concerned whenever migrating to an entirely new system to gain their support; (h) enjoin department heads to cooperate and commit themselves to pave the way for the completion of the project before the general revision of assessed values in CY 2017.
observations.managementAction:
Management agreed to meet with the system developer, INFOMAN, and other concerned department heads to resolve the issue on the delay in implementation of the Geographical Information System until everything is done in favor to the City.  Further, management informed the Audit Team that additional billings on top of the original cost of the contract was agreed upon by concerned department heads which  are supported and accompanied by written documentations. 

As a rejoinder, the Audit Team suggests that additional billing should not be paid to the system provider despite written agreement among the parties. Problems should be resolved in favor of the City, especially with regard to the imposition of liquidated damages for the long delay of the project. The system should be operational and should be better than the current system in place considering the materiality of money spent for the said project.

observations.recommendation:
Accountable officers with outstanding balances be required
to immediately submit their liquidation report/documents
pursuant to Section 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997.

The Accounting Section be required to record the
liquidation documents submitted by the concerned Accountable
Officers so that the corresponding expense or related
accounts will be booked up.

Management should impose sanctions on Accountable Officers
who fail to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed
period thru the withholding of their salaries as provided under
Section 5.1.3 of the above Circular, especially those with
unliquidated balances totaling more than P50,000.00 and those
with cash advances which have remained outstanding for more
than one (1) year.

Management should strictly comply with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the granting,
utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction:
AAR   
2012  
Significant amount of unliquidated cash advances
were related to confidential expenses. Liquidations
were already sent to COA Central Office and still
waiting for the Credit Notice, hence still reflected
as part of the unliquidated cash advances. Demand
letters were also given to other concerned officers
and employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the agency create a GAD Focal Point System for better ways of performing its task. The above mentioned guidelines serve as the proper way of structuring the GAD Focal Point System. Roles and responsibilities could have been acted upon, only if the GAD Focal Point System is fully-functional. Also, GFPS must submit the agency’s GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) within the month of January to February of each year to see to it that the GPB of the municipality is aligned with the priorities of the provincial government. Likewise, from the PPDO the GPB must be forwarded to the DILG Regional Office within the month of March of each year for appropriate review and approval. Actual GAD projects and activities be also aligned with the GAD plan per COA Circular 14-001 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: Create a GAD Focal Point System.
observations.reason: Apparent disregard of the importance of Gender and Development there being no allocation of the mandatory 5%.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the agency create a GAD Focal Point System so that duties and responsibilities will be properly carried out and objectives in promoting gender and development will be attained. The above mentioned guidelines serve as the proper way of structuring the GAD Focal Point System. Roles and responsibilities could have been acted upon, only if the GAD Focal Point System is fully-functional. Also, GFPS shall submit the agency’s GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) within the month of January to February of each year to see to it that the GPB of the municipality is aligned with the priorities of the provincial government. Likewise, from the PPDO the GPB shall be forwarded to the DILG Regional Office within the month of March of each year for appropriate review and approval. Actual GAD projects and activities should also be aligned with the GAD plan per COA Circular 14-001 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01. Lastly, the 5% allotment for GAD budget should be followed to maximize GAD projects and activities.
